## Thread: IBM Mainframe COBOL - What versions do you use? — 26 this month / 33 total

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

_Thread started [2002-02](2002-02.md#t-36e02170ca)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM Mainframe COBOL - What versions do you use?

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-03-01T11:02:54+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c7eae72_1@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<3C7D65E2.91358189@attglobal.net> <3C7DF6DE.453B4F28@worldnet.att.net> <3C7E3472.FD3782EE@certcoinc.com>`

```

Pat Hall <phall@certcoinc.com> wrote in message
news:3C7E3472.FD3782EE@certcoinc.com...
>
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> at y2k time with a few exceptions.  I've got 4 COBOL programs that
interface
> with
> assembler routines.  The assembler routines were written in the 60's and
> unless absolutely forced to
> do something they probably will not be touched.  Everthing else is
chugging
> along just fine.
>
> PatH who thinks assembler programmers are pretty scarce
>

If I was in your situation I would do a bit of research on Assembler. It
isn't hard. In the days when I was a young programmer (and computers were
new and exciting) if you wanted to call yourself a Programmer, you would
take on anything that was programmable.

I have little sympathy for people who have key functionality in their
business but can't maintain it because "It's in Assembler (or ALGOL, or
JOVIAL, or some other "obscure" language) and we don't "do" that...").

 If you don't "do" that, then why did you do it in the first place?

If the answer is historical (and it usually is...) why didn't you get it
converted to something that you DO do while you still had the expertise to
do that?

If you realised too late that this was essential functionality and you have
no ability to maintain it, you have two options:

1. Learn how to do it.
2. Pay someone to do it for you.

(There are other options but they follow from these two...)

Neither of the above is particularly attractive. The first means having to
learn something and that implies effort and dealing with new situations
which means overcoming inertia so it's much easier to just say "Well, I'm
much too busy..."  The second means spending money and we're CERTAINLY not
going to do that...<G>

So the situation is tolerated. And people who call themselves "Programmer"s
are stuck with code they cannot maintain.

And that's why I have no sympathy for them <G>.

Pete.



> >
> >
>
```

```
sympathy.  I have 3 assembler programs that have been chugging along just fine
for 30 years doing exactly what they were meant to do.  They are small and
simple and if I wanted to rewrite/change them I would did out my assembler
manuals.  I have 3 or 4 programs in 'old ' COBOL that interface with them.
They too have been running for 30 years and don't need changing.  I really have
no desire to change any of it.  If I couldn't figure out how to change them I
would hire somebody.   The original programmer has since passed on so I'm
afraid he isn't available.

programs out there that I choose not to do anything to.
```

- **References:** `<3C7D65E2.91358189@attglobal.net> <3C7DF6DE.453B4F28@worldnet.att.net> <3C7E3472.FD3782EE@certcoinc.com> <3c7eae72_1@Usenet.com>`

```
"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:
> 
> Pat Hall <phall@certcoinc.com> wrote in message
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
> take on anything that was programmable.

I'm not the world's greatest assembler programmer, but I have done it
before.  I wouldn't mind doing it again and learning some more, but I
have a Boss, and the Boss has a Budget.  Therefore I don't always have
a free hand to work on what I want to.

There's also a "turf" problem in my shop.  There are several different
development departments.  In some cases we use an assembler subprogram
owned by another group.  We're not allowed to change it and must wait
for the owners to do so.  Or perhaps we own it, but if we change it
the other groups that use it would also have to test it.  Since they
don't have additional testing in their budget they veto it.  That's a
management problem.

> 
> I have little sympathy for people who have key functionality in their
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>  If you don't "do" that, then why did you do it in the first place?

In some cases, these assembler programs were written before I got out
of programming school...23 years ago.  Certainly before I started
working here.  The old programs were working fine, but the current
need for changes are driven by the need to upgrade to OS/390 2.10 and
to turn on Language Environment ALL31(ON).

> 
> If the answer is historical (and it usually is...) why didn't you get it
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> Pete.

One difficulty with some of these old assembler programs is that they
use SVC's or control blocks that must reside below the line.  So it
makes it difficult to build the assembler program as RMODE(ANY).  Not
impossible, but difficult.

Actually the biggest problem we have is with some Assembler MAIN
programs that call COBOL subprograms.  The assembler main program
cannot pass the Language Environment ALL31(OFF) parameter to the COBOL
subprogram without source code changes to the assembler main (CEEPIPI
or CEEENTRY).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: IBM Mainframe COBOL - What versions do you use?

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2002-03-01T04:51:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020228235101.21215.00000867@mb-mp.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3C7EF9E9.A55EAA7D@sprynet.com>`

```
>From: Frank Swarbrick infocat@sprynet.com 
>Date: 2/28/02 10:47 PM Eastern Standard Time

>
>We have yet to test any CICS programs under LE.  Does anyone know of any
>reasons why we might have to recompile either old-COBOL or COBOL II? 
>From all I've read we shouldn't have to.  (hope, hope!!)
>

What version of CICS are you using?  One thing we ran into during our Y2K
conversion when we went from 2.1.2 to 4.1 and LE is WORKING-STORAGE is not
automatically cleared like it used to be unless there is a setting in LE -
WSCLEAR(0) or something like that (definately starts with W and has the (0) at
the end).  We didn't have it set and it caused all sorts of grief with messy
screens and ugly data. Past programmers didn't initialize WS ::sigh::.  I
believe there was something else that had to be set as well but memory eludes
me. If you have any programs using TWA and something RELOAD the system wasn't
happy.   I didn't use the statements so they didn't stick in my mind. 

In any event it was highly recommended to us to get everything above the line
so that ment getting everything to at least COBOL II.
```

```
We're not going to change CICS versions at this point, so hopefully that
will make things easier.  Of course TS will be the next step...so...
```

```
"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> Frank,
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> believe this information is MUCH more current - even if it is for z/OS.

Well exactly.  I *have* read the COBOL for VSE/ESA Migration Guide and I
see nothing to indicate to me that I need to do anything special
(recompile/relink whatever) in order to run my DOS/VS COBOL programs or
my VS COBOL II programs under LE/VSE.  You seem to be making it sound
like there *are* some of these issues, so I was just wondering
specifcally what these issues are.  We have no ILC, other than some
assembler subroutines.  All of our COBOL II programs are RES/RENT.  The
one issue we had was the inclusion of DLZBPJRA in out COBOL II batch
DL/I programs, which in turn included ILBDSET0.  Apparently the LE
version of ILBDSET0 needs to be link-edited in.  However, it says we
don't have to include DLZBPJRA at all either under COBOL II or LE
runtimes, so we are simply recompiling these now under COBOL II.  So
when we get to LE we won't have a re-compile issue at that time.

Anyway, I do thank you for your help.  I just have yet to find any big
issues other than the one mentioned above and maybe some run-time
settings.  So if I'm missing something huge I'd like to hear about it! 
:-)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: IBM Mainframe COBOL - What versions do you use?

- **From:** Colin Campbell <cmcampb@attglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2002-03-01T06:26:43-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C7F8FA3.8ADEBA8A@attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<3C7D65E2.91358189@attglobal.net> <3C7EF9E9.A55EAA7D@sprynet.com>`

```
Frank Swarbrick wrote:

> <Big snip>

> One question...why is your customer not using the LE runtime for their
> old-COBOL batch programs?  As I said, once we verify that all of our
> batch programs (old-COBOL and COBOL II) can run under LE that's what
> we'll do.
>

Frank,
That customer is using the OS/VS COBOL run time library in the system Link
List ahead of the LE library.  I wasn't around when that decision was made,
but my strong suspicion is that they simply have kept running the way they
were running before LE came along to mess up their simple world.

One of our first steps in upgrading them is going to have to be trying to run
their batch programs with LE support in place of OS/VS COBOL support.  This is
something that they should have done at least 4 - 5 years ago.  There will no
doubt be interruptions in service, lots of JCL "fixes", etc.  I'm guessing
that these things were foreseen, and that's why they've never tried to use the
new run time.
Colin
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM Mainframe COBOL - What versions do you use?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-01T13:16:09-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a5ok1u$447$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3C7D65E2.91358189@attglobal.net> <3C7EF9E9.A55EAA7D@sprynet.com> <3C7F8FA3.8ADEBA8A@attglobal.net>`

```
The one thing that IBM is ABSOLUTELY clear on is
  NEVER,
     NEVER,

concatenate run-time libraries (either in the Steplib, Joblib, OR linklist).

This puts you into SERIOUS jeopardy.  Not only are you (they) unsupported -
but they SHOULD expect disastrous results, the minute they DO start any
migration.  If you/they need any documentation on this, please let me know.

P.S.  It *is* supported to have OS/VS COBOL in linklist, and LE in Steplib -
or vice versa - assuming you do NOT mix object code produced by multiple
levels of the compiler.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM Mainframe COBOL - What versions do you use?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-03-01T19:48:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a5oln4$bnv$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<3C7D65E2.91358189@attglobal.net> <3C7EF9E9.A55EAA7D@sprynet.com> <3C7F8FA3.8ADEBA8A@attglobal.net> <a5ok1u$447$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

On  1-Mar-2002, "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

> The one thing that IBM is ABSOLUTELY clear on is
>   NEVER,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> linklist).

We do this all the time.  Could you clarify the problem?

> This puts you into SERIOUS jeopardy.  Not only are you (they) unsupported
> -
> but they SHOULD expect disastrous results, the minute they DO start any
> migration.  If you/they need any documentation on this, please let me
> know.

Where is the difficulty, when one called and calling programs have been
compiled by different compilers?

Here's an example of my step lib:
//TEFI#04.STEPLIB    DD DSN=UMS.TEST.LOAD,DISP=SHR
//           DD DSN=UMS.STG1TEST.LOAD,DISP=SHR
//           DD DSN=UMS.PROD.LOAD,DISP=SHR
//           DD DSN=CEE.SCEERUN,DISP=SHR
//           DD DSN=IDMS.SYSTEM&SYSNUM..LOADLIB,DISP=SHR
//           DD DSN=IDMS.&VER..LOADLIB,DISP=SHR

The first two steps are used for test.  The subsequent 4 steps are what the
production proc uses.  We used to have a mixture of ANSI 68 & 72, but now
virtually everything is CoBOL for MVS & VM 1.2.2.   We have a few
applications that we haven't been able to get converted from CoBOL 2 (and C)
which are used for communicating with cash machines, and we have a
complicated procedure to compile everything in order for them.

The CoBOL for MVS programs usually have calls, not counting what the
pre-compiler puts in them.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM Mainframe COBOL - What versions do you use?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-03T15:20:19-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a5u42t$od4$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3C7D65E2.91358189@attglobal.net> <3C7EF9E9.A55EAA7D@sprynet.com> <3C7F8FA3.8ADEBA8A@attglobal.net> <a5ok1u$447$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <a5oln4$bnv$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
OK ,
  Let look at what happens when you concatenate run-time libraries (either
in Steplib or Linklist)

1) If the LE library is FIRST, then you will *not* have any run-time
problems. Why?  Because, you simply will NEVER use any of the routines from
the "OldBOL"  (OS/VS COBOL, VS COBOL II, DOS/VS COBOL, whatever) library.
EVERY routine that exists in the OldBOL library has an IDENTICALLY named
(but NOT necessarily identical object code) routine in the LE library.
  This means that your "concatenation" PROBABLY won't hurt you - it simply
does NOTHING - and you never will use any of the routines from the 2nd
library in the concatenation.

2) If the "OldBOL" library is FIRST in the concatenation, then you won't
have any problems *IF AND ONLY IF* the only application object code that is
executed - comes from the OldBOL compiler.  In other words, if you NEVER use
any routines from the LE library.  Again, this is a USELESS concatenation,
but shouldn't have "too many" problems.

So where do the problems come in?

If you have the OldBOL library FIRST *and* if even ONE program in the
application uses even ONE routine from the LE library.  Why?
  LE does a LOT of set-up when the first program in the run-unit is
"loaded".  This includes setting up of an "internal" table of where to
"find" all the COBOL (ILBO, ILBD, IGZ, whatever) routines.  Therefore, if
the first COBOL program in your run-unit is OS/VS COBOL (or VS COBOL II or
DOS/VS COBOL), this table is NOT set up, but instead the OLD (pre-LE) method
of finding run-time routines is "started".  Then when there is an
application need to "get to" one of the new routines occurs, it simply isn't
there BUT this isn't a main program, so
   RESULTS ARE UNPREDICTABLE.

Similarly,  If the COBOL main program is an IBM COBOL for this-and-that
program, and the OldBOL library is first in the concatenation, the "new"
(LE-ish) table *is* correctly set up and points to where to get all the
correct COBOL routines *FROM* the LE library - this includes BOTH IGZ *and*
ILBO routines (OldBOL and NewBOL).  Therefore, if even one OldBOL
application program is called *or* a NewBOL program "needs" an OldBOL
routine, the table of support routines points to the LE library *BUT* the
concatenation points to the OldBOL library. Again
  RESULTS ARE UNPREDICTABLE.

BOTTOM LINE:
  If you concatenate  LE before OldBOL libraries, this won't hurt you - it
just is USELESS
  If you concatenate OldBOL before LE, then things are OK
    BUT only as long as you never use any routines from the LE library.
    If you *do* use some routines from one library and other routines from
the other library then the BEST you can hope for is  "RESULTS ARE
UNPREDICTABLE".  (This doesn't mean that every program will "die" - it just
means that you are NOT supported and that someday, some modification to your
application is going to "die" and THEN you will find out why you never
should have done this in the first place.)
```

```

linklist).
>
> This puts you into SERIOUS jeopardy.  Not only are you (they)
unsupported -
> but they SHOULD expect disastrous results, the minute they DO start any
> migration.  If you/they need any documentation on this, please let me
know.
>
> P.S.  It *is* supported to have OS/VS COBOL in linklist, and LE in
Steplib -
> or vice versa - assuming you do NOT mix object code produced by multiple
> levels of the compiler.

Worked for a client having 10,000+ load modules, and concatenated Runtime
STEPLIBs, with no trouble. They even have some in the procs as symbolics,
then overridden the JCl. So long as the order was OK, I didn't hear of
problems. It seemed to be OK during the conversion from OS/VS & COBOLII to
370.

>
> --
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> > That customer is using the OS/VS COBOL run time library in the system
Link
> > List ahead of the LE library.  I wasn't around when that decision was
> made,
> > but my strong suspicion is that they simply have kept running the way
they
> > were running before LE came along to mess up their simple world.
> >
> > One of our first steps in upgrading them is going to have to be trying
to
> run
> > their batch programs with LE support in place of OS/VS COBOL support.
> This is
> > something that they should have done at least 4 - 5 years ago.  There
will
> no
> > doubt be interruptions in service, lots of JCL "fixes", etc.  I'm
guessing
> > that these things were foreseen, and that's why they've never tried to
use
> the
> > new run time.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM Mainframe COBOL - What versions do you use?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-03T15:27:14-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a5u4ft$38f$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3C7D65E2.91358189@attglobal.net> <3C7EF9E9.A55EAA7D@sprynet.com> <3C7F8FA3.8ADEBA8A@attglobal.net> <a5ok1u$447$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3Mag8.21000$FY5.904698@news2.west.cox.net>`

```
For the "support" statement on this issue, see

     http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/igy3mg00/2.1.3.2.1?

which says (in part),

"Do not make more than one COBOL run-time library available to your
applications at execution time. For example, there should be one and only
one COBOL run-time library, such as SCEERUN for Language Environment, in
LNKLST. If you have more than one, you will either get hard-to-find errors
or you will have an unused load library in your concatenation. When you add
Language Environment to LNKLST/LPALST, remove any other COBOL run-time
libraries, such as COBLIB and COB2LIB."
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM Mainframe COBOL - What versions do you use?

- **From:** Frank Swarbrick <infocat@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-02T00:12:08-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C807B48.5CA9945@sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<3C7D65E2.91358189@attglobal.net> <3C7EF9E9.A55EAA7D@sprynet.com> <3C7F8FA3.8ADEBA8A@attglobal.net>`

```
Colin Campbell wrote:
> 
> Frank Swarbrick wrote:
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> new run time.

Hmm...  Our DOS/VS COBOL programs are link-edited with the DOS/VS COBOL
library, but they're run with the COBOL II runtime.  (We don't have
DOS/VS COBOL in our system standard LIBDEF.  It's only used in the
DOS/VS COBOL compile jobs).  Anyway, we've never had any problem that I
know of with this.  Things seem to work fine running with the LE runtime
as well, though we haven't done much testing yet.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM Mainframe COBOL - What versions do you use?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-02T12:39:41-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a5r69m$aog$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3C7D65E2.91358189@attglobal.net> <3C7EF9E9.A55EAA7D@sprynet.com> <3C7F8FA3.8ADEBA8A@attglobal.net> <3C807B48.5CA9945@sprynet.com>`

```
Although the DOS/VS COBOL (like the OS/VS COBOL) compiler is "LONG" out of
service (and support), are you, your programmers, and your USERS aware that
*if* you link-edit your programs with the LE run-time library, that they ARE
supported - while if they stay with the DOS/VS COBOL routines in their
link-edit, they are not?

What this "means" is that
 - IBM simply WILL not "fix or support" the compiler
       HOWEVER
 - if you have a run-time problem (due to a change in OS, CICS, or whatever)
*and* your application is link-edited with the LE run-time library's
routines, that you ARE supported.

To me this is REAL advantage for doing re-link-edits either in a "batch" or
as quickly as possible.

P.S.  Are you RES or NORES in your non-CICS programs?  This makes a
medium-big difference in upgrades.  CICS programs have been RES for years,
so I assume that all those programs are RES (with BOTH DOS/VS COBOL *and* VS
COBOL II)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM Mainframe COBOL - What versions do you use?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Frank Swarbrick <infocat@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-02T13:11:21-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C8131E9.96228BC@sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<3C7D65E2.91358189@attglobal.net> <3C7EF9E9.A55EAA7D@sprynet.com> <3C7F8FA3.8ADEBA8A@attglobal.net> <3C807B48.5CA9945@sprynet.com> <a5r69m$aog$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> Although the DOS/VS COBOL (like the OS/VS COBOL) compiler is "LONG" out of
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> as quickly as possible.

Yes, I was aware of the link-edit situation.  I'm sure that once we get
LE running it production we will use the LE library for our DOS/VS COBOL
link-edits.  Our eventual goal is, of course, to get rid of DOS/VS COBOL
(and COBOL II, of course) altogether, but most of our batch DOS/VS COBOL
programs include *many* uses of the defunct "WRITE...AFTER POSITIONING"
and thus are not a 'slam dunk' when it comes to converting to the ANSI85
standard.  For CICS we are lucky to have only 48 remaining DOS/VS COBOL
programs and those only need a few tweeks to convert, such as
- commenting out uncommented REMAKRS.
- removing BLL cells and replacing with POINTERs and ADDRESS OF
references
- converting usage of EXAMINE or TRANSFORM to INSPECT

That's pretty much it (I just went over it yesterday) so that should be
fairly painless, thank goodness.
 
> P.S.  Are you RES or NORES in your non-CICS programs?  This makes a
> medium-big difference in upgrades.  CICS programs have been RES for years,
> so I assume that all those programs are RES (with BOTH DOS/VS COBOL *and* VS
> COBOL II)

RES/RENT for everything, batch and CICS.  In fact we have the COBOL II
compiler set to not even allow NORES or NORENT.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: IBM Mainframe COBOL - What versions do you use?

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2002-03-02T09:44:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C809EE0.F4878C1B@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<3C7D65E2.91358189@attglobal.net> <3C7EF9E9.A55EAA7D@sprynet.com>`

```
Frank Swarbrick wrote:
> (Snip)
> We have yet to test any CICS programs under LE.  Does anyone know of any
> reasons why we might have to recompile either old-COBOL or COBOL II?
> From all I've read we shouldn't have to.  (hope, hope!!)

I relocated an old OS/VS COBOL program with BMS map from a CICS 2.1.2
region to a CICS 4.1 region with LE, and the program worked just fine
even though I did not expect it to.  COBOL II should not be a
problem.  But if you upgrade your CICS to Transaction Server, you may
want to plan on eventually recompiling all your COBOL programs with an
LE-enabled COBOL compiler.

We're currently running CICS TS 1.3 with many programs that were last
compiled with COBOL II.

> 
> Most of this has come to a head in recent weeks because of storage
> violations in our CICS regions.  IBM's answer is "use COBOL/VSE, LE and
> CICS TS".  Will it really make a difference?  Who knows.  Personally,
> I'm all for it simply because I hate using old-COBOL.

I believe CICS 4.1 and Transaction Server have better storage
isolation than earlier releases of CICS, so it's possible that CICS
Transaction Server would prevent some storage violations.  But you
probably will need to fix the application program logic in some cases.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM Mainframe COBOL - What versions do you use?

- **From:** Frank Swarbrick <infocat@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-02T13:16:06-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C813306.BE122D54@sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<3C7D65E2.91358189@attglobal.net> <3C7EF9E9.A55EAA7D@sprynet.com> <3C809EE0.F4878C1B@worldnet.att.net>`

```
Arnold Trembley wrote:
> 
> Frank Swarbrick wrote:
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> probably will need to fix the application program logic in some cases.

Yeah, I heard that about TS.  I don't know how soon we'll get there, but
that's definately the eventual goal.  I think we'll have eliminated all
old-COBOL CICS programs before we get to TS.  Not sure if we'll have
recompiled all of our COBOL II with COBOL/VSE by then.  And if TS points
out some programming issues, well so be it.  We'll fix them and move on.
```

#### ↳ Re: IBM Mainframe COBOL - What versions do you use?

- **From:** Harry Carter <hpcarter@attbi.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-01T15:13:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ut5v7uovcgmgil1qi1i45ph9mjggab61jo@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<3C7D65E2.91358189@attglobal.net>`

```
On Wed, 27 Feb 2002 15:04:02 -0800, Colin Campbell
<cmcampb@attglobal.net> wrote:

>I'm part of a team studying the implications of our customers remaining
>on OS/VS COBOL.  The study started from one CICS shop that read the CICS
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

>There doesn't seem to be any imminent deadline for executing load
>modules compiled with OS/VS COBOL.  CICS made noises in this direction,

>Since looking at the first shop, the customer has told us that at least
>two more of their divisions are in similar shape.  (The first shop is
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>now.  This shop supports another large customer, and I recently learned

>I'm curious.  Those of you who can share the information, what does your
>shop look like in terms of load modules compiled (and executing) with
>OS/VS COBOL vs. VS COBOL II vs. "new COBOL" (COBOL/370, COBOL for MVS &
>VM, COBOL for OS/390 & VM, etc.)?

My shop believes in using only IBM supported compilers/DBs/etc. and so
we're continuously upgrading We trashed all OS Cobol as part of our
Y2k. Indeed going into Y2k it was a goal (not required if you had a
good reason not to) to go to Cobol for MVS. When IBM stopped support
for VS Cobol II, we recompiled all existing Cobol II source and
eliminated all Cobol II loads. As far as I know going to Cobol for MVS
from Cobol II was not traumitic. OS Cobol to Cobol for MVS, had its
fun, particularly in CICS (goodbye Service Reload).

We have just installed in prod, Cobol for 390 V2.2? (I've forgotten
the exact point release). And FWIW, we'd love to move up to a Z/OS
release from OS/390 if we could just get rid of DECDTF (not sure if
the acronym is quite right - it's a product that does data transfer
between a DEC and IBM that's not supported under Z/OS). This is
becoming an issue as I believe our version of OS/390 will go
unsupported once IBM releases the next  version of Z/OS.
```

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-03-01T11:11:31+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c7eae76$1_1@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<20020226192014.20502.00000621@mb-fz.aol.com> <a5lo2v$17$4@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net>`

```

D De Villiers <~ddevilliers99@lando.co.za> wrote in message
news:a5lo2v$17$4@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net...
> LacyFive,
>
>
> When I was in school, they taught us Turbo Pascal (v7.0) not something
like
> COBOL or even Java (now in schools they taught Java - Can you beleive that
> !)
>
JAVA is an IDEAL language for students because it introduces OO concepts
right from the start. People who have not had their senses dulled into
believing COBOL is the ONLY way to code (and that set certainly includes
students) take to OO like ducks to water. The people who have the most
problem, are those who have had years writing procedural languages (like
COBOL, PL/1, etc.) and have built a mindset (usually unconsciously) that
this is how things HAVE to work when it comes to programming...

If South Africa is teaching JAVA in schools I can only commend them on their
perspicacity.

Pete.
```

## Thread: e-mail — 7 this month / 13 total

_Thread started [2002-02](2002-02.md#t-155c2d9f40)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: e-mail

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-03-01T11:19:32+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c7eae79_1@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<898878c12e9cc777ea7355c4d685daa6.69693@mygate.mailgate.org> <e9ie8.39883$2v1.1245335@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com> <a5lo2u$17$3@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net>`

```

D De Villiers <~ddevilliers99@lando.co.za> wrote in message
news:a5lo2u$17$3@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net...
> I've read this email in detail and as a COBOL * newbie * I find it hard to
> beleive that COBOL doesn't have some package for sending email (Delphi
have
> such components, as does VB and Java)
>
You put your finger on it when you said "components"...

EMail capability is NOT part of the underlying Language (Delphi - Pascal
Based - has NO innate EMail capability, JAVA does it by invoking Classes or
components - there is NO built-in JAVA EMail capability.)

COBOL can do exactly the same. OO COBOL can interface directly to EMail (or
any other OO component) and standard COBOL can interface to components,
provided there is some "wrapping" of the interface.

Pete.

```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: e-mail

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-03-01T07:31:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C7F2E57.FBA27C70@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<898878c12e9cc777ea7355c4d685daa6.69693@mygate.mailgate.org> <e9ie8.39883$2v1.1245335@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com> <a5lo2u$17$3@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net>`

```
D De Villiers wrote:
> 
> I've read this email in detail and as a COBOL * newbie * I find it hard to
> beleive that COBOL doesn't have some package for sending email (Delphi have
> such components, as does VB and Java)

Delphi is a proprietry langauge somewhat based on Pascal.
VB is a proprietry language somewhat based on Basic.

'Pascal' and 'Basic' do not have EMail.

CobolScript is a proprietry language somewhat based on Cobol.
It does have EMail built in.

EMail packages are available for various Cobol implementations as can be
found in a few seconds with Google.

For sending EMails it is easy enough to access sendmail (or cmdmail for
windows) to do the job.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: e-mail

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "D De Villiers" <~ddevilliers99@lando.co.za>
- **Date:** 2002-03-02T12:46:09+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a5r4jb$f64$7@ctb-nnrp1.saix.net>`
- **References:** `<898878c12e9cc777ea7355c4d685daa6.69693@mygate.mailgate.org> <e9ie8.39883$2v1.1245335@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com> <a5lo2u$17$3@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <3C7F2E57.FBA27C70@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston,

> 'Pascal' and 'Basic' do not have EMail.

Yes! Some "Pascal" and "Basic" dialects (eg. Turbo Pascal and/or QBasic)
doesn't have EMail. But Delphi do (see Internet tab on component pallete)
and Visual Basic do it via MS Windows ActiveX components.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: e-mail

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-03-03T07:38:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C81D2DD.2C61C193@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<898878c12e9cc777ea7355c4d685daa6.69693@mygate.mailgate.org> <e9ie8.39883$2v1.1245335@e3500-atl1.usenetserver.com> <a5lo2u$17$3@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <3C7F2E57.FBA27C70@Azonic.co.nz> <a5r4jb$f64$7@ctb-nnrp1.saix.net>`

```
D De Villiers wrote:
> 
> Richard Plinston,
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> and Visual Basic do it via MS Windows ActiveX components.

You seem to have difficulty distinquishing between a language and its
dialects and derivitives.

There are ANSI (and other) Standards for Pascal, Basic and Cobol.  None
of these incorporate the specific ability to do anything with EMail.

Your comments was surprise that 'Cobol' could not do EMail.  Well nor
can 'Pascal' or 'Basic'.

Specific versions of these languages or derivitives and added libraries
or component can enable _any_ language to do this, or most other things.

As I have already pointed out there are everal ways of sending EMail
from various implementations of Cobol.  There is no reason why these, or
similar, cannot be done using Turbo Pascal or evem QBasic.
```

```
> that I can just use a combination of date and time but I'd prefer a proper
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
If you do not have the intrinsic function RANDOM, you can use
the code in this program:

IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
PROGRAM-ID.    RNDGEN.
AUTHOR.        FIMATOR AB  FIM W�STBERG.
DATE-WRITTEN.  2002-03-01 12:05 IN J�RF�LLA.

* THIS PROGRAM GENERATES RANDOM NUMBERS > ZERO AND < 1


ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
CONFIGURATION SECTION.
*=====================

SPECIAL-NAMES. DECIMAL-POINT IS COMMA.



DATA DIVISION.
WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
*=======================
*
01  DIVERSE.
    10  SVAR                    PIC X(1).

01  SLUMPTALSVARIABLER.
    10  SLUMPTAL-DISPLAY        PIC 9,999999999999.
    10  SLUMPTAL                PIC 9(3)V9(12).
    10  FILLER REDEFINES SLUMPTAL.
        20  SLUMP-HELTALSDEL    PIC 999.
        20  SLUMP-DECIMALDEL-1  PIC X(6).
        20  SLUMP-DECIMALDEL-2  PIC X(6).



PROCEDURE DIVISION.
STARTING SECTION.
*===============
*
    MOVE ZERO TO SLUMPTAL.
    ACCEPT SLUMP-DECIMALDEL-1 FROM TIME.
    ACCEPT SLUMP-DECIMALDEL-2 FROM TIME.


NEXT-RANDOM-NUMBER SECTION.
*==========================
*
GENERATE.
    COMPUTE SLUMPTAL = SLUMPTAL * 137.
    MOVE ZERO TO SLUMP-HELTALSDEL.

DISPLAY-IT.
    MOVE SLUMPTAL TO SLUMPTAL-DISPLAY
    DISPLAY SLUMPTAL-DISPLAY.

ASK-FOR-MORE.
    DISPLAY "MORE? Y/N"
    ACCEPT SVAR
    IF SVAR = "Y"
        GO TO NEXT-RANDOM-NUMBER.

SLUT.
    STOP RUN.


Regards
Fim.Wastberg@fimator.se
```

```
"Fim W?tberg" <fim.wastberg@fimator.se> wrote in message news:<7kJf8.22279$n4.4158024@newsc.telia.net>...
> "Dan" <dan@weekender.fsnet.co.uk> skrev i meddelandet
> news:a5mpjn$jrf$1@news7.svr.pol.co.uk...
…[76 more quoted lines elided]…
> Fim.Wastberg@fimator.se

This works, except for two things.

First, the multiplier 137 is too small to have good statistical
properties. Tack on a few more decimal digits before the 137 in your
multiplier. Second, since you are not adding a constant after the
multiply, your seed value must not be zero, or a multiple of 2 or a
multiple of 5, lest the sequence degenerate. Just check the last
decimal digit of SLUMP-DECIMALDEL-2 and
adjust if need be. [My old compiler also wants a paragraph name in the
STARTING SECTION.]
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Generating Random Numbers

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** epc8@juno.com (E P Chandler)
- **Date:** 2002-03-05T10:44:19-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7f64e2ff.0203051044.320cc9e@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<a5mpjn$jrf$1@news7.svr.pol.co.uk> <7kJf8.22279$n4.4158024@newsc.telia.net> <7f64e2ff.0203031144.4698f07e@posting.google.com> <zr%g8.23079$n4.4390067@newsc.telia.net>`

```
> > > AUTHOR.        FIMATOR AB  FIM Wï¿½STBERG.
> > > DATE-WRITTEN.  2002-03-01 12:05 IN Jï¿½RFï¿½LLA.

> > > GENERATE.
> > >     COMPUTE SLUMPTAL = SLUMPTAL * 137.
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> Fim.Wastberg@fimator.se

First, I was partially mistaken about the multiplier 137, it turns out
that it would be better to be 139. 137 is fine if the seed has only 4
decimal digits or less, but 139 doubles the period for seeds of 5 or
more decimal digits. See Donald Knuth's "The Art of Computer
Programming", 2nd ed. volume 2, page 20. [I investigated seeds with
1-4 decimal digits myself this past weekend. Knuth only talks about 5
or more decimal digits.]

The problem with 137 is that it is too small with respect to the
modulus. Let me refer you to an old article that discusses using pairs
of randon numbers to generate random values conforming to a normal
(gaussian) distribution. Although the authors conclude that the method
for generating normal values (the Box-Muller Transform) is at fault,
it turns out that the wild results they obtain arise because their
multiplier is too small.

Ref: Neave, H.R. On using the Box-Muller transformation with
multiplicative congruential pseudorandom number generators, Applied
Statistics, 22 (1973), 92-97.

Correlation between successive values is the problem. You might say
that it is easy to "cook" up a statistical or graphical test that
"flunks" a particular generator. This is true. All psuedo random
number generators that use the linear congruential method suffer from
some degree of "granularity".

For a discussion of the underlying theoretical problem, see

Marsaglia, George. Random Numbers fall mainly in the planes,
Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 61 (1968), 25-28.

For a more recent article , see the next citation and the discussion
that follows.

Park, Stephen K and Miller, Keith W, Random number generators: good
ones are hard to find, Communications of the ACM (Association for
Computing Machinery), 31(10), 1192-1201, 1998.

Meanwhile, I'll try to cook up a nice example and post it as a follow
up.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Generating Random Numbers

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** epc8@juno.com (E P Chandler)
- **Date:** 2002-03-05T20:05:16-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7f64e2ff.0203052005.5cbf9c35@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<a5mpjn$jrf$1@news7.svr.pol.co.uk> <7kJf8.22279$n4.4158024@newsc.telia.net> <7f64e2ff.0203031144.4698f07e@posting.google.com> <zr%g8.23079$n4.4390067@newsc.telia.net> <7f64e2ff.0203051044.320cc9e@posting.google.com>`

```
> You are right about the seed. Bettter to use date + time.
> 
> But you are saying "the multiplier 137 is too small to have good statistical
> properties". Prove it !!
 
I wrote fixed point decimal fractions to a file, then used two draws
to create 2 letter sequences of letters A to Z. I tabulated their
frequencies and computed chi-square, expecting 20 counts per cell.

10 runs with a multiplier of 137 gave these chi-square values with 675
degrees of freedom:

690.3 777.6 787.7 839.6 743.1
777.1 797.7 776.7 754.7 718.2

Since the critical value, at the 5% level of significance, of
chi-square for 675 degrees of freedom is approximately 736.4, having 8
out of 10 runs with extreme values of the statistic is good evidence
that the numbers are not in fact uniformly distributed.

Using a slightly larger multiplier of 1137 I obtained:

647.9 648.8 566.6 690.6 641.8
610.4 585.3 700.6 704.7 711.8

None of these values are extreme.
```

#### ↳ Re: Excel Sort method

- **From:** "Mike Kinasz" <mkinasz@cbspayroll.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-01T02:51:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<C0Cf8.15289$bs1.366952@sccrnsc01>`
- **References:** `<a41dabc6.0202281345.50e03da3@posting.google.com>`

```
Greetings....    the problem is the .Sort method has a specific declaration
if you are not going to be using named parameters...

in the VBA editor this is valid...   (probably similar to what you saw when
you recorded your macro)

Selection.Sort Key1:=Range("A7"), Order1:=xlAscending, Key2:=Range("B7") _
        , Order2:=xlAscending, Header:=xlGuess, OrderCustom:=1, MatchCase:=
_
        False, Orientation:=xlTopToBottom, DataOption1:=xlSortNormal

That previous call equates to

Selection.Sort Range("A7"), xlAscending, Range("B7"), , xlAscending, , ,
xlGuess, 1, False, xlTopToBottom

The parameters are this
.Sort(Key1, Order1, Key2, Type, Order2, Key3, Order3, Header, OrderCustom,
MatchCase, Orientation, SortMethod)

in your case you're not using the Type or the 3rd Range Key and 3rd order so
you just need to figure out how to pass all of the parameters but not
actually specifying a 3rd range.....   obviously in VBA it's nice and
easy.... just leave the parameter blank... ehhe.

Well hopefully that's a start for ya....  please let us know what
happens....   if you get stuck maybe I can also give it a whirl (also have
NE3.1 over here... just drop me an email or something).....

Good Luck,
Mike Kinasz




"Chet" <cseidel@ftlife.com> wrote in message
news:a41dabc6.0202281345.50e03da3@posting.google.com...
> I am looking for some advice from anyone who has successfully executed
> the Excel sort method from COBOL (MicroFocus NetExpress 3.1 flavor).
…[87 more quoted lines elided]…
```

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>

```

Chet <cseidel@ftlife.com> wrote in message
news:a41dabc6.0202281345.50e03da3@posting.google.com...
> I am looking for some advice from anyone who has successfully executed
> the Excel sort method from COBOL (MicroFocus NetExpress 3.1 flavor).

I have never used SORT in EXCEL and I don't use MF N/E so I would seem
highly unqualified to answer this question.

However, I do know something about OO and invoking Methods from OLE servers
so I'm hoping my observations might at least be a bit helpful :-)

the first thing you should do is look up what the parameters to the Method
are. This will help to explain why you are getting such weird results with
it working sometimes and not others.

Try the Object Browser in EXCEL. [Tools/macro/Visual Basic Editor/Object
Browser]

It shows the syntax as:

Function Sort([Key1], [Order1 As XlSortOrder = xlAscending], [Key2], [Type],
[Order2 As XlSortOrder = xlAscending], [Key3], [Order3 As XlSortOrder =
xlAscending], [Header As XlYesNoGuess = xlNo], [OrderCustom], [MatchCase],
[Orientation As XlSortOrientation = xlSortRows], [SortMethod As XlSortMethod
= xlPinYin])
    Member of Excel.Range

Given the above syntax, you can see that some of your invocations just pass
the wrong thing in the wrong place...

Now, it also tells us that the SORT Method is part of the EXCEL.RANGE
object. It might be worth having a look at the properties of this Object...
Maybe you can set some properties BEFORE you invoke the SORT method and that
MIGHT save passing some parameters at invoke time...


> I have been struggling with this one for too long and I can't seem to
> get the results I need.
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> initialize rangestring.

I haven't looked at the getRange Method but it seems curious to me that you
initialize rangestring then immediately move a value to it....

> move "A2" to rangestring.
> invoke ExcelObject "getRange"
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>

So, ColumnsRange now contains an object pointer of type "EXCEL.RANGE"
...good to go on invoking the SORT Method...


> This works:
>
It complies with the syntax so we would expect it to...

> invoke ColumnsRange "Sort"
>        using Key1Range.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
As the syntax requires "Key1, Order, Key2, type, Order"...I am surprised
that this works. I would have expected
a NULL parameter or a specified Order...

> invoke ColumnsRange "Sort"
>        using Key1Range
>              Key2Range.
>

> Also works:
>
This one complies with the syntax.

> invoke ColumnsRange "Sort"
>        using Key1Range
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
It is possible that the parameters are being passed by name rather than
position, but I wouldn't take a chance on that....
> invoke ColumnsRange "Sort"
>        using Key1Range
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
You need to be sure that the Order contains a value of type XlSortOrder and
I see you have a Usage that specifies that, so it should be OK. Have you
initialised it properly?

> invoke ColumnsRange "Sort"
>        using Key1Range
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Maybe I need to use the olesup class method "setNamedParameters"?

Yes, many OLE servers can accept named parameters. Personally, I STILL try
and keep them in the sequence specified and use NULL if I am omitting any.

> Same results.  Then add in the "Header" named parameter and it throws
> an exception.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Well, on the basis of "any advice" I have offered my 2cents worth...<G>

The bottom line is, use the Object Browser and try to really understand
exactly WHAT the Method requires, then provide it.

Good Luck!

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Excel Sort method

- **From:** david.sands@microfocus.com (David Sands)
- **Date:** 2002-03-03T02:03:47-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<89388452.0203030203.46efcb43@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<a41dabc6.0202281345.50e03da3@posting.google.com> <3c7f0f63_2@Usenet.com> <3c7f6966.50662697@news-server>`

```
Hi Chet,

An optional parameter in COM is actually a Variant of type VT_ERROR
with a value of DISP_E_PARAMNOTFOUND. You can create one of these in
COBOL as follows:-

      ***** Create an Optional parameter
           move VT-ERROR to vtype
           invoke OLEVariant "newwithtype" using vtype
               returning MissedParam
           invoke MissedParam "getVariantAddress" returning vpointer
           set address of aVariant to vpointer
           move DISP-E-PARAMNOTFOUND to VARIANT-VT-SCODE of aVariant

You will need the following in Linkage
       01  aVariant             VARIANT.
and also have copybook mfole included.

You should then be able to use the sort method as follows:-

           invoke SortRange "sort"
               using SortKey1
                     MissedParam   *> Ignore. Use DefaultOrder
                     SortKey2
                     MissedParam   *> Ignore the Type parameter
                     sortorder

You can use the MissedParam as a placeholder for any optional
parameters you want to miss. This should also apply to other methods
that can take optional parameters.

Regards
David.
```

```
The solution for the problem with sorting can be found here:

http://support.microsoft.com/default.aspx?scid=kb;en-us;Q229107

Contrary to the type library and help file, the correct order for
parameters for the sort method is 

Key1, Order1, Type, Key2, Order2, etc.

Thanks to everyone for the help.
- Chet Seidel
```

- **Date:** 2002-03-01T15:02:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<c02fd744.0202260929.582e965f@posting.google.com>`

```
On 26 Feb 2002 09:29:13 -0800, nivas_shyam@indiatimes.com (shyam
nivas) wrote:


>We are trying to create a DDL for a sample report in the Custom
>Statement Formatter. We have defined a maintenence card member that
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I'm not a CSF jockie, but in good old-fashioned Cobol work, a 4038
frequently means a file attribute mismatch (i.e. the LRECL in the
program doesn't match the dataset or the DCB info if you're creating
it). Whenever I get this error, there is ususally a sysout message
telling you that the program expected dataset XXX to have a record
length of x but the file specified had one of Y.  Of course, this all
assumes you're running this on a mainframe which I think you are.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
hello:

you need review a screen definition, if you give a name to each field,
you will be able read each field individualy, there are some examples to
do this in your samples.

example:

if you put this:

 SCREEN SECTION. 01 DISP-SCR BLANK SCREEN BACKGROUND-COLOR IS 7
 FOREGROUND-COLOR IS 0. 02 LINE 5 COLUMN 13 VALUE "ID-NUMBER:". 02
 ID-NUMBER-AREA LINE 5 COLUMN 25 FOREGROUND-COLOR IS 1 PIC X(6) TO
 ID-NUMBER AUTO.

the field ID-NUMBER-AREA is a -NAME- of field on screen, and you can
accept this field individualy
```

```
for the "free" installs - it's the portion of the compiler handling
the embedded SQL.  (I'm guessing based on what sample10 might be).

Optimization - so make sure you don't use the Optimize compiler
directive.
```

<a id="t-275e624fa0"></a>
## Thread: problem linking with MS Cobol 4.5 in NT — 2 msgs

### problem linking with MS Cobol 4.5 in NT

- **From:** Frederik Himpe <frederik.himpe.f1256.NO@SPAM.student.hogent.be>
- **Date:** 2002-03-02T17:01:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<pan.2002.03.02.18.01.01.66551.6944@SPAM.student.hogent.be>`

```
Hi,

I am using MS Cobol 4.5 in Windows NT 4 Workstation. Compiling goes fine,
but linking always fails with this message:

E:\cvs\cobol>c:\cobol\binb\link main +adiskey +adisinit +extfh +adis

Microsoft (R) Segmented-Executable Linker  Version 5.15
Copyright (C) Microsoft Corp 1984-1991.  All rights reserved.

Run File [main.exe]:
List File [NUL.MAP]:
Libraries [.LIB]:
Definitions File [NUL.DEF]:
LINK : warning L4051: COBAPI.lib : cannot find library
Enter new file spec:

C:\COBOL\LIB\COBLIB.lib(.\HSCORE.ASM) : error L2029: 'DOSWRITE' : unresolved ext
ernal
C:\COBOL\LIB\COBLIB.lib(HLOADR.ASM) : error L2029: 'DOSGETPROCADDR' : unresolved
 external
C:\COBOL\LIB\COBLIB.lib(.\HSCORE.ASM) : error L2029: 'DOSEXIT' : unresolved exte
rnal
C:\COBOL\LIB\COBLIB.lib(HLOADR.ASM) : error L2029: 'DOSLOADMODULE' : unresolved
external


There were 4 errors detected

Any ideas if this could be solved? Or is it just because NT does not have
a real DOS anymore and thus unsolvable?

Frederik
```

#### ↳ Re: problem linking with MS Cobol 4.5 in NT

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-03-03T08:03:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C81D8EB.67A6DEFB@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<pan.2002.03.02.18.01.01.66551.6944@SPAM.student.hogent.be>`

```
Frederik Himpe wrote:
>
> C:\COBOL\LIB\COBLIB.lib(.\HSCORE.ASM) : error L2029: 'DOSWRITE' : unresolved ext
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> a real DOS anymore and thus unsolvable?

NT includes an OS/2 1.x 'personality' and MS Cobol 4.5 (but not 5.0)
includes the ability to compile OS/2 16 bit programs. Whan a 'family'
(DOS-OS/2) application is run it will test to see whether protected mode
is available and run as an OS/2 app if it can.  I suspect that you
installed MS Cobol with all options and managed to get the compiler to
run as an OS/2 program.  The default is to produce code for the same
environment as the compiler.

However, to link an OS/2 program it requires the OS/2 library which is
not provided by the compiler.  DOSWRITE etc are OS/2 API calls.

You need to specify that it is to use DOS libraries.
```

### Require recommendations for a Cobol Compiler

- **From:** "Mark Miles" <milesfam@idirect.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-02T19:50:52-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<u82sri4ih438f0@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Dear ALL,



            I have been using a Fujitsu Compiler Student version 3 to proof
concept an idea to my employer.  My employer has brought into my idea for
using Cobol for a future project and I want to now purchase a Cobol
compiler.



            The environments I intend to run my programs on will be: -



            3 Sun ES6500 14 CPU's, 6GB Ram running Solaris 2.6 (Production
Servers)

            2 Sun ES3500 4 CPU's, 4GB Ram one running Solaris 2.6, the other
2.8 (development servers).



            What I would like to do is code and system test on a Windows NT
2000 and XP Pro workstation then move the source code to one of the
development Unix servers to compile and produce binaries then QA test on the
Dev Unix systems.  When the QA process is complete I want to just copy the
binaries to the production servers.



            So I think I need a full development tool set for 3 Windows
workstations and 1 for the Unix Dev server, and 4 runtime license for the
other Dev server and 3 production systems.



            Which Cobol system would suite my requirements or do I stick
with Fujitsu, I have been told by my employer cost is not an issue.



Any recommendations?





Mark Miles
```

#### ↳ Re: Require recommendations for a Cobol Compiler

- **From:** "JJ" <jj@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-03T03:08:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Isgg8.181$rc.181297@monger.newsread.com>`
- **References:** `<u82sri4ih438f0@corp.supernews.com>`

```
This is not a recommendation, but more of a suggestion of something to
consider.

You could look at Acucobol.  One thing that I like about it (as a software
vendor) is that it compiles to psuedo-code (p-code, byte-code,
intermediate-code, or whatever you want to call it) instead of native binary
code, although the latest release can optionally do native code on some
systems.

This means that you could purchase the development license only for Win32,
where you want to develop and test.  Since the object code is portable, you
would only need runtime licenses for the Sun development and production
systems.  There would be no recompile necessary to run on the Sun systems.

Yes, native code runs faster, but that's not always a factor - in our case,
our software is not that compute-intensive.  Portable object code is much
more important for us.  Internally, we have Win32, Linux, and AIX systems,
but our end users also have Sun, HP-UX, SCO, etc.  We don't have any of
those systems, but we don't have to compile on end user systems or go to
porting centers - the object code is all the same.

This is not a knock against Fujitsu (in fact, we are looking at their
Cobol.NET).
```

<a id="t-ddebcc5964"></a>
## Thread: Mainframers: Take back the light (spotlight, that is) — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Mainframers: Take back the light (spotlight, that is)

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2002-03-04T18:23:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020304132358.17490.00001896@mb-mo.aol.com>`

```
Hi, 

I've been thinking about the perennial cry of "the mainframe is dead" and what
it means to us. Although we may laugh or pooh-pooh it, as insiders, I am afraid
there is a very real danger of us mainframe oriented folks getting so insular
we don't see the reality until it is too late.

The reality is, simply, we are allowing ourselves to be marginalized. In a
company, everyone knows about the PCs around; when people talk about "the
computer" they mean the PC. When management talks about spending money for IT,
it's for PCs, JAVA, and the Web. When kids go to college / university to learn
computer science, it's UNIX or Windows products.

Meanwhile, mainframes are quietly running the bread and butter applications of
the business. Telephone companies are not going to email 10,000,000 customer
bills each month, nor print them on 1,000 PCs lashed together. 

What we have learned (hoepfully) over the years is to use the right tool for
the work at hand. UNIX, PCs, and the Web are important: make no mistake. But we
in the mainframe world have lost the edge we once had, and we find few young
people interested in going into our part of the business (you can't blame them
if they don't know we exist or they consider us just dinosaurs in dead-end
work). With no attention from the up and coming youth, we have little future. 

What happens is often disasterous. I had one client where a manager made the
fiat: all new work will be UNIX and C, and we will be off mainframes in five
years. This for a shop with over 40 MVS images with 80% of the code written in
COBOL (and virtually no C skills inhouse). The result is he was let go after
two or three years into the program (with hefty a severance package, grrrrr),
the company lost ground, and morale was decimated.

Today, when we call to see about training applications programmers on the
mainframe, few of the people we initially reach even know their company has a
mainframe. If they know that much, they don't know who to put is in touch with.
We (mainframers) don't exist in the mind share of many folks.

But we can change that. We can take back the [spot]light.

When I joined IBM back in 1968 (!), they told us "Everybody sells". Those of us
who joined for reasons of learning the technology were at first put off by
this: sales people, after all, are slimeballs, right? But we gradually came to
understand that we sell to our colleagues, spouse, friends, family: we try to
convince people to do some activity we want to do, or to act in some way we
want them to act, and so on. So in this larger sense, it is true: everybody
sells (or if you don't you lose the sale).

We techies are very good talking to each other about arcane, or practical,
issues of code and options and how things work. And we are being blindsided by
a growing disinterest. We need to take some difficult [for most technical
folks] steps and reach out and promote our industry, as a matter of self
preservation. Or we will have no relevance in the larger world.

What can we do? Here's some ideas:

* have some events to involve a wider audience
  + open house / data center tour
  + brown bag luncheons to talk about applications
* reach out to internal publications, let them know there is a mainframe
in-house and the role it plays
* build web pages on the corporate website that explain what the mainframe
does, and who the relevant people are

I've put together a few skeleton web pages for such an activity, free for the
taking, if anyone is interested. Go to our home page at
http://www.trainersfriend.com and click on the link to "Free internal
marketing" which will give you a list of some skeleton pages ready for you to
tailor and upload to your corporate intranet.

I am personally very concerned, careerwise, and I hope many of you are, too.
I'm happy to add other skeleton pages at this location if anyone wants to
contribute to this project. And I'm happy to publicize other things we can do
to regain some awareness in the corproate and public mindset for mainframe
computing.

Of course, there are exceptions to everything I've said above: there are
schools that still teach mainframe topics; there are companies where the
mainframe is well known and well respected; there are managers who have some
balance and perspective; there are some young people going into the mainframe
part of IT. But they are in the minority. And we have to ask ourselves if that
is the right proportion.

Kind regards,

-Steve Comstock
The Trainer's Friend, Inc.
http://www.trainersfriend.com
800-993-8716 (from US and Canada)
303-393-8718 (worldwide)
```

#### ↳ Re: Mainframers: Take back the light (spotlight, that is)

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-03-05T12:18:39+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c8401f3_7@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<20020304132358.17490.00001896@mb-mo.aol.com>`

```
Thought-provoking post, Steve.

My comments below...

S Comstock <scomstock@aol.com> wrote in message
news:20020304132358.17490.00001896@mb-mo.aol.com...
> Hi,
>
> I've been thinking about the perennial cry of "the mainframe is dead" and
what
> it means to us. Although we may laugh or pooh-pooh it, as insiders, I am
afraid
> there is a very real danger of us mainframe oriented folks getting so
insular
> we don't see the reality until it is too late.
>
You are absolutely correct.

Time and again I find mainframe people resisting "new technology" with a
condescending attitude that PCs are toys and the REAL work is done on the
mainframe. In the last decade this attitude has changed into one of fear as
Client/Server has made large inroads into what was traditionally mainframe
territory.

Of course the mainframe isn't dead. It is an essential part of the Corporate
IT arsenal, but it no longer has the place as "top of the heap" which it
once held when it was "the only game in town" (prior to 1983). Neither
should it have, if we judge on merit and not self interest.

The fact is that true IT professionals will not owe special allegiance to
either the mainframe environment or the Workstation one. They should be
capable across IT platforms irrespective of what those platforms are. They
should be seeking to serve the rest of the enterprise with solutions that
utilise the best tools for the particular job, and that means hardware AND
software. The question of which platform should be favoured, or which should
have the "spotlight" is about as relevant as saying which photocopier or
mail sorter should make the cover of this month's internal magazine.

Today's IT Professional should be an all-rounder. Capable in (at least)
COBOL and Java; able to use the Workstation GUI interface just as easily as
the mainframe CLI; aware of the strengths and weaknesses of IT technology
and aware of emerging technology which could be of value to the corporation;
but, above all, instilled with a sincere desire to assist user departments
in achieving meaningful and useful solutions, in a spirit of goodwill and
comradeship. Using IT technology to make life easier for the rest of the
enterprise should be the primary goal. User unfriendly systems should not be
tolerated, whether mainframe based or based on the Network.The IT
professional should be proud to be a servant of the rest of the enterprise.
That means providing support not just at a technical level, but also able to
listen and understand the problems with departmental procedures and
policies; an understanding of the way the business works, not just the bits
and bytes.

When this comes about (and I've seen it in a few places) the IT department
is no longer sidelined. Their services are in demand and they are valued by
the rest of the organization. They don't need to claim the spotlight, it is
placed on them by the rest of their colleagues... And they usually turn the
accolades back to the User departments.

Any mainframers who develop this kind of attitude will NOT find themselves
sidelined. Not only that, but their careers can be expected to take off...

> The reality is, simply, we are allowing ourselves to be marginalized. In a
> company, everyone knows about the PCs around; when people talk about "the
> computer" they mean the PC. When management talks about spending money for
IT,
> it's for PCs, JAVA, and the Web. When kids go to college / university to
learn
> computer science, it's UNIX or Windows products.
>
There are a number of reasons for this. The arrogant historical attitude of
IT departments towards the rest of the Organization is not without weight
here. User departments were made to go cap in had to IT and ask for
solutions. They were very often made to feel like  idiots because they
didn't understand the technology, and the solutions they required were long
in coming and often inadequate when they arrived. The "technocrats" were
often a law unto themselves and were almost "unmanageable" even by their own
Managers. If anyone upset them they moved down the road and took their
expertise wth them. (To be fair, there were a lot of bad IT Managers around
too, which didn't improve the situation).

Suddenly people found they had a computer at home that their kids were
using, and it could do things that the multi-million dollar mainframe at
work couldn't. And, instead of reaching for this technology and building it
into the corporate IT solution, the IT people closed ranks, sniffed at the
"toy computers" being purchased by departments out of their own budgets and
tried to enforce restrictive rules to control the corporate data asset.

And so the traditional mainframe IT department became sidelined.

It was sidelined because (with few exceptions) it consistently failed to
deliver, and not only did it fail to deliver but it made people feel bad.

Small wonder then that user departments preferred to work with their own
systems, learning spreadsheets and word processing and databases by trial
and error, rather than be humiliated by IT programmers. At least if they got
it wrong it was private... As time has progressed there are more and more
"computer literate" people arriving in the workplace. These people have
higher expectations. They won't accept waiting months for the mainframe
Waterfall to trickle towards system delivery.


> Meanwhile, mainframes are quietly running the bread and butter
applications of
> the business. Telephone companies are not going to email 10,000,000
customer
> bills each month, nor print them on 1,000 PCs lashed together.
>
Don't be too sure...If it made sense to do so they certainly would. The days
of people requiring printed bills are numbered. Telephone bills can be
easily e-mailed to subscribers for printing on their own local printer. Your
argument is that the volumes involved  (say, 10,000,000 of them) are beyond
the capability of current networks, and the infrastructure (like a PC and
printer in every home that has a telephone) is not in place yet.

Both of these factors just require a little time... give it a few years. My
ISP bills me by e-mail (actually, notifies me of my usage and details of my
charges, then takes the money automatically by a direct debit.) The ways we
used to do things are no longer the ONLY ways...

> What we have learned (hoepfully) over the years is to use the right tool
for
> the work at hand. UNIX, PCs, and the Web are important: make no mistake.
But we
> in the mainframe world have lost the edge we once had, and we find few
young
> people interested in going into our part of the business (you can't blame
them
> if they don't know we exist or they consider us just dinosaurs in dead-end
> work). With no attention from the up and coming youth, we have little
future.
>
So, what are the options?

1. Just give it up and BE a dinosaur? (immerse yourself in
"mainframeabilia", become a system programmer and give up on applications
altogether. There are some people for whom this is the correct option, but
not for most of us...)

2. Expand your horizons beyond the mainframe into IT in general. (My
personal choice. Made it in 1983 when PCs first became generally available.
Never been sorry I did and now find working with TSO/ISPF as I did for
nearly 20 years before that, like wading through treacle...<G>. For me, the
instant response of a dedicated processor allows me to run at the pace I
want to...I would take going back to a time shared environment very hard.
But that's just me...)

3. Encourage youngsters to do more with mainframes.
(We should certainly be doing this in the simple interest of ensuring that
we have well-rounded IT people in the future...my argument cuts both ways.)

4. Several of the above...(I guess the point I am trying to make is that it
is no more desirable to be a "mainframer" than it is to be a "UNIXer" or a
"Windows developer"; any one of these, stunts your growth and limits your
maketability. However, a knowledge of all of them makes you a  VERY
marketable commodity.)

IT professionalism is about IT solutions. Why limit yourself?

I'm not sure I agree that mainframers have "lost the edge". Most of the
sites I work on have very competent mainframe staff who, for the most part,
really know their stuff. Certainly, I don't think the level of expertise now
is any worse than it was 25 years ago...(And we have a lot more standard
procedures and Methods in place than we did then...). If you mean "the edge"
in terms of corporate perception as to the importance of mainframe
technology, then I would agree. But, as I argued above, that is deserved and
accurately reflects the changing role of the mainframe in corporate IT.

> What happens is often disasterous. I had one client where a manager made
the
> fiat: all new work will be UNIX and C, and we will be off mainframes in
five
> years. This for a shop with over 40 MVS images with 80% of the code
written in
> COBOL (and virtually no C skills inhouse). The result is he was let go
after
> two or three years into the program (with hefty a severance package,
grrrrr),
> the company lost ground, and morale was decimated.
>
As I mentioned previously, there were (are?) some very bad IT Managers
about...this one got what he deserved. Corporations, (like individuals) must
be allowed to make mistakes so they can learn from them.

> Today, when we call to see about training applications programmers on the
> mainframe, few of the people we initially reach even know their company
has a
> mainframe. If they know that much, they don't know who to put is in touch
with.
> We (mainframers) don't exist in the mind share of many folks.
>
I'm sure that's true. There are also many who DO know you exist but try and
keep out of your way, as they regard mainframes and mainframers with
distrust and suspicion (historical, see above)

> But we can change that. We can take back the [spot]light.
>
> When I joined IBM back in 1968 (!), they told us "Everybody sells". Those
of us
> who joined for reasons of learning the technology were at first put off by
> this: sales people, after all, are slimeballs, right? But we gradually
came to
> understand that we sell to our colleagues, spouse, friends, family: we try
to
> convince people to do some activity we want to do, or to act in some way
we
> want them to act, and so on. So in this larger sense, it is true:
everybody
> sells (or if you don't you lose the sale).
>
See my comments on developing the right attitude, above. You are correct
that there is an element of sales in all of this. If being nice to people,
not humiliating them for lack of technical knowledge, and genuinely trying
to understand and help them constitutes "sales" then I'm all for it <G>

> We techies are very good talking to each other about arcane, or practical,
> issues of code and options and how things work. And we are being
blindsided by
> a growing disinterest. We need to take some difficult [for most technical
> folks] steps and reach out and promote our industry, as a matter of self
> preservation. Or we will have no relevance in the larger world.
>
This again, is right on the button, Steve.

The disinterest is being caused by an underlying Marketplace requirement for
less technicality. Users are demanding to be able to interact with complex
technology and make it do what they want. Everybody in the World is NOT
going to get a Computer Science degree... The FACT is that new generations
of smarter software will and are delivering this kind of ability. It is just
a question of time before "fortress mainframe" succumbs to it also. If
everyone on Earth had a mainframe in the garage (like most of them have a PC
in the living room) we would already be seeing much of the complexity in
mainframe software being removed. The whole idea of having to maintain a
specialist to apply PTF fixes to the OS  etc. is ludicrous in this day and
age. It will change.

> What can we do? Here's some ideas:
>
> * have some events to involve a wider audience
>   + open house / data center tour
>   + brown bag luncheons to talk about applications
I really like this... Excellent ideas.

> * reach out to internal publications, let them know there is a mainframe
> in-house and the role it plays
> * build web pages on the corporate website that explain what the mainframe
> does, and who the relevant people are
>
All of the above are good ideas. I would seek for bridges to groups working
with VB and Java and even the network control people... Maybe, look at some
of the newer features of COBOL...OO, COM, OLE, Net Services, CGI etc. (then
explain why these are NOT being implemented on the mainframe <G>).
Seriously, it wouldn't hurt to put mainframe programming into context and
examine the role that the mainframe fulfills within the organisation. Where
there are common threads (like Java) at least get mainframe and PC people to
know each other. It isn't a contest. It is an effort to understand all
aspects of  IT technology, in the interest of better serving the
organisation.

> I've put together a few skeleton web pages for such an activity, free for
the
> taking, if anyone is interested. Go to our home page at
> http://www.trainersfriend.com and click on the link to "Free internal
> marketing" which will give you a list of some skeleton pages ready for you
to
> tailor and upload to your corporate intranet.
>
> I am personally very concerned, careerwise, and I hope many of you are,
too.
> I'm happy to add other skeleton pages at this location if anyone wants to
> contribute to this project. And I'm happy to publicize other things we can
do
> to regain some awareness in the corproate and public mindset for mainframe
> computing.
>
All good stuff, but there is no harm in any/all of usexpanding our skill
sets also...

> Of course, there are exceptions to everything I've said above: there are
> schools that still teach mainframe topics; there are companies where the
> mainframe is well known and well respected; there are managers who have
some
> balance and perspective; there are some young people going into the
mainframe
> part of IT. But they are in the minority. And we have to ask ourselves if
that
> is the right proportion.
>
I have another question... rather than "Is a suitable proportion of trainees
going into mainframe programming?"

My question would be: "Are trainees in general being given a suitable
background in all aspects of their profession?"

That would include review of mainframe operations, programming, and
capabilities.

The object should be to produce well-rounded IT professionals for the needs
of tomorrow's workplace.

As for TODAY's workplace...I can only re-iterate, if you are planning a
career in IT, expand your skillset NOW!

Pete.




 Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services
----------------------------------------------------------
    ** SPEED ** RETENTION ** COMPLETION ** ANONYMITY **
----------------------------------------------------------        
                http://www.usenet.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Mainframers: Take back the light (spotlight, that is)

- **From:** gnuoytr@rcn.com (robert)
- **Date:** 2002-03-05T13:01:29-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<da3c2186.0203051301.6c1bcecb@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<20020304132358.17490.00001896@mb-mo.aol.com>`

```
scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock) wrote in message news:<20020304132358.17490.00001896@mb-mo.aol.com>...
> Hi, 
> 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
<snip>

> What happens is often disasterous. I had one client where a manager made the
> fiat: all new work will be UNIX and C, and we will be off mainframes in five
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
<snip>

it's not the mainframe that's dying, it's the traditional low-level
file based data model that has to be shed.  it can only work in a
one tier application.  true client/server (two tier) won't even
tolerate it.

from the point of view of a unix/database/etc. type who has ended up in 
a "dinosaur pit", the problem is less about the language war or the 
gui/chui war, but the data war.  while it was appropriate 20 years ago,
the COBOL/VSAM mindset is truly bad today.  this is especially true
where organizations (mine, in particular) transliterates VSAM file defs
into database schema, and think they've "converted" to <name DB of your
choice>.  the application COBOL I/O routines are kept intact, the 
metadata is still in the tables, SQL overhead plus procedural processing, 
and so on.  guaranteed to fail, but penny wise.  

data modeling, and accepting the word of Codd, are what will bring
COBOL-ers into the next millennium.  it's the other kind of reversing
the mindset:  procedure vs. object, procedural vs. relational.  the
three (or more) tier approach is as much about sets (the relational
model) as about objects.  and, by the bye, object databases are nonsense.

toodles,
robert
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Mainframers: Take back the light (spotlight, that is)

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-03-06T10:52:32+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c85410b_8@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<20020304132358.17490.00001896@mb-mo.aol.com> <da3c2186.0203051301.6c1bcecb@posting.google.com>`

```
Staggered by your post, Robert!

So much good sense in so little space...<G>

I believe you have put your finger on exactly what is "wrong" with the
"fortress mainframe" mindset.

However, I think you may be being a little too hard on the people who are
converting from VSAM/ISAM. Everybody has to start somewhere and it would not
be permitted to throw away everything and start over (even though, as you
correctly point out, this might well be the proper course to follow).

Personally, I'd rather see them converted to an open Relational Database
(even an imperfect one) than see COBOL locked into the file system and
slowly isolated until it dies...

Opening up the organisation's file-system-based data is a good first step.
As their facility with SQL improves and their understanding of the
Relational Model (even though much of this "understanding" is at a
subconscious level at first, and may not necessarily be verbalized into a
discourse on Normalization; they just do it because they can "see" that it
is more efficient (and more flexible) to remove repeating groups and combine
key dependencies...) improves, then the full power of the multi-tier model
becomes apparent and people start smiling again...<G>.

I have been advocating the use of multi-tier databases and the conversion of
COBOL systems to an open RDB for some years in this and older COBOL forums.
The resistance has been incredible. But it is understandable. When people
spend a lifetime doing something a certain way, and it "works", it is very
hard to persuade them that things have changed and a different approach is
now required.

Your post sums it up beautifully.

Pete.

P.S. I share your reservations about Object Databases and I think it's time
it was pointed out that Object Oriented programming doesn't NEED a special
database technology; Relational is fine....


robert <gnuoytr@rcn.com> wrote in message
news:da3c2186.0203051301.6c1bcecb@posting.google.com...
> scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock) wrote in message news:<20020304132358.17490
.00001896@mb-mo.aol.com>...
> > Hi,
> >
> > I've been thinking about the perennial cry of "the mainframe is dead"
and what
> > it means to us. Although we may laugh or pooh-pooh it, as insiders, I am
afraid
> > there is a very real danger of us mainframe oriented folks getting so
insular
> > we don't see the reality until it is too late.
> >
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> > What happens is often disasterous. I had one client where a manager made
the
> > fiat: all new work will be UNIX and C, and we will be off mainframes in
five
> > years. This for a shop with over 40 MVS images with 80% of the code
written in
> > COBOL (and virtually no C skills inhouse). The result is he was let go
after
> > two or three years into the program (with hefty a severance package,
grrrrr),
> > the company lost ground, and morale was decimated.
> >
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> robert
```
