<a id="t-997517c101"></a>
## Thread: COBOL and z/OS.e — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### COBOL and z/OS.e

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-04T21:56:45-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a61fma$ls8$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
There was a lot of "discussion" about using IBM COBOL pre-compiled programs
that use LE's pre-initialization interface in a z/OS.e environment (and
whether it was or was not "supported") - around the time of the original
z/OS.e announcement.  I think that I have found (at least for the moment) a
pretty definitive statement that they ARE supported.

See:
 http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/ap0ovr00/A.0?

Which says (in part):

"EDC5244I The program, module or DLL is not supported in this environment.





Explanation: The program, module or DLL specified on this C/C++ function
call contains one or more languages that are not supported on this version
of the operating system. FORTRAN and COBOL, with the exception of
precompiled COBOL DB2 stored procedures, and other precompiled COBOL
applications, using the Language Environment preinitialization interface
(CEEPIPI), are not supported."

Given that EXPLICIT exception, I have to believe that all COBOL pre-compiled
programs that DO use the LE  preinitialization interface *ARE* supported
under LE.

Similarly,
 http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/ap0ovr00/1.6?

which says (in part),

 "Customers who choose z/OS.e are not licensed for the traditional
workloads. z/OS.e disables traditional workloads. z/OS.e will not execute
CICS, IMS, COBOL, or FORTRAN applications. However, precompiled COBOL stored
procedures and other precompiled COBOL applications using the Language
Environment preinitialization interface CEEPIPI are supported. You cannot
use the following compilers: COBOL, PL/I, VisualAge PL/I, and FORTRAN."

Seems clear (to me) that

A) You MAY not (cannot?) run the COBOL, PL/I, or FORTRAN compilers on your
z/OS.e system

B) You MAY run COBOL (DB2) "stored procedures" under z/OS.e

C) You MAY run "precompiled COBOL applications using the Language
Environment preinitialization interface CEEPIPI"

D) Precompiled COBOL programs that are not "preceded" by LE
preinitialization services in the "run-unit" will "die a horrible death" at
run-time

E) COBOL programs that expect IMS and CICS (or non-COBOL programs for that
matter) are "out-of-luck" under z/OS.e

  ***

FYI - for those who missed "how easy" it is to use CEEPIPI with a COBOL
program, check out (again)

 "5.3.5 An example program invocation of CEEPIPI"
at

  http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/ceea2120/5.3.5?

which walks you "hand in hand" thru what is required.
```

---

<a id="t-dd56d97033"></a>
## Thread: Candidates for the *next* (post 2002) COBOL Standard — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### Candidates for the *next* (post 2002) COBOL Standard

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-04T22:29:29-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a61hjp$nui$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
A (slightly) updated version of the currently recognized list of
"enhancement request" for some future COBOL Standard (after -hopefully -
there are Technical Reports on "FINALIZER" and "OO Classes") is available
out on the web at:

 http://www.cobolportal.com/j4/files/02-0065.doc

A few things that I would say before (or after <G>) you read this list.

1)  Whether or not you do or do not believe in the current "standardization
process" - I find it HARD to believe that the Standard after this one will
be developed in exactly the "same way"  - much less as SLOWLY as the one
that is due out the end of this year.  Whatever you think should/might be
added to this list, PLEASE separate it from whatever you think of the
current process.  (If you don't believe in the current process and/or its
future, then simply don't pay any attention to this list).

2) The description in this list are INCREDIBLY brief.  Some of them refer to
previous J4 documents that may or may not be "easy" to get a hold of.  If
you are "interested" in a topic that you want to see the referenced document
for, contact the J4 chair.  (but please do NOT flood him with "useless"
requests - if you aren't willing to WORK on something)

3) My latest "quick" glance seems to me to point out some items that DID
make it into the 2002 revision.  Just because something is on this list, do
NOT assume that it won't be in this revision.  On the other hand, I haven't
really studied this - so I could be wrong.

4) Just because something is on the CANDIDATE list, please do not think that
the majority of current J4/WG4 members think it is a "good idea" -  much
less that it is "guaranteed" to be in some future Standard. If you think it
would be a GREAT enhancement to COBOL, then it may still be useful for you
to submit input on WHY you want it - and how you would like to see it
"defined".

5) Is there is something on this list, that you think is AWFUL, please feel
free to send your reasons to the J4 Chair at
   Don Schricker [Don.Schricker <at> microfocus.com]
      or
  (preferably) via "normal" J4 paper submissions if you know how to do this.

6) If there is something that is NOT on this list (and not already
identified for a "planned" technical report) that you think really belongs
in "native COBOL" as soon as possible, then send a DETAILED note/paper to
the J4 chair at:
  Don Schricker [Don.Schricker <at> microfocus.com]
      or
  (preferably) via "normal" J4 paper submissions if you know how to do this.

Note: It is "useful" to point to existing implementor extensions for
something that "works" but isn't in the Standard.  On the other hand, you
still need to explain WHY this is useful if you want other J4/WG4 members to
agree with you.

   ****

PLEASE remember

A) that items on this (or any list) will NOT be part of any future Standard
"instantaneously" (i.e. before AT LEAST three-to-five more years - and I
personally think that is pretty "optimistic").

B) If *no* user takes the time/effort to actually SUBMIT "requirements" to
J4 (and/or internationally via WG4 or your national standards body), then
you really SHOULD assume that it will NEVER make it into the Standard.
```

## Thread: Using the winapi socket call select() with NetExpress — 4 msgs

### Using the winapi socket call select() with NetExpress

- **From:** dcantua@co.la.ca.us (Daniel G. Cantua)
- **Date:** 2002-03-05T07:58:06-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6cca870b.0203050758.18645043@posting.google.com>`

```
Help, I need to be able to use the select() in my COBOL program that
will be a HL7 Server.  I need it to be able to wait for a message and
if one does not come in 5 minutes to be able to check to see if the
connect is still active.  I think I can do this with the select()
statement.  Any help will be appreciated.

Daniel G. Cantua
Los Angeles County - ISD
```

#### ↳ Re: Using the winapi socket call select() with NetExpress

- **From:** Frank Swarbrick <infocat@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-05T18:31:20-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C857168.905A5BEA@sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<6cca870b.0203050758.18645043@posting.google.com>`

```
"Daniel G. Cantua" wrote:
> 
> Help, I need to be able to use the select() in my COBOL program that
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> statement.  Any help will be appreciated.

I don't program under Windows, but I do use select.  What is your
question exactly?  I can tell you that if the peer has closed his end of
the socket the select will return with a read bit set (assuming you set
it when you called select).  When you do the read it will return with 0
which means the peer has disconnected.  In other words it will return as
soon as TCP receives the FIN segment.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Using the winapi socket call select() with NetExpress

- **From:** dcantua@co.la.ca.us (Daniel G. Cantua)
- **Date:** 2002-03-06T07:10:15-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6cca870b.0203060710.84f4110@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<6cca870b.0203050758.18645043@posting.google.com> <3C857168.905A5BEA@sprynet.com>`

```
Frank Swarbrick <infocat@sprynet.com> wrote in message news:<3C857168.905A5BEA@sprynet.com>...
> "Daniel G. Cantua" wrote:
> > 
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> soon as TCP receives the FIN segment.

I am using the windows api, this is how the listen() is coded in
NetExpress:
           CALL WIN32API 'listen' using
               BY VALUE LISTEN-SOCKET
               BY VALUE 5 SIZE 4
               RETURNING WS-RETURN-CODE
           END-CALL.
           IF (WS-RETURN-CODE = SOCKET-ERROR)
               DISPLAY 'Listen Error: ' 
           ELSE
               DISPLAY 'Listen Successful: ' 
           END-IF.
I need to be able to code select() in NetExpress.  If you can also
explain the parameters that are used in the select() command.

Thank you for any help.
Daniel G. Cantua
Los Angeles County - ISD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Using the winapi socket call select() with NetExpress

- **From:** Frank Swarbrick <infocat@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-06T18:59:15-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C86C973.42AB363D@sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<6cca870b.0203050758.18645043@posting.google.com> <3C857168.905A5BEA@sprynet.com> <6cca870b.0203060710.84f4110@posting.google.com>`

```
"Daniel G. Cantua" wrote:
> 
> Frank Swarbrick <infocat@sprynet.com> wrote in message news:<3C857168.905A5BEA@sprynet.com>...
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> explain the parameters that are used in the select() command.

This is only a guess, since I don't actually use it, but based on your
example...

01  TIMEOUT.
    05  TO-SEC		PIC S9(4) BINARY.
    05  TO-USEC		PIC S9(4) BINARY.

01  BITMAPS.
    05  RSOCKS		PIC X(4).
    05  WSOCKS		PIC X(4).
    05  ESOCKS		PIC X(4).

ADD 1 TO MYSOCK GIVING MAXSOCK
MOVE LOW-VALUES TO RSOCKS
MOVE LOW-VALUES TO WSOCKS
MOVE LOW-VALUES TO ESOCKS
CALL 'SETBIT' RSOCKS MYSOCK
MOVE 60 TO TO-SEC
MOVE 0 TO TO-USEC
CALL WIN32API 'select' using
    BY VALUE MAXSOCK
    BY REFERENCE RSOCKS
    BY REFERENCE WSOCKS
    BY REFERENCE ESOCKS
    BY REFERENCE TIMEOUT
    RETURNING WS-RETURN-CODE
END-CALL

MAXSOCK has to be set to 1 more than the highest socket value you want
to check for.  (Actually, I heard in Winsock this is ignored, but better
safe than sorry.)

RSOCKS, WSOCKS and ESOCKS are all 'bit maps' with bits set for each
socket you want to check for 'reading', 'writing' or 'exception'.  In
this example we're just checking one socket and we're checking it for
reading.  TIMEOUT is a structure with the first field being seconds and
the second being milliseconds.  If TIMEOUT has a NULL address it will
block indefinately, or until a socket is available for R/W/E (whichever
you've chosen).  (The two 05 levels may be fullwords (PIC S9(8) BINARY)
instead of halfwords.  I forget which!)

The return value is the number of selected sockets actually available. 
If ir returns zero then the select has timed out with no activity.

'SETBIT' would have to be a subroutine to set the specified bit in the
bitmap.  In other words, if MYSOCK had a value of 1 it would set RSOCKS
to b'00000010000000000000000000000000' (x'02000000').  I don't know if
your COBOL has a built in subroutine to do this, but I wouldn't be
surprised if it did.

Your 'bitmaps' must be long enough to hold all the bits you're setting. 
In other words, if MYSOCK is more than 31 you better have more than a
PIC X(4) or you'll be writing over later working-storage.

Once the select returns with your bit set you should be able to do a
read/recv and get some data without it blocking.

There's more to it than this, but I hope this gets you started.  Check
out this webpage I just found:
http://www.lowtek.com/sockets/select.html

Unfortunately the examples are in C, but it still looks useful.
```

## Thread: COBOL GMT Conversion Help — 4 msgs

### COBOL GMT Conversion Help

- **From:** bvbv22@yahoo.com (boB)
- **Date:** 2002-03-05T11:16:42-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<85cea87b.0203051116.5532c31d@posting.google.com>`

```
Hey Y'All,

I have been tasked to convert a C program to COBOL that converts a
passed date-time to a GMT date-time.  We run on a IBM Mainframe using
COBOL LE.  I have researched some of the intrinsic functions for LE as
well as CEEGMTO.  One of the big issues I am running into is
converting historic dates, trying to determine if the date is in
daylight savings time or not.  So, the question is, am I overlooking a
pre-existing function somewhere that does what I am looking for
already?  If not, is there a routine that determines whether a
historic date is in DST or not?  Also, as input I have the time zone,
whether or not daylight saving applies to that location  and the
offset from GMT.

Basically what I'm saying is I'm being given as input a timezone,
whether DST applies or not, a date and a time and I need to save the
GMT version of this data into a DB2 table.

Any help would be appreciated. 

Thanks, boB

Atlanta Ga
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL GMT Conversion Help

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-05T13:30:01-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a636c3$69u$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<85cea87b.0203051116.5532c31d@posting.google.com>`

```
Are you certain that your input (historic) times do include some that are
"daylight savings time" - and others that are not?

If this is true, then you are going to have a MONSTROUS time trying to
"un-do" this.  Remember that DST does *not* take place in all time-zones at
the same time.  (For example, UK usually is about a week "off" from when the
US changes.

Also, are all the input times from the same location (not just same
time-zone)?  If not, you will run into problems like

  US - Arizona and parts of Indiana vs other places in the same timezone
  International - British "double daylight savings time" for certain years.

    ***

If you DO know for certain that
  A) all your input times are from the same location
  B) that they really DID use DST time for some entries

Then, I think your best bet is going to be to get a "table" of dates for
years on exactly when they changed in that specific location - and NO -
there is no general service (that I know of) to do this.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL GMT Conversion Help

- **From:** jacksleight@hotmail.com (Jack Sleight)
- **Date:** 2002-03-06T20:15:45-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<85cea87b.0203051116.5532c31d@posting.google.com>`

```

Jack

### sample program(s) to get to OS/390 "control blocks"

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-05T13:37:32-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a636ps$ata$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
As pointed out recently, Gilbert Saint-flour's web-page has moved.  I will
update the FAQ with the new address, but if anyone is interested in this
files before that:

    http://mywebpages.comcast.net/gsf/tools/cob2job.txt

has a program with the following comments:

"      *----------------------------------------------------------------
      *
      *    This program retrieves specific job-related data from MVS
      *    control blocks and moves it to Working-storage.
      *
      *    The name of the control-block is indicated in pos 1-6 of
      *    the Procedure Division lines.
      *    The layout of the MVS control blocks is described in the
      *    MVS Data Areas manuals, which can be found on any MVS or
      *    OS/390 CD collection or viewed on-line by going to:
      *        http://www.s390.ibm.com/bookmgr-cgi/bookmgr.cmd/library
      *    and searching for:
      *        MVS DATA AREAS
      *----------------------------------------------------------------*

while

    http://mywebpages.comcast.net/gsf/tools/cob2sys.txt

has a program with the following comments:

      *----------------------------------------------------------------
      *
      *    This program retrieves specific system-related data from
      *    MVS control blocks and moves it to Working-storage.
      *
      *    The name of the control-block is indicated in pos 1-6 of
      *    the Procedure Division lines.
      *    The layout of the MVS control blocks is described in the
      *    MVS Data Areas manuals, which can be found on any MVS or
      *    OS/390 CD collection or viewed on-line by going to:
      *        http://www.s390.ibm.com/bookmgr-cgi/bookmgr.cmd/library
      *    and searching for:
      *        MVS DATA AREAS
      *
      *----------------------------------------------------------------
```

<a id="t-bf91316384"></a>
## Thread: MF COBOL + ADISCTRL mouse + Win98 = memory allocation error — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### MF COBOL + ADISCTRL mouse + Win98 = memory allocation error

- **From:** oswafr01@hotmail.com (FLO)
- **Date:** 2002-03-05T13:11:37-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2adee7d2.0203051311.6851c0b6@posting.google.com>`

```
We recently adapted our screens to take advantage of MicroFocus ADIS'
ability to utilize the mouse in a very simple manner; to select the
data entry field only (none of the complicated routines).  This is on
Windows98-and-higher boxes.  Works like a charm.  We'd previously only
utilized an ADISCTRL (the default one, not customized) for F-keys. 
Problem is, if the mouse is clicked more than six times, upon leaving
the screen via an F-key (the only way we allow, in order to re-index),
Win-DOS displays the following:

"Memory Allocation Error
Cannot start Command.com, exiting"

Any ideas on what could be the cause?  

The only unusual observation is that the Microsoft mouse driver
"Windows Pop-up, CTL-C to close" box appears after all else...though I
don't recall loading it myself and it does not appear using the .exe's
without the mouse-code...
Thanks,
FLO
```

#### ↳ Re: MF COBOL + ADISCTRL mouse + Win98 = memory allocation error

- **From:** oswafr01@hotmail.com (FLO)
- **Date:** 2002-03-06T08:49:32-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2adee7d2.0203060849.75dd46e3@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<2adee7d2.0203051311.6851c0b6@posting.google.com>`

```
O.k., eliminated the TSR "windows-pop up" on closing -it was DOSKEY
being loaded.  Still have the "memory allocation, can't start
Command.com" errors on exiting the batch that starts the COBOL Exe. 
Strange thing is, not all of the reworked .exe's exhibit the
error...but I haven't found any differences (yet!)...any ideas gladly
accepted!

oswafr01@hotmail.com (FLO) wrote in message news:<2adee7d2.0203051311.6851c0b6@posting.google.com>...
> We recently adapted our screens to take advantage of MicroFocus ADIS'
> ability to utilize the mouse in a very simple manner; to select the
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> FLO
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MF COBOL + ADISCTRL mouse + Win98 = memory allocation error

- **From:** oswafr01@hotmail.com (FLO)
- **Date:** 2002-03-07T10:09:17-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2adee7d2.0203071009.6c94fc74@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<2adee7d2.0203051311.6851c0b6@posting.google.com> <2adee7d2.0203060849.75dd46e3@posting.google.com>`

```
Can anyone point me to in-depth technical info on MicroFocus' ADIS
implementation?  It appears that ADIS is almost loaded as a TSR in the
16-bit VM.  When the mouse is enabled from within the program, the
memory space used by ADIS grows.  Unfortunately, for some reason once
the mouse has been used to click on a field >5 times AND the mouse is
"unloaded" (that is, "move 0 to usage-parameter") the command
interpreter (COMMAND.COM) has either been overwritten or there is a
hole in the lower memory of the DOS box and COMMAND.COM cannot
reload...any ideas or observations?  Anyone?


oswafr01@hotmail.com (FLO) wrote in message news:<2adee7d2.0203060849.75dd46e3@posting.google.com>...
> O.k., eliminated the TSR "windows-pop up" on closing -it was DOSKEY
> being loaded.  Still have the "memory allocation, can't start
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> > FLO
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MF COBOL + ADISCTRL mouse + Win98 = memory allocation error

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-03-08T07:51:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C886D67.C1A95330@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<2adee7d2.0203051311.6851c0b6@posting.google.com> <2adee7d2.0203060849.75dd46e3@posting.google.com> <2adee7d2.0203071009.6c94fc74@posting.google.com>`

```
FLO wrote:
> 
> Can anyone point me to in-depth technical info on MicroFocus' ADIS
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> reload...any ideas or observations?  Anyone?

I've had ADIS mouse built into some systems for many years and never had
this problem reported, but then the users probably haven't used the
mouse much as these are keyboard based systems.

With MF 16 bit .EXEs systems I have always carefully contrived to load
the modules by having a small core module that CALLs/CANCELs each
application module and menu program in turn.  Before doing this it opens
an ISAM file to load EXTFH and does a display AT to load ADIS.  This
seems to load them low so that the applications being called/cancelled
have a contiguous area to use.

Have you got a SET COBPOOL=10 or similar ?
```

<a id="t-7bbb70314e"></a>
## Thread: Separators (commas and semicolons) — 8 msgs

### Separators (commas and semicolons)

- **From:** duylam@hotmail.com (Duy Lam)
- **Date:** 2002-03-05T14:54:45-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d372b267.0203051454.a0bc2a8@posting.google.com>`

```
I'm new at COBOL, and I was just wondering which statements commas or
semicolons can appear in. For example, I know you can use it in the
DISPLAY statement and the VALUES part of an 88 variable declaration.
Can you use it in any statement that has a list of identifiers, or
literals, and such?

Duy
```

#### ↳ Re: Separators (commas and semicolons)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-05T17:09:33-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a63j7e$c0r$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<d372b267.0203051454.a0bc2a8@posting.google.com>`

```
The separators ", " and "; " may be used ANYWHERE a separator space may be
used - and it means just as much and as little as a space.

Therefore, although I doubt that any "real programmer" would code it, the
following is perfectly valid.

Procedure, Division.
 Para-1. ,
    Move ; this , to ; that, (1: , 2)
     . , , ; , ; ;
 Para-2 ; .
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Separators (commas and semicolons)

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-06T16:11:18-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a66b76$18ao$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<d372b267.0203051454.a0bc2a8@posting.google.com> <a63j7e$c0r$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Bill Klein wrote:

<<The separators ", " and "; " may be used ANYWHERE a separator space may be
used - and it means just as much and as little as a space.

Therefore, although I doubt that any "real programmer" would code it, the
following is perfectly valid. ...>>

After well over a quarter-century supporting system software for a computer
manufacturer (eighteen of that exclusively in languages), I have come to the
conlusion that characterizations like this, or like "nobody in their right
mind would ever do such a thing", are ultimately meaningless.  Whether the
programmer is "real" or not is irrelevant; the compiler is wrong if it
doesn't accept the code example Bill provided!

    -Chuck Stevens
```

#### ↳ Re: Separators (commas and semicolons)

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-05T17:31:34-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<g6la8uku3flv8h8v2q3dc9oq5pp9vr9goc@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<d372b267.0203051454.a0bc2a8@posting.google.com>`

```
On 5 Mar 2002 14:54:45 -0800 duylam@hotmail.com (Duy Lam) wrote:

:>I'm new at COBOL, and I was just wondering which statements commas or
:>semicolons can appear in. For example, I know you can use it in the
:>DISPLAY statement and the VALUES part of an 88 variable declaration.
:>Can you use it in any statement that has a list of identifiers, or
:>literals, and such?

You can use them pretty much wherever you want. They are logically treated as
a space (except within PICture clauses and literals).
```

#### ↳ Re: Separators (commas and semicolons)

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@invalid.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-06T13:34:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<iVoh8.283364$Aw2.22473926@bin7.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<d372b267.0203051454.a0bc2a8@posting.google.com>`

```

"Duy Lam" <duylam@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:d372b267.0203051454.a0bc2a8@posting.google.com...
> I'm new at COBOL, and I was just wondering which statements commas or
> semicolons can appear in. For example, I know you can use it in the
> DISPLAY statement and the VALUES part of an 88 variable declaration.
> Can you use it in any statement that has a list of identifiers, or
> literals, and such?

Here's an easy way to remember. Don't use commas or semi-colons. At all.
Ever.

You can't go wrong.
```

```
Hear hear.

Donald

- **Date:** 2002-03-07T14:00:02+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c86bd52_11@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<d372b267.0203051454.a0bc2a8@posting.google.com> <iVoh8.283364$Aw2.22473926@bin7.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```

JerryMouse <nospam@invalid.com> wrote in message
news:iVoh8.283364$Aw2.22473926@bin7.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com...
>
> Here's an easy way to remember. Don't use commas or semi-colons. At all.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Not written much SQL lately then Jerry? <G>

Or put decimals into DMarks or Euros?

Or deconstructed a CSV file?

Pete.



 Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services
----------------------------------------------------------
    ** SPEED ** RETENTION ** COMPLETION ** ANONYMITY **

                http://www.usenet.com

#### ↳ Re: Separators (commas and semicolons)

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-03-06T15:22:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a65bvj$g5h$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<d372b267.0203051454.a0bc2a8@posting.google.com>`

```
I don't use commas much - probably because I started off when we read our
programs printed out by impact printers and a comma can look like a period.

But I do use semi-colons more than most people do, for clarity and to make
it obvious that I did mean for this to be the end of a clause and not the
end of a sentence.
```

which maintains it will continue to develop its own Solaris Unix

technologies--especially those from IBM--are a means to that end.

THE MISSING LINK | Hacker claims Web worm meant to combat sexism

Is this what the Spice Girls meant by "girl power"? A 17-year-old
Dutch girl, who claims to have bred the "Sharpei" worm to nip
Microsoft's .NET Web services platform, said she did it to show that
women can write viruses too. She told security firm Sophos that she
didn't want the worm to do any damage, but she did want it to spread
a message -- that cyberspace isn't just a man's world. I am woman,
hear me hack? 

Source: Reuters

at NT.  There may be a few copies of the OS/2 version out there (try
eBay, etc.), you cannot get it from IBM.  It was never cheap and the
current NT version is $3000.

## Thread: Fujitsu Compiler — 12 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Fujitsu Compiler

- **From:** foodman123@aol.com (Anthony Dilworth)
- **Date:** 2002-03-06T14:54:40-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<93dfc7c7.0203061454.1c8deffb@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi:

I am presently evaluating the Marshallsoft email routines for COBOL.

Because there is no Marshallsoft for my compiler, I am using the
Fujitsu one.

Because I despise using the indescribably annoying Fujitsu 
'Programming Staff' to compile and link, I looked up the command-lines
to do that. I have no problem compiling, but when I link using the
following
line, I get error L1101 - invalid OBJECT module.

LINK XFM94.OBJ SEE32.LIB SP2.LIB project.res F3BICIMP.LIB LIBC.LIB
KERNEL32.LIB USER32.LIB

If I use the insufferably crude 'Programming Staff' thing to link, it 
links fine and displays in a DOS window all the things linked together
to form the EXE. All of the things listed in the DOS window are
in my command line (above) and are correct. (I stuck 'project.res' 
in the Link line because it appears in the DOS window, however, 
that does not appear in the Fujitsu manual.) Either way, it doesn't
work.

The thought of continuing to use the utterly absurd 'Programming
Staff'
gives me the heebie-jeebies, anyone know what's up?

Thanks

Tony Dilworth




E
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu Compiler

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-03-06T19:28:20-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9tyh8.13462$4E4.2417940@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<93dfc7c7.0203061454.1c8deffb@posting.google.com>`

```
Tony, if you have set it up and tested the linkage under PSTAFF, then it
created a make file in the same directory as the project.  Just go in and
look at it with your text editor.  I tend to use PSTAFF to build the make
files, then set up batch runs to use the make files, as there may be several
"projects" in a "project".  (That Pstaff will not handle a project that is
another project is it's biggest problem, in my opinion).

Donald

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu Compiler

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-03-07T09:46:44-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<_1Lh8.16412$7Y4.2517880@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<9tyh8.13462$4E4.2417940@news20.bellglobal.com> <20020307073025.07895.00000037@mb-ms.aol.com>`

```

"Sff5ky" <sff5ky@aol.comxxx123> wrote in message
news:20020307073025.07895.00000037@mb-ms.aol.com...
> In article <9tyh8.13462$4E4.2417940@news20.bellglobal.com>, "Donald Tees"
> <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> writes:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> >files, then set up batch runs to use the make files, as there may be
several
> >"projects" in a "project".  (That Pstaff will not handle a project that
is
> >another project is it's biggest problem, in my opinion).
> >
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I am refering to a project as anything that PSTAFF gives a PRJ extension too
... what it defines as a "project".  Yes, you can have lots in one, but
often a complete system cannot be easily organized into one project, simply
because various modules cannot be started before others are finished.  For
example, you may have DLL's that will not compile until another DLL has been
compiled and linked correctly.  Since PSTAFF will not allow you to order the
compilation, putting both of them in the same project is a recipe for
disaster.

Example... Object A inherits Object B.  Pstaff has them listed as Object A
first.  I do a rebuild, and the library for DLL B gets deleted.  Object A
will no longer compile because it requires the repository of object B.
Object B will no longer compile because the build craps out at object A.

The nice way of handling that would be three projects.  Each object set up
as a PSTAFF project, then a third project that includes the two sub projects
in the correct order.  It cannot be done with PSTAFF, but the two projects
*can* be set up easily, and a batch file made that uses the make files of
the first two to create a master rebuild.

I have three systems I sell.  Each has about 300 programs/modules, but about
100 of those are common to all three projects.  If Pstaff was capable of
nesting, it would make life much easier.

Donald
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu Compiler

- **From:** foodman123@aol.com (Anthony Dilworth)
- **Date:** 2002-03-07T05:33:21-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<93dfc7c7.0203070533.2396f7ab@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<93dfc7c7.0203061454.1c8deffb@posting.google.com> <9tyh8.13462$4E4.2417940@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Hi:

This example of encountering problems in what should be the simplest
of operations which should require absolutely no effort or thought,
and turns out to take hours to figure out is the perfect example
of how bad, in general, most software is.

It takes 8 clicks to compile and 13 clicks to link. What should
require no more than ONE KEYSTROKE or CLICK to accomplish takes 
several seconds in two separate steps.

Why not ONE KEYSTROKE or CLICK to compile, compile and link, or 
compile link and test?

ONE!

No, we get to do 21 clicks.

Donald, thank you for your advice. Unfortunately I do not understand
your suggestion. I do compile and link and everything in the same
directory and I don't see anything which I might interpret as a make
file.

Of course, I don't know what a make file is and wouldn't know what
to do with it!

And, I don't really WANT to know and why should I HAVE to?

All I want to do is write a simple program to send email. That should
be as easy as pie, no?

I just don't like WASTING MY TIME trying to figure things out
because of inferior software.

It must cost the world billions of dollars a year in wasted time
and resources when programmers spend so much time wrestling with
arcane problems.  And, the programmers don't seem to mind.

What percentage of the average COBOL programmer's time is spent
actually writing application-specific COBOL code versus all
the other stuff which is purely technical? Does management
know what the percentage is?  Does anyone know what the 
percentage is? Does anyone care what the percentage is?

Why does the COBOL community not YELL at Fujitsu to come
up with something that a programmer could actually ENJOY using
for its simplicity and efficiency? Why not? Someone please tell
me why. Why does Fujitsu not recognize the need for something
simple and efficient?

Not being able to use command lines is not the end of the world
since I can compile and test my program using my regular compiler
(without the MarshallSoft software) and then compile it for
final test with the Fujitsu. So, I won't really be getting
any heebie-jeebies - just in case anyone was worried!

Thanks

Tony Dilworth
```

Which version are you running ?  Version 3 is several years old and was
almost a Beta, but that is what I recall PSTAFF being part of.  Version
4 and above has 'Project Manager' and works OK-ish. It does write
makefiles which can be collected and extended for all programs, then you
can ignore all Fujitsu tools and just edit source as required, issue a
MAKE to have everything built.

> Donald, thank you for your advice. Unfortunately I do not understand
> your suggestion. I do compile and link and everything in the same
> directory and I don't see anything which I might interpret as a make

click through everything.

> to do with it!

Don't you use make ?  How then do you arrange compiles ?  Batch files
<choke> ?

 
> All I want to do is write a simple program to send email. That should
> be as easy as pie, no?

Yes it is.  Go find CMDMAILER using Yahoo.  Have you MS Cobol program
write a text file with:

-------------------------------------
To: email@wherever.com
From: me@myaddress
Subject: I can send EMail

This is an EMail

-------------------------------------

And then execute CMDMAILER with a few parameters including this file
name using CALL x"91".

 
> I just don't like WASTING MY TIME trying to figure things out
> because of inferior software.

You mean like software given away for free to students ?  Software that
has been replaced and upgraded 4 times already ?

And are you sure it is the inferior software that is inhibiting learning
?

> It must cost the world billions of dollars a year in wasted time
> and resources when programmers spend so much time wrestling with
> arcane problems.  And, the programmers don't seem to mind.

No.  Students get version 3, write their code and click 21 times and
learn and understand the processes involved.  They don't want a 'black
box' automatic solution from which they learn nothing.

Version 3 is a _learning_ tool.

You don't want to learn PSTAFF or make, or anything different than what
you have been doing for twenty years, so buy the professional version
that has a reasonable 'project manager'.

> What percentage of the average COBOL programmer's time is spent
> actually writing application-specific COBOL code versus all
> the other stuff which is purely technical? Does management
> know what the percentage is?  Does anyone know what the
> percentage is? Does anyone care what the percentage is?

I have spent about 25% of my time over the last 30 years learning new
things. Much was learnt and discarded, most has had some use.  I expect
to continue to spend around 25% on 'purely technical' otherwise I would
become arcane myself.  What was your percentage over the last 20 years ?


> Why does the COBOL community not YELL at Fujitsu to come
> up with something that a programmer could actually ENJOY using
> for its simplicity and efficiency? Why not? Someone please tell
> me why. Why does Fujitsu not recognize the need for something
> simple and efficient?

Well actually _I_ did.  After I got the original version 3 just after
its release one of the Fujitsu people posted saying that their IDE was
so wonderful.  I wrote telling them how poor it actually was right here,
but that was several years ago.  Version 4 was better, version 6 is OK
if you like that sort of thing.  I prefer XTreeGold/TSE edit/make and
can get Fujitsu to compile without clicking.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu Compiler

- **From:** "398199821" <warren.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2002-03-07T20:11:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<pPPh8.20277$106.1650284@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<93dfc7c7.0203061454.1c8deffb@posting.google.com> <3C87CF4A.B79E7E85@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
KISS is something we all know about. At one time or another someone has said
"keep it simple..stupid".
Uniformity is a mark of simplicity.  Trouble with that is the people
deciding the uniform doen't wear it.
Case in point. On the Univac I, there were tapes, period. The OS was a
simple load the first block of a tape,
and it would contain any operational features needed. At our site, this
developed into a tape library with
project objects on a master tape with variables coded to allow a
pre-application process to substitute the
current values for the variables. Eash program on the library contained the
"first block" logic, and was enhanced to
provide the program or the operator to select and load the next program.

After several years of this kind of operation, the Univac people sold us a
"650" type machine for one of the plants.
Data was on a drum. We specified the same kind of system for this operation
to obtain uniformity. Can't be done was
the answer as the system was built to run from a card reader. Not enough
columns to put the procedures needed in
one card. We gave in, but insisted they develop a consitant method to
provide the consitnancy we desired.

A few years later, they came up with one, and acknowledged that it taught
them a lesson. Big deal. Product
planning was in it's childhood then. Often today's hardware support seems to
still be in it's childhood.

Otherwise, why would we need "implementor defined"?

Warren Simmons
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3C87CF4A.B79E7E85@Azonic.co.nz...

<a id="t-16f43ec1c4"></a>
## Thread: z/OS Version 1 Release 3 and OS/VS COBOL — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### z/OS Version 1 Release 3 and OS/VS COBOL

- **From:** rsposato@csc.com (Ron Sposato)
- **Date:** 2002-03-06T15:06:37-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1de0eb0f.0203061506.6c3a023a@posting.google.com>`

```
As I read IBMs documentation, the Language Environment in z/OS Version
1 Release 3 only supports the IBM Enterprise COBOL compiler.  I also
read that IBM Enterprise COBOL compiler only supports ANSI 85 COBOL.

This tells me, OS/VS COBOL load modules will run in the runtime
environment in z/OS Version 1 Release 3, but they cannot be compiled
in it.

Can anyone confirm this for me?
```

#### ↳ Re: z/OS Version 1 Release 3 and OS/VS COBOL

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-06T18:52:06-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a66djp$jbi$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<1de0eb0f.0203061506.6c3a023a@posting.google.com>`

```
I think you are "bit confused".

LE does not (never has, never will) "support" any compiler - what it does is
support the object code PRODUCED by various compilers.

The LE in z/OS Version 1, Release 3 still supports load modules created by
all COBOL compilers from OS/VS COBOL V2.4 thru VS COBOL II (all releases)
thru SAA ADCycle COBOL/370 thru IBM COBOL for MVS & VM thru IBM COBOL for
OS/390 & VM thru IBM Enterprise COBOL for z/OS & OS/390.

Support for all of the compilers listed above up to and including IBM COBOL
for OS/390 & VM has been withdrawn or has announced withdrawal dates.
Support for all releases of OS/VS COBOL and VS COBOL II was withdrawn QUITE
a while ago.  HOWEVER, if you have (or even create new) load modules that
are link-edited with and run under a supported release of LE, they (the
object code) *IS* supported - as long as it does not use any "undocumented"
feature of the earlier compiler.

There has been an announcement that the release of CICS TS that comes AFTER
the latest one will NOT include support for OS/VS COBOL programs.  (CICS
"used to" need special support for OS/VS COBOL because it did not have a
REAL "re-entrant" feature).  Similarly, preprocessor support for currently
unsupported COBOL compilers is not available with the latest release of
CICS.
```

some cobols will do this when reading a line sequential
file if you make the record definition too short.

next part of first record, etc.

<a id="t-1bf54a0ee2"></a>
## Thread: Microfocus vs AcuCobol — 30 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Microfocus vs AcuCobol

- **From:** ssmith@adelaidebank.com.au (Simon Smith)
- **Date:** 2002-03-07T14:24:59-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<64826f50.0203071424.2d72933b@posting.google.com>`

```
Hello

I'm looking for some objective insight into the relative benefits of
MicroFocus Cobol and AcuCobol in a Win2000 environment.

One of our subsidurary companies is currently running an application
written in ICOBOL and accessing ISAM files.  The 4 developers have no
IDE and little support infrastructure.  The plan is to migrate to
Win2000 and SQL/Server (to replace the ISAM).  This will involve quite
a bit of redevelopment but it should give the application a new lease
on life.  It should also significantly enhance the developer's ability
to respond to change request from business units.

Thanks

Simon Smith
Adelaide Bank Limited
```

#### ↳ Re: Microfocus vs AcuCobol

- **From:** none@none.com (wildman)
- **Date:** 2002-03-08T00:20:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c88159d.1805034@news.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<64826f50.0203071424.2d72933b@posting.google.com>`

```
I just finished porting a system from a mini computer to NT with Net
Express and I'm very happy with the results.  

The ported application is more portable than it ever was.  It can now
be ported to Linux or Unix - easily.  Cobol & SQL are both highly
portable languages and once combined, the application will deliver for
a long time - perhaps longer than it already has.

As for the IDE, well, your legacy folks will love it.  The compiler
license isn't cheap - but you get what you pay for.  I found the
support well above average.

On 7 Mar 2002 14:24:59 -0800, ssmith@adelaidebank.com.au (Simon Smith)
wrote:

>Hello
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>Adelaide Bank Limited
```

#### ↳ Re: Microfocus vs AcuCobol

- **From:** "Erlend Moen" <erlend.moen@disystemer.no>
- **Date:** 2002-03-08T11:42:24+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c889590@news.wineasy.se>`
- **References:** `<64826f50.0203071424.2d72933b@posting.google.com>`

```
"Simon Smith" <ssmith@adelaidebank.com.au> wrote in message
news:64826f50.0203071424.2d72933b@posting.google.com...
> Hello
>
> I'm looking for some objective insight into the relative benefits of
> MicroFocus Cobol and AcuCobol in a Win2000 environment.

I've been working with AcuCobol for 10 years now.
Our first project was converting from the old MS Cobol (2.1?) to Acu. No
problems at all.
In 1995 I started in a new job where we converted from Wang-Cobol to Acu
running on SCO-Unix server with Win-clients. Again - no problems at all.

I began with GUI-programming in 2000 on AcuGT as it is called now. We have
WinNT, Win2K, WinXP and 98/ME as clients, works like a dream. I can do
almost anything directly from Acu, I can call Windows API, DLL's etc. etc.
We have a tight interaction with several Delphi-application as well,
communicating via DLL's.

One of the things I like best with AcuCorp, is their willingness to support
their customers. I can email a question and I will get an answer the same
day in 9 out of 10 times. They are also very responsive when it comes to
actually bug-reports. They do not try to hide the bugs, but file them and
give me feedback when they have solved them. If it's a critical bug for me,
I can order a patch if I cant wait for the next release.

The compiler is very good, but so is Micro Focus I believe, but you should
also emphasize the companys willingness to take care of you as a customer. I
have never regret that we chose AcuCorp, and I see no reason not to stay
with them further.

Regards,
Erlend Moen
```

#### ↳ Re: Microfocus vs AcuCobol

- **From:** davidcfield@yahoo.com.au (David Field)
- **Date:** 2002-03-08T20:29:15-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b8915964.0203082029.3a585e08@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<64826f50.0203071424.2d72933b@posting.google.com>`

```
ssmith@adelaidebank.com.au (Simon Smith) wrote in message news:<64826f50.0203071424.2d72933b@posting.google.com>...
> Hello
> 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> Adelaide Bank Limited

Hello Simon,

We have been developing with Acucobol for 8 years. We started off with
Icobol in 1981, switched to Ace Cobol (an ICobol compatible product)
in 1983, attempted to move to RM/Cobol in 1992 and eventually moved to
AcuCobol in 1993. We have also used Microfocus Cobol and Net Express
on occasions over the years.

Our original selection criteria was based purely on Icobol
compatibility - we did not have the resources to do a lot of
redevelopment and our Landmark product had to be ported to a Prime
platform in a very short period of time. Our original port was done
with Acucobol 1.5 - we had to make a few changes but it was relatively
painless. If we had the opportunity to wait until Acucobol version
2.0, we would have had to do virtually no work at all, as additional
Icobol compatibility switches were added by Acucorp in that release.

We have used many of the facilities Acucorp offers, including doing a
full GUI version of our product, using Acu4GL for Informix and Oracle,
extensive use of AcuODBC and integration of external facilities such
as IBM MQSeries, Active X controls and messaging via our e-mail
integration engine, MapiLand. We are also active members of the
Acucorp beta test program.

We have found the overall quality of the product range to be very good
and if you are moving from an Icobol environment, can highly recommend
the compability levels.

Hope this helps

David Field
Director
Landmark Software Pty Ltd
President Acucorp Australia Developers Group
```

#### ↳ Re: Microfocus vs AcuCobol

- **From:** geert.borstlap@anubex.com (Geert)
- **Date:** 2002-03-13T05:59:19-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fe0f0846.0203130559.37f42d70@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<64826f50.0203071424.2d72933b@posting.google.com>`

```
... to replace the ISAM ...

It is simpy said, but in practice it is not that evident.
It is a risky rewriting/re-engineering process!

We've already done many of those conversions, so I'll try to give you
some
background: About a year ago, we developed a tool for a number of
banks in Luxemburg and Switzerland that is doing an automated
conversion from ISAM to RDBBMS. It's called the "anugen-IO"
(generating IO-modules), and it converts
automatically all COBOL-programs containing ISAM file-IO to the same
COBOL-programs but with it's data stored in an RDBMS. It also
generates the needed
CTRL files to load the data in the RDBMS. Normally we're doing
migrations of legacy platforms towards modern technology, and the
"upgrade" of Index-sequential COBOL files to a RDBMS tables (and
changing all infected sources automatically) is a spin-off project
leading it's own life now.

Of course we can't simply change every READ in a FETCH, a WRITE in an
INSERT, REWRITE -> UPDATE, ...   This would be too easy.

There are 3 mayor problems when moving from C-ISAM towards RDBMS.
1) A sequence of COBOL file actions (OPEN, START, READ NEXT, READ
PREVIOUS, WRITE, INSERT, CLOSE, ...) works differently in an RDBMS.
You
can READ on the primary key, continue READing on an alternate key,
switch direction by a READ PREVIOUS, ... and the fun has just begon.
2) The datatypes available in Oracle/SQL-server/DB2/... is only a
subset of
all datatypes available in COBOL.
3) An FD (File Description) in COBOL can be defined with levels (03,
05,
07, ...) and OCCURS (arrays) whereas Oracle/SQL-server/DB2 can only
use a
flat buffer whithout OCCURS.

All problems are solved in an IO-module. This IO-module (one per file,
so in Oracle/SQL-server/DB2 terms one per table) ensures that we get
the same data, as
you would get with the ISAM version. All COBOL-programs are parsed,
stored in a COBOL-tree and regenerated, and every FILE-statement
(READ, WRITE, OPEN, ...) is automatically changed into a CALL
statement, calling the appropriate IO-module.

Well, the work is done in 5 steps. (which means you run the
"anugen-IO"-tool 5 times)

Step 1) (optional) analyse all COBOL sources and count the numbers of
ISAM files used and the number of "file-IO statement" replacements
that needs to be done. This gives you an idea how much work you have
if you want to do it manually.
Step 2) generates the CREATE TABLE scripts.
Step 3) generates an IO-module per FD description (ISAM file)
Step 4a) (fast data migration): generates the CTRL files per FD
description
Step 4b) (slow data migration): generates a small COBOL program that
reads the complete ISAM file and INSERT record per record in the
RDBMS.
Step 5) replaces every file IO statement in your COBOL program, and
replace it by the appropriate CALL IO-module statement. These new
statements are doing functional exactly the same as the original ones.

Now you create the tables in the database, migrate the data, compile
the IO-modules (.pco -> .o), compile and link your final exe's (with
the generated IO-modules) and you are ready.
After these steps you have your old COBOL-application working exactly
the same, however the data is stored in an RDBMS now. No manual work
needs to be done.

Some optimisation which are built in:
1) renaming: It is possible to choose new names for your fields in the
RDBMS table.
2) The IO-module can be generated for all keys (primary and alternate
keys) or for a certain key alone. This produces smaller object-code.
3) The IO-module can be generated in an optimalisation mode. When you
would read your complete file  in certain COBOL programs (in
RDBMS-terminology i.e a full table scan) this would boost performance.

We got good results in production. There was no performance penalty to
be paid.

If you want to know more: www.anubex.com and push the "the migration
Centre" button.

Hope this info helps.
Geert
```

#### ↳ Re: Microfocus vs AcuCobol

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-03-14T10:13:58+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c8fc2af_6@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<64826f50.0203071424.2d72933b@posting.google.com>`

```
Simon,

I have a tool I wrote (ISAM2RDB) that analyses the COBOL source and
generates an equivalent RDB in third normal form. It has been used
successfully in Australia and I can give you a Company reference in
Melbourne.

It is a 3 step process:

1. Create the database.
2. Create the modules to load the database.
3. Load the database.

The final step of  converting the application is "manual with assistance". I
thought about writing something to automate this, but the possibilities are
so huge and there hasn't been the demand that would warrant it. It is also a
good thing to review the existing code as part of the conversion.

Alternatively, I will gladly give a fixed price to deliver the converted,
loaded, database. (It works out at around $Aus30.00 per ISAM file for SQL
Server, if you have more than 50 files).

You will still need to convert the Application.

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Microfocus vs AcuCobol

- **From:** lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca
- **Date:** 2002-03-16T18:16:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-JouWQL9DCqXY@thishost>`
- **References:** `<64826f50.0203071424.2d72933b@posting.google.com> <bfdfc3e8.0203160804.7c30726d@posting.google.com> <a6vvtj$hj1ga$1@ID-134254.news.dfncis.de>`

```
On Sat, 16 Mar 2002 17:37:17 UTC, "Christer O. Jonsson" 
<christer.o.jonsson@home.se> wrote:

> Native code that I know, but P-code whats that?
> 
> Christer O. Jonsson
> 
> 

I think it refers to the Pascal "P-code" that was executed by a 
virtual machine. Java and other languages use the same idea, the 
source is translated into a "machine language" that is executed by a 
virtual machine rather than the souce code being translated in machine
language that is executed directly by the computer hardware. 

The Microfocus compiler can generate both "native code" (.obj files) 
and P-code (.int files)

The foregoing was overly simplistic but ... :-)
```

```
While I'm at it - 

Sometimes people migrate to RDB for no discernable reason other then
their fear/ignorance if ISAM files.  SQL server certainly won't be
less expensive - is there a good reason for the conversion to such for
data storage?


ssmith@adelaidebank.com.au (Simon Smith) wrote in message news:<64826f50.0203071424.2d72933b@posting.google.com>...
> Hello
> 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> Adelaide Bank Limited
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Microfocus vs AcuCobol

- **From:** lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca
- **Date:** 2002-03-16T18:31:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-yyqDHmzb2jzy@thishost>`
- **References:** `<64826f50.0203071424.2d72933b@posting.google.com> <bfdfc3e8.0203160806.34326187@posting.google.com>`

```
Usually conversion to using an RDBMS system is to allow other 
applications access to the data processed by the COBOL (or any other 
langauge) programs.

The RDBMS data is readily available to third party query and report 
writer tools that support an ODBC interface. One can choose tools that
provide ease of use to the end user community without requiring a 
large investment in additional custom code. 

The cost of the RDBMS plus "shrink wrap" query/reporting is low in 
comparison to the cost of custom code to provide the same level of 
functionality.

I would like to offer that SQL Server is not the best tool for the 
RDBMS engine however. If you are going to invest in an RDBMS something
like IBM's DB2 will offer much more functionality.

My personal preference is Microfocus COBOL over AcuCOBOL but that is 
because I prefer applications that are built as EXEs and DLLs rather 
than many individual interpreter code files.

Lorne

On Sat, 16 Mar 2002 16:06:37 UTC, thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane 
Hubbell) wrote:

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Microfocus vs AcuCobol

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-03-17T10:09:45+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c9537c9_5@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<64826f50.0203071424.2d72933b@posting.google.com> <bfdfc3e8.0203160806.34326187@posting.google.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-yyqDHmzb2jzy@thishost>`

```
Absolutely endorse this 100%, Lorne.

The arguments about which RDBMS is "best" is trivial. ANY of them are better
than being locked into the COBOL file system and thereby isolated. I have
posted at length on this subject both here and elsewhere...the file system
is one of the major factors contributing to the "Death of COBOL" and the
perception of COBOL within Corporates as something which "...the sooner we
are rid of it, the better..."

There is nothing intrinsically wrong with the COBOL file system. We all know
that superb data storage and retrieval systems have been built with it and
many of them are still in operation.

But the World has moved on, and Data must be shared. Writing interfaces and
downstream feeds to the Corporate Data resource is NOT an acceptable
solution any more. The data resource must be OPEN to access by standard
software and able to be queried by non-technical people without having to
wait for as long as it takes the IT department to prioritise, develop, test,
and debug, a COBOL program... RDB meets this need and that is why it is
successful.

Pete.

know

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Microfocus vs AcuCobol

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-03-20T03:43:36+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c975e98_10@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<64826f50.0203071424.2d72933b@posting.google.com> <bfdfc3e8.0203160806.34326187@posting.google.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-yyqDHmzb2jzy@thishost> <3c9537c9_5@Usenet.com> <yutl8.66$xs2.41950340@newssvr11.news.prodigy.com>`

```

Tom Morrison <t.morrison@liant.com> wrote in message news:yutl8.66> >
> > But the World has moved on, and Data must be shared.
>
> [At the risk of posting an advert]
>
> But Pete, why abandon that which works to gain something that may be
gained
> using another tool.
>
For the same reason that we moved on from Ox-carts, sleds, and flint tools
(all of which worked), to Ferraris, speedboats, titanium saws and laser
drills. The current is replaced by the new, not because the current doesn't
work, but because the new works BETTER.

> Relativity (http://www.liant.com/products/relativity/) can make COBOL data
> quite accessible to the ad hoc query (not to mention ASP programmer, PHP
> programmer, Java programmer, ...) without affecting the 'back office'
> application.
>
Tom, not having used Relativity, I cannot make fair comment. There are a
number of tools that can "bridge" between the file system and RDB technology
(I've written one myself...<G>). At the end of the day, it isn't the Bridge
that is needed, it is open access directly by all "standard" corporate
tools.  It isn't just about ad hoc queries, it is about the extension and
joining of new requirements with the existing data resource. RDB technology
is our best bet until Content Addressable File Store (CAFS) becomes a viable
and cheap alternative (about 15 years, I reckon).

Pete.

I give ICL credit for at least researching and building something along
these lines. Unfortunately, it was too expensive, too complex and
consequently, non-viable. I don't know what the state of this is today at
ICL. Under the Fujitsu umbrella it may have been shelved.

I know of at least one project which is currently investigating this area
but I am not allowed to give any clues...<G>

Seems a pretty fair bet that as hardware efficiency accelerates, the idea of
a CAFS will be recycled and somebody somewhere will achieve the right
hardware/software balance to implement it. When it finally arrives it may
not be in the form of disk storage at all...

Pete.

Ah!

RDBMS "is to allow other applications access to the data processed by the
COBOL..."

In other words this method of storage helps avoid using uniform and standard
metods! Yes? No?

I was given the purpose of Relational data organization was because it made
finding it faster.
Use a table concept, read right and down to the answer kind of a thing could
follow math
logic and not involve looking procedurally for the answer except with
another tool called .... which
had to be added to the front end of the search of the data base.

I see the use of other applications via call and return, I don't see the
segmentation of lookup logic
to some interface not a part of the uniform and standard I/O package Except
as a method of
blocking one's competitors or one's users from a clear cut procedure.

Just what was the feature requiring the use of RDBMS?  Make it simple to
compute, and you can stand
on your head to use it. Why couldn't the need be added to the standard? To
whose advantage was that?

How much hardware and software did it sell? Just who made out?

Old men have a time adjusting to the world that has not stopped "to think".

IMNSHO,

Warren Simmons

An RDBMS engine is not faster than COBOL ISAM file I-O. There is a lot
more overhead in processing in an RDBMS engine. Although some 
processing is faster with an RDBMS engine, but a well written COBOL 
progam can probably do faster processing for particular application 
requirements.

which meant the wells each had two miles of pipe sticking up toward the
clouds.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Microfocus vs AcuCobol

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca
- **Date:** 2002-03-17T04:08:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-yGbH2SfJO4pP@thishost>`
- **References:** `<64826f50.0203071424.2d72933b@posting.google.com> <bfdfc3e8.0203160806.34326187@posting.google.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-yyqDHmzb2jzy@thishost> <1vUk8.265598$pN4.16524374@bin8.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
On Sun, 17 Mar 2002 03:57:49 UTC, "JerryMouse" <nospam@invalid.com> 
wrote:

> 
> <lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca> wrote in message
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Well ...

The administrator of the RDBMS package is responsible for the granting
of access to the data tables in the database. Any good RDBMS allows 
the DBA to grant "read only" permission to a data table for a user or 
group of users. If this is done properly then the "user written, death
to data integrity" programs do not have the opportunity to screw 
around with the data.

If the DBA does not do her/his/its job then data destruction is the 
normal result.

AFAIK an RDBMS like IBMs DB2 does allow the restriction of users to 
"read only" access to the data tables. Of couse you could do something
really idiotic and store your data in an "MS Access" database and 
allow unrestricted access to the data.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Microfocus vs AcuCobol

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-03-18T13:13:15+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c954508_8@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<64826f50.0203071424.2d72933b@posting.google.com> <bfdfc3e8.0203160806.34326187@posting.google.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-yyqDHmzb2jzy@thishost> <1vUk8.265598$pN4.16524374@bin8.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-yGbH2SfJO4pP@thishost>`

```
Lorne,

you are incorrect on a couple of points in this thread, though in general, I
would agree with your arguments in favour of RDB.

1. ISAM I-O is faster than RDB IO.

This HAS to be qualified. For large sequential volumes of data it is
certainly true... In all other cases it MAY not be. I don't want to go into
this in detail because it is of little interest to the majority of people
here, but consider an ISAM file where second level overflow has occurred or
the indexes have not been re-organised for some time...The RDB is self
re-organising (think of VSAM on your DB2 database) and there is therefore a
fair chance that on random access it will kill the ISAM file. Anyway, it is
NOT the speed (or otherwise) of access which points us towards RDBs, it is
the openness of access and the removal of the requirement for a COBOL
program to be written every time a user department wants data.

2. Your scathing comments regarding MS ACCESS are quite out of place and
indicate that you have not used the latest versions of ACCESS.

ACCESS is a true RDB which implements the current standard for SQL. It has
ALL the permissions and restrictions which DB2 has. (In fact , I have
converted a Mainframe DB2 application to the PC and used exactly the same
SQL against ACCESS as was used against DB2...it was a doddle.) The only
difference is that it can be used by users without the control of the IT
department. (Some IT departments are not happy about this...)

I use ACCESS to simulate major file conversions and take them off-line from
the mainframe or whatever platform they are currently existing on. It is an
excellent implementation of the Relational Model. You can simulate the
proposed system and get all the SQL right and the tables built and loaded
without any impact on the existing platform. Then simply export the table
definitions and load to the target. It is every bit as much an RDB as
INGRES, ORACLE, DB2, UDB, DATACOM, or SQL SERVER (in fact, thanks to the
miracle of ODBC I have used it with all of the above...)

The poor reputation of ACCESS stems from people writing very bad "Database
applications" using ACCESS BASIC. If it is used purely as a repository,
accessed with COBOL/SQL/ODBC it is superb.

Pete.

Peter points out some problems with ISAM I-O.
I find this concern out of wack with my experience.
ISAM as first implemented dealt with overflow requiring re-orgs.
Many organizations found ways to enhance ISAM to remove this.
However, since my exposure is very old, my experience may be unique,
maybe just in my mind, but I remember trying to get the code from
..... in the 60's.

Warren Simmons

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ DataBase Security - WAS Re: Microfocus vs AcuCobol

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-03-17T04:55:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C942162.BA974FDD@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<64826f50.0203071424.2d72933b@posting.google.com> <bfdfc3e8.0203160806.34326187@posting.google.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-yyqDHmzb2jzy@thishost> <1vUk8.265598$pN4.16524374@bin8.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```


JerryMouse wrote:

> <lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca> wrote in message
> news:ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-yyqDHmzb2jzy@thishost...
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> clouds.

Valid observation and raises the issue of security of DB information. Let's
expand on Jerry's comment :-

I'm in the process, (like Jerry - it's the oil/gas industry) of switching from
COBOL files (ISAMs etc.) to Microsoft Access. M/S Access is being used solely as
the repository for the data, with data input being validated or calculated via
COBOL programs.

Sole reason I'm switching - most of the major corporations here in Calgary as HQ
for oil/gas, use some form of DB package, DB2, Oracle etc. Inevitably what I am
storing in either COBOL files or a DB is not necessarily the format the oil
company maintains their system in. Two options :-

- (1) Restrict user access to the core DB - would necessitate me writing  CSVs
each time they wanted to extract to do something different. (While an irritant,
also a plus for me - extra bucks <G>).

- (2) Allow them entry directly into M/S Access - obviates the necessity of my
writing or receiving CSVs and they can manipulate the data, (generating
additional report tables etc.), as they see fit..

#2 is where Jerry's downside comes into play. By storing current thicknesses of
'Items' the purpose of the application is to trigger when sufficient corrosion
has occurred to indicate either (a) Below acceptable Minimum thickness, or (b)
The Life Expectancy (number of operative years left) is starting to get
dangerously close.

There is no logical reason that an end user should need to change the inspection
data recorded by inspection companies from various Non-Destructive Testing
methods, (Ultrasonics, X-rays, Radiography, Coupons  etc.). Both down there in
Texas and up here in Alberta it is a government requirement that oil
corporations confirm on an annual basis that all vessels (production units) have
been inspected.

Assume some failure due to corrosion, ( a plant shutdown could mean over
$3million lost per day). But again, think of something as disastrous as Bophal,
India. Logically the inspection company's data, (stored via COBOL), should have
indicated this to the end user corporation. Now assume somebody in the oil
company 'monkies' with the inspection data to hide the obvious corrosion. Who is
responsible :-

- Me, the application developer
- the Inspection Company, or
- the Oil Corporation  ? (Don't forget the latter can employ a bevy of legal
sharks to 'prove' their nose is clean.)

Now translate the same sort of problem to a financial DB used by our friends at
say, Enron.

Anybody out there got some recommendations about DB security, or can point us to
references on the topic ?

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: DataBase Security - WAS Re: Microfocus vs AcuCobol

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca
- **Date:** 2002-03-17T15:34:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-LGpX7hfBxRwI@thishost>`
- **References:** `<64826f50.0203071424.2d72933b@posting.google.com> <bfdfc3e8.0203160806.34326187@posting.google.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-yyqDHmzb2jzy@thishost> <1vUk8.265598$pN4.16524374@bin8.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3C942162.BA974FDD@shaw.ca>`

```
Yes I would say you are one of the "idiots" <G>  (from other post)

On Sun, 17 Mar 2002 04:55:45 UTC, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> 
wrote:

> 
> 
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

If your COBOL programs use ODBC to access the data in the "MS Access" 
database, they can do the same thing if the data tables are stored in 
some other RDBMS. All you have to do is write the program so that it 
uses SQL 92 compliant SQL code and just about any RDBMS engine can be 
used to store the data. I recently wrote an "add in" to the 
Grandmaster Suite Payroll package using the Microfocus   COBOL ESQL 
option that allows export/import to an ODBC compliant database engine.
It works with DB2, SQL Server, and even Excel and "text files" through
the ODBC interface.

> Sole reason I'm switching - most of the major corporations here in Calgary as HQ
> for oil/gas, use some form of DB package, DB2, Oracle etc. Inevitably what I am
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Revenue is revenue, and a good thing :-)

> - (2) Allow them entry directly into M/S Access - obviates the necessity of my
> writing or receiving CSVs and they can manipulate the data, (generating
> additional report tables etc.), as they see fit..
> 

You should have option 3 - allow the user to choose the database where
the data is stored. The ODBC interface shipped with Windows allows a 
DSN to be defined for just about any database engine. Providing option
3 allows the user of the application to determine where the data is 
stored.

> #2 is where Jerry's downside comes into play. By storing current thicknesses of
> 'Items' the purpose of the application is to trigger when sufficient corrosion
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> - Me, the application developer

Yes, you are responsible as you did not provide a third option for 
support of user determined data storage and security. 

> - the Inspection Company, or

They carry a responsibility if they cannot show that the data was 
"monkied" with

> - the Oil Corporation  ? (Don't forget the latter can employ a bevy of legal
> sharks to 'prove' their nose is clean.)
> 

They carry a responsibility if their employees "monkied" and the 
changes were ordered my "management".

If you provide the "user determined" storage and security then the 
legal sharks will have a harder time of placing the blame on the 
application developer. You can make it an item in the contract/license
for the use of the software - it may not help much, but something is 
better than nothing.

> Now translate the same sort of problem to a financial DB used by our friends at
> say, Enron.
> 

Based on the news bits I have seen, the scenario of outright fraud 
supported by the "management" and the outside auditors will defeat any
security measures that can be put in place.

> Anybody out there got some recommendations about DB security, or can point us to
> references on the topic ?
> 
> Jimmy, Calgary AB
> 

You can use "triggers" in the database to timestamp modifications of 
the rows in the data tables and capture the time and user that did the
modifications. Coupled with a decent RDBMS security set up this will 
at least identify the culprit doing the data modifications, although 
this can be bypassed by a competent DBA - but then the responsibility 
for data security rests on that same DBA.....

The data tables would have to have a "created timestamp" and a 
"modified timestamp" for each row. The values set could determine if 
the row was modified after it was inserted into the table.

Use of triggers for this is supported by DB2, SQL Server and Oracle at
least (probably PostgreSQL as well).


Both DB2 and Oracle support individual user access settings per table,
SQL Server sort  of does this but it is a bit complicated.

You would have to allow for the application to create its data tables 
for each of the database engines supported, as there are differences 
in the data definition langauges (DDL) for each engine. The SQL syntax
used for data manipulation should adhere to the SQL 92 standard as 
this is supported by most database engines and/or ODBC drivers.


<Start Plug>
You can download a personal developers edition of IBMs DB2 product 
from < 
http://www14.software.ibm.com/webapp/download/category.jsp?s=c&cat=dat
a > This is a single user version of the full DB2 product. You can use
it to develop applications without any license fees.

I believe an equivalent is available from Oracle as well < 
http://www.oracle.com >

and PostgreSQL is freely available and is open source as well < 
http://www.postgresql.org/ >

<End Plug>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: DataBase Security - WAS Re: Microfocus vs AcuCobol

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-03-18T13:29:16+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c954519_8@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<64826f50.0203071424.2d72933b@posting.google.com> <bfdfc3e8.0203160806.34326187@posting.google.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-yyqDHmzb2jzy@thishost> <1vUk8.265598$pN4.16524374@bin8.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3C942162.BA974FDD@shaw.ca>`

```
Jimmy, Lorne has covered this in his response and it is fine.

Couple of points...

If you use a multi-tiered architecture you don't need database triggers to
timestamp the access. It can be built directly into the Update Pathway.
Update permission is restricted only to programs in that level.

It's not that there is anything wrong with triggers per se, but they lock
you in to a particular database and they are not part of your application
control.

You should also consider generating an audit trail where parties other than
yourself are granted update access or permission to use the update path.

I would disagree with Lorne that you need another RDB (I don't share his
contempt for ACCESS) but that is really something for you to decide.

As Lorne observed, if people really want to bend things they will. The best
you can hope for is to know who stole the horse and when, before you bolt
the stable door...

Pete.

#### ↳ Re: Microfocus vs AcuCobol

- **From:** "Sal Cambareri" <salcam@teleport.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-19T17:37:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MHKl8.1388$s8.146731@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<64826f50.0203071424.2d72933b@posting.google.com>`

```
Simon-
The conversion from ICOBOL to AcuCobol is trivial. Not only is the Acu
instruction set a complete superset of all of the ICOBOL verbs (e.g. DELETE
FILE xxxx), there are flags and switches available for both the compiler and
the runtime that account for all of the ICOBOL oddities (aka ANSI 85
variances) including, but not limited to, an identical implementation of the
Screen Section, initialization of the Working Storage, and DG specific file
statuses. As an ICOBOL users, you'll be very pleased with the speed and
flexibility of the Vision ISAM package included in the runtime. As someone
who lived for a long time with the ICOBOL ISAM limitations, I continue to be
impressed by its power speed and flexibility of the Vision ISAMs.  Some
people (e.g. Thane H.), for reasons I don't understand don't like the Acu
debugger.  I love it, especially the 5.1 version which empowers me to debug
in an intuitively reasonable way threaded COBOL.
In converting to Acu from ICOBOL, the only real headache my clients had 10
years ago (when we migrated) was that the #P passfile utility did not exist.
I ended up writing a COBOL replacement that has served pretty well. It was
possible to do so because AcuCOBOL provides so many useful COBOL extensions.
I haven't used MicroFocus for a couple of years, and frankly I just didn't
like it. The major annoyances were the too complex Animator, the Screen
Section variances with ICOBOL, and the limitation of source to 80 column
mode.  I've never tried to convert from ICOBOL to MicroFocus but I imagine
that if your source is in terminal mode, you will find the 80 column
conversion very time consuming.
Regards,
Sal
(No, I don't own stock in AcuCOBOL, but there are times I wish I did.)
