## Thread: Nested Reads Syntax — 8 msgs

### Nested Reads Syntax

- **From:** "John Marinatos" <marj0008@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2002-03-07T19:06:52-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c9Th8.6193$Nn6.574225@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com>`

```
My first problem was:

> I am trying to read in a sequential file to create an employee directory.
> However, the record is split into two lines with a carriage return in
> between.  Is there any way to ignore those carriage returns?
>
> I could use the help.


I was told to do two reads, one to read the first line of the
record and the second read for the second line of the record.  What would be
the syntax of that?
```

#### ↳ Re: Nested Reads Syntax

- **From:** jnjsle@aol.com (Jnjsle)
- **Date:** 2002-03-08T03:03:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020307220318.02576.00000079@mb-mu.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<c9Th8.6193$Nn6.574225@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com>`

```
Hi John,

I guess what you're asking is how do I wind  up with 1 line without the CR
bytes.  Assuming you can just ignore the CRs once you have a single record set
up, you can do the following:

In Working Storage  

01  2-PART-REC.
     05  PART-1.
          10  TEXT-1        PIC X(079).
          10  CR-1           PIC X(001).
     05  PART-2.
          10  TEXT-2        PIC X(079).
          10  CR-2           PIC X(001).

In the PROCEDURE DIVISION

        READ IP-FILE INTO PART-1
        READ IP-FILE INTO PART-2

If the CRs REALLY annoy you you can:

In Working Storage  

01  2-PART-REC.
     05  PART-1.
          10  TEXT-1        PIC X(079).
     05  PART-2.
          10  TEXT-2        PIC X(079).

In the PROCEDURE DIVISION

        READ IP-FILE INTO PART-1
        READ IP-FILE INTO PART-2 

In the 2nd example the CRs are truncated by the generated move into the 79 byte
PART-x data fields.

HTH, Jack.
```

#### ↳ Re: Nested Reads Syntax

- **From:** jnjsle@aol.com (Jnjsle)
- **Date:** 2002-03-08T03:20:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020307222051.02576.00000080@mb-mu.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<c9Th8.6193$Nn6.574225@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com>`

```
Hi John,

You might try this, if you can ignore the CRs when you work on the record:

In working storage:

      01  2-PART-REC.			
          05  PART-1        PIC  X(079).
          05  CR-1          PIC  X(001).
          05  PART-2        PIC  X(079).
          05  CR-2          PIC  X(001).

In the PROCEDURE DIVISION

         READ IP-FILE INTO PART-1
         READ IP-FILE INTO PART-2

If the CRs REALLY, REALLY annoy you, you can:

In working storage:

      01  2-PART-REC.			
          05  PART-1        PIC  X(079).
          05  PART-2        PIC  X(079).

In the PROCEDURE DIVISION

         READ IP-FILE INTO PART-1
         READ IP-FILE INTO PART-2  
          
The moves generated by the reads will truncate the CRs. Naturally, Im assuming
80 byte recs, but you get the idea.

HTH, Jack.
```

```
Hi John,

You might try this, if you can ignore the CRs when you work on the
record:

In working storage:

      01  2-PART-REC.			
          05  PART-1        PIC  X(079).
          05  CR-1          PIC  X(001).
          05  PART-2        PIC  X(079).
          05  CR-2          PIC  X(001).

In the PROCEDURE DIVISION

         READ IP-FILE INTO PART-1
         READ IP-FILE INTO PART-2

If the CRs REALLY, REALLY annoy you, you can:

In working storage:

      01  2-PART-REC.			
          05  PART-1        PIC  X(079).
          05  PART-2        PIC  X(079).

In the PROCEDURE DIVISION

         READ IP-FILE INTO PART-1
         READ IP-FILE INTO PART-2  
          
The moves generated by the reads will truncate the CRs. Naturally, Im
assuming 80 byte recs, but you get the idea.

HTH, Jack.

#### ↳ Re: Nested Reads Syntax

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-03-08T14:24:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a6ahbv$q4j$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<c9Th8.6193$Nn6.574225@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com>`

```

On  7-Mar-2002, "John Marinatos" <marj0008@bellsouth.net> wrote:

> > I am trying to read in a sequential file to create an employee
> > directory.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> be the syntax of that?

Usually with header and detail records, there is a field that we can use to
determine which type of record we are reading.  If you don't have that, you
have to assume that every time one record will be followed by the next and
you won't ever have skips.

Can you do this:

01    MY-RECORD.
        05    MY-RECORD-TYPE             PIC X(05).
        88    MY-HEADER                                           VALUE
       'HEAD'.
        05    FILLER                                PIC X(75).

Or possibly
   IF MY-FIELD NUMERIC
           MOVE MY-RECORD          TO MY-DETAIL
   ELSE
            MOVE MY-RECORD          TO MY-HEAD
   END-IF.
```

#### ↳ Re: Nested Reads Syntax

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@invalid.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-09T12:40:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lpni8.8602$hO6.807520@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<c9Th8.6193$Nn6.574225@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com>`

```

"John Marinatos" <marj0008@bellsouth.net> wrote in message
news:c9Th8.6193$Nn6.574225@e3500-atl2.usenetserver.com...
> My first problem was:
>
> > I am trying to read in a sequential file to create an employee
directory.
> > However, the record is split into two lines with a carriage return in
> > between.  Is there any way to ignore those carriage returns?
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> record and the second read for the second line of the record.  What would
be
> the syntax of that?

READ IN-FILE INTO AREA-1.
READ IN-FILE INTO AREA-2
(processing)
WRITE OUT-REC FROM AREA-1.
WRITE OUT-REC FROM AREA-2.
```

## Thread: FAQ *formatting* input — 19 msgs

_Topics: [`Meta: FAQs, group policy, charter`](topics.md#meta)_

### FAQ *formatting* input

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-07T23:09:50-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a69h37$v1j$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I have (finally) started updating the COBOL FAQ.  I have lots and LOTS of
technical updates to do, but I wanted to check if the "revised" version is
useable/visible via all currently used web-browsers.

Please check out:

    http://cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

and let me know if you see any "formatting" problems.  When you reply (via
the NG or private email), please let me know what browser you are using
(name, release, version) and under what operating system.

I *will* be asking for technical review - once I catch up on the errors that
I already know are there.

P.S.  I may or may not "get around" to updating the auto-posted FAQ reminder
before I finish my updates.  Don't worry about when it says the last updates
were.
```

<a id="t-d7e1a4d6fa"></a>
## Thread: Help with regard to VSAM file open error RC=39 — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Help with regard to VSAM file open error RC=39

- **From:** arunprasath420@yahoo.com (Arun Prasath)
- **Date:** 2002-03-08T08:14:20-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a3ef804e.0203080814.9ab0290@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi all,

i tried to open a vsam file in a cobol program, but got an open error
message with the file status flag as 39.
On browsing the vsam file using file aid, i found that some key values
had some spaces in them at the end of the key value(some 5 records out
of 5000 had this). However, when i deleted these 5 records and ran the
job, it ran good.

Can anyone explain, how the program threw an open error when there
should have been a read error.

Thanks in advance,
Arun Prasath
```

#### ↳ Re: Help with regard to VSAM file open error RC=39

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-08T11:39:06-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a6at00$nlg$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<a3ef804e.0203080814.9ab0290@posting.google.com>`

```
File status of 39 is a "fixed attribute conflict". My guess (and it is only
a GUESS) is that those records (with the spaces at the end of the key)
actually had keys LONGER than that specified in your program's description
of the file.  When those records were removed, the file then had only keys
that matched the SIZE of what you specified in your program.

Other possibilities exist - depending on exactly HOW you deleted the "bad"
records.  It is possible that in the process of deleting them (possibly via
File/AID) you managed to "correct" some other problem in the "fixed
attribute" information for that file.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Help with regard to VSAM file open error RC=39

- **From:** arunprasath420@yahoo.com (Arun Prasath)
- **Date:** 2002-03-11T09:21:56-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a3ef804e.0203110921.e70ff1d@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<a3ef804e.0203080814.9ab0290@posting.google.com> <a6at00$nlg$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Hi William,

Thanks for the tip. I tried to run the job again with the same type of
'corrupt' records but with fewer records and it ran fine.

Also i discovered a new message in the spool for the last time the job
abended The message was :
R005 FXI030DS INDD - VOLUME COUNT FOR DD EXCEEDED:SPECIFIC- 10
NON-SPECIFIC- 108

,although this step ran with return code 0(this step runs befor the
step where the abend occurs). Could this message be in anyway
connected to the abend.

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message news:<a6at00$nlg$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>...
> File status of 39 is a "fixed attribute conflict". My guess (and it is only
> a GUESS) is that those records (with the spaces at the end of the key)
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Arun Prasath
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help with regard to VSAM file open error RC=39

- **From:** jacksleight@hotmail.com (Jack Sleight)
- **Date:** 2002-03-12T21:26:30-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a2d426e.0203122126.3e64b41b@posting.google.com>`

Hi Arun,

Answering your 1st question. This takes me back a bit, but I seem to
remember that OPEN on input primes the buffers. So, it's possible to
get a "READ" type of error on OPEN.

BTW, the last error you encountered seems to indicate that you didn't
specify the correct number of volumes to contain the dataset. Don't
know how that can happen on input, since the OS keeps track of that.

Jack

### FAQ "accessed counter" (and other changes)

```
Hopefully, I have changed the "accessed counter" so that it is visible via
non-M$ viewers.  Please let me know if it still is NOT viewable via other
browsers.

I have also indented the TOC entries (something that I had planned to do
eventually).  This does "point out" that numbering is NOT corrected (yet).

Just so it is handy (for those checking it out), here is the URL again.

 http://cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

P.S.  I am WELL aware that Word is "notorious" for the HTML that it
produces.  (The latest version is BETTER than older ones, but still not
great).  I am curious, I can produce a "filtered" web page which is only
(ONLY???) about 210kb instead of the current "full" (easier for me to
maintain via Word) version that is 307kb.  As I have a DSL line, I was
wondering how much this matters to others?  For those of you who have 50k
(or similar) "download" restrictions, this won't help much - but I don't
know how much of an improvement in "accessibility" this change would be.
Comments?

P.P.S.  For those of you seeing "funny" characters, my guess is that you are
using a 7-bit rather than an 8-bit character set.  Some of these characters
are for "tm" and similar special characters.  Unless there is a STRONG
feeling otherwise, I plan on keeping those "as is".
```

#### ↳ Re: FAQ "accessed counter" (and other changes)

- **From:** Edward Reid <edward@paleo.org>
- **Date:** 2002-03-08T13:24:04-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01HW.B8AE6BF404345C1E0F751990@news-east.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<a6asnn$fa9$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
On Fri, 8 Mar 2002 12:34:41 -0500, William M. Klein wrote
> I can produce a "filtered" web page which is only
> (ONLY???) about 210kb instead of the current "full" (easier for me to
> maintain via Word) version that is 307kb.  As I have a DSL line, I was
> wondering how much this matters to others?

I've got ISDN now, but I well remember modem access (and not fondly).

On a fast computer, it took about 80 seconds to download the entire FAQ 
via one B channel on an ISDN connection. With two B channels, it not 
surprisingly took about half that time. My experience is that the 
advantage of ISDN is considerably greater than the nominal bit rate 
would lead me to expect, so I believe that loading the FAQ will 
generally take modem users at least two minutes.

At that rate, I would say that a 1/3 size reduction is certainly worth 
pursuing, but not an absolute necessity.

Of course, another possibilty is breaking it up. That's a two-edged 
sword. When I'm reading a large part of an FAQ, I like to have it all 
in one page, but if I'm just looking for a very specific answer, then 
I'd rather have it broken up. Then of course there are the maintenance 
issues. In the (unrelated) FAQ that I maintain, I've broken it up, but 
a lot of that was to bypass the size restrictions in many news servers 
and newsreaders back 6-8 years ago.

Edward Reid
```

#### ↳ Re: FAQ "accessed counter" (and other changes)

- **From:** Edward Reid <edward@paleo.org>
- **Date:** 2002-03-08T14:12:52-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01HW.B8AE776404370B9A0F751990@news-east.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<a6asnn$fa9$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
On Fri, 8 Mar 2002 12:34:41 -0500, William M. Klein wrote
> P.P.S.  For those of you seeing "funny" characters, my guess is that you are
> using a 7-bit rather than an 8-bit character set.  Some of these characters
> are for "tm" and similar special characters.  Unless there is a STRONG
> feeling otherwise, I plan on keeping those "as is".

All of these have standard HTML encodings, and it's almost guaranteed 
that they will fail somewhere if you don't use the encodings. Despite 
my overall cynicism about Word, I'm still surprised that it isn't doing 
the conversions for you.

The tags related to what you mentioned are

  &trade;     for the trademark symbol (superscripted TM)
  &reg;       for the registered symbol (R in a circle)
  &copy;      for the copyright symbol (C in a circle)

For a complete list, see

  http://www.w3.org/TR/html4/sgml/entities.html

Edward Reid
```

#### ↳ Re: FAQ "accessed counter" (and other changes)

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-03-09T11:50:16+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c8944f4_7@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<a6asnn$fa9$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Bill, I think this is as good a FAQ as I have seen.

It serves its intended purpose and is easy to navigate. I liked the sample
code postings and I liked the simple style of writing.

I have some stuff I'll send you which might be of use for it.

The ONLY thing I saw which made me grind my teeth was this:

<teeth grind>
On discussing the use of PACKED Decimal...

<begin quote>
..."Standard" more or less numeric data types include USAGE BINARY and USAGE
PACKED-DECIMAL. These were introduced in the '85 Standard and provide for
portable source code - but NOT NECESSARILY PORTABLE STORAGE. Taking the most
simple example,

05 How-Many-Bytes Pic 9(04) Packed-Decimal.

may be stored as either 2 or 3 "bytes" (a concept that isn't in the current
Standard - but will be in the next one) of storage - depending on whether
your compiler "requires" a sign-nibble for even unsigned packed-decimal
items. The general rule is that all "meaningful" digits of a packed-decimal
item take 1 nibble (half of a byte) and the sign takes another. But when the
data definition does NOT include a sign (S), then some systems still require
the storage and some don't; some store an unsigned numeric field with the
same sign-nibble as a positive number and some don't. (Most common
examples - Most IBM-compatible PC's treat positive and unsigned  numeric
fields with a X'3' sign-nibble - while most IBM compatible mainframes treat
positive numeric fields with a X'C' while unsigned have a X'F' sign-nibble).
<end quote>

Now, I can see the dilemma here... It is trying to be all things to all
people and covers the bases very well  but I ask myself whether someone
consulting the COBOL FAQ really needs to know or care about this stuff. It
has been highly influenced by your own exposure to the forthcoming standard,
which I might remind you is NOT implemented yet. People accessing a FAQ are
not really interested in the THEORY. They want simple solutions to REAL
problems NOW.

Students coming to the FAQ are looking for some simple rules to use when
dealing with packed fields.

I teach the following:

"ALWAYS make packed fields an ODD number of  digits and ALWAYS specifiy a
sign."

It is a simple and effective rule, yet the "most simple example" above,
violates both of these considerations <G>.
(Anyone from an IBM background will understand that specifying UNSIGNED
packed fields in COBOL requires the compiler to generate extra code to force
the positive sign nibble, and specifying an EVEN number of digits forces the
allocation of an ODD number anyway, but I don't think there's any need to
burden  young minds with this level of detail unless they ask why.)

The "Wisdom of Dashie" then continues...<G>

"Don't use Packed unless you have to, for compatibility with incoming data.
It is an archaic and outmoded representation that was developed to save
space when memory and external storage were prohibitively expensive, and to
provide fixed point decimal working on an architecture that was designed for
binary. "

So, we end up with three simple things to remember:

1. Don't use Packed unless you HAVE to... (I am hopeful that we can
eventually remove ALL packed data from ALL systems if the rising generation
implement this...<G>)

2. If you do use it, put a SIGN in the picture.

3. If you do use it, have an ODD number of digits in the picture.

As this format was invented by IBM, and is implemented these days by other
vendors because of the necessity for IBM compatibility, it seems reasonable
to me to explain it in terms of IBM's implementation, THEN mention that some
other implementations may hold different sign nibbles etc. People who really
want that level of platform specific detail should RTFM or post to CLC, not
look for it in a FAQ. As for the pending standard deciding that storage
could be two or three bytes or whatever, I have two observations:

1. Do I care? (COBOL is supposed to shield me from this...)
2. Are we ever likely to see "The Word made Flesh"?

</teeth grind> <G>

Pete.

PS further comments below...

William M. Klein <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:a6asnn$fa9$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net...

> non-M$ viewers.  Please let me know if it still is NOT viewable via other
> browsers.

to do a download (this usually happens when foreign character sets are being
used, like Cyrillic) but the download fails and it shows the site anyway.

> I have also indented the TOC entries (something that I had planned to do
> eventually).  This does "point out" that numbering is NOT corrected (yet).
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> Comments?

I use a 56K PCMCIA modem and it was not an extraordinary wait. In fact, it
loaded quite well.
>
> P.P.S.  For those of you seeing "funny" characters, my guess is that you
are
> using a 7-bit rather than an 8-bit character set.  Some of these
characters
> are for "tm" and similar special characters.  Unless there is a STRONG

"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:
> 
> Bill, I think this is as good a FAQ as I have seen.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> On discussing the use of PACKED Decimal...

[most deleted]

Hi Peter,

What do you advocate in place of using packed-decimal?  Binary? 
Display?  Our shop uses packed-decimal almost exclusively for numeric
fields that are used in computations.  Is it not true that using Display
is much less efficient because it has to convert the field to
packed-decimal before doing the arithmatic?  (This is, obviously, on an
IBM mainframe.)  The only time I would use Display for a numeric field
used in a calculation is if I were to write it to/read it from a file
that was going to be shared with another platform.

And as for binary, I would almost never concider it, especially for a
field being written to/from a file.  It's just too hard for me to read
in a dump.  I only use binary data for subscripts and for CICS length
fields.  (Maybe a few others, but that's all I can think of.)

I wish my compiler (COBOL II under VSE, soon converting to COBOL for
VSE/ESA) had a no-sign packed-decimal field (vs. an unsigned
packed-decimal).  We get transaction data from Visa where X'0000010000'
represents 100.00.  The conversion is kind of a pain.  ah well...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Packed decimals WAS "FAQ "accessed counter" (and other changes)"

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-03-10T02:46:46+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c8a1532_6@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<a6asnn$fa9$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <3c8944f4_7@Usenet.com> <3C896D2E.A0C4F032@sprynet.com>`

```

Frank Swarbrick <infocat@sprynet.com> wrote in message
news:3C896D2E.A0C4F032@sprynet.com...
> "Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> packed-decimal before doing the arithmatic?

Actually, it converts the field to binary, then to Packed...<G>

So you care about efficiency? On a 360-30 in 1967 it definitely mattered.

As a 386 Intel processor (remember them? <G>) had about 1.7 times the power
of  Gene Amdahl's fabulous 60s machine, and today's pentium is over 500
times more powerful still, and your 390 Mainframe eats the Pentium (well,
that one's arguable, (if we are talking > 1GIG chips) but you catch my
drift), how much arithmetic are you actually doing? The requirement to
convert is a negligible overhead, compared to the requirement to move say,
4K of data around in memory. I would take bets that your programs do a LOT
more data movement than they do arithmetic...

If you really think packed format is efficient, why don't you define your
subscripts that way?...Oh, you do...sorry...<G>

On the rare occasions (these days) when I program IBM mainframes, I use COMP
or BINARY for arithmetic.I use packed ONLY when the incoming data is in that
format, so I have little choice.

The original 360 architecture (still implemented in your 390) was intended
for use with binary and floating point. Packed decimal was an optional extra
that Amdahl built to capture the commercial market where currencies needed 2
decimals. Remember also, that memory was made by hand, disks had not been
invented yet, and both internal and external storage were EXPENSIVE. (I just
did some quick calculations, and today, for the same price as we paid for
32k of main storage in 1965 (around $US100,000 in today's money!) , you
could buy several TRILLION bytes!)   His famous ROS (Read Only Storage)
cards allowed the instruction set of the machine to be altered, so the
option for packed decimal was easily implemented. However, the control logic
had to go stepping through ROS to execute each instruction and this slowed
the system and made it lose in benchmarks. The solution was to move the
"decimal" (packed) instruction set to hardware and stop making it an
option...

I stand by what I said: It is an archaic system of representing numbers that
is neither use nor ornament in today's world. In its time, and for the
purposes for which it was originally conceived, it was useful. Now it isn't.
It just puts another hurdle in the way of open systems and inter platform
communication.


(This is, obviously, on an
> IBM mainframe.)  The only time I would use Display for a numeric field
> used in a calculation is if I were to write it to/read it from a file
> that was going to be shared with another platform.
>
Good. Fair comment. There will be a lot more of this sort of thing going on
in the next few years...

> And as for binary, I would almost never concider it, especially for a
> field being written to/from a file.  It's just too hard for me to read
> in a dump.
Ah, so you DON'T care about efficiency after all? <G> Especially not if it
causes you any possible inconvenience...<G>? If you used binary you wouldn't
be looking at so many dumps...My experience is that around 80% of dumps
caused by COBOL Programmers are S0c7s....caused by......PACKED FIELDS!

This exception is raised ONLY by the DECIMAL instruction set... rest my case
<G>.

I would agree that keys in binary are troublesome, but they are in packed
format too... It comes back to the power of modern hardware; make your key
fields display (they're not usually used for arithmetic anyway...)


 I only use binary data for subscripts and for CICS length
> fields.  (Maybe a few others, but that's all I can think of.)
>
Why?<G> If you only care about efficiency when it suits you, why not leave
the subscripts as packed or display?<G> If you think efficiency is
important, then why not make everything binary?(That's what the machine
"likes"....Ah, but it's harder to read in dumps...I forgot...<G>)

> I wish my compiler (COBOL II under VSE, soon converting to COBOL for
> VSE/ESA) had a no-sign packed-decimal field (vs. an unsigned
> packed-decimal).  We get transaction data from Visa where X'0000010000'
> represents 100.00.  The conversion is kind of a pain.  ah well...
>
Ah, now I see why you have trouble reading dumps....<G> The above could
NEVER represent decimal 100.00 in base 16....any more than 10 hex digits can
represent an integral number of bytes. I'm honestly not sure what you mean
by the above being Hex. If it was, it would represent the number 1048576 in
decimal.

If you are saying the above is a string of (unsigned) Packed decimal digits
(more correctly Binary Coded Decimal digits) then it would represent 5 bytes
of data and could be easily converted by ANDing x'F' in the low order nibble
then treating it as a signed packed field.

(Easy in Assembler, more tedious in COBOL. Here's the COBOL code I would
use...:

...
working-storage section.
01  five-bytes-from-visa pic x(5).

01  packed-decimal-factory.
      12 pdf-in                     pic x(5).
      12 pdf-ANDx0F         pic x value x'0F'.
01   packed-value   redefines packed-decimal-factory pic s9(11)      COMP-3.
01   packed-amt     redefines packed-value                 pic s9(9)v99
COMP-3.
...
procedure division.
...
get-visa-amount.
      *>  move the input to the factory...
      move five-bytes-from-visa to pdf-in
      compute packed-value = packed-value / 100
      *>  factory leaves a "normal" packed amount with correct decimal
alignment in "packed-amt"...
      *>  If you wish to re-use the factory you must reset the x'0F' in
pdf-ANDx0F.
      move packed-amt to.............(whatever)

This way there are 2 COBOL instructions to convert the "no-sign" input...and
one of those is setting it up. Not really "painful" is it? Notice that I
take my own advice and make packed fields signed, and an odd number of
digits <G>...

Frank, I really didn't mean to be hard on you and despite the frivolous tone
of my response, I do honestly believe that Packed Decimal is something we'd
all be better off without.

For example, would it really be so terrible if your VISA input field
occupied 10 bytes instead of 5? It has to be converted to something usable
anyway... I agree there might be a problem if I said make it 2K instead of
5, but 10 bytes?...

Most commercial applications are not "compute" intensive. Manipulating data
requires many more CPU cycles (irrespective of how fast and plentiful they
are) than doing some arithmetic conversions on a few data fields.

Most of the ways we do things in COBOL are historic and stem from a time
when processors and storage were incredibly limited by today's standards. We
need to re-examine some of the "accepted techniques" we use to manipulate
data.

I believe COMP-3 is a good candidate for retirement.

(Others may argue that I am a good candidate for retirement...<G> As time
goes by, I find this harder to refute <G>)

Pete.




 Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services
----------------------------------------------------------
    ** SPEED ** RETENTION ** COMPLETION ** ANONYMITY **
----------------------------------------------------------        
                http://www.usenet.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Packed decimal is good format for quality control.

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "ups" <upsi@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-09T03:22:25+11:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c8a3683@news.alphalink.com.au>`
- **References:** `<a6asnn$fa9$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <3c8944f4_7@Usenet.com> <3C896D2E.A0C4F032@sprynet.com> <3c8a1532_6@Usenet.com>`

```
In defence of packed decimals:

Remember that programmers sometime forget to initialise fields, or sometimes
move crud into a numeric field.

I would much rather be woken up in the middle of the night to fix a S0C7
data exception,
that try to figure out two weeks later why the accounts don't reconcile.

Binary fields do not provide such protection - that extra sleep is not a
good trade off in the long term.
Getting rid of packed fields is like shooting the messenger, instead of
fighting the war.
I would favour quality control over "quick and dirty" most of the time.

However, I don't say that no fields should be binary - it's a matter of
horses for courses.
For example, I use indexing rather than subscripts most of the time.

I doubt the suggestion that packed fields get converted to binary format for
arithmetic.
More likely the circuits are decimal adders.
Not quite as quick as a binary adder,
but a lot faster than coverting the whole number into binary and back again.

By the way some of IBMs midrange computers have packed decimals with no sign
nybble,
so it is a shame that their own mainframes don't have better support for it.

The suggested code for converting should have specified division by 10 not
100.

Strictly speaking the x'0F' sign nybble was incorrect for a signed field.
For full compatibility with all software, I suggest using x'0C' for signed
non-negative numbers.
The CPU may not care, but some software out here is fussy about this sort of
thing.

I would also suggest using DIVIDE rather than COMPUTE, to avoid the chance
of high precision intermediates,
which some compilers sometimes go overboard with.
------------------------------------------------------------

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Packed decimal is good format for quality control.

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-03-10T14:01:55+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c8ab24d_5@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<a6asnn$fa9$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <3c8944f4_7@Usenet.com> <3C896D2E.A0C4F032@sprynet.com> <3c8a1532_6@Usenet.com> <3c8a3683@news.alphalink.com.au>`

```
A good response, but it fails to persuade me...<G>

If the only reason you use Packed is to ensure data integrity and "Quality
Control", then you would have to argue that all the billions of lines of
COBOL written on systems that DON'T implement this format were never Quality
Controlled....

The point is that QC is a separate issue that involves more than just the
programmer.

If data is validated by programs it really doesn't matter whether it is in
packed format or not.On this basis you could argue the "other side of the
coin", that having hardware detection of data exceptions actually ENCOURAGES
sloppy programming and poor QC because the programmer knows that if the
validation logic fails (or doesn't get written) the system will pick up
errors anyway...<G>

You may be right that modern hardware uses decimal adders, but I can assure
you that the original generated code used CVB then PACK.

I see you share Frank's (and most IBM Mainframers') ambivalent attitude to
the use of binary...<G>. We all do what we were taught and what we have
"grown up" with... that was part of what my post was aimed at. The
re-examination of some of the things that "everybody knows", particularly in
regard to packed decimals.
(As a matter of record, I ALWAYS define tables with indexes and use indexes
and the SEARCH verb to manipulate them. I may kid myself it is for
efficiency, but it is really because the source code looks more elegant.
These days I am nowhere near as concerned with efficiency as I was 30 years
ago. (See my previous post here on "Re: Redefinition of item containing an
OCCURS DEPENDING ON clause", posted on February 21st this year.)

Thanks for the correction to the code. It should have been 10 as you pointed
out. The sign byte could be x'0c' or 'x0f'; I chose x'0f' because this is
the state it would be in before arithmetic was performed on it. But it
really doesn't matter.

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Packed decimal is good format for quality control.

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-09T20:35:57-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a6egqo$afv$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<a6asnn$fa9$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <3c8944f4_7@Usenet.com> <3C896D2E.A0C4F032@sprynet.com> <3c8a1532_6@Usenet.com> <3c8a3683@news.alphalink.com.au> <3c8ab24d_5@Usenet.com>`

```
For the "current" IBM position (or at least part of it) on this whole topic,
see:

 http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/igy3pg00/7.1.2.1?

which says (in part),

"When you use a data item mainly for arithmetic or as a subscript, code
USAGE BINARY on the data description entry for the item. The operations for
manipulating binary data are faster than those for manipulating decimal
data.
However, if a fixed-point arithmetic statement has intermediate results with
a large precision (number of significant digits), the compiler uses decimal
arithmetic anyway, after converting the operands to packed-decimal form. For
fixed-point arithmetic statements, the compiler normally uses binary
arithmetic for simple computations with binary operands if the precision is
eight digits or fewer. Above 18 digits, the compiler always uses decimal
arithmetic. With a precision of nine to 18 digits, the compiler uses either
form.

To produce the most efficient code for a BINARY data item, ensure that it
has:


  a.. A sign (an S in its PICTURE clause)

  b.. Eight or fewer digits


For a data item that is larger than eight digits or is used with DISPLAY
data items, use PACKED-DECIMAL. The code generated for PACKED-DECIMAL data
items can be as fast as that for BINARY data items in some cases, especially
if the statement is complicated or specifies rounding."
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Packed decimal is good format for quality control.

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-03-10T17:18:05+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c8adf56_6@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<a6asnn$fa9$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <3c8944f4_7@Usenet.com> <3C896D2E.A0C4F032@sprynet.com> <3c8a1532_6@Usenet.com> <3c8a3683@news.alphalink.com.au> <3c8ab24d_5@Usenet.com> <a6egqo$afv$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Thanks Bill, this is "interesting" but it misses the whole point...

The issue is not whether Packed or Binary is efficient on Mainframes.

Most of us who are not working on IBM mainframes don't care...<G>
(In fact, there are mainframe programmers who don't care and quite properly,
given the power of modern hardware.)

The issue is whether PACKED decimal has a place in the modern World.

IBM will argue that it does. (As they are still charging people an arm and a
leg for hardware that implements it, they WOULD say that...<G>)

I'm arguing that it doesn't.

I summarise my position as follows:

"PACKED format was originally implemented to save expensive resources and
provide fixed point decimal arithmetic on an architecture that was
intrinsically binary. It did this very well. At the time there was little
requirement to transfer data across platforms. Today, the resources it was
designed to save are no longer expensive, processor power has increased to
the point where an arithmetic conversion overhead (except in really
exceptional circumstances) is no problem, and the sharing of data between
platforms is becoming ESSENTIAL.

I therefore submit that this is a stumbling block to the development of open
systems, it provides little gain even on the architecture for which it was
originally intended (how many COMMERCIAL calculations involve numbers of
more than 18 (or even 8) digits?), and it is of arguable use in controlling
the "quality" of data being processed on these platforms.

For the above reasons, I suggest that IBM Mainframe programmers should avoid
using it, and new programmers should be advised to do likewise."

There are millions of lines of COBOL written on platforms that DON'T support
PACKED Decimal and those applications manage fine without it. Many of the
old BUNCH  (Burroughs, Univac, NCR, CDC, and Honeywell, also include ICL
(now Fujitsu) in there... ) systems had their COBOL converted to other
platforms and still continued to run fine without the need for Packed
Decimal...

I realise this position will not be popular (especially at IBM <G>) but I
hold it nonetheless...

The sooner this data format disappears from IT, the better for all
concerned.

Pete.

> > > > digits <G>...
> > > >
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > > of my response, I do honestly believe that Packed Decimal is
something
> > > we'd
> > > > all be better off without.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > > Most commercial applications are not "compute" intensive.
Manipulating
> > > data
> > > > requires many more CPU cycles (irrespective of how fast and
plentiful
> > they
> > > > are) than doing some arithmetic conversions on a few data fields.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Packed decimal is good format for quality control.

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Bill <wfsent@optonline.net>
- **Date:** 2002-03-10T05:03:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C8AE91E.4309E267@optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<a6asnn$fa9$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <3c8944f4_7@Usenet.com> <3C896D2E.A0C4F032@sprynet.com> <3c8a1532_6@Usenet.com> <3c8a3683@news.alphalink.com.au> <3c8ab24d_5@Usenet.com> <a6egqo$afv$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3c8adf56_6@Usenet.com>`

```
Hi Peter,
	I much prefer browsing data files that store numbers in comp-3 format.
You can easily see what the 'numbers' are. , debugging datafiles that
are s9(18) comp is a real pain. 

>>(how many COMMERCIAL calculations involve numbers of more than 18 (or even 8) digits?)

	Most 'real' world applications already use more than 8 digits. My 401k
statements quote my stock holdings to 3 places of decimal (1.234 etc).
And in the good old days when you had to deal with multicurrency trades
lira was always good for eating up decimal places. (the euro has taken a
lot of the fun out of it).

	Several of the applications I've worked on in the past used well over
18 digits (stock trades, bank balances).
When people are trading millions of anything the 'fractions' tend to add
up to something worth keeping track of.


Bill 	

 

"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:
> 
> Thanks Bill, this is "interesting" but it misses the whole point...
…[501 more quoted lines elided]…
>                 http://www.usenet.com
```

Bill <wfsent@optonline.net> wrote in message
news:3C8AE91E.4309E267@optonline.net...
> Hi Peter,
> I much prefer browsing data files that store numbers in comp-3 format.
> You can easily see what the 'numbers' are. , debugging datafiles that
> are s9(18) comp is a real pain.

You can see them even better if the numbers are display... <G>

>
> >>(how many COMMERCIAL calculations involve numbers of more than 18 (or
even 8) digits?)
>
> Most 'real' world applications already use more than 8 digits. My 401k
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Ha! I was taking bets with myself that someone would mention "lira" before
this thread is finished....<G>

> Several of the applications I've worked on in the past used well over
> 18 digits (stock trades, bank balances).

Yes, I have also worked on applications that did this. But they are not the
NORM. Analyse the numeric pictures in say, 100, of the COBOL programs on
your site and see how many exceed 18 digits, how many exceed 8, do a
statistical distribution of the picture sizes in ALL numeric formats. I
believe you will be surprised...

> When people are trading millions of anything the 'fractions' tend to add
> up to something worth keeping track of.
>

Yes, and you don't need packed decimal to do it.

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Packed decimal is good format for quality control.

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca
- **Date:** 2002-03-10T05:40:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-tUqiPZoqzL63@thishost>`
- **References:** `<a6asnn$fa9$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <3c8944f4_7@Usenet.com> <3C896D2E.A0C4F032@sprynet.com> <3c8a1532_6@Usenet.com> <3c8a3683@news.alphalink.com.au> <3c8ab24d_5@Usenet.com> <a6egqo$afv$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3c8adf56_6@Usenet.com>`

```
On Sun, 10 Mar 2002 04:18:05 UTC, "Peter E. C. Dashwood" 
<dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

> Thanks Bill, this is "interesting" but it misses the whole point...
> 
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

If you deem the sharing of data between platforms as "essential" then 
you should campaign for a "standard scaled decimal number format" that
is available for all platforms and is supported by the instruction set
of the underlying hardware. This would be far more useful than 
anything else.

> I therefore submit that this is a stumbling block to the development of open
> systems, it provides little gain even on the architecture for which it was
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

I have written several applications that require the use of more than 
8 digits. Just about any reasonable financial or payroll package will 
require the use of more than 8 digits (assuming you are including the 
digits to the left and right of the decimal point.

Not that the number of digits has anything to do with packed decimal, 
as long as the compiler supports the number of decimal digits required
by the standard, I do not really care how they are stored, but if the 
hardware supports decimal arithmetic, I would think that whatever 
format is necessary to use the hardware support should be used.

> For the above reasons, I suggest that IBM Mainframe programmers should avoid
> using it, and new programmers should be advised to do likewise."
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

I would rather the hardware manufacturers started supporting a 
standard "scaled decimal" number format that would eliminate the 
ridiculous problems that appear when applications use floating point 
and the digits to the right of the decimal point start disappearing 
..

> I realise this position will not be popular (especially at IBM <G>) but I
> hold it nonetheless...
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Pete.

Unfortunately all those terabyte databases on DB2 using the decimal 
data type will take some time to convert to whatever your favorite 
number format is.

<snipped the rest of the quoted post>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Packed decimal is good format for quality control.

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-03-11T00:58:18+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c8beedb_7@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<a6asnn$fa9$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <3c8944f4_7@Usenet.com> <3C896D2E.A0C4F032@sprynet.com> <3c8a1532_6@Usenet.com> <3c8a3683@news.alphalink.com.au> <3c8ab24d_5@Usenet.com> <a6egqo$afv$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3c8adf56_6@Usenet.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-tUqiPZoqzL63@thishost>`

```

<lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca> wrote in message
news:ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-tUqiPZoqzL63@thishost...
>
> If you deem the sharing of data between platforms as "essential" then
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Yes, that WOULD be nice... In the meantime we can achieve this effect by
using 8 bit bytes - DISPLAY format for COBOL programmers. The immediate
arguments against this are based on the inefficiency of it and many years of
brainwashing by working with packed...<G>. In case you haven't looked
recently, today's processors are hundreds of times more powerful than
yesterday's, and the manipulation of data is many times more onerous on them
than converting a few numbers to an efficient internal format.

> > I therefore submit that this is a stumbling block to the development of
open
> > systems, it provides little gain even on the architecture for which it
was
> > originally intended (how many COMMERCIAL calculations involve numbers of
> > more than 18 (or even 8) digits?), and it is of arguable use in
controlling
> > the "quality" of data being processed on these platforms.
> >
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
You don't need packed decimal to do this.

> Not that the number of digits has anything to do with packed decimal,
> as long as the compiler supports the number of decimal digits required
> by the standard, I do not really care how they are stored, but if the
> hardware supports decimal arithmetic, I would think that whatever
> format is necessary to use the hardware support should be used.

So why not use binary or display? Because the hardware supports decimal
arithmetic? It supports binary also. (But that's hard to read in
dumps...<G>) The COBOL compiler will generate code that is also very capable
of converting display format to whatever the hardware prefers, if you do
arithmetic on two display fields...

You don't NEED packed decimal any more. It's use is purely a habit
encouraged by IBM to keep people on their mainframes...<G> Believe me, if
you moved your job to hardware that didn't have packed arithmetic, within 3
months you'd be wondering what all the fuss was about. (I know; in my
reckless youth I made a point of moving around different hardware to get
immersed in different Corporate "philosophies" (I intended to become a
consultant right from the start and wanted as broad and diverse a background
as I could get) and some of the major platforms I worked on didn't have
packed decimal. (I'll whisper this Heresy here... but, "IBM don't have the
monoply on good ideas...Other companies achieve excellent results in a
completely different way". Hard to believe, I know, when you work on an IBM
site <G>, but true, nevertheless...)


>
> > For the above reasons, I suggest that IBM Mainframe programmers should
avoid
> > using it, and new programmers should be advised to do likewise."
> >
> > There are millions of lines of COBOL written on platforms that DON'T
support
> > PACKED Decimal and those applications manage fine without it. Many of
the
> > old BUNCH  (Burroughs, Univac, NCR, CDC, and Honeywell, also include ICL
> > (now Fujitsu) in there... ) systems had their COBOL converted to other
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> ..

Well, Lorne, I'm very sympathetic to this and I agree that floating point
can be problematic. It's interesting that CURRENCY formats in ACCESS are
comp-2 (long floating point) but the convention is that they have 4 decimal
places (equivalent to s9(14)v9(4) in COBOL). This is so that when they are
rounded to 2 places, they give the correct result. My databases carry large
currency amounts without losing any and without using packed
decimal...(Unfortunately most of the amounts being tracked on them don't
belong to me...<G>)
>
> > I realise this position will not be popular (especially at IBM <G>) but
I
> > hold it nonetheless...
> >
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Now, don't be too smug...<G>.

The DECIMAL type is available with most RDBs and there is standard software
to unload DB2 and export it. I have spent the last couple of years building
ISAM/VSAM and RDB conversion tools and I don't see a problem here...

Neither do I have a "favourite" data type. I just reckon that packed is not
helping, and has outlived its usefulness.

(I am realistic enough to realise that Satan will be wearing an anorak
before people abandon Packed; but why should the reality stand in the way of
a stimulating discussion <G>? Besides, I DO tell COBOL students to "avoid"
using packed).

If reading this thread causes ONE IBM Mainframer to question what they do,
or think abut it (even if it is to think about why they disagree with what
I'm saying), then I'll consider my time well spent.

Pete.

                http://www.usenet.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Packed decimal is good format for quality control.

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2002-03-11T04:55:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020310235517.15360.00000528@mb-cj.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3c8beedb_7@Usenet.com>`

```
>format for quality control.
>From: "Peter E. C. Dashwood" dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz 
>Date: 3/10/02 6:58 AM Eastern Standard Time

>If reading this thread causes ONE IBM Mainframer to question what they do,
>or think abut it (even if it is to think about why they disagree with what
>I'm saying), then I'll consider my time well spent.
>

Way ahead of you Pete :)  We've been converting packed fields to display for
years as we 'improve' our various files etc.  Our only problem are our DB2
databases, when we ask for one to be created and tell the DBA we need a numeric
field the declgen gives it as COMP-3.  

I have one file that is really going to give me a headache as it will have to
be converted soon as we're expanding a field in it and the dang thing has a
really ugly layout.  I'd like to know what drugs the original designer was on
when he packed each sub field of the date MM PIC S99 COMP-3 etc. 

As for S0C7s - you can get them with DISPLAY as easily as COMP-3.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Packed decimal is good format for quality control.

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-03-12T01:53:44+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c8cb167_8@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<3c8beedb_7@Usenet.com> <20020310235517.15360.00000528@mb-cj.aol.com>`

```

YukonMama <yukonmama@aol.com> wrote in message
news:20020310235517.15360.00000528@mb-cj.aol.com...
>
> Way ahead of you Pete :)  We've been converting packed fields to display
for
> years as we 'improve' our various files etc.  Our only problem are our DB2
> databases, when we ask for one to be created and tell the DBA we need a
numeric
> field the declgen gives it as COMP-3.
>
Remember that the DECLGEN only creates an interface. Data on the RDB itself
may be stored completely differently (in the case of DB2 though, it probably
IS actually stored as COMP-3.) The fact that it is stored as COMP-3 doesn't
really matter if your Host Variables use DISPLAY. Conversion will occur as
soon as you say INTO...

Glad to see there is at least one installation where they are thinking about
the future... :-)


> I have one file that is really going to give me a headache as it will have
to
> be converted soon as we're expanding a field in it and the dang thing has
a
> really ugly layout.  I'd like to know what drugs the original designer was
on
> when he packed each sub field of the date MM PIC S99 COMP-3 etc.
>
I bet, if you could ask him he'd say "it saves space... 3 bytes instead of
6..." <G>

> As for S0C7s - you can get them with DISPLAY as easily as COMP-3.
>
Yikes!!!  Of course!!  I forgot that the underlying conversion from display
will generate code to PACK so it can use the decimal instruction set... It's
a long time since I worked programming IBM mainframes but I should've
remembered that. Thanks for the timely reminder...

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Packed decimal is good format for quality control.

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "398199821" <warren.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2002-03-10T18:19:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9tNi8.1293$tP2.144978@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<a6asnn$fa9$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <3c8944f4_7@Usenet.com> <3C896D2E.A0C4F032@sprynet.com> <3c8a1532_6@Usenet.com> <3c8a3683@news.alphalink.com.au> <3c8ab24d_5@Usenet.com> <a6egqo$afv$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3c8adf56_6@Usenet.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-tUqiPZoqzL63@thishost> <3C8B50BE.1C70EB40@optonline.net>`

```

Bill points out that how something is done is up to the implementor. This
argument was a staple in the early days of
COBOL specification (IMO) because vendor reps did not want to "leak"
techniques of hardware logic that favored
the reps acceptance of an approach.  Segmentation was such a feature that
stood out among vendor abilities to implement.
There was a time when using this argument seemed to occupy most of the
discussions. Yet, approval of vendor user/efforts was based upon a mix of
needs vs. haves.  The whole process seems to have grown. Anyone can create a
standard, but
forcing it's adoption is a USER responsibility.

Warren Simmons

subscripts were used when populating the table. With no other changes, the
application ran 5 times as long as before.

&2DP for amounts became the standard.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Packed decimal is good format for quality control.

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-03-11T13:23:16+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<a6asnn$fa9$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <3c8944f4_7@Usenet.com> <3C896D2E.A0C4F032@sprynet.com> <3c8a1532_6@Usenet.com> <3c8a3683@news.alphalink.com.au> <3c8ab24d_5@Usenet.com> <a6egqo$afv$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3c8adf56_6@Usenet.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-tUqiPZoqzL63@thishost> <eoKi8.11585$5S6.496077@news2.west.cox.net>`

```

Rufio <davecawdell@cox.net> wrote in message
news:eoKi8.11585$5S6.496077@news2.west.cox.net...
>
> Don't forget subscripts - I once inadvertantly change the subscripts used
to
> initialize a 3X36X33 table from COMP-3 to display fields. Other COMP-3
> subscripts were used when populating the table. With no other changes, the
> application ran 5 times as long as before.
>
Now that's interesting, Dave.

I had a similar experience, but it was on a 360-30 in 1970. From then on I
always coded subscripts as binary (until I wised up and switched to
indexes...<G>).

If I could run the same program on today's platforms I wonder if I would
notice any significant difference?

I'm assuming that the experience you quote is much more recent...how long
ago was it and on what platform? And it would be useful to know how long
"long" is too...

See, if it took 5 seconds with packed subscripts and then took 25 without
them is that such a big deal? If it took 5 hours with packed subscripts and
then took 25 hours without them, then that is definitely a big deal...you
see what I'm saying?

If you had changed the subscripts to binary (as IBM recommend) maybe it
would have been -5 times as long...we can't know.

If you ran that same program on a modern processor (either Pentium 1 gig or
system 390) maybe it would take the same time with unpacked subscripts that
it took previously with packed ones...

The major part of the workload in commercial processing is in DATA
MANIPULATION. If you really want to cut run times look at optimizing that...
a few arithmetic conversions make little difference in the overall scheme of
things.

(Arithmetic conversions are CPU intensive. IO operations are not. From the
CPU's point of view, Ice Ages come and go while it waits for data.
Optimizing IO is a bit like "Global Warming"; it makes the Ice Age shorter
and the periods between them shorter too... A little activity with number
conversion  slips easily into the "shadow" of the huge waits for IO. This
whimsical and over-simplified model changes when we start multithreading,
but it serves the purpose to illustrate my point...<G>)

Summary:

1. When lengths of time are in Nanoseconds, or even Milliseconds,  they are
below Human normal perception. A process that takes 50 Nanoseconds can take
10,000 times as long and still be less than the average Human reaction time.

2. What happened 30 or even 20 years ago is no basis for programming today.
The whole business of using Packed needs re-appraisal.

> > >
> >
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > I therefore submit that this is a stumbling block to the development
of
> open
> > > systems, it provides little gain even on the architecture for which it
> was
> > > originally intended (how many COMMERCIAL calculations involve numbers
of
> > > more than 18 (or even 8) digits?), and it is of arguable use in
> controlling
> > > the "quality" of data being processed on these platforms.
>
> FYI - I worked at Amex for 8 years. When we'd finished rewriting their
North
> American merchant marketing processing last year, 13 digits for counts &
15
> &2DP for amounts became the standard.
>
OK. They could still be binary or even display. IBM stated that only when
the numbers EXCEED 18 digits is packed a better option. If you used DISPLAY,
the compiler would decide what was best. New hardware comes out, recompile.
(And the dumps are MUCH easier to read...and there will be fewer of
them...<G>)

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Packed decimal is good format for quality control.

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-03-10T13:57:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<QCJi8.6376$KK5.1523@nwrddc01.gnilink.net>`
- **References:** `<a6asnn$fa9$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <3c8944f4_7@Usenet.com> <3C896D2E.A0C4F032@sprynet.com> <3c8a1532_6@Usenet.com> <3c8a3683@news.alphalink.com.au> <3c8ab24d_5@Usenet.com> <a6egqo$afv$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3c8adf56_6@Usenet.com>`

```
Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3c8adf56_6@Usenet.com...
> ...
> The issue is whether PACKED decimal has a place in the modern World.
> .... I'm arguing that it doesn't...I summarise my position as follows:
>.... At the time there was little requirement to transfer data across
platforms.
> Today... the sharing of data between platforms is becoming ESSENTIAL.

I am essential agnostic on the main premise, but just a note about data
sharing.... if the sharing is between IBM mainframe and Wintel boxes, and
part of that sharing is language interoperability, then the "endianess"
difference between IBM mainframe binary and Wintel binary becomes a factor,
too.

If middleware is used to convert between big-endian binary and little-endian
binary, that middleware may as well convert between mainframe packed-decimal
and Wintel binary; or, the "standard" binary format for COBOL may as well be
COMP-4 or COMP-5 to allow the language products to share numeric the data
without middleware.

Don't know about you, but most of my mainframe client personnel don't have a
clue about the use of COMP-4 or COMP-5 and have opted over the years only
for COMP-3 (packed-decimal) or COMP (Binary). (I am not even digging into
the character set conversion issues).

The solution seems to be as someone else suggested: a 'local standard'  for
'shareable' data should include the requirement that numeric data be
expressed in DISPLAY format. From a practical perspective, though, I'd have
to guess there are more than a few hundred lines of code in production which
don't use a DISPLAY standard....

MCM
```

While you are busy getting rid of BCD, how about the same for those dumb
little one bit sign conventions.  "Sign Separate"  would probably get rid of
99% of all conversion problems that getting rid of BCD misses.

My standard for years has been display, sign leading separate.  But then I
have been uploading from PC to mainframe now since CPM days.  All my systems
have been mixed mainframe/pc for years and years.

Donald.

the old hardware design is useless in this case.

"light" that appears (to me), is the International

head of the world we live in, we would not
need so many different "standards."

Warren Simmons

"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:
> 
> Frank Swarbrick <infocat@sprynet.com> wrote in message
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> So you care about efficiency? On a 360-30 in 1967 it definitely mattered.

Well, I wasn't even born in 1967, but somewhere along the line I seem to
have learned that efficiency is at least somewhat important...
 
> As a 386 Intel processor (remember them? <G>) had about 1.7 times the power
> of  Gene Amdahl's fabulous 60s machine, and today's pentium is over 500
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
> communication.

I work for a bank so I would think that we do a lot of calculations and
that most of them are decimal, not floating point.  (I've never once
seen a floating-point field in any of our programs.)

> (This is, obviously, on an
> > IBM mainframe.)  The only time I would use Display for a numeric field
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> <G>.

Personally, I'd rather get a data exception (S0c7) than a bad
calculation!  At least with a data exception you know something has gone
wrong and you can fix it.

> I would agree that keys in binary are troublesome, but they are in packed
> format too... It comes back to the power of modern hardware; make your key
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> "likes"....Ah, but it's harder to read in dumps...I forgot...<G>)

Umm, I'm guessing you don't know CICS?  I use binary because it is
required.  If you were to use Display or packed-decimal where binary was
required you'd be in a world of hurt!
 
> > I wish my compiler (COBOL II under VSE, soon converting to COBOL for
> > VSE/ESA) had a no-sign packed-decimal field (vs. an unsigned
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> then treating it as a signed packed field.

That's exactly what I'm saying.  Instead of, say,
X"F0F0F0F0F0F1F0F0F0F0" (which is what MasterCard uses), Visa uses
X"0000010000".  I'm not going to try to justify it.  I'm just telling
you what the facts are.

As for doing a logical AND, unfortunately that is not available in any
IBM version of COBOL for our operating system (VSE/ESA).  I don't recall
what logic is used, as I didn't write it (and it's a vendor product in
any case).  Obviously it's not biggy since the code exists and works.  I
just thought it would be nice to have a 'natural' field to use instead
of some confusing conversion.
 
> (Easy in Assembler, more tedious in COBOL. Here's the COBOL code I would
> use...:
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> digits <G>...

Hmm, not sure that looks much like our code.  Of course our code (the
vendor's code, that is) must be able to handle fields of different
lengths, so that may require more work.
 
> Frank, I really didn't mean to be hard on you and despite the frivolous tone
> of my response, I do honestly believe that Packed Decimal is something we'd
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 5, but 10 bytes?...

Well, as I said, it's not a choice we have.  Visa has defined how the
data is to look and there's not much we can do about it.

In any case it may make you happy to know that the intermediate
working-storage field used to store the messages to/from Visa are USAGE
DISPLAY.  Of course I believe they're later moved to packed-decimal, but
you can't have everything.  :-)

> Most commercial applications are not "compute" intensive. Manipulating data
> requires many more CPU cycles (irrespective of how fast and plentiful they
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> goes by, I find this harder to refute <G>)

Well, we've always found it to be useful, and I doubt you'd find a
single person at our shop looking to get away from it.  In fact, I'd
probably be a more likely person than most anyone else there.
```

Frank Swarbrick <infocat@sprynet.com> wrote in message
news:3C8C50F5.7A1A2D4B@sprynet.com...
>
> Well, I wasn't even born in 1967, but somewhere along the line I seem to
> have learned that efficiency is at least somewhat important...
>
Absolutely! But there is efficiency and efficiency... Part of what I'm
trying to achieve with this post is  to get people to re-examine some of the
"Everybody knows..." on efficiency.

It doesn't make sense to me to spend time worrying about the inherent
efficieny of doing arithmetic conversion, if said conversion represents less
than 5% of the load on the processor... By focussing on the other 95%, gains
are much more likely to be made.

> > As a 386 Intel processor (remember them? <G>) had about 1.7 times the
power
> > of  Gene Amdahl's fabulous 60s machine, and today's pentium is over 500
> > times more powerful still, and your 390 Mainframe eats the Pentium
(well,
> > that one's arguable, (if we are talking > 1GIG chips) but you catch my
> > drift), how much arithmetic are you actually doing? The requirement to
> > convert is a negligible overhead, compared to the requirement to move
say,
> > 4K of data around in memory. I would take bets that your programs do a
LOT
> > more data movement than they do arithmetic...
> >
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I too have worked in numerous Banks and you are quite correct. The only time
I have seen floating point used in COBOL programs was many years ago when I
wrote some programs that used it so I could find out what it did...<G>

I wasn't advocating that we should move to FP for decimal processing
(although it works fine with ACCESS and SQL Server databases which store
currencies as COMP-2. (Well, they don't really...it just looks that way to a
COBOL program. It is true that they store amounts as 64 bit floating point
fields but the decimal is not really floating...)).
>
> Personally, I'd rather get a data exception (S0c7) than a bad
> calculation!  At least with a data exception you know something has gone
> wrong and you can fix it.
>
As I mentioned elsewhere, there is no substitute for proper Quality Control.
If you validate data fields correctly, and test programs thoroughly, there
is no need for them to require hardware exception trapping...

>
> Umm, I'm guessing you don't know CICS?  I use binary because it is
> required.  If you were to use Display or packed-decimal where binary was
> required you'd be in a world of hurt!
>
I was writing CICS programs in the 70s, and in the 90s I worked at Hursley
Park where CICS was invented!

I still prefer IMS/DC and TASKMASTER as technically superior TP monitors,
but I have to admit CICS has shown more tenacity than any of the competition
<G>

I understand why you use binary fields in your CICS programs.

>
> That's exactly what I'm saying.  Instead of, say,
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> IBM version of COBOL for our operating system (VSE/ESA).

No, I realised it was unfair to use an Assembler solution so I posted the
same effect in COBOL...
(Mind you,  I made 2 mistakes in it (that I know about...<G>) 1: I divided
by 100 instead of 10, 2: I said AND when it should've been OR... )
>
> > (Easy in Assembler, more tedious in COBOL. Here's the COBOL code I would
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> > 01   packed-value   redefines packed-decimal-factory pic s9(11)
COMP-3.
> > 01   packed-amt     redefines packed-value                 pic s9(9)v99
> > COMP-3.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> >
I had the division wrong and this was pointed out to me. I have corrected it
to be 10 instead of the 100 I had originally. The AND/OR mistake I noticed
when I was writing this...(Hangs head in shame and blushes...)

> Hmm, not sure that looks much like our code.  Of course our code (the
> vendor's code, that is) must be able to handle fields of different
> lengths, so that may require more work.
>
If the incoming BCD digits numbered more than 16 (instead of the current 10)
then the code shown would need to be modified to something quite different.
Up to 16 digits can be catered for easily by providing it with the number of
incoming bytes and using reference modification to load pdf-in. Like this...

 ...
 working-storage section.
01  n-bytes-from-visa pic x(8). *> Currently, COBOL supports a maximum of 18
digits
                                                *> and this algorithm 2 less
than that...8 bytes gives 16 BCD digits.

01  bytes-in                       pic s9(4) comp.
01  packed-decimal-factory.
      12 pdf-in                     pic x(8) value low-values.
      12 pdf-ORx0F         pic x value x'0F'.
01   packed-value   redefines packed-decimal-factory pic s9(17)
COMP-3.
01   packed-amt     redefines packed-value                 pic s9(15)v99
COMP-3
...
procedure division.
...
get-visa-amount.
      *>  move the input to the factory...
       move n-bytes-from-visa (1:bytes-in) to pdf-in (9 - bytes-in:)
      *>  input is assumed to be left justified...easily changed if it
isn't...
       compute packed-value = packed-value / 10
      *>  factory leaves a "normal" packed amount with correct decimal
      *>  alignment in "packed-amt"...
      *>  If you wish to re-use the factory you must reset the x'0F' in
      *>  pdf-ORx0F and zeroise pdf-in...
        move packed-amt to.............(whatever)

You can see that modifications are minimal...


> >
> > For example, would it really be so terrible if your VISA input field
> > occupied 10 bytes instead of 5? It has to be converted to something
usable
> > anyway... I agree there might be a problem if I said make it 2K instead
of
> > 5, but 10 bytes?...
>
> Well, as I said, it's not a choice we have.  Visa has defined how the
> data is to look and there's not much we can do about it.
>
If a number of their customers raised the fact that display fields would be
easier all round, who knows?

> Well, we've always found it to be useful, and I doubt you'd find a
> single person at our shop looking to get away from it.  In fact, I'd
> probably be a more likely person than most anyone else there.
>
Copy them on this mail. If nothing else they may think again. A very good
case can be made for avoiding it, or at the very least keeping it well away
from files...

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Packed decimals WAS "FAQ "accessed counter" (and other changes)"

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2002-03-12T08:14:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C8DB8A8.FD15FF1@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<a6asnn$fa9$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <3c8944f4_7@Usenet.com> <3C896D2E.A0C4F032@sprynet.com> <3c8a1532_6@Usenet.com> <3C8C50F5.7A1A2D4B@sprynet.com> <3c8cbd5b_6@Usenet.com>`

```
"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:
> 
> Frank Swarbrick <infocat@sprynet.com> wrote in message
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> easier all round, who knows?

You're talking about Visa's implementation of the ISO-8583 standard
for financial messages.  They have literally tens of thousands of
customers around the world exchanging messages in realtime using this
format.  How could you possibly change the realtime message format and
get all the customers around the world to convert at the same
millisecond?

During the Y2K run-up there was serious discussion about changing the
ISO-8583 standard to convert existing dates from 2-digit years to
4-digit years, and also to change expiration date formats on magnetic
stripes from 2-digit years to 4-digit years.  There was a debate in
comp.software.year-2000 about changing ACH (Automated Clearing House)
message formats for 4-digit years.  It never happened.
```

- **References:** `<a6asnn$fa9$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <3c8944f4_7@Usenet.com> <3C896D2E.A0C4F032@sprynet.com> <3c8a1532_6@Usenet.com> <3C8C50F5.7A1A2D4B@sprynet.com> <3c8cbd5b_6@Usenet.com> <3C8DB8A8.FD15FF1@worldnet.att.net>`

Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net> wrote in message
news:3C8DB8A8.FD15FF1@worldnet.att.net...
>
> You're talking about Visa's implementation of the ISO-8583 standard
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Answering your question, I can see several ways to do this...<G>

Probably the best one would be to provide them with smart bridging software
(a .DLL or module) that detects the format of the message and handles it in
either the "old" or the "new" format transparently. A similar module would
ensure that new messages were sent in only the "new" format... Within a
month or two you could throw away the bridge...(if it was adding a
significant overhead; of course you would balance all the priorities, right
<G>?)

The point is that it CAN be done, it is just a question of having the will
to do it. (Like most things in IT, actually...<G>)

> During the Y2K run-up there was serious discussion about changing the
> ISO-8583 standard to convert existing dates from 2-digit years to
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I'm sorry to hear that. It sounds as though inertia won over good sense.
This is, sadly, too often the case and we end up "stuck" with poor solutions
because they "kind of work" and we're "too busy" to do anything about it,
and it would cost money, and ...yada yada yada....

Only when the current solution approaches criticality and prevents the
implementation of some new, highly desirable, functionality, is anything
actually done about it. It's kind of like managing on a "cope" basis...<G>.
You can get by with it, but in the long run it is more restrictive and
costly than doing some forward planning and attempting to build flexibility
into your current solutions.

I see the role of an IT Professional as being prepared to use skill,
knowledge, and experience to challenge the Status Quo and improve it. Nobody
worth their salt should simply accept a bad solution, until everything
within their power has been done to change it. (At the very least, you get
to say "I told you so..." <G>).

Pete.

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** stephenjspiro@mail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2002-03-12T16:33:11-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<928495c6.0203121633.69dca80@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<a6asnn$fa9$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <3c8944f4_7@Usenet.com> <3C896D2E.A0C4F032@sprynet.com> <3c8a1532_6@Usenet.com> <3C8C50F5.7A1A2D4B@sprynet.com> <3c8cbd5b_6@Usenet.com>`

```
"Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message news:<3c8cbd5b_6@Usenet.com>...

> 
> I still prefer IMS/DC and TASKMASTER as technically superior TP monitors,
> but I have to admit CICS has shown more tenacity than any of the competition
> <G>
> 
I did a Taskmaster to Command Level CICS conversion about 15 years
ago.  The original vendor no longer supported Taskmaster, and we had
the source code.  I was surprised to find that the source was
Assembler with Macro Level CICS! Apparently, the Taskmaster people had
decided that CICS was more efficient than whatever native stuff they
had been using.  It is very possible that bringing up Taskmaster
actually brought up a predefined CICS region....  It was
extraordinarily stable; we were converting only because of an inherent
Taskmaster limit on the number of terminals in the system.

Stephen J Spiro
```

Wow!

You have completely ghasted my flabber, Stephen..<G>

I used TASKMASTER back in the  late 70s/ early 80s, and was extremely
impressed with the simplicity, stability, and efficiency of it. I had no
idea it was based on CICS (which it competed with at that time...).

Do you suppose they might have converted the original product after there
was no longer support for it?

I figured it just went to the wall because they couldn't market it against
CICS...

(BTW, I am NOT suggesting that CICS is a poor product; just that there have
been better ones, even from IBM (I reckon IMS/DC is MUCH more efficient in
its conversational processing  (using transaction dequeueing) than CICS is
in its pseudo conversational processing (enqueueing on a given terminal). I
liked TASKMASTER better than both of them so I am genuinely surprised at
your revelation.)

Anyway, whatever happened to SHADOW <G>?

Pete.

This discussion reminds me that a few decades ago we had such a discussion.
I believe it was the 360. I wanted to use
the packed format, and someone else wanted to use display for just the
reasons currently being supported. My position (at that time) was based upon
support people saying that display had to be in packed format to print.  I
had no time for verifying that then, and today it seems likely if it were
true, at least the printer hardware has improved.

Also, it has been mentioned that other vendors have ways of doing things
that are better. One that I know of did not use
the binary addressing method. "K" ment 1000. I don't know if this vendor had
another use for the extra 24 in each conventional "K" but their purpose in
the design was to simplify. A great concept.

Warren Simmons

intended

extra
> > that Amdahl built to capture the commercial market where currencies
needed 2
> > decimals. Remember also, that memory was made by hand, disks had not
been
> > invented yet, and both internal and external storage were EXPENSIVE. (I
just
> > did some quick calculations, and today, for the same price as we paid
for
> > 32k of main storage in 1965 (around $US100,000 in today's money!) , you
> > could buy several TRILLION bytes!)   His famous ROS (Read Only Storage)
> > cards allowed the instruction set of the machine to be altered, so the
> > option for packed decimal was easily implemented. However, the control
logic
> > had to go stepping through ROS to execute each instruction and this
slowed
> > the system and made it lose in benchmarks. The solution was to move the
> > "decimal" (packed) instruction set to hardware and stop making it an
> > option...
> >
> > I stand by what I said: It is an archaic system of representing numbers
that
> > is neither use nor ornament in today's world. In its time, and for the
> > purposes for which it was originally conceived, it was useful. Now it
isn't.
> > It just puts another hurdle in the way of open systems and inter
platform
> > communication.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: FAQ "accessed counter" (and other changes)

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@invalid.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-09T23:09:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bDwi8.327232$Aw2.26088704@bin7.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<a6asnn$fa9$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <3c8944f4_7@Usenet.com> <3C896D2E.A0C4F032@sprynet.com>`

```

"Frank Swarbrick" <infocat@sprynet.com>
>
> Hi Peter,
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> IBM mainframe.)

Settle it for yourself. Write a program to time 100,000,000 computations in
packed, binary, and display. Note the difference. Then make a big note of
whether the difference matters. 10 seconds, twelve seconds, twenty seconds.
Who cares? I bet the choice for internal arithmetic ultimately resembles the
choice of variable names.

In our shop, we use a binary for internal stuff (subscripts, counters, etc.)
but all data moving into and out of the programs are display. You can't
imagine how many problems disappear following this rule (at least for us).

Too, there's the question of communicating with other languages (VB, C) or
other applications (Excel) where packed decimal is an enigma.
```
