```

JerryMouse <nospam@invalid.com> wrote in message
news:bDwi8.327232$Aw2.26088704@bin7.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com...
>
> "Frank Swarbrick" <infocat@sprynet.com>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Settle it for yourself. Write a program to time 100,000,000 computations
in
> packed, binary, and display. Note the difference. Then make a big note of
> whether the difference matters. 10 seconds, twelve seconds, twenty
seconds.
> Who cares? I bet the choice for internal arithmetic ultimately resembles
the
> choice of variable names.

Sound advice. There is no arguing with the empirical approach...<G>

>
> In our shop, we use a binary for internal stuff (subscripts, counters,
etc.)
> but all data moving into and out of the programs are display. You can't
> imagine how many problems disappear following this rule (at least for us).
>
I agree. This is the modern view, which is much less insular than "mainframe
only". We are entering an age of interconnectivity. Anachronistic formats
like "Packed" just slow the process of cross platform communication down...

I believe everyone should move towards the "local standard" you describe.

> Too, there's the question of communicating with other languages (VB, C) or
> other applications (Excel) where packed decimal is an enigma.
>
Less important, but still a consideration. The whole question of Language
InterOperability is slowly being overtaken by events...

Pete.



 Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services
----------------------------------------------------------
    ** SPEED ** RETENTION ** COMPLETION ** ANONYMITY **
----------------------------------------------------------        
                http://www.usenet.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: FAQ "accessed counter" (and other changes)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Frank Swarbrick <infocat@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-10T23:59:42-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C8C55DE.43284B6C@sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<a6asnn$fa9$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <3c8944f4_7@Usenet.com> <3C896D2E.A0C4F032@sprynet.com> <bDwi8.327232$Aw2.26088704@bin7.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
JerryMouse wrote:
> 
> "Frank Swarbrick" <infocat@sprynet.com>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> choice of variable names.

Perhaps I will try that!
 
> In our shop, we use a binary for internal stuff (subscripts, counters, etc.)
> but all data moving into and out of the programs are display. You can't
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> other applications (Excel) where packed decimal is an enigma.

Contrary to what may be inferred from my previous post, I'm not all that
against using DISPLAY instead of COMP-3.  But our shop standard is to
used signed packed-decimal, and I have enough battles to get more
important things changed.  Perhaps as our long-timers retire (which is
happening), but, well, keep going at it and maybe you'll convince me. 
:-)  

Oh, and as for C, unless I'm mistaken it doesn't even support DISPLAY
arithmatic, just binary or floating point, so...  I haven't used C in
over 10 years for anything other than little test networking programs on
Linux, but that's what I recall.  (I believe you'd have to use atoi() or
atol() to convert to binary and then use the results for your
calculations.)
```

```
***4 Mainframe Sr. Analyst/Programmer positions in MD & DC!

PLEASE NOTE THERE ARE 3 JOB DESCRIPTIONS BUT 4 JOBS BELOWï¿½

Position Title:  Sr. Analyst/Programmer Cobol, OS/390, Assembler    (2
openings)
Location:  New Carrollton, MD. 
http://maps.yahoo.com/py/maps.py?BFCat=&Pyt=Tmap&newFL=Use+Address+Below&addr=&csz=new+carrollton%2C+md&country=us&Get%A0Map=Get+Map

Full/Part: Full Time
Work Status: Must be U.S. citizen or permanent resident. Can not
sponsor Visas.
Referral Bonus:  Yes.  $500.00

independent contractors only.  Candidates electing to be an employee

POSITION DESCRIPTION:
Location: New Carrollton, MD
Full/Part: Full Time

acceptance test cases and procedures.  Develop test data
specifications.  Document testing results and track issues using MS
Access.  Develop reports using MS Access.  Interface heavily with

Requires experience in IBM mainframe environment, TSO, High-Level

written communication skills and experience in client interaction.
Requires experience in systems analysis and testing stages of the
systems development life cycle.  Requires capability of working in a
team environment.
8 years of experience and a Bachelors (or 12+ years if no degree). 
Major Preferred: Computer Science, Information Systems, or related
discipline
DESIRED:
Experience working with the IRS's tax processing systems.  Experience

Location:  Washington DC

Full/Part: Full Time

sponsor Visas.
Referral Bonus:  Yes.  $500.00
Over Time:  Straight Time for Approved Over Time hours for 1099
independent contractors only.  Candidates electing to be an employee
with benefits status are exempt from Over Time pay.
POSITION DESCRIPTION:	
Defines system architecture.  Identifies alternative solutions to task
problems.  Consults with customers attends planning meetings. 
Performs elaborate analyses and studies. Reviews and prepares the
documentation required for the software coding.  Uses knowledge of IRS
to ensure the system development and documentation will produce a
viable system.  Develops documentation required by IRS for IT systems.
 Produces coding based on the documentation.
REQUIREMENTS:
8 years of experience in the field of Information Technology,
including a minimum of four (4) years of hands on expertise in related
application development and a Bachelors (or 12+ years if no degree). 
Major Preferred: Computer Science, Information Systems, or related
discipline.   Hands on expertise in following areas is required: Must
have the skills available to design, develop, and implement
application programs.  Must be familiar with the IRS (Internal Revenue
Service) SDLC (Software Development Life Cycle). Must have excellent
communication and writing skills.  Must be knowledgeable in IBM ALC,
and C++; IBM 3090-600s mainframe running MVS/ESA 5.2.2 operating
system; Job Control Language (JCL); and test plans and test case
specifications.  Selection of candidate will be based on overall match
to position requirements. No relocation is available.

DESIRED:
Provides highly technical and specialized guidance and solutions to
complex Information Technology problems Performs elaborate analyses
and studies.  Prepares reports and gives presentations.  Works
independently or as a member of a team.  Very knowledgeable in all
aspects of Information Technology.  Has extensive experience in the
specific Information Technology discipline(s).



Position:  Programmer/Analyst Lead - ALC, Cobol, IBM 390/MVS
Location:  New Carrollton, MD
Salary: $65K to $75K + Benefits or $41 to $47/hr 1099.
Full/Part: Full Time
Work Status: Must be U.S. citizen or permanent resident. Can not
sponsor Visas.

Over Time:  Straight Time for Approved Over Time hours for 1099

POSITION DESCRIPTION:

(developers-programmers-analysts-testers) Provide guidance in the
following areas but not limited to: S/W development life cycle
processes, ensure reviews of ALC and COBOL programming code in a IBM
390/MVS environment, adhere to the company's coding and process
```

<a id="t-86ff19eef4"></a>
## Thread: "Tools" for COBOL FAQ — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Meta: FAQs, group policy, charter`](topics.md#meta)_

### "Tools" for COBOL FAQ

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-08T21:45:34-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a6c0h7$otu$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Dear CLC readers,

Among the notes in my (long) "to do" list was the following:

"Hello,
We've read your nice site.

At the moment we're working on few projects to convert cobol textual reports
to PDFs using our tools

txt2pdf

    http://www.sanface.com/txt2pdf.html

txt2pdf PRO

    htp://www.sanface.com/txt2pdfPRO.html

txt2pdf PRO + crypt

    http://www.sanface.com/txt2pdfPROcrypt.html

<snip>

Do you think your FAQ readers are interested to know of a similar
possibility?

We hope they're :)

<snip>"


  ***

I have just looked at their web-site and I don't see ANY references to
"COBOL".  On the other hand, I can (easily) imagine some COBOL
sites/programmers being interested in this type of "tool".

My question to CLC people is:

  Should I - or should I not - add "tools" that MIGHT be of interest, but
that don't actually "mention" COBOL in their own online documentation?  (I
have mixed feelings on this, but will "listen" to what others think)

Comments? Suggestions?
```

#### ↳ Re: "Tools" for COBOL FAQ

- **From:** Bernard Giroud <bgiroud@free.fr>
- **Date:** 2002-03-09T08:34:00+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C89BAE8.F393A149@free.fr>`
- **References:** `<a6c0h7$otu$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

"William M. Klein" a ï¿½crit :

> (...snip...)

> Do you think your FAQ readers are interested to know of a similar
> possibility?
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>

No, I think the file will be much too huge if every potentially interesting
tool is mentioned. Plus the fact that it will be harder to keep pace.

>
> --
> Bill Klein
>  wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

Bernard Giroud
```

#### ↳ Re: "Tools" for COBOL FAQ

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <frederico_fonseca@eudoramail.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-09T10:56:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<mtoj8u80krjcjtlf16gook2p79dnkar7h0@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<a6c0h7$otu$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
On Fri, 8 Mar 2002 21:45:34 -0600, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:
...
>
>I have just looked at their web-site and I don't see ANY references to
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>have mixed feelings on this, but will "listen" to what others think)
I think the following would be a good aproach on such decision.

1- If a product is one that is either written specifically to work
with COBOL, either by allowing an interaction with COBOL programs
(such as Flexus SP2) or with COBOL files (Liant's Relativity), or
supplies a set of "routines" that are called directly from COBOL using
normall "CALL" (I am ignoring OO for the moment) then I might include
it.

2- If it is only supplyed as a callable program (Perl on this case)
used as a "call "system" using command line" then I would not include
it.


I ignored OO, because in that case all applications that supply and
OCX/VBX/OLE aproach would be included. (and on this case it would be
better to include a link like
 "for more COBOL tools see www.tucows.com " !!!)


Just my 2 cent (Yes I also have them now)

Frederico Fonseca



Just as a minor note regarding the interest of PDF conversions there
is (at least) another company http://www.pdflib.com/ that supplies a
PDF tool that is included in option 1 above. (I have used it from
Liants'RM COBOL using normal call's to the supplied routines)
Example:
            call "PDF_translate"
                    USING P-PDF, temp1, temp1.
            call "PDF_setcolor"
                    USING P-PDF, "both", "rgb", 0, 0, 1, 0.
            call "PDF_save" USING P-PDF.
            call "PDF_setlinewidth" USING P-PDF, 2.
            perform varying temp1 from 0 by 6 until temp1 > 354
                call "PDF_rotate" USING P-PDF, 6
                call "PDF_moveto" USING P-PDF, radius, 0
                call "PDF_lineto" USING P-PDF, temp2, 0
                call "PDF_stroke" USING P-PDF
            end-perform.
```

```
>From: "William M. Klein" wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com 
>Date: 3/8/02 10:45 PM Eastern Standard Time

>
>  Should I - or should I not - add "tools" that MIGHT be of interest, but

I get the impression from the 'commercial' that in the case of a mainframe the
output would be written to a file instead of a sysout and then passed through
this tool to obtain a pdf file.  While not exactly using COBOL (seems any
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: "Tools" for COBOL FAQ

- **From:** sanface@sanface.com (SANFACE Software)
- **Date:** 2002-03-12T06:40:35-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<18712ccd.0203120640.195edd6b@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<a6c0h7$otu$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <20020310030649.04468.00000670@mb-ms.aol.com>`

```
This is SANFACE Software point of view :)
Our tools are not specific made for Cobol.
In the same way our tools are not specific made for OpenVMS (they work
on OpenVMS only using the perl code) but official VMS FAQ cointain a
link to our tools http://www.openvms.compaq.com/wizard/openvms_faq.html
.

A lot of tools and libraries can allow you to create PDFs.
Few can allow you to convert your old textual reports with a simple
pipe (our tools supports STDIN and STDOUT) into a pdf like
http://www.sanface.com/pdf/heraldbill.pdf
You've simply to design the background layer and put your data on it.

Obviously it's our point of view.

SANFACE Software team

P.S. We'll wait for your answer to post announces of new release of
our tools into cobol newsgroup :)
```

## Thread: Porting Macros on MicroFocus Cobol — 1 msgs

```
<clages@attglobal.net> wrote in message

> Hi folks,
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> C.Lages

C,

Fujitsu is one of the largest corporations in the world. Even if the company
did go bankrupt, it would still exist. If the company ceased to exist,
Fujitsu would sell its assets - and customer base - to another company
before closing the doors for good. The only problem you could have is if
Fujitsu VANISHES from the earth at exactly the same time as your need for an
activation code becomes critical (If Fujitsu disappears before your need for
an activation code, you could copy out your code).

Should that happen (Fujitsu disappears and you have an urgent need), come
back to this newsgroup (assuming it still exists) and ask for help. I'm sure
someone can fix you up.
```

## Thread: Tab delimited — 10 msgs

### Tab delimited

- **From:** "ronald leenheer" <r.leenheer@wanadoo.nl>
- **Date:** 2002-03-11T16:02:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Ky4j8.159$kH5.904@castor.casema.net>`

```
Anyone an idea to how to read a tab delimited file and fill 3 fields with
the data out off the record?

Situation 1:
          SELECT IMP ASSIGN TO RANDOM, IMP-ACNM
              ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL
              FILE STATUS IS IMP-STATUS.

       FD  IMP                             LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD.
       01  IMP-REC.
           03  IMP-INH                     PIC X(50).

      01  AH-TAB.
           03  TAB-AH                  PIC 9(5) COMP-1 VALUE 2338.
       01  AH-TAB-R REDEFINES AH-TAB.
           03  TABJE                   PIC X.
           03  FILLER                  PIC X.

        UNSTRING IMP-REC DELIMITED BY TABJE INTO  FIELD1  FIELD2 FIELD3.
 due to line sequential there is no tab anymore so just field1 is filled.

Situation 2:
         SELECT IMP ASSIGN TO RANDOM, IMP-ACNM
              ORGANIZATION IS BINARY SEQUENTIAL
              FILE STATUS IS IMP-STATUS.

       FD  IMP                             LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD.
       01  IMP-REC.
           03  IMP-INH                     PIC X(50).

      01  AH-TAB.
           03  TAB-AH                  PIC 9(5) COMP-1 VALUE 2338.
       01  AH-TAB-R REDEFINES AH-TAB.
           03  TABJE                   PIC X.
           03  FILLER                  PIC X.

        UNSTRING IMP-REC DELIMITED BY TABJE INTO  FIELD1  FIELD2 FIELD3
works for the first record but due to binary sequential i'm getting too much
data in imp-rec so my second record is corrupted
```

#### ↳ Re: Tab delimited

- **From:** "Tom Morrison" <t.morrison@liant.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-11T18:33:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<WL6j8.10664$vc1.2385628219@newssvr11.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<Ky4j8.159$kH5.904@castor.casema.net>`

```
[Ronald replied via private email that this was for RM/COBOL v 6.06.  I am
replying to comp.lang.cobol for the "historical record"]

Ronald:

To eliminate or modify tab stop processing on line sequential files on
RM/COBOL, use a configuration file.  This is described in Chapter 10 of the
User's Guide.

Your configuration file can be built with any text editor.  It should
contain the record:

RUN-SEQ-FILES TAB-STOPS=0

You direct the runtime to process a configuration file using the C option on
the runcobol command.  For example:

runcobol program.cob C=config.txt

On later versions of RM/COBOL, configuration files may be automatically
located by the runtime without the use of the C option.

Best regards,
Tom Morrison
Liant Software Corporation
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Tab delimited

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** merhottin@aol.com (Merhottin)
- **Date:** 2002-03-11T23:18:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020311181837.05656.00001032@mb-co.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<AC8j8.10689$%94.2397838475@newssvr11.news.prodigy.com>`

```
Its been awhile since I had to read a Tab delimited file, but I use to define
the tab field :

01  TAB-DELIM     PIC X  VALUE X'09'

(I think Tab hex is X'09')

Then:

Read IN-FILE INTO RECORD-HOLD.
UNSTRING RECORD-HOLD DELIMITED BY TAB-DELIM INTO
FIELD-1,
FIELD-2,
FIELD-3,
FIELD-4.

That was with a 16 bit Realia Compiler.  I wouldn't think the compiler would
make a difference, but I may be wrong.  Hope that helps.
```

```
Since Mr. Morrison is taking care of the problem, probably I shouldn't
be posting this, but what you are trying to do, I've done LOTS of
times.
The main diferences are:
- Normaly I would cicle thru the record eg.:

WS: 01           TAB-CHAR         PIC X VALUE H"09".
    01           S-CURSOR         PIC 9(3).

PROCEDURE:

...
    PARSE-RECORD.
           MOVE 1 TO CURSOR
           INITIALIZE veldje ING-REKNR-VAN-A ING-REKNR-NAAR-A9
           UNSTRING IMP-REC DELIMITED BY TAB-CHAR INTO veldje POINTER
S-CURSOR
           UNSTRING IMP-REC DELIMITED BY TAB-CHAR INTO ING-REKNR-VAN-A
POINTER S-CURSOR
           UNSTRING IMP-REC DELIMITED BY TAB-CHAR INTO
ING-REKNR-NAAR-A9 POINTER S-CURSOR
            .
... proceed with logic

I've done this with various RM/Cobol versions (5, 6 and 7) on Windows,
DOS, Linux, Unix System V, SCO Unix, HP/UX, (but not on Solaris).

regards,
Paulo Vieira
```

```
result.
```

<a id="t-2cb48e4fa8"></a>
## Thread: Convert Rpt Carriage control? — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration)_

### Convert Rpt Carriage control?

- **From:** fir@eskimo.com (Fir)
- **Date:** 2002-03-11T17:27:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a6ipet$q1t$1@eskinews.eskimo.com>`

```
Converting VS Cobol pgms to Cobol 390 on IBM Mainframe.

These old batch report programs fill the first character 
of report lines with a carriage control figure and do a
write after positioning.  I'm looking to convert those
carriage control characters to "write after page", 
"write after 1", etc.

Is there a translation table somewhere for that carriage control
character in the first position of a report line?

Thank you.
```

#### ↳ Re: Convert Rpt Carriage control?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-11T13:22:13-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a6j05h$61a$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<a6ipet$q1t$1@eskinews.eskimo.com>`

```
Check your ADV/NOADV compiler option (on both the OLD and new compiler).
This works (basically) the same in all IBM mainframe compilers that I know
of.  If you set your compiler options correctly, you shouldn't need any
conversion.

NOTE: If you actually have OS/VS COBOL programs with
   WRITE ... AFTER POSITIONING

rather than AFTER ADVANCING, then you will want to look at the Migration
Guide at:

 http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/igy3mg00/4.1.7?

for the section
 "WRITE AFTER POSITIONING statement"
```

#### ↳ Re: Convert Rpt Carriage control?

- **From:** "Ron" <NoSoy@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 2002-03-11T21:29:21-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a6jsmj$gun$1@suaar1ab.prod.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<a6ipet$q1t$1@eskinews.eskimo.com>`

```
What I usually do with these things is just leave the carriage control alone.
Disturb the works as little as possible. Be sure you have NOADV when you compile.
Be sure you have RECFM=FBA in the JCL even if it's on a SYSOUT. Drop the
"after positioning" and just say "write record-name". It works the same.

Be glad you don't have any "before positioning" statements. It gets really fun then.

Anyway advancing,  '1' is top of page, space is 1 line, '0' is 2 lines, '-' is 3 lines.
There's a suppress advancing too, like "after advancing 0 lines". I'm not a 100%
sure but I think it must be a "+' sign.

=====================================================


"Fir" <fir@eskimo.com> wrote in message news:a6ipet$q1t$1@eskinews.eskimo.com...
> Converting VS Cobol pgms to Cobol 390 on IBM Mainframe.
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thank you.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Convert Rpt Carriage control?

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2002-03-13T00:50:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c1ca29$0608e280$1634e641@chottel>`
- **References:** `<a6ipet$q1t$1@eskinews.eskimo.com> <a6jsmj$gun$1@suaar1ab.prod.compuserve.com>`

```
My "green" card lists the following for ANSI-Defined printer control
characters:

  Code    Action before printing record

  blank    Space 1 line
  zero     Space 2 lines 
  -          Space 3 lines
  +         Suppress space
  1         Skip to line 1 on new page

Ron <NoSoy@swbell.net> wrote in article
<a6jsmj$gun$1@suaar1ab.prod.compuserve.com>...
> What I usually do with these things is just leave the carriage control
alone.
> Disturb the works as little as possible. Be sure you have NOADV when you
compile.
> Be sure you have RECFM=FBA in the JCL even if it's on a SYSOUT. Drop the
> "after positioning" and just say "write record-name". It works the same.
> 
> Be glad you don't have any "before positioning" statements. It gets
really fun then.
> 
> Anyway advancing,  '1' is top of page, space is 1 line, '0' is 2 lines,
'-' is 3 lines.
> There's a suppress advancing too, like "after advancing 0 lines". I'm not
a 100%
> sure but I think it must be a "+' sign.
> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> "Fir" <fir@eskimo.com> wrote in message
news:a6ipet$q1t$1@eskinews.eskimo.com...
> > Converting VS Cobol pgms to Cobol 390 on IBM Mainframe.
> >
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Convert Rpt Carriage control?

- **From:** "ups" <upsi@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-13T03:12:53+11:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c8f8e81@news.alphalink.com.au>`
- **References:** `<a6ipet$q1t$1@eskinews.eskimo.com>`

```
Consider looking to see what channels have been defined in the FCB,
in case the program requires more than just channel "1".

If  the DCB shows "M" rather than "A" for the printer control (eg FBM rather
than FBA),
it may be using machine (ie printer) internal codes rather than ANSI control
codes.

I seem to remember having to look these up in a manual once,
but it was far too long ago for me to remember which one.

Just a thought, it probably doesn't apply - you would have to be very
unlucky to strike this.


"Fir" <fir@eskimo.com> wrote in message
news:a6ipet$q1t$1@eskinews.eskimo.com...
> Converting VS Cobol pgms to Cobol 390 on IBM Mainframe.
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thank you.
```

<a id="t-3e167c679a"></a>
## Thread: Packed-Decimal, VSAM, et all — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### Packed-Decimal, VSAM, et all

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-11T14:36:18-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a6j4gf$hjh$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Starting a new thread (and hopefully, I have read all the notes on the
existing one),  I wanted to talk (a little) about designs, trade-offs, and
working in and with IBM "architecture".

Rule 1 (IMHO - and I won't add this to all future "rules") - there are NO
rules that apply to every application, program, operating system, etc.  Any
"design decision" (or "shop/programming standard") *must* be subject to
modification based on specific application "needs".

Rule 2 - Among other things, what makes COBOL, COBOL - is the fact that it
is "highly" self-documenting.  Programmers who write code that is
intentionally (or even unintentionally) obscure, deserve what they get.
HOWEVER, what is "self-documenting" really DOES depend on who will be doing
maintenance on a program (short, medium, AND long-term).  If you are a "one
person shop" - then no matter how odd (to me) your "style is" - as long as
you are consistent, it PROBABLY will be self-documenting to you (even 5 or
10 years from now); just don't "die" or stop programming while you have
"active" users.  SIMILARLY, if your shop has 100's of programmers who ALL
use COBOL Report-Writer, then "any one" should be able to pick up a RW
program and maintain it - while if you are the only one in your shop who
knows/understands it, it will be a NIGHTMARE to anyone else who must do
"fixes" or maintenance. (Same for anything from complicated UNSTRINGs to OO
to 3rd party products)

Rule 3 - if run-time performance is your GOAL, then (almost?) everyone
agrees that the place to start, is the underlying programming logic.  As IBM
says - at:
  http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/igy3pg00/7.1?

"Examine the underlying algorithms for your program. For top performance, a
sound algorithm is essential."

Rule 4 - It is extremely rare (but not impossible) that "tricky tuning" of
some part of your program that isn't executed "frequently" will result in
real-world performance enhancements.  On the other hand, a "simple change"
(even moving a data description from DISPLAY to Packed-Decimal before
entering a loop) in a "tight loop" of a routine that is performed millions
of times, MAY out-weigh any (potential?) maintenance/portability problems.
Testing should ALWAYS include "system tests" as well as unit tests (or
examining "generated code") to see if "performance enhancements" are WORTH
the "trouble" (current and future).

Rule 5 - "portability" of both source code and output data (files,
databases, whatever) is a WONDERFUL thing to have.  9 time out of 10, it
will make some FUTURE port or maintenance of an existing program much, MUCH
easier.  On the other hand, 1 time out of 10, making something "portable"
will cause sufficient "degradation" of existing requirements, that it should
be "avoided like the plague".  It does NOT mater how fast/powerful existing
processors are, if you need to finish a "nightly batch run" in 3 hours and
your "enhancement" makes it take 3 1/2 hours, something has to GIVE.
Similarly, if you are supporting multi-thousands of simultaneous online
users, changing from 1.8 to 2.2 second response time, may loose you (and
your boss) a job!

Rule 6 - "Isolating" compiler-specific, OS-specific, or site-specific
programming "stuff" may well solve trade-offs of performance vs
maintainability vs portability.  Certainly, moving user-interaction (in an
online environment) and data base (flat, relational, whatever) AWAY from
"business logic" is a great idea.  Use of COBOL CALL statements, OO
facilities, even COPY statements, can do this for you.  Unfortunately
(again - on the other hand), you might find that "highly modularized" or OO
applications do NOT meet performance requirements; only systems testing in
YOUR environment will answer this question. (If you DO find a problem, you
might want to contact your "vendor of choice" to see if they will ENHANCE
their product's performance in such environments)

Rule 7 - If you have a  common subroutine (used by lots and LOTS of
applications) or a file (database, whatever) that is common to MANY, many
programs - all on your "single" platform *and* if that routine is relatively
"stable" (as such files and routines often are), then the chances are that
it SHOULD BE optimized for the environment where it runs/is used.

Rule 8 - If you have a file (database, whatever) that is often (even
primarily) used for "communicating" information from  platform to platform;
or environment to environment, then the chances are that it should be
optimized for PORTABILITY.

   ***

So, back to the "subject" of this post.

If you work in an IBM mainframe environment (or - it should be added an
OS/400 environment), use of:
   Packed-Decimal
   VSAM
   EBCDIC
   even CICS (or IMS) is many (most?) cases

should and CAN be assumed
  - that all/most programmers know
  - that all other same-platform "tools" (programming languages, etc) will
understand
  - that both the compiler and all supporting "things" will be OPTIMIZED for
run-time performance

It can even (probably) be assumed that IF you use these "proprietary"
features *and* need to interface occasionally with other environments where
they are NOT supported, that there will be PLENTY of tools for making the
"transition" easy - and probably even pretty efficient.

This does not mean (IMHO - again) that you should AUTOMATICALLY use these
OS-specific "things" when creating a new application or a new file, but it
does mean that you are SAFE in doing so - AS LONG AS your *primary* target
is an IBM mainframe. What's more, if you are REQUIRED to interface with
existing programs or data, the chances are that this is the BEST way to
work.

If you think that your "new" (or even modified) application will be
interfacing with non-IBM environments (or even IBM workstation ones), then
you really SHOULD look seriously at:

  - Relational databases (for almost all data - except "report files" and/or
"logs")
  - USAGE DISPLAY / SIGN IS SEPARATE numeric fields (for "external" - not
internal data)
  - ASCII (or better yet Unicode/10646) character data
  - *ALL* OS specific "code" in isolated modules (subprograms and/or
methods/objects)
  - Compiling programs (at least once) with ANSI/FIPS flagging turned on so
you are aware of what implementor extensions your code does use
```

#### ↳ Re: Packed-Decimal, VSAM, et all

- **From:** "Ron" <NoSoy@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 2002-03-11T22:05:26-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a6juq8$p1g$1@suaar1aa.prod.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<a6j4gf$hjh$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
> Rule 2 - Among other things, what makes COBOL, COBOL - is the fact that it
> is "highly" self-documenting.  Programmers who write code that is
> intentionally (or even unintentionally) obscure, deserve what they get.

Hear, hear. Clarity, not cleverness. Or should I say, not cleverness at the expense
of clarity. I really shot myself in the foot doing this once. Way back when VS COBOL II
first arrived and you could suddenly have more than 3 dimensions in a table, I had this
big report program to write. Having a lot of time to just screw around I loaded all the data,
headings, detail, totals, subtotals and what not, into a humongous 6D table and then unloaded
it all back to print with a bunch of "perform varying, after, after,...." statements. It was the
most confusing thing but worked pretty good until a couple of years later when it to be
changed. The changes just would not quite fit into the tables. One of the occurs had to change
for the new data which messed up the logic for the existing data, which messed up the performs,
which messed up the printing etc etc. After a few more years and several go-rounds of this,
I finally had to spend a weekend rewriting it all - extract program, jcl sort, print program with
standard control breaks like it should have been in the first place. Learned a valuable lesson -
don't be cute, it'll bite you in the butt later. Be clear, be straightforward, don't be
unnecessarily
obscure and if something must be a bit unusual, use that "*" in column 7 and say what in the
heck you're doing. You might be the one that has to fix it some day.
```

```
well I'll just treat that with the contempt it deserves....<G> If the Batch
window can't sustain whatever you are doing then you don't do it. Simple.
What you are saying here is that you ahave a requirement to do something
(like make the system portable) but doing it will degrade performance to the
point where it goes out the Batch window. I don't know about you, but I'd be
looking at the existing data manipulation to cut out some of the unnecessary
extracts, sorts and downstream feeds that have stayed with us because nobody
was game to change the JCL Procedures... A complete review of the overall
Batch processing sounds on the cards to me. If I STILL can't get my open
system data exchange after that, then it's time to look at a hardware
upgrade...we are too close to the edge of the envelope to continue
```

<a id="t-63f4a381b6"></a>
## Thread: Child Process — 3 this month / 4 total

### Child Process

- **From:** "Lars Buch-Jepsen" <lars_buch_jepsen@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-12T08:29:17+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c8e31a7$0$272$ba624c82@nntp02.dk.telia.net>`

```
Hi.
How do I make a child proces in COBOL ? My problem is that I want to use
concurrent programming in my program to avoid unresponsivenes during I/O. I
have sifted throu over 10 books, I have searched the internet and searched
the newsgroups to find an answer to my problem.

OBS This is not homework, but a hobby program of mine.

Lars Buch-Jepsen
Student of Computer Science
Denmark
```

#### ↳ Re: Child Process

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-03-13T10:59:33+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c8e895d_1@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<3c8e31a7$0$272$ba624c82@nntp02.dk.telia.net>`

```
Lars,

COBOL has no intrinsic capability to start a subtask.

You CAN run multi-threaded COBOL tasks if you have a runtime that supports
it (Fujitsu does...).

Obviously, it is an OS function to allow and control timeslicing between
subtasks or threads.

You don't say what environment you are running in, but as it is a "hobby"
program, I am assuming it is NOT running on a mainframe...<G>? (IBM
Mainframes DO support subtasking, but you need to initiate them from CICS or
from Assembler, not from COBOL)

If you are developing under Windows for instance, you CAN start different
COBOL threads as subtasks. You would need to use the Win32API or a
proprietary development tool targeted at that environment (I use Fujitsu
PowerCOBOL).

I know very little about LINUX or UNIX style OSes, but I would think they
have the same facilities.

The lack of such a facility in COBOL has never been a big deal. COBOL is
designed to run across platforms and environments and the approach has been
that COBOL will call the facilities in the Environment if it needs
environment-specific functions (like multi-threading). Part of the problem
has also been that COBOL has never had the facility to save and restore
registers so that true re-entrancy of shared code can be achieved. (Some
very ingenious schemes for "simulating" this in COBOL have been around for
many years and are implemented in CICS and IMS/DC, but the code is said to
be "serially reusable" rather than truly re-entrant.)

If you use Object Oriented COBOL (and I strongly recommend you do...<G>)
then you can instantiate as many instances of a process (or sub-process) as
you like and they can be processed concurrently if the OS and/or Run Time
System supports it.

Hope this helps,

Pete.

Lars Buch-Jepsen <lars_buch_jepsen@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:3c8e31a7$0$272$ba624c82@nntp02.dk.telia.net...
> Hi.

> concurrent programming in my program to avoid unresponsivenes during I/O.
I
> have sifted throu over 10 books, I have searched the internet and searched
> the newsgroups to find an answer to my problem.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
Bill Klein (very kindly to save me embarrassment) responded privately that
IBM now has a THREAD facility in their "IBM Enterprise COBOL for z/OS and
OS/390" so it look as though IBM at least are implementing a native subtask
capability in COBOL.

Pete.
```

<a id="t-3395142b88"></a>
## Thread: Converting Macros from HP-Cobol II under MPE/iX to MF Cobol under HP-UX 11i — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration)_

### Converting Macros from HP-Cobol II under MPE/iX to MF Cobol under HP-UX 11i

- **From:** raghunandans@hotmail.com (Raghunandan S)
- **Date:** 2002-03-12T01:28:13-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d2a417af.0203120128.45280d24@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi

Need help/suggestion to convert some of the programs written in
HP-Cobol II (under MPE/iX) having macros to MF Cobol (under HP-UX
11i).

Because, Web resources revealed me that no other Cobol compiler
supports macros other than HP-Cobol.

Thanks in advance
Raghu
```

#### ↳ Re: Converting Macros from HP-Cobol II under MPE/iX to MF Cobol under HP-UX 11i

- **From:** "Ira D. Baxter" <idbaxter@semdesigns.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-12T13:02:43-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c8e4ea4$1@giga.realtime.net>`
- **References:** `<d2a417af.0203120128.45280d24@posting.google.com>`

```
By macros, I assume you mean something other than copybooks
"with replacing".

Our DMS Software Reengineering Toolkit is used to handle custom
conversions, and we have a lot of experience with macro processors
as front ends to programming langauges (C, C++, COBOL. (copy-replacing)).

DMS could be configured to parse the HP-COBOL files
containing whatever peculiar macros exist, and either expand
them or change them into more standard copybook schemes.
(DMS already parses COBOL).

See http://www.semdesigns.com/Products/DMS/DMSToolkit.html.
```

#### ↳ Re: Converting Macros from HP-Cobol II under MPE/iX to MF Cobol under HP-UX 11i

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-12T18:23:41-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a6m66e$657$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<d2a417af.0203120128.45280d24@posting.google.com>`

```
I know that there has already been at least one question about exactly what
"macros" are in HP-COBOL II.  I have received the following information from
one of my "usually reliable sources".  Hopefully, this will help others to
answer the original question.

(My personal guess is that Micro Focus "Vocabularies" may well do exactly
what is desired.  What I don't know is whether that facility is or is not
available in their HP-UX product.)

"Bill,
Thanks for noticing this. I used to monitor comp.lang.cobol, but just
haven't had the time recently.

Our COBOL II/iX compiler implements a macro facility. For sites that have
used it extensively (and many of our users do seem to be enamored with it),
it will be one of the major obstacles to migration off of the HP e3000.

Here's a simple example of what you can do with it.

Define a macro:



000100$DEFINE %PERFORM=

000200 PERFORM !1

000300 VARYING !2 FROM !3 BY !4

000400 UNTIL !5.#


Call the macro:



001200 %PERFORM(CHEK-PARA#,CTROL#,INIT#,OFFSET#,A = B#)


The compiler expands the macro:



000200 PERFORM CHEK-PARA

000300 VARYING CTROL FROM INIT BY OFFSET

000400 UNTIL A = B.


Some of our users have developed hundreds of macros to handle common code
sequences. Some of their macros are dozens, if not hundreds, of lines.

If you want the full documentation, try this link:

http://docs.hp.com/cgi-bin/doc3k/B3150090013.11820/199

Other vendors are adding support for HP COBOL-style macros in their COBOL
compilers. You might point the poster to Acucorp, http://www.acucorp.com/ ,
or LegacyJ, http://www.legacyj.com/ . Both of them discuss their support for
HP COBOL extensions and mention macros in particular.

Feel free to post any or all of this to comp.lang.cobol or to forward it to
the original poster.
```

## Thread: Anyone fancy a beer? — 18 msgs

### Anyone fancy a beer?

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-03-13T11:38:54+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c8e8961_1@Usenet.com>`

```
I'll be arriving in Los Angeles on the 31st of this month and plan to spend
a week in the US en route to Europe. (I'll be in Germany in April if anyone
there is reading this).

It just occurred to me that some of you might be interested in meeting up
for a beer and discussion of anything you like...<G>

This may be my last trip to the US, as I plan to take a contract in Europe
for the next couple of years, then go home for good.

If you would be interested in a social get-together and are within
reasonable distance of L.A. (or Frankfurt) please mail me privately.

I'd really like to meet some of the people who post here...<G>

Regards,

Pete.



 Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services

                http://www.usenet.com
```
```

Thane Hubbell <thaneh@softwaresimple.com> wrote in message
news:bfdfc3e8.0203122151.300af805@posting.google.com...
> Pete - not coming to the COBOL Expo?
>
Well I was supposed to be presenting at the original one and I prepared
material for it and extended my stay in NZ when it was postponed. (That was
no hardship; we are having a beautiful Summer here). My understanding was
that it would be in April so I arranged travel around that time. Then
September 11th happened and the airline re-scheduled me to 31st March.

Now I see it is in June, and, as I haven't been invited to the current one
(I guess it must have been something I said...<G>), I figured I should just
go back to work. So the answer to your question is "No"... although I wish
them every success.

> What is your route across the US?

L.A. to Frankfurt...<G>    However, I COULD be persuaded to do an internal,
as long as it was a REALLY good party, and I can be back in L.A. to make the
Frankfurt connection <G> (I can extend my stay in the US if required).

There have been a few private responses to this mail and I'll try and
organise so I can get to see at least some of the people.

Pete.
>
>
```

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>

```
Hi Willem,

After living and working in Madrid for two years back in the 80s, I would be
very happy to come to Spain.

I loved it. And I've never been to Granada.

I expect to be working in either England or Germany by the end of May, but
my plans are fluid. I would certainly come to Granada for a visit at some
stage before going back to New Zealand for good.

(Just the thought of good Spanish Rioja is making me restless...<G>)

I'll drop you an e-mail when I know what I'm doing.

Thanks for the response,

Pete.
```

```
Thanks Chris.

From the responses to date I will probably not get out of  California.

It is very interesting though and I am thinking about doing a kind of
"whistle stop" tour of the US and Canada as there are so many interesting
places, and people who have expressed interest. Probably in a year or so,
but definitely before I go back to Godzone (NZ...God's own Country <G>) for
good.

Maybe we can have a "travelling COBOL Show" ... <G>

I know you will enjoy your time Down Under and with an interest in Rugby you
can't go wrong...

I'm sorry I won't be here for the World Cup. I'll miss the Rugby but may get
to see some of it on www.nzoom.com.

How come Milwaukee is the FORMER beer capital? What happened? Where is the
CURRENT beer capital, then?

Thanks for the response.

Pete.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Anyone fancy a beer?

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-03-16T09:11:44+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c925b2d_11@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<3c8e8961_1@Usenet.com> <00020ff9vcoen@enterprise.net>`

```

Vince Coen <vcoen@enterprise.net> wrote in message
news:00020ff9vcoen@enterprise.net...
>
> Where's home ?

Tauranga. (it means "safe anchorage" in Maori...and is a thriving container
port) in the Western Bay of Plenty, North Island, New Zealand.  37.70S,
176.18E (Check your Atlas...<G>) It is a really beautiful place and I am
always sorry to leave, but the business is in Europe.


> Where in the UK?

No idea. Depends where I end up working. I'll be visiting friends in Norfolk
and I tend to avoid London as a place to live, but other than that, I really
don't know. I go where the work takes me and have done for the last 25
years. It might not be in the UK at all.

Pete.
```

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>

```
stringer <stringer@es.co.nz> wrote in message
news:a6u0jg$t19$1@lust.ihug.co.nz...
> Been to Tauranga on honeymoon (some years ago) - Lovely place - Almost as
good
> as Christchurch (NZ)!
>
Long time since I was in Christchurch. It is a lovely City, but I like
Tauranga better. (Climate for a start... then there is the Mount, the
beaches, the hot pools, the Mediterranean lifestyle... I find ChCh a little
too English...but I like the Wizard<G>)

> I don't want to start an Off Topic war on which City/Island/Country is
best!
> BUT one thing I will say is I would prefer London to Auckland (both of
which I
> have been to)
>
Well, THAT's hardly a fair comparison! Given London has over 7,000,000 and
Auckland has around 1.2...

I expect to be in London by the end of April after a 2 year absence. I hear
the cost of living has continued rising and all the natives have moved
out... I'll give you my impressions after I get there. I lived in London for
8 years and Auckland for 10. I like both Cities but I wouldn't compare them.
The trouble with Cities is they keep on changing... you go away for a while
and come back to find a new shopping mall where your favourite pub used to
be...it is very unsettling. Maybe that's why I like small towns...like
Tauranga.
```

- **From:** Herwig Huener & Josella Simone Playton <webmaster@Josella-Simone-Playton.de>
- **Date:** 2002-03-20T11:19:21+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C986229.1000002@Josella-Simone-Playton.de>`
- **References:** `<3c8e8961_1@Usenet.com>`

```
2002-03-20 11:11:11 MET

Peter E. C. Dashwood wrote:

> ...


> If you would be interested in a social get-together and are within
> reasonable distance of L.A. (or Frankfurt) please mail me privately.


Frankfurt? Not Munich? Pity. In Munich,
I would recommend the


   Molly Malone Irish Pub

    Kellerstrasse 21, 81667 München
    (directly behind the City-Hilton)
    S-Bahn Rosenheimer Platz
    +49 89 688 75 10
    open 17:00 - 01:00, on saturdays
    and sundays 12:00 - 01:00++

There they have exquisite Irish food and,
of course, Draught Guinness, - the best
Bavarian Beer ever.

And you don't have to wait for it - except,
maybe, at the 17-th of March.

During daytime at weekends, the place is quiet,
and you could bring along your laptop and work
there.

Herwig
```

### Cobol Test code

- **From:** "Vince Coen" <vcoen@enterprise.net>
- **Date:** 2002-03-13T00:15:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<00020ff4vcoen@enterprise.net>`

```
++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++
+ The Air Applewood BBS Internet Gateway - +44-1279-792300 v.All
+                                          +44-1279-794008 ISDN
+
+ FidoNet user address: f609.n257.z2 (2:257/609) - FidoNet user name: Vince Coen
+
+ User email address:   vcoen@enterprise.net
+ User FidoNet address: 2:257/609
++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

Hello All!

Many years ago I obtained from the (US) [Navy Programming Languages Division
Office of Information Systems Planning & Development at room 5D840 Pentagon,
Washington, DC 20350 with a phone no. of (202) 0X5-4750], a large set of
Compiler test Routines.

These routines are used to test various implementations of different Cobol
Compilers at the time. The listings date is 6th March 1977.

Now I only have these in listing form so not very useful.

Can anyone tell me if there is up to date versions of this code and if so where
can I obtain them from.

I tried to get in touch with the above office via the internet (couldn't get
access to any Navy site other than simple info) but with no success and the
phone number has no meaning to the UK phone system.

Again can anyone help me?

Thanks,

Vince
.
All context/ content herein is not the responsibility of 
Air Applewood BBS nor it's ISP or agents.

No liability will be therefore be so held.
```

<a id="t-95b07c6e77"></a>
## Thread: COBOL FAQ "caught up" (I think) — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Meta: FAQs, group policy, charter`](topics.md#meta)_

### COBOL FAQ "caught up" (I think)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-12T21:26:25-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a6mgt4$il1$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I think that I have fought my way thru my entire (multi-year old) "TO DO"
list for the COBOL FAQ. For the latest version, see:

     http://cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

 I have 3 notes still to implement:

 - Document "COBANAL" from the CBT tape (for analyzing IBM COBOL load
modules)
 - Document FORCEDOS for "dos emulation" in newer Windows environments
 - Add information on NetCOBOL from Fujitsu

    ***

I have *not* yet read thru the entire FAQ myself.  I do believe that there
is "significant" out-of-date or incorrect information that I hope to work
on - without additional input.

HOWEVER,
  I would be interested (now) in any additional topics, corrections,
additions, whatever that "you all" have.  Some of the "new" topics that I
have added (and that probably need the most review) are:

�        Added a new section (and subsections) "How do I get started with
COBOL? Where can I get education? Tutorials? Etc"

�        Started a new section (and subsection) "IBM Mainframe Debugging and
Development Tools"

�        Add information on the various RainCode products

�        Added information about Fujitsu-Siemens commercial COBOL products

***

Please feel free to send me private email - or post via the NG - any updates
that you would like to go into my "next" TO  DO list.
```

```
Hi everyone,

I'm looking for some good cobol user groups on the internet or usenet. I'm
trying to figure out which commercial COBOL products exist and would like to
read some reviews about them, preferably written by programmers cause they
really know the pros and cons of the different products. Can someone give me
some url's, or perhaprs your opinion about the different cobol products
available?

Thanx.

Rik Hendriks
Tilburg University
The Netherlands
```

```
micro has some user groups around the country in various places and at
various levels of interest.  Most of them have meetings about general
cobol stuff as well as micro focus, but many meetings are specific to
micro focus products.  there used to be a good list of them on the micro
focus page, but that kinda died in the transition from merant back to
micro focus.  work is under way to rejuvenate the groups and improve the
page.  until that fully happens, Stacey Goff-Johnson
(Stacey.GoffJohnson@merant.com) can tell you where there are existing
groups.....
```

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-03-15T00:20:06+00:00

- **References:** `<qa4k8.5171$DE4.742@afrodite.telenet-ops.be> <a6qojt$tbc$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <xP5k8.5575$DE4.707@afrodite.telenet-ops.be> <3C912383.81FAA441@dced.state.ak.us> <luak8.6599$DE4.948@afrodite.telenet-ops.be>`

```


Wim Stockman wrote:

> Indeed I found a few books to ,but it is still not what I'm looking for
> maybe I've got an old version of cobol I don't know.
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Yes the old MICROSOFT compiler is a 're-badged" MICRO FOCUS. Take the list that
Bill referred you to :-

-> 1. Product Documentation
-> 2. Net Express 3.0 or 3.1
-> 3. Programmer's Guides
-> 4. Character User Interfaces.

Now # 4 is NOT exactly what you want but will be very close to the Microsoft
compiler - talks about ADIS (AcceptDISplay) plus this section also has example
programs.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

<a id="t-3338c8e3bd"></a>
## Thread: COBOL FAQ — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Meta: FAQs, group policy, charter`](topics.md#meta)_

### COBOL FAQ

- **From:** wmklein@ix.netcom.com (William M. Klein)
- **Date:** 2002-03-15T11:27:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol,news.answers,comp.answers,alt.answers
- **Message-ID:** `<computer-lang/cobol-faq_1016191593@rtfm.mit.edu>`

```
Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-Modified: 2000/07/02
Version: 3.01
URL: http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm
Reply-To: wmklein@ix.netcom.com


                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Sunday, July 2, 2000

The COBOL FAQ has moved.  To view the most recent version, please check out the
web version at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send private
email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

The following is Appendix B.5 - Changes to create Version 3.01

 o Recorded new location of the FAQ
 o Updated some old (bad) web links
 o Added some new COBOL books (particularly those written or contributed to - by
frequent comp.lang.cobol submitters).
 o Added information about LegacyJ (PERCobol) and changed the company name from
"Synkronix" to "LegacyJ".
 o Updated information for changes in product and company names and addresses
where available
 o Updated the URLs for downloading the draft of the next COBOL Standard and
CobCy
 o Started the Samples/Example Section
 o Tried to get rid of most specific references to Windows/NT, Windows/95,
Windows/98, and Windows/2000.
 o Added links to Xinotech
 o Added information on Norcom and GUI ScreenIO
 o Added (back) information on Wang
 o Added (back) information on the Tiny-COBOL ('74 Standard) project
```

```
Hey, that's nice. There are some things in life that I really appreciate:
Having nice communication around the world as well as always learning new
things. Being welcomed in a net community. And  - sorry for that ;-)  on the
net I like to be called Thomas or ThoK. And of course I like people with a
good portion of humor - so I enjoy this NG!

Besides that I only work with Cobol because my job forced me to do so since
one year, and  I really have problems with all the experience I got in the
last 25 years with PL/I  to forget the good things you have there. OTOH
Cobol has nice feaures too! What I really miss is that - other than in PL/I
as well as C++ and most of my other languages - I don't know of a way to
create functions in Cobol. You know, something like:
MOVE MYFUNCTION Argument-1 TO RESULT
or as I would code it in PL/I:
RESULT = MYFUNKTION(Argument_1);
which really is an elegant way of coding.
Anyone got a clue? Or did I myself miss anything that already is available?

Bye for now

Thomas
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: test

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-03-17T20:37:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C94FE35.5917BEA7@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<00020ffavcoen@enterprise.net> <a721n1$pv7$1@suaar1ac.prod.compuserve.com> <a72c36$j2d$1@panix1.panix.com> <a72jtu$i4h$1@suaar1ac.prod.compuserve.com>`

```


Thomas Kregeloh wrote:

>
> Besides that I only work with Cobol because my job forced me to do so since
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thomas

First thing off, I've found it is not easy to try and obtain a  straight one for
one relationship between the syntax of different languages. Currently I'm
reading up on Visual Basic to see what they have to say about components. The
book has a chapter on creating classes - believe me, although the theory is the
same, creation of VB classes has no bearing on how you set them up under COBOL -
OK so COBOL is verbose.

Your query about functions. Currently can be handled by both Fujitsu and Micro
Focus (Net Express) OO compilers, when writing methods contained in classes.

The J4 specification for COBOL 2002 (due December), has the following :-

- UDFs(User Defined Functions)
- OO - Classes containing methods

UDFs are another way of expressing Methods without getting into OO. May not be
quite what you are after but :-

*>----------
Method-id. "ImpToMetric".
*>-----------------
Local Storage Section.
*> 01 ImpToMet   CONSTANT    value 25.4.
*> I'm not using above line, just included to show you how it can be used
Linkage section.
01 ImpValue                         pic 9(02)v9(03).
01 MetValue                        pic 9(03)v9(02).
Procedure Division using ImpValue returning MetValue.
 compute MetValue rounded = ImpValue * 25.4
End Method "ImpToMetric".
*>----------------------------------------

UDFs parallel the above format.

Note that COBOL 2002 allows for the '25.4' to be stored as a CONSTANT, (cast in
stone), either in Global Working Storage or within a method's Local-Storage
Section. At the current time in Micro Focus I can use a Level 78 either globally
or in the method Local Storage :-

    78 ImpToMet                value 25.4.

Working in OO you'll appreciate that I don't have a need for UDFs, but they will
be available to non-OO developers and you can build libraries of UDFs.

You could emulate UDFs currently by calling sub-programs, particularly useful if
you grouped a series of 'like minded' functions within one non-OO program and
were using entry points. Like minded functions - that's the whole point about OO
classes, isn't it, coupled with reusability ?

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
 TK> OTOH Cobol has nice features too! What I really miss is that - other than
 TK> in PL/I as well as C++ and most of my other languages - I don't know of a

 TK> got a clue? Or did I myself miss anything that already is available?

do work in a very similar way. You can also create/add your own functions.
```

<a id="t-9a35bc402c"></a>
## Thread: Some questions about ICL Mainframe COBOL. — 8 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Some questions about ICL Mainframe COBOL.

- **From:** LHebblethwaite@transoft.com (Leigh hebblethwaite)
- **Date:** 2002-03-15T06:05:48-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<12e866ae.0203150605.264e6fb9@posting.google.com>`

```
I am looking at some ICL Mainframe COBOL code, without access to any
documentation.

I would greatly appreciate it if someone could enlighten me about a
few things.

1) The programs use a 'I' character in PIC strings. An example is:
     01 XYZ PIC 99I/99I/99.
What are those 'I' characters are for?

2) The programs use a verb called TRANSLATE. This verb seems to be
implemented on ICL only. What does the TRANSLATE verb do?

Thanks,

Leigh.
```

#### ↳ Re: Some questions about ICL Mainframe COBOL.

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-03-16T09:28:17+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c925b2e_11@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<12e866ae.0203150605.264e6fb9@posting.google.com>`

```

Leigh hebblethwaite <LHebblethwaite@transoft.com> wrote in message
news:12e866ae.0203150605.264e6fb9@posting.google.com...
>
> 1) The programs use a 'I' character in PIC strings. An example is:
>      01 XYZ PIC 99I/99I/99.
> What are those 'I' characters are for?
>
It is an Insertion characer. Allows whatever follows to be treated as an
insertion to the picture.

> 2) The programs use a verb called TRANSLATE. This verb seems to be
> implemented on ICL only. What does the TRANSLATE verb do?
>
It allows one character set to be mapped against another. The ICL machines
had a 24 bit word mapped as 4 x 6-bit characters. This meant that the 6-bit
characters needed to be mapped against incoming 8 bit EBCDIC, and vice versa
if it was required to write standard EBCDIC data.

Back in the days of the Great Byte Wars (circa late 1960s) it was touch and
go whether the Industry would adopt a 6 bit "byte" or an 8 bit "byte".
Control Data, NCR, and ICL all  used 6 bit characters as part of a  64 bit,
24 bit, and 24 bit "word", respectively.  ICL had a project to convert their
24 bit architecture into EITHER 4 x 6-bit bytes or 3 x 8-bit bytes (pretty
smart, huh? <G>) but it was scrapped as too expensive.

Eight bit bytes won the day (partly because they were backed by the might of
IBM, but probably more because they provided a 256 character set, as opposed
to a 64 characte set that required ESCAPE/CONTROL codes for anything
esoteric.)

TRANSLATE did the mapping.

Pete.

> Thanks,
>
> Leigh.



 Posted Via Usenet.com Premium Usenet Newsgroup Services
----------------------------------------------------------
    ** SPEED ** RETENTION ** COMPLETION ** ANONYMITY **
----------------------------------------------------------        
                http://www.usenet.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Some questions about ICL Mainframe COBOL.

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-15T15:19:00-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a6tvh4$1ghv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<12e866ae.0203150605.264e6fb9@posting.google.com> <3c925b2e_11@Usenet.com>`

```
I can't speak to ICL, but I can state that Unisys V Series had a similar
extension.

But in this particulare case I believe the "I" characters are unnecessary,
as the slash character is already allowed as a simple insertion character
according to COBOL rules, and that's true as far back as my archives allow
for me to delve.  "PICTURE 99/99/99" is a valid edited-numeric PICTURE
string, and has been all along.

Were the picture "99I-99I-99", yes, I'd agree unconditionally.  But because
"99/99/99" is valid, I can't help but wonder why the programmer would want
to do manual insertion of the slash using the "I" where standard simple
insertion of the same character "/" would serve as well (and better,
precisely because it's standard COBOL!).  Am I missing something here?

    -Chuck Stevens.

 "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3c925b2e_11@Usenet.com...
>
> Leigh hebblethwaite <LHebblethwaite@transoft.com> wrote in message
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…

6-bit
> characters needed to be mapped against incoming 8 bit EBCDIC, and vice
versa
> if it was required to write standard EBCDIC data.
>
> Back in the days of the Great Byte Wars (circa late 1960s) it was touch
and
> go whether the Industry would adopt a 6 bit "byte" or an 8 bit "byte".
> Control Data, NCR, and ICL all  used 6 bit characters as part of a  64
bit,
> 24 bit, and 24 bit "word", respectively.  ICL had a project to convert
their
> 24 bit architecture into EITHER 4 x 6-bit bytes or 3 x 8-bit bytes (pretty
> smart, huh? <G>) but it was scrapped as too expensive.
>
> Eight bit bytes won the day (partly because they were backed by the might
of

opposed

…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Some questions about ICL Mainframe COBOL.

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-03-16T17:52:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C938657.E03192C0@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<12e866ae.0203150605.264e6fb9@posting.google.com> <3c925b2e_11@Usenet.com>`

```
Peter E. C. Dashwood wrote:
> >
> It allows one character set to be mapped against another. The ICL machines
> had a 24 bit word mapped as 4 x 6-bit characters. This meant that the 6-bit
> characters needed to be mapped against incoming 8 bit EBCDIC, and vice versa
> if it was required to write standard EBCDIC data.

While it is true that the ICL 1900s, 2903/4, ME29 and 2900 DME systems
had 24bit words, it is actually more likely that Leigh is using a 2900
VME mainframe which is 32 bit word, 8 bit byte.

In fact my references have TRANSLATE in the 2900 VME C2 compiler, but
not in XEC2 for the 24 bit machines.
```

```
Given that you have some documentation, could you tell us what TRANSLATE
achieves on the 2900 VME machine?

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Some questions about ICL Mainframe COBOL.

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Vernon Perkins" <Vernon@notchfield.fsnet.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2002-03-19T00:30:31
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a760p6$q2d$1@newsg4.svr.pol.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<12e866ae.0203150605.264e6fb9@posting.google.com> <3c925b2e_11@Usenet.com> <3C938657.E03192C0@Azonic.co.nz> <3c9544ea_8@Usenet.com> <12e866ae.0203180211.39962918@posting.google.com>`

```
"Leigh hebblethwaite" <LHebblethwaite@transoft.com> wrote
> "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote
> > Given that you have some documentation, could you tell us what
TRANSLATE
> > achieves on the 2900 VME machine?
> >
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Here it is in full.
I find it very useful - I used it to convert a binary data file
containing ASCII text to the native EBCDIC.

Vernon Perkins.

PS  This is easier to read in a fixed pitch font.
________________________________________

The TRANSLATE statement (C2 only)

1 Function
The TRANSLATE statement enables data to be translated from one character
set to another.

2 Format
                              -                   -
                             |     {ISO         }  |
TRANSLATE {identifier-1} ... | TO  {EBCDIC      }  |
                             |     {identifier-2}  |
                             |     {literal-1   }  |
                              -                   -

3 Programming rules
  Identifier-1 ...
    Identifier-1 ... must reference an elementary item with implicit or
    Explicit usage DISPLAY or a byte aligned group
    Identifier-1 ... and elementary subfields of identifier-1 ... which
    have implicit or explicit usage DISPLAY may be described by OCCURS
    clauses. If the DEPENDING option of OCCURS is used, the TRANSLATE
    statement will act on the current size of the field in question and
    not on its maximum size
    Identifier-1 ... must not have level-number 66
  Identifier-2
    Identifier-2 must reference an unedited elementary field with
    implicit or explicit usage DISPLAY or a byte aligned group field.
    It must not reference a non-byte aligned group field
    Identifier-2 must not have level-number 66
  Literal-1 must be a nonnumeric literal. It must not be a figurative
    Constant or an ALL literal
  If the TO clause is omitted, the effect is as though TO ISO had been
  specified

4 Execution of the TRANSLATE statement
If the TO phrase is omitted or if the ISO option is used,
identifier-1 ... is assumed to be in EBCDIC. If the EBCDIC option is
used, identifier-1 ... is assumed to be in ISO. In both these cases,
the TRANSLATE statement causes each character in identifier-1 ... to be
replaced by the corresponding character of the other collating sequence.
If the identifier-2 or literal-1 option is used, the TRANSLATE statement
Causes each character in identifier-1 ... to be interpreted as a bit
pattern (value N, say) and then to be replaced by the (N+1)th character
in identifier-2 or literal-1 . If any character in identifier-1 ... has
a value N such that (N+1) is greater than the number of characters in
identifier-2 or literal-1 , the effect is unpredictable.
If identifier-1 ... is a group field, each of its elementary subfields
With implicit or explicit usage DISPLAY will be translated as above
except that the following will be unaffected:
  Elementary items containing a REDEFINES clause
  Elementary items subordinate to a group item containing a REDEFINES
  clause where the group item is subordinate to identifier-1 ...
  Elementary items described as FILLER items
If the storage occupied by identifier-1 ... and identifier-2 overlaps,
The result will be unpredictable.

5 Efficiency considerations
The following recommendations apply when using the identifier-2 or
 literal-1 option:
  The number of characters in the translation table specified by
  identifier-2 or literal-1 should be either 256 or (N+1), where N is
  the highest value of any character that may appear in identifier-1 ...
  For example, if identifier-1 ... can contain any character in the VME
  character set, then the highest value of any character that may appear
  is hexadecimal FF, which is 255 in decimal, and so the translation
  table should be 256 characters long
  The translation table should not contain any uninitialised positions.
  If some positions in the table represent characters which cannot
  validly appear in identifier-1 ..., then these positions should be
  assigned some standard error character (for example, SPACES or
  HIGH-VALUES or ALL "?"), so as to avoid any problems that might
  otherwise arise in the event of identifier-1 ... containing invalid
  data If some of the replacement characters in the translation table
  do not have graphic representations, then identifier-2 can be
  initialised either by reading it in from a file or by describing
  any such positions in the table as 8-bit bit items and initialising
  them with numeric initial values. For example,
01   TRANSLATE-TABLE.
     02 T-1 PIC X(7) VALUE "ABC*%56".
     02 T-2 PIC X(10) VALUE SPACES.
     02 T-3 PIC 1(8) COMP-5 VALUE 49.
     etc.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Some questions about ICL Mainframe COBOL.

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-03-19T06:18:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C96D83B.ADD9B1E1@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<12e866ae.0203150605.264e6fb9@posting.google.com> <3c925b2e_11@Usenet.com> <3C938657.E03192C0@Azonic.co.nz> <3c9544ea_8@Usenet.com> <12e866ae.0203180211.39962918@posting.google.com>`

```
> > Given that you have some documentation, could you tell us what TRANSLATE
> > achieves on the 2900 VME machine?

The reference card to C2 has:

       TRANSLATE identifier-1 [identifier-2]
                 { ISO           } 
            TO   { EBCDIC        }
                 { identifier-3  }
                 { literal       }

I would assume that identifier-3 or literal are a table of 256 values.
```

```
Version 1 Release 2 or 3?

I am looking forward to the requirement and would like to discuss
issues.
```

#### ↳ Re: OS/VS COBOL and z/OS Version 1 Release 3

- **From:** Steve Thompson <sthompsonNOSPAM@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-31T21:55:03-05:00

```
Ron Sposato wrote:
> 
> Is there anyone out there running OS/VS COBOL applications under z/OS
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> issues.

I am working at a client site that is in the process of
migrating from VSE (using 74 COBOL which is very much like
VS/COBOL as I recall) to z/OS 1.3. We are not having a good
time. There are many problems with FILE STATUS errors of the
type where there is no further information (generally these
are the n0 errors). Mind you that a VSE to MVS translator
system is being used and we are still having problems. The
CICS COBOL seems to be working fine, it is just the BATCH
COBOL that seems to have these kinds of problems. Also,
there seems to be some interesting problems with CALLs to
the library routines from time to time.

If you post back to me, I am generally at the client site
all week and only home on weekends...
```

<a id="t-5d05dff6e1"></a>
## Thread: Sections — 76 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Sections

- **From:** "Eddy Scheire" <fa099784@skynet.be>
- **Date:** 2002-03-16T10:59:58+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c931758$0$29682$ba620e4c@news.skynet.be>`

```
Hi,

I'd like to know the difference between the next coding examples :

DO-SOMETHING section.
    IF condition
       statement
    ELSE
       statement
    END-IF
    PERFORM something
    MOVE something TO something
1000.
   EXIT.

and

DO-SOMETHING.
    IF condition
       statement
    ELSE
       statement
    END-IF
    PERFORM something
    MOVE something TO something
    .

I mean why should one use sections? To me it looks like unnecessary code.
```

#### ↳ Re: Sections

- **From:** none@none.com (freewheelin)
- **Date:** 2002-03-16T12:34:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c934702.42107902@news.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<3c931758$0$29682$ba620e4c@news.skynet.be>`

```
It depends on *how* you use them.   SECTION and PARAGRAPH are most
usefull with a nested structure. They provide a two tier control break
with the PERFORM statement.  The PERFORM SECTION has a higher level
control break than PERFORM PARAGRAPH, sort of like an implied PERFORM
THRU.   A SECTION can nest many PARAGRAPHs (with no SECTION labels)
and the SECTION is terminated by the beginning of the next SECTION
header or the physical end of the program.  Now then, consider PERFORM
THRU with SECTION labels and you create a third, higher level control
break.  Coding no SECTIONs or coding all paragraphs as SECTIONs hides
this feature and that is the usage you are questioning.  

On Sat, 16 Mar 2002 10:59:58 +0100, "Eddy Scheire"
<fa099784@skynet.be> wrote:

>Hi,
>
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Sections

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@invalid.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-16T14:39:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yOIk8.16804$1g.904321@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<3c931758$0$29682$ba620e4c@news.skynet.be>`

```

"Eddy Scheire" <fa099784@skynet.be> wrote in message
news:3c931758$0$29682$ba620e4c@news.skynet.be...
> Hi,
>
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> I mean why should one use sections? To me it looks like unnecessary code.

SECTIONS are an artifact, a fossil, a construct from a primative time.

Their major original function was to partition the code into overlayable
chunks to conserve memory. SECTIONS today permit no unique (or even useful)
purpose. Their presence does, however, tend to confuse or bewilder those who
have never encountered them - or those, like me, who haven't seen SECTIONS
in twenty years.

Check out LEVEL numbers for SECTIONS for more confusion:

   (PRINT-IT SECTION 73.).
```

```
sections is the simple statement EXIT SECTION.

Using sections doens't mean programmer can use GOTO en Perform ...
thru ...  

At our office we use sections because it allows the use of exit
section and it's easier to search for specific parts. Paragraphs
cannot be easily searched, while section. shows the start of every
section, just like Working storage section and file-section. 

It's something that's COBOL-own, and like I said earlier, doesn't let
the porgrmmer free to write junk. 
just like the point to exit an IF-statment. 

Both make programs very unclear to people who need to perform
maintenance on the program later.
```

- **From:** stephenjspiro@mail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
