##### ↳ ↳ Re: SEQMENTATION

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-03-25T10:05:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C9EF672.7AA5C8A3@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<DyPk8.15605$Ex5.1395006@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <VAwl8.32020$Es6.811372@news2.west.cox.net>`

```
Rufio wrote:
> 
>>> Q. How do you write STRUCTURED CODE, without using sections, or the highly
>>> fallible Perform of paragrpah ranges.

> REPORT-PROCESS SECTION.
> R1000-HEADER-CHECK.
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> A-N-OTHER SECTION.

I think that you have a very understanding of what constitutes
'structure'. You have merely scattered unreferrenced and spurious
paragraph labels and non-functional 'EXIT' statements throughout the
section.  

They are _not_ structural, they are merely cosmetic, they do not aid
understanding they just complexify the code.  Those labels may be better
by being made into comments or removed entirely.

This 'style' may make you feel better because there are 'lots of little
functions', but they are _not_ _functional_.  They merely give the
appearance of structure without their being any, and worse, they obscure
the actual structure.

Whenever a program needs to be understood by someone not the author then
they must go through certain steps to ensure they are actually
understanding it and are not mislead.  At every label they must
understand the flow of the code.  Your code entirely misleads readers.
If there is a piece of code that goes:

      named-label.
         some code
         .
      named-exit.
         exit.

Then it is reasonable to expect that this is a function where
'named-label' is the reference to it and it will exit at the point
marked.

Your code scatters these randomly through the actual code.  R400-Exit
_is_ an actual exit point, the rest are not.  This forces the reader to
investigate each and every label and exit to see whether is is actual or
false.

Your paragraphs are like thumb-tacking extra 4x2s onto a house, they
don't add structure, they don't improve the house, they merely get in
the way and make it difficult to find where the real structure is.

Looking at a block of code there is no indication at all which of these
labels are performed. You may understand that only the sections are but
the next programmer does not know that yet, and may take several hours
before they assure themselves this is the case.  You have wasted your
time putting these labels in, the only nett effect is that the next
programmer will have his time wasted doing the best thing for these
labels, consigning them to the bit bucket.

Here is a functional version of your code (ie removing all
non-functional, cosmetic parts):

REPORT-PROCESS SECTION.
    IF LINE-COUNT > 58
        WRITE REPORT-HEADER1 AFTER PAGE
        WRITE REPORT-HEADER2 AFTER 1
        LINE-COUNT = 3
    END-IF
    MOVE Prod-Code        TO Report-Product
    MOVE Prod-Description TO Report-Description
    MOVE Prod-Unit        TO Report-Unit
    MOVE Prod-Quantity    TO Report-Quantity
    MOVE Prod-Price       TO Report-Price
    WRITE REPORT-LINE1 AFTER 2
    WRITE REPORT-LINE2 AFTER 1
    WRITE REPORT-LINE3 AFTER 1    
    ADD 4      TO LINE-COUNT
    READ INPUT-FILE-2 INTO INPUT-BUFFER-2
      AT END 
         SET INPUT-FILE-2-EOF TO 'Y'
    END-READ       
    .
A-N-OTHER SECTION.

There is no _need_ for comments, and especially no need for
dis-functional labels.  The _code_ should say what it is doing
(otherwise just write in C ;-).  The more lines there are, the more
labels, the more misleading exits, the more difficult it is to
understand and the more complex it is to work on.

Having dealt with how unstructured your labels make the code, I can now
look at what your code actually does.  It is entirely a poor example of
how a structured program should be.

Your 'process-report' section has in it elements of dealing with
page-headings and elements of accessing the file.  While you attempt to
separate these out with non-functional paragraphs these are _cosmetic_,
not _structural_.  You disguise your lack of program design by adding a
layer of paint.  If you paint the house then no one will notice that it
is held together with thumb-tacks.

In fact it seems that you _sole_ criteria for whether it is 'structured'
is how many lines are in a paragraph.  Too many lines can be 'fixed' by
banging in another couple couple of labels and an exit.

I would suggest that you read some books on program design and try to
work out what you should be trying to do. 

PS: you don't get paid or measured by the line of code (LoC) do you ?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: SEQMENTATION

- **From:** "Rufio" <davecawdell@cox.net>
- **Date:** 2002-03-26T23:37:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4D7o8.1248$Va.75328@news2.west.cox.net>`
- **References:** `<DyPk8.15605$Ex5.1395006@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <VAwl8.32020$Es6.811372@news2.west.cox.net> <3C9EF672.7AA5C8A3@Azonic.co.nz>`

```

"Richard Plinston" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3C9EF672.7AA5C8A3@Azonic.co.nz...
> Rufio wrote:
> >
> >>> Q. How do you write STRUCTURED CODE, without using sections, or the
highly
> >>> fallible Perform of paragrpah ranges.
>
…[128 more quoted lines elided]…
> PS: you don't get paid or measured by the line of code (LoC) do you ?

What I did, using Procedural Decomposition was break down the particular
print function into its elementary sub-functions, and place each in it's own
paragraph.This encapsulates elementary functions, in their own structure
(paragraph). I could then have PERFORMed each paragraph -
i.e
PERFORM  R1000-HEADER-CHECK
PERFORM R2000-POPULATE-PRINT-LINES
PERFORM R3000-PRINT LINES
PERFORM R4000-WRAP-UP

But as I know that they will always be performed together, I grouped them in
a section, so that anyone who isn't interested, just has to skip the PERFORM
REPORT-PROCESS statement, at the higher level, rather than the 4 PERFORMs.
Yes - the paragraph statements and their matching Do-nothing paragraph
EXIT's appear to function just as comments. But once I add comments to the
code, it will still be immediately obvious where each elementary function
begins & ends, without having to troll through every comment in the source.
As for GOTOs. No well written top-down program should need a GOTO, as there
should be no need to act a lateral redirect. NOTE: I didn't say I never use
GOTO's - just I don't use them in new programs.

Whatever the programming methodology, the key is always adherence to
standards - paragraph names must be consistent and reflect their contents,
etc. Also, the "secret" is, any time spent in design pays back (perhaps
twofold) in coding.

I don't claim to be the worlds greatest programmer. But after a couple of
decades in the business, working on a dozen or so teams (including being
hired onto the same team 3 TIMES in 7 years), on a couple of continents, and
knowing for a fact that NO-ONE, no colleague, manager or client has made any
adverse comments about my code, in over 10 years - and they keep paying me-
I figure perhaps I know a little something about programming. I code for a
number of different things - efficiency, robustness, ease of support & ease
of development, are some of them. My program aren't the fastest and I
occasionally include bugs. But the last major project I managed (designed &
did some of the coding for - released in 2001) took processes, from 3
different applications, migrated the ultimate repository from DLI to DB2,
cost under $350,000 (paid back in about 11 months), cut 96% off CPU time,
and it has a lower failure rate than it's predecessor, so my code isn't just
pretty - it works too!

Again, I ain't the greatest - but this is how I use sections to enhance my
code - I welcome constructive comments about how BETTER to do so. This is
how I earn a living, so I ALWAYS look for ways to be more beneficial to my
clients.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: SEQMENTATION

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-03-27T17:40:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CA2041A.AE5FD95A@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<DyPk8.15605$Ex5.1395006@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <VAwl8.32020$Es6.811372@news2.west.cox.net>`

```
Rufio wrote:
> What I did, using Procedural Decomposition was break down the particular
> print function into its elementary sub-functions, and place each in it's own
> paragraph.This encapsulates elementary functions, in their own structure
> (paragraph). 

No. Wrong.  You haven't 'encapsulated' them at all.  You have merely
added some decoration.  They are not 'elementary sub-functions' but just
have the appearance.  They are not 'in their own structure', there is no
structure, just flappery.

> I could then have PERFORMed each paragraph -
> i.e
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> PERFORM R4000-WRAP-UP

And if you had done so the program would then show signs of there being
a structure, of actually using encapsulation, instead of pretending to.

You have added all the overheads as if there was some actual stucture,
but have gained none of the benefits that arise out of structured
programming.  Those paragraphs aren't reusable in any real sense, they
are not 'elementary', they cannot be moved to group them by
functionality because they are not encapsulated.  The labels are merely
a hindrence to understanding what is really happening and a trap for the
unwary.

> But as I know that they will always be performed together, I grouped them in
> a section, 

You could also have done a PERFORM R1000-Header-Check THRU R400-Exit,
with much the same effect of the 'structure'.  But you seem to recognise
that PERFORM THRU is falable, Please explain why you think that there is
any difference at all in your use of SECTIONs in this way.

> so that anyone who isn't interested, just has to skip the PERFORM
> REPORT-PROCESS statement, at the higher level, rather than the 4 PERFORMs.

The whole point of structured programming is to expand from a higher
level of abstraction down to the details in a way that enables the
program to be understood easily and at the levels required to safely do
changes.  Your 'style' does not do this.  It hides the actual structure
and requires that the different levels of abstraction be physically
positioned together because it is _not_ abstracted.

> Yes - the paragraph statements and their matching Do-nothing paragraph
> EXIT's appear to function just as comments. 

They don't 'function' at all.  In fact the do-nothing exits are worse,
they appear to be exit points but should never be used as such.  

Within a PERFORMed section if there are any paragraph labels at all
(except where the compiler requires an initial paragraph) then the only
point of them is to allow a GO TO (or require a GO TO).

The only point of having a xxxx-Exit paragraph label is to cater for a
GO TO.  There is no other functional reason.

Within your performed section a GO TO xxxxx-Exit would operate
correctly, but if this was changed to the 4 performs then these GO TOs
represent a loss of control.  


> But once I add comments to the
> code, it will still be immediately obvious where each elementary function
> begins & ends, 

Why don't you try doing _actual_ encapsulation, making these into
_actual_ 'elementary functions' instead of just pretending to.

For example the 'Header-Check' could actually be used from 'Print-Lines'
instead of being part of the 'Report-Process'.  In this way it would be
the responability of the 'print-lines' routine to ensure that headers
are on the page.  Doing this in a re-usable way (such as in a copybook,
called routine, or class) would reduce the need to re-invent the
'header-check' each time.  It would also mean that if a new site
standard had some new requirement for what was in the page headers it
could be changed in _one_ place instead of having to change each program
separately.

You have failed to understand _how_ structured programming is done
(though you know what it looks like) and you have failed to understand
_why_ structured programming is done.

If your program was to be extended (by the next programmer), by, say,
building a table as the report is processed and then printing out a
summary at the end, then, this too, will need 'Header-Check'.  They
can't just PERFORM the existing one - it is _not_ encapsulated, it is
_not_ an 'elementary function', they would have to copy-and-paste, or
write a new one.

If they did just do a PERFORM R1000-Header-Check then the program could
be destroyed if some other programmer decided that a 'GO TO R1000-Exit'
would be better than adding some complex condition.  The reason that
this would destroy it is that the program _does_not_have_ the structure
that it appears to, they are only cosmetic and should never be used.

Normally I wouldn't take issue with what other programmers use (well OK,
I would), but you queried "Q. How do you write STRUCTURED CODE, without
using sections" as if they were a requirement and then recommended "if
you want to write code structured as much as possible, in a way that
makes it easier to read, I recommend Sections".

I suggest that you find out how to write structured programs from a
design viewpoint, rather than just how to make them have the superficial
appearance of being structured.
```

## Thread: PC_PRINTER_REDIRECTION_PROC routine — 6 msgs

### PC_PRINTER_REDIRECTION_PROC routine

- **From:** rondos@zahav.net.il (davidc)
- **Date:** 2002-03-17T07:31:26-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4a5699b5.0203170731.38af20d@posting.google.com>`

```
I'm using NetExpress 3.0 and I would like to know how to use the printer
routine PC_PRINTER_REDIRECTION_PROC.

I have not found any documentation about this routine.

Thanks
```

#### ↳ Re: PC_PRINTER_REDIRECTION_PROC routine

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-03-17T19:17:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C94EB98.F3BBE1A5@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<4a5699b5.0203170731.38af20d@posting.google.com>`

```


davidc wrote:

> I'm using NetExpress 3.0 and I would like to know how to use the printer
> routine PC_PRINTER_REDIRECTION_PROC.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks

Is it possible it isn't included in Version 3.0. This is the on-line help
for V 3.1 :-

Jimmy, Calgary AB
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

PC_PRINTER_REDIRECTION_PROC.

Registers a user function that is called when a program uses OPEN OUTPUT
syntax and the ASSIGN TO PRINTER clause.

call "PC_PRINTER_REDIRECTION_PROC" using flags
                                         user-func

Parameters:
flags pic x(4) comp-5.

user-func procedure pointer.
status-code See Key

On Entry:

flags Reserved for future use. Must be set to 0.

user-func A pointer to a procedure that will manipulate the printer.

On Exit:

status-code Status code
Comments:

This routine enables you to set and manipulate fonts on the printer while
using standard COBOL OPEN OUTPUT syntax and the ASSIGN TO PRINTER clause.

A call to PC_PRINTER_REDIRECTION_PROC registers the user function pointed to
by user-func with the run-time system.

The user function should have the form:

entry "user-func" using by value printer-handle
 ...
 ...
exit program returning return-value.

printer-handle should be defined as a pic x(4) comp-5 data item.

When an OPEN statement opens a printer the user function is called and the
printer handle returned in printer-handle. The user function can then
manipulate the printer using the PC_PRINTER_ library routines. The user
function should return a value to the run-time system to determine the next
sequence of events.

You should code your user procedure to return a return-code 0 if it is
called successfully, and any other value if it is not. If your user function
does return a non-zero value, the run-time system closes the printer and a
file status 9/005 is returned on the OPEN OUTPUT statement that invoked the
user function.

Copyright (C) 2000 MERANT International Ltd. All rights reserved.
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
```

<a id="t-19e7447a0e"></a>
## Thread: Error after OS 390 v2.10 upgrade — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Error after OS 390 v2.10 upgrade

- **From:** bardsfamily@yahoo.com (Alexa)
- **Date:** 2002-03-18T06:11:16-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<80ae94d9.0203180611.5076aba5@posting.google.com>`

```
After we upgraded to v2.10, we started receiving abends in one of our
applications. We get abends in different programs, different ddnames,
and different jobs, but the first occurrence always seems to start
with a message like the following:

There was an unsuccessful OPEN or CLOSE of file SYS040 in program
RI531S at relative location X'092C'. Neither FILE STATUS nor an ERROR
declarative were specified. The status code was 90. From compile unit
RI531S at entry point RP531S at compile unit offset +0000092C at
address 38C1CFDC.

In our procedures, we have the following dd statement in different
steps

//sys005  dd ddname=sysin

When we want to override this statement, our jcl looks as follows:

//step.sysin  dd dsn=whatever

If we change the override to be

//step.sys005  dd dsn=whatever

then the abend goes away.

We may get other abends, but we always get the one above.  And the
abend is consistent.  We will continue to get it until we change the
override.   What confuses me is that this abend never occurs for any
other application even though they also use the same way to define and
override sysin.  There must be something different about this
application, but I can't figure out what.  We are using IBM COBOL for
OS/390 & VM  2.2.0.

Does anyone have any ideas as to why we are getting this and why it is
only this one application?  Note that the abend message is always for
a ddname that follows sys005 in the proc.

Any suggestions would be deeply appreciated as this is driving me
nuts!

Alexa
```

#### ↳ Re: Error after OS 390 v2.10 upgrade

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-18T12:55:48-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a75d7u$kcu$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<80ae94d9.0203180611.5076aba5@posting.google.com>`

```
Taking a WILD guess on this, have you verified that your LE run-time options
are identical in the old and new environments. Particularly, have you
(intentionally or unintentionally) changed the value of the
   CBLQDA
run-time option?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Error after OS 390 v2.10 upgrade

- **From:** bardsfamily@yahoo.com (Alexa)
- **Date:** 2002-03-19T05:16:29-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<80ae94d9.0203190516.43251e30@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<80ae94d9.0203180611.5076aba5@posting.google.com> <a75d7u$kcu$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message news:<a75d7u$kcu$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>...
> Taking a WILD guess on this, have you verified that your LE run-time options
> are identical in the old and new environments. Particularly, have you
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> --


I had to do some research to see what this was as I really only dabble
in COBOL and am no systems programmer.  After checking, we have CBLQDA
set to ON, which is the IBM default.  Since the options are the same
for the applications that work and the ones that don't, I wouldn't
think this would impact the issue anyway.  I was wondering if there
was something in the program itself that might impact this, although I
can't imagine what.  Or maybe the order of the dd statements? Hmm. I
think I will play with rearranging the dd statements and see if moving
the ddname=sysin to the last position will make any difference.  The
file getting the message is a temporary file so maybe CBLQDA is
impacting it after all.  Thanks.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Error after OS 390 v2.10 upgrade

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-19T08:21:10-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a77hh2$nf4$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<80ae94d9.0203180611.5076aba5@posting.google.com> <a75d7u$kcu$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <80ae94d9.0203190516.43251e30@posting.google.com>`

```

```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Error after OS 390 v2.10 upgrade

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-19T08:26:20-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a77hqn$b57$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<80ae94d9.0203180611.5076aba5@posting.google.com> <a75d7u$kcu$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <80ae94d9.0203190516.43251e30@posting.google.com>`

```
CBLQDA(ON) is particularly dangerous in a multi-step job.  If you make a
typo in your JCL for the DDName in one step, it will create a file which
does NOT get cataloged - and which canNOT be found in the next step.  This
is why your proc override gave me the clue that it was probably what your
problem was.

Although CBLQDA(ON) was the default in earlier releases of LE, even IBM has
changed to "OFF" as the default in the latest releases.  This violates some
rules of the ANSI Standard, but makes life MUCH easier for most OS/390
shops.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Error after OS 390 v2.10 upgrade

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** bardsfamily@yahoo.com (Alexa)
- **Date:** 2002-03-20T05:55:20-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<80ae94d9.0203200555.44c1042d@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<80ae94d9.0203180611.5076aba5@posting.google.com> <a75d7u$kcu$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <80ae94d9.0203190516.43251e30@posting.google.com> <a77hqn$b57$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Thanks!  I will check with our systems programmers and see if we can't
get it changed.

I was discussing this with someone else again and that triggered
something I had forgotten about.  Isn't SYSIN always supposed to be 80
bytes?  Because in the application with the problem, SYSIN always has
88 byte datasets pointed to it.  The applications that work always
have 80 byte datasets.  Maybe this is why the override can't be SYSIN.

Thanks for all your help.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Error after OS 390 v2.10 upgrade

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** bardsfamily@yahoo.com (Alexa)
- **Date:** 2002-03-20T10:02:43-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<80ae94d9.0203201002.12031dad@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<80ae94d9.0203180611.5076aba5@posting.google.com> <a75d7u$kcu$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <80ae94d9.0203190516.43251e30@posting.google.com> <a77hqn$b57$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Changing the CBLQDA did not help.  However, the systems programmer is
in the middle of applying an IBM fix and he ran the job in his test
system and it worked there with no problem.  So evidently it was an
IBM issue all along.  It was fixed by IBM UW80819.  However, I still
don't understand why only the one application has the issue unless it
really is related to the SYSIN being more than 80 bytes.  I guess I
won't worry about it any more.

Thank you for all your help.
```

#### ↳ Re: is mfcobol 2 assembly possible?

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-03-19T08:05:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C96F12C.87B7F447@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<428ac1d0.0203181045.34afb3d4@posting.google.com>`

```
mark edwards wrote:
> 
> is there an option to cause the mfcobol compiler to produce assembly code?

In the listing, yes.

Try combinations of COPYLIST NOERRLIST REF SOURCEASM ASM"filename.ASM"
```

```
And most importantly, have you already ready ANY of the IBM "COBOL Migration
Guides", for example:

 http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/IGY3MG00/CCONTENTS?
```

#### ↳ Re: Q: COBOLII vs COBOL LE vs COBOL

- **From:** stephenjspiro@mail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2002-03-18T21:51:55-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<928495c6.0203182151.5d65ca12@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<20020318152904.09235.00001964@mb-cs.aol.com>`

```
djschlief@aol.comnojunk (DJSchlief) wrote in message news:<20020318152904.09235.00001964@mb-cs.aol.com>...
> My question is what is the different between COBOL, COBOLII, and COBOL LE ?

COBOL is the name of the language, all dialects, all releases, all
standards.

COBOL II is the name given to the 1985 standard as implemented by
several compiler makers.  Also called COBOL 2 or COBOL 85 by other
compiler makers.

LE COBOL or COBOL LE is  the current version of COBOL as implemented
by IBM for operating systems which use IBM's Language Environment
product.


Stephen J Spiro
Member, J4 COBOL Standards Committee
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Q: COBOLII vs COBOL LE vs COBOL

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-19T05:08:46-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a77689$p9f$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<20020318152904.09235.00001964@mb-cs.aol.com> <928495c6.0203182151.5d65ca12@posting.google.com>`

```
From the fact that the poster mentions "COBOL LE" - my guess is that he is
talking totally about IBM (probably IBM mainframe environment).  Certainly
"COBOL II" is *not* "the name given to the 1985 standard" as Stephen writes,
but is a common (mis-)name given to IBM's compiler that was FIRST a '74
Standard compiler, then an '85 Standard compiler. The *actual* (official)
"relatively" modern list of IBM compiler names (for the MVS environment, VM
is a little different VSE is more different<G>) is:

  ANS COBOL
  OS/VS COBOL
  VS COBOL II
   IBM SAA AD/Cycle COBOL/370
   IBM COBOL for MVS & VM
   IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM
   IBM Enterprise COBOL for z/OS & OS/390

Many of these have had different "releases" and/or versions.   Some
supported multiple ANSI Standards (via compiler options, e.g. LANGLVL and
CMPR2).  The compilers that are listed AFTER "VS COBOL II" use LE (Language
Environment) as their run-time; the compilers up to VS COBOL II all provided
their own run-time component.  Only the last two are still officially
supported (as compilers) by IBM anymore.  Although the object code from all
of them is still supported in an LE environment.
```

#### ↳ Re: Q: COBOLII vs COBOL LE vs COBOL

- **From:** Jim Reinhart <jreinhart1@cox.net>
- **Date:** 2002-03-21T03:15:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<B8BE9F6E.1449%jreinhart1@cox.net>`
- **References:** `<20020318152904.09235.00001964@mb-cs.aol.com>`

```
in article 20020318152904.09235.00001964@mb-cs.aol.com, DJSchlief at
djschlief@aol.comnojunk wrote on 3/18/02 1:29 PM:

> 
> My question is what is the different between COBOL, COBOLII, and COBOL LE ?
> 
> 
> 
According to my research of the IBM COBOL migration guides from the
Redbooks, ...

COBOL 1968 - (D)OS/VS COBOL
COBOL 1974 - (OS)VS COBOL
COBOL 1985 (i.e. COBOL2) - VS COBOL II, COBOL/VSE, COBOL/LE/VSE (per
http://www.redbooks.ibm.com/pubs/pdfs/redbooks/sg244277.pdf)

COBOL/VSE and COBOL/LE/VSE have many extensions over any other version from
IBM.  COBOL/LE/VSE v.4 is the current enterprise product.

The best way to determine what can do what is to look at what libraries are
required to run and viewing the compile maps.  The current level of COBOL
from IBM can be run recursive, re-entrant, interfaces with the web, is CORBA
compliant with SOM objects, runs in a mixed environment, (both language and
OS)...

If corporations would use tools like Visual COBOL, new programmers could
easily move to supporting COBOL environments and old programmers could
sharpen their skills.

Jim Reinhart
```

<a id="t-1af3bd22d8"></a>
## Thread: Help Needed displaying binary (COMP-3) numbers — 9 this month / 10 total

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Help Needed displaying binary (COMP-3) numbers

- **From:** "Mark Miles" <milesfam@idirect.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-18T19:18:46-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<u9d0vesaoho968@corp.supernews.com>`

```
I have a file and the first four bytes is a segment length (this is what it
looks like in hex 6CBF0000 00500000 00000000 00000000), 6CBF in hex is 27839
in decimal.  I have tried defining a PIC clause of 9999 BINARY and the value
displayed is 7839.  How do I correctly display the number?

Mark
```

#### ↳ Re: Help Needed displaying binary (COMP-3) numbers

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@NOSPAM-hot.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-19T01:52:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lRwl8.29470$Dv6.1023682@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<u9d0vesaoho968@corp.supernews.com>`

```
You don't say what machine this is on, but on a mainframe, this would wind
up being in a fullword.
Pic 9(4) is not large enough. So you need to do something like this:

In your FD, put in a

    05 FOUR-BYTE-BINARY-VALUE               PIC X(4).

Then down in Working-Storage, do something like:

    01 BINARY-VALUE                                      USAGE IS BINARY.

finally in the procedure division, do something like:

    MOVE FOUR-BYTE-BINARY-VALUE TO BINARY-VALUE
    DISPLAY BINARY-VALUE

You should get the correct answer. You might have to add a PIC s9(9) before
the usage is binary
clause to force a fullword, but I don't think so, and I unfortunately cannot
test this till tomorrow, at least
on a mainframe.

Yours,
-Paul


"Mark Miles" <milesfam@idirect.com> wrote in message
news:u9d0vesaoho968@corp.supernews.com...
> I have a file and the first four bytes is a segment length (this is what
it
> looks like in hex 6CBF0000 00500000 00000000 00000000), 6CBF in hex is
27839
> in decimal.  I have tried defining a PIC clause of 9999 BINARY and the
value
> displayed is 7839.  How do I correctly display the number?
>
> Mark
>
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Help Needed displaying binary (COMP-3) numbers

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@invalid.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-19T03:45:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<zvyl8.15257$4I.1690408@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<u9d0vesaoho968@corp.supernews.com>`

```

"Mark Miles" <milesfam@idirect.com> wrote in message
news:u9d0vesaoho968@corp.supernews.com...
> I have a file and the first four bytes is a segment length (this is what
it
> looks like in hex 6CBF0000 00500000 00000000 00000000), 6CBF in hex is
27839
> in decimal.  I have tried defining a PIC clause of 9999 BINARY and the
value
> displayed is 7839.  How do I correctly display the number?
>
> Mark

For sure it's not COMP-3.

COBOL (generally) assigns storage depending on the PIC size and the format.

For binary numbers (BINARY or COMP-4):

PIC 9 to PIC 9999 COBOL assigns two bytes.
PIC 9(5) to PIC 9(9) COBOL assigns 4 bytes.

So, your job is now to get the first 2 bytes of your record to the
right-most two-bytes of a 4-byte field.  You want 6CBF to look like 00006CBF
(defined as PIC9(9) COMP-4). Note that if the field begins with "8" or more,
you're dealing with a negative number.

Here's one way:

01  MYRECORD.
   02  MY2BYTE-FIELD    PIC X(2). *>The 6CBF value

WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.


01  WORK-BYTES.
   02  WORK-BYTES-A   PIC X(2) VALUE LOW-VALUES.
   02  WORK-BYTES-B   PIC X(2).
01  FILLER REDEFINES WORK-BYTES.
   02  BIG-COMP    PIC 9(9) COMP-4.

MOVE MY2BYTE-FIELD TO WORK-BYTES-B.

*> At this point, WORK-BYTES (and BIG-COMP) contains: 00 00 6C BF.

DISPLAY BIG-COMP.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Help Needed displaying binary (COMP-3) numbers

- **From:** jnjsle@aol.com (Jnjsle)
- **Date:** 2002-03-19T23:21:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020319182122.00701.00001815@mb-ft.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<zvyl8.15257$4I.1690408@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
Hi Mark,

In a mainframe environment the key to the solution of your problem is the
compiler option TRUNC(BIN).
This could be put in a PROCESS card before the 1st COBOL stmt, e.g.:

PROCESS TRUNC(BIN)

Then the definition below:
 
01 YOUR-FLD PIC 9(004) USAGE BINARY. 

will accept a the maximum value allowable. One caveat though, if the
installation default is specified as other than BIN, you may have to use
Paul’s solution or a variation.

HTH, Jack.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help Needed displaying binary (COMP-3) numbers

- **From:** jnjsle@aol.com (Jnjsle)
- **Date:** 2002-03-20T06:29:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020320012902.13417.00001833@mb-fn.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<20020319182122.00701.00001815@mb-ft.aol.com>`

```
Sorry Mark,

I thought you wrote "2 bytes". The field s/b defined as follows for 4 bytes:

01 YOUR-FLD PIC 9(009)  BINARY.
```

Hi Mark,

In a mainframe environment the key to the solution of your problem is the
compiler option TRUNC(BIN).
This could be put in a PROCESS card before the 1st COBOL stmt, e.g.:

PROCESS TRUNC(BIN)

Then the definition below:
 
01 YOUR-FLD PIC 9(009) BINARY. 

will accept a the maximum value allowable. One caveat though, if the
installation default is specified as other than BIN, you may have to use
Paul's solution or a variation.

HTH, Jack.





"Mark Miles" <milesfam@idirect.com> wrote in message news:<u9d0vesaoho968@corp.supernews.com>...

#### ↳ Re: Help Needed displaying binary (COMP-3) numbers

- **From:** Jim Reinhart <jreinhart1@cox.net>
- **Date:** 2002-03-21T02:05:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<B8BE8EFF.143F%jreinhart1@cox.net>`
- **References:** `<u9d0vesaoho968@corp.supernews.com>`

```
in article u9d0vesaoho968@corp.supernews.com, Mark Miles at
milesfam@idirect.com wrote on 3/18/02 5:18 PM:

> I have a file and the first four bytes is a segment length (this is what it
> looks like in hex 6CBF0000 00500000 00000000 00000000), 6CBF in hex is 27839
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
The problem you are having is that using BINARY items, 9999 BINARY is a half
word (i.e. 2 BYTES), Truncating the first 2 bytes.

For binary use this as a reference
Half Word = 2 Bytes uses PIC 9(4) BINARY or less.
Full Word = 4 Bytes uses PIC 9(5) through 9(9) BINARY.
Double Word = 8 Bytes uses PIC 9(10) through 9(18) BINARY.

For your purposes, use at least PIC 9(5) BINARY or 99999 BINARY.  Don't go
higher than 9(9) BINARY.

Good COBOL programming uses only PIC 9(9) BINARY for full word definitions.

To display just move the variable to a display variable.

COMP = BINARY
COMP-3 = PACKED DECIMAL

Don't use a COMP-3.

Regards,

Jim Reinhart
```

<a id="t-f6593868bf"></a>
## Thread: Varying record lengths — 11 msgs

### Varying record lengths

- **From:** "Mark Miles" <milesfam@idirect.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-18T19:21:21-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<u9d14rfbrvmlc0@corp.supernews.com>`

```
I would like to ask a dumb question, can I read a record of varying lengths,
I know I can write a record of varying record sizes to the same file but
when I tried the same technique on an input file I got a compile error.

Any examples are welcome.

Thanks

Mark
```

#### ↳ Re: Varying record lengths

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-03-19T01:44:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C9697AE.C2DA96F2@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<u9d14rfbrvmlc0@corp.supernews.com>`

```


Mark Miles wrote:

> I would like to ask a dumb question, can I read a record of varying lengths,
> I know I can write a record of varying record sizes to the same file but
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Mark

Subject to compiler yes you can - and others will comment.

However here's something that will be less than apparent. You can READ in
varying records PROVIDING  the file you are reading in was created in the first
instance with varying specified; just changing the  format specification of an
existing file to say it is now varying, wont get you very far. (I know, I
recently found out the hard way <G>).

If you currently already have a large file on which you want to do this, you'll
have to write an intermediary program -  Old file = fixed, New file = varying,
then write Old records to New records. Now you can use the latter as input or
output with varying.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Varying record lengths

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-18T21:04:21-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a769s2$jct$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<u9d14rfbrvmlc0@corp.supernews.com> <3C9697AE.C2DA96F2@shaw.ca>`

```
It also may depend on your environment - and whether you do or don't need to
worry about "line sequential" vs "record sequential" files.  Show us the FD
(and Select/Assign) of the existing file that worked when you did WRITES -
and we can probably tell you why you are having problems with your reads.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Varying record lengths

- **From:** "Mark Miles" <milesfam@idirect.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-19T20:48:11-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<u9fqj9j0cgl54a@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<u9d14rfbrvmlc0@corp.supernews.com> <3C9697AE.C2DA96F2@shaw.ca> <a769s2$jct$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
The input file is given to me by an external vendor and a daily basis, they
send me a bunch of files and all but one is fixed length.

The one I'm having trouble seems varied, layout is: -
File Position      Description.
1-4                   Segment Length
5-5                   Segment Type
6 - 300             Holds data for type A
6 - 50               Holds data for type B
6 - 4096           Holds data for type C
6 - 50               Holds data for type D

This goes on for a further 15 types each being different lengths but the
Segment length allows me to position to the next segment, I have succesfull
coded this in VB but how do I read in varied records.

I first used the following: -
 FILE-CONTROL.
     SELECT INPUT-FILE-NA ASSIGN TO "C:\TEMP\NA.DAT"
                          ORGANIZATION SEQUENTIAL.
 DATA DIVISION.
 FILE SECTION.
 FD  INPUT-FILE-NA
     RECORD IS VARYING IN SIZE DEPENDING ON INPUT-RECORD-LENGTH.
 01  INPUT-REC-NA.
     02 FILLER                              PIC X(32000).
 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
 01  INPUT-RECORD-LENGTH                    PIC 9(5) COMP VALUE ZERO.

The compiler I'm using is Fujitsu Ver 3

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Varying record lengths

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-19T20:33:35-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a78se0$1dp$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<u9d14rfbrvmlc0@corp.supernews.com> <3C9697AE.C2DA96F2@shaw.ca> <a769s2$jct$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <u9fqj9j0cgl54a@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Try the following (slight) modification:

  FD  INPUT-FILE-NA
      RECORD IS VARYING IN SIZE DEPENDING ON INPUT-RECORD-LENGTH.
  01  INPUT-REC-NA.
      02 Each-Byte occurs 1 to 4096 times
                Depending on  Input-Record-Length
                    Pic X.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Varying record lengths

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2002-03-20T03:02:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020319220237.07549.00000230@mb-fb.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<u9fqj9j0cgl54a@corp.supernews.com>`

```
In article <u9fqj9j0cgl54a@corp.supernews.com>, "Mark Miles"
<milesfam@idirect.com> writes:

>FILE SECTION.
> FD  INPUT-FILE-NA
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

This syntax will ALWAYS result in a fixed length record as the compiler
sees only one possible record length defined.
You must have multiple 01 levels each with a different record length
for any of the Variable length syntax to work.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Varying record lengths

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-03-20T04:33:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C98103E.711DC767@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<u9fqj9j0cgl54a@corp.supernews.com> <20020319220237.07549.00000230@mb-fb.aol.com>`

```


Sff5ky wrote:

> In article <u9fqj9j0cgl54a@corp.supernews.com>, "Mark Miles"
> <milesfam@idirect.com> writes:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> for any of the Variable length syntax to work.

I don't think so - look at the following. Since originally writing this I
have split the method "access-file" into separate methods per file VERB,
which is a much more efficient way of doing it, rather than using EVALUATE
in this instance. But note the  'varying' logic remains the same. When I
run this through the debug Animator, as each record is read in I can query
the field ws-record-size which contains the 'actual' value for the incoming
record, regardless of the fact that I have specified  :-

01  Data-record           pic x(1530).              *> +++

Memorywise, I'm no longer exactly "tuned in" to the following - because I'm
currently in the process of switching to SQL, with a separate class per
Table.

If the person originally querying was interested in different record types,
yes record types could be part of the format, but he would only require
different 01 levels if the incoming records contained different field
definitions. The other alternative could be one Level 01 and use REDEFINES.
But even that doesn't affect this program - it is a "black box" with a
record of 1,530 characters. It is the copyfile in the Business Logic which
would be interested in the different record formats.

Note that the logic of this class would allow CSVs to be written from/to
either SQL Tables or the COBOL file system.

Jimmy, Calgary AB

*>-----------------lsqfile.cbl-----------------------------------

Class-id.          LineSequentialFile
                   inherits from FileMethods.

Class-control.     LineSequentialFile      is class "lsqfile"
                   FileMethods             is class "filemthd"
                   .

*>---------------------------------------------------------------
FACTORY.
*>---------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "new".
*>---------------------------------------------------------------
Linkage section.
01 lnk-self                object reference.
Procedure Division returning lnk-self.
   invoke super "new" returning lnk-self
End Method "new".
*>------------------------------------------------------------
End FACTORY.
*>------------------------------------------------------------
OBJECT.
*>------------------------------------------------------------

*> **************************************************************
*> see maxrecsize.cpy - lines marked +++ must equal MaxRecordSize
*> see mthod "record-size-error" in super DBIMethods.
*> **************************************************************

INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.

     Select               Data-File
     assign               Data-filename
     organization         line sequential
     access               sequential
     file status          ws-filestatus.

*>---------------------------------------------------------------
FILE SECTION.

FD  Data-File
    record varying from 1 to 1530 characters        *> +++
       depending on ws-record-size.
01  Data-record           pic x(1530).              *> +++

*>---------------------------------------------------------------
OBJECT-STORAGE SECTION.
copy "LinkErr.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==ws==.
copy "wsresult.cpy".
01 Data-filename                      pic x(100).

*> ++++ ws-record-size contains a dummy value of '1' so that
*> if application aborts and attempts to 'close file' the
*> method "access-file" has a value when using reference
*> modification to move to/from os-record info

01 ws-record-size                     pic x(4) comp-5 value 1.

01 os-record.
      10  occurs 1 to 1530                          *> +++
          depending on ws-record-size    pic x.

*>----------------------------------------------------------
Method-ID. "access-file".
*>-----------------------------------------------------------
Local-storage section.
01 ls-result                             pic x(4) comp-5.
Linkage Section.
copy "fileactn.cpy".
01 lnk-result                            pic x(4) comp-5.

Procedure Division Using     file-action
                   returning lnk-result.

*> "evaluates" below are listed in most likely frequency sequence
*> for this particular file

   set file-result-ok to true
   initialize ws-Error-Data

   if   dont-initialize
        continue

   else initialize os-record(1:ws-record-size)
   End-if

   Evaluate TRUE

     When read-next

          Read Data-File next record
            At End
              Set file-finis to True
            Not at End
              Move Data-record  (1:ws-record-size)
                   to os-record (1:ws-record-size)
          End-Read

     When write-record

          Move os-record     (1:ws-record-size)
               to Data-record(1:ws-record-size)
          Write Data-record

     When close-file         Close Data-File
     When open-input-file    Open input Data-File
     When open-output-file   Open output Data-file

     When delete-file
          call "CBL_DELETE_FILE" using     Data-Filename
                                 returning ls-result
           move "00" to ws-filestatus

    When other
      invoke super "invalid-action" using file-action
      set file-error to true

   End-evaluate

   *> Code below is not moving a 'key' but the first 50
   *> characters of the line-sequential record

   if      ws-filestatus <> "00"
           move os-record(1:10)  to ws-error-key
           move os-record(11:40) to ws-error-name
           invoke super "check-file-status"
                  using     ws-ErrorFields
                            file-action
                            ws-file-result
                  returning lnk-result

   else    move ws-file-result to lnk-result
   End-if

End Method "access-file".
*>---------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "set-file-info".
*>---------------------------------------------------------------
Linkage section.
01 lnk-Parent                    object reference.
copy "makefile.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==lnk==.

Procedure Division using     lnk-Parent
                             lnk-makefile-params.

  initialize               ws-ErrorFields
  move lnk-filenum      to ws-filenum
  move lnk-filename     to Data-Filename
  move lnk-record-size  to ws-record-size             *> +++
  invoke super "set-window-title" using lnk-Parent
                                        lnk-makefile-params
End Method "set-file-info".
*>---------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "get-file-object".
*>---------------------------------------------------------------
*> Gets the data object after a READ NEXT
Linkage section.
01 lnk-record.
   10  occurs 1 to 1530                               *> +++
       depending on ws-record-size    pic x.
Procedure division returning lnk-record.
 Move os-record(1:ws-record-size)
   to lnk-record(1:ws-record-size)
End Method "get-file-object".
*>---------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "set-file-object".
*>---------------------------------------------------------------
*> Sets the data object prior to a WRITE
Linkage section.
01 lnk-record.
   10  occurs 1 to 1530                               *> +++
       depending on ws-record-size            pic x.
Procedure division using lnk-record.
 Move lnk-record  (1:ws-record-size)
      to os-record(1:ws-record-size)

End Method "set-file-object".
*>---------------------------------------------------------------

End OBJECT.
End CLASS LineSequentialFile.

*>---------------------------------------------------------------
```

```
Hi Mark,

I see now the reason for your other ques. Generally a file construct as you
described will usually have an associated copybook or record description
containing REDEFINES based on the type field. You may want to contact the file
supplier to get the copybook/description. 

It may be a good idea to let us know what you plan to do with the data in the
file. You may not need to know the details, but that depends...

Let us know your intent.

Regards, Jack.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Varying record lengths

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@invalid.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-20T14:19:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<RT0m8.76718$1g.6223441@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<u9d14rfbrvmlc0@corp.supernews.com> <3C9697AE.C2DA96F2@shaw.ca> <a769s2$jct$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <u9fqj9j0cgl54a@corp.supernews.com>`

```

"Mark Miles" <milesfam@idirect.com> wrote in message
news:u9fqj9j0cgl54a@corp.supernews.com...
> The input file is given to me by an external vendor and a daily basis,
they
> send me a bunch of files and all but one is fixed length.
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Segment length allows me to position to the next segment, I have
succesfull
> coded this in VB but how do I read in varied records.

I don't think you can do directly what you want. Without the file being
written by Fujitsu as a variable-length record file, the file itself
contains no usable information for the OS to deconstruct the individual
records (most variable-length routines actually write the record length at
the start of each record). Obviously the program that wrote the original
file had a similar technique, but the original technique is incompatible
with Fujitsu's (FJ uses 8 bytes at the beginning of each record to designate
the length and you have only four).

If you can get your vendor to build the file as:
Record Length - 4 bytes
Filler 4 bytes with binary zeros
(rest of record the same)

Your problem goes away as the file now conforms to Fujitsu's method.

Fujitsu does provide some external routines (CBL_xxx) to access files.
Here's how we made their CBL_READ_FILE routine work for non-conforming
variable length files:

StartByte = 0
LOOP:
BytesToRead = 32000
Read
Extract record from beginning of block,
Add this record's length to StartByte (don't forget the 4 bytes at the
beginning)
GOTO LOOP

Here's how you might configure the code for your case:

StartByte = 0
LOOP:
BytesToRead = 4
Read into BufLen
Add 4 to StartByte
Set BytesToRead to BufLen
Read into data area
(process)
Add BufLen to StartByte
GOTO LOOP


An alternate way is to read the file one byte at a time, building each
record manually.

This, of course, is a HUGE performance hit.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Varying record lengths

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** jnjsle@aol.com (Jnjsle)
- **Date:** 2002-03-21T06:21:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020321012139.12525.00001887@mb-fw.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<u9fqj9j0cgl54a@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Hi Mark,

In a previous thread you showed us a record fragment in hex: 

6CBF0000 00500000 00000000 00000000

This looks like the typical VB record format for the 1st record in the block.
The 1st 4 bytes (27,839) being the block size and the 2nd 4 bytes being the
record length (80).

If this is true, then you can read the file using the following in your COBOL
pgm:

     FILE SECTION.
         FD  YOUR-VB-FILE
             RECORDING MODE IS V
             BLOCK CONTAINS 0 RECORDS
             RECORD IS VARYING FROM 1 TO xxxx
             DEPENDING ON BYTES-IN-REC.
         01  YOUR-VAR-REC.
             05  FIL OCCURS xxxx DEPENDING ON
                 BYTES-IN-REC           PIC  9(008).

  where xxxx is the length of the largest record defined by the external vendor
*excluding* the 4 byte segment length.

In the JCL DD stmt the BLKSIZE s/b the largest blocksize expected, including
the 4 byte BDW and the 4 byte RDWs.

When you do it this way you won't see the hex stuff, just the user data.

I forgot the most important part! The "user data" length of the record
(excluding the seg length field length) just read will be in BYTES-IN-REC.

I'm not sure what you want to do after you get your hands on the data, but I
think this should get it for you.

HTH, Jack
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Varying record lengths

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** jnjsle@aol.com (Jnjsle)
- **Date:** 2002-03-21T06:45:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020321014516.12529.00002138@mb-fw.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<20020321012139.12525.00001887@mb-fw.aol.com>`

```
Sorry Mark,

The record desc s/b:

         01  YOUR-VAR-REC.
               05  FIL OCCURS xxxx   DEPENDING ON
                    BYTES-IN-REC        PIC  X(001).

In Working Storage, code:

01  BYTES-IN-REC       PIC 9(008).
```

## Thread: Annoying AIX COBOL Compiler.... — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Annoying AIX COBOL Compiler....

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@NOSPAM-hot.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-19T02:09:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<O5xl8.29511$Dv6.1039534@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`

```
Okay, I'm out of my depth again. Would some of you guys with the wise (if
not older!) heads
give me a hand here?

I just have to call a C function from COBOL, something that should be very
easy. In fact, it is
terrifically easy under Windows or OS/2, or better yet, on the Mainframe or
on an AS/400.
And indeed, it *works* under AIX 5L.  But it gives me the following annoying
error that I cannot
seem to eliminate. (Go figure - it will linkedit the .o file and create an
executable file even with the
rotten error in there...)

I wrote a super simply little COBOL and C program to test this. They are
reproduced below.  Ignore the case in the COBOL program; it is there just
because I typed it in vi instead of a COBOL aware editor... ;)  The function
in the C program is in uppercase because I have been trying different
combinations of PGMNAME(UPPER) and
PGMNAME(MIXED). Mixed *always* seem to come out LONGMIXED, which may be the
problem.
I don't know how to track this down however.


Anyways: Here is the error I get -

# cc -c cgic.c
# cob2 -o cgi cgicob.cbl cgic.o
PP 5765-548 IBM COBOL Set for AIX       1.1   in progress ...
LineID  Message code  Message text
     3  IGYDS1046-E   A user-defined word was used as a program under the
                      "PGMNAME(LONGMIXED)" option.  The name was accepted in
                      its uppercased format.
Messages    Total    Informational    Warning    Error    Severe
Terminating
Printed:       1                                    1
End of compilation 1,  program CGICOB,  highest severity: Error.
Return code 8
# ./cgi
Content-type: text/html

<hr>This from the C program<hr>
<br>C return code is 000000056<br>
<br>From the COBOL program<hr>
#

----
And here are the pesky sample programs. The IBM C Set for AIX compiler
version 5 is installed, and
COBOL Set for AIX version 1.1.

-Paul

C program:

#include <stdio.h>
int CGIC () {
   int rc;

   fprintf (stdout,"Content-type: text/html\n\n");
   fprintf (stdout,"<hr>This from the C program<hr>\n");
   return (56);

   }


COBOL Program:

    CBL PGMNAME(M)
    identification division.
    program-id. cgicob.
    data division.
    working-storage section.
    01 program-name             pic x(8) value 'cgic'.
    01 rc                                PIC s9(9) comp.

    procedure division.
        call 'CGIC' returning rc
          end-call
        display '<br>C return code is ' rc '<br>'
        display '<br>From the COBOL program<hr>'
        goback.
```

#### ↳ Re: Annoying AIX COBOL Compiler....

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-18T20:49:43-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a7690i$64t$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<O5xl8.29511$Dv6.1039534@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`

```
Boy is it nice to have an "easy question" - and to understand (an obscure)
compiler message.

Change

 program-id. cgicob.
    to
 program-id. "cgicob".

For documentation on this, see:
  http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/igy3lr00/3.1.1?

which says (in part),

"PGMNAME (LONGMIXED)
Program-name must be specified as a literal. "
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a7838g$6uv$1@ins22.netins.net>`

I have just (within the last few minutes) received a new book on COBOL 
entitled -

	Title:   COBOL Programming using the .NET Framework
	Author:  Ronald D. Reeves
	Pub:     Prentice Hall
	ISBN:    0-13-066843-5

Has anybody who is using Fujitsu COBOL (the book is co-branded with 
Fujitsu name) seen or used this text?  I probably have to continue using 
Version 6 of Fujitsu for at least another year, but are trying to put 
ourselves into a position to use .NET products next year with the use of 
VB.NET in another course.  I need to begin to evaluate the possibility of 
moving toward the .NET environment.

Thank you for any comments.

Floyd Johnson
```

Warren Simmons

##### ↳ ↳ Re: New Book

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-19T14:52:45-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a788eu$c89$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<a7838g$6uv$1@ins22.netins.net> <QoNl8.640$aF.47252@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Warren,
  Have you actually looked at the Fujitsu NetCOBOL product?

Whatever it is, it MOST CERTAINLY IS NOT anything like their previous
compiler(s) - or anyone else's.  It is missing BUNCHES of ANSI'85 Standard
stuff (at compile-time) and what it "produces" is totally different than
"traditional" object code.  (Obviously, it also include bunches of NEW stuff
as well as what it doesn't support)

So far, no other compiler vendor (that I know of) has indicated a desire
(much less an intention) to provide a ".NET" COBOL compiler - although the
.NET environment does provide "reasonable" tolerance of non-.NET programs.

I believe that Fujitsu is providing a "good" play-toy to get started with
this new environment (for learning purposes).  Furthermore, I think there is
at least a 50-50 chance that this will be an important market (probably less
than 50-50 for COBOL) some time in the future.

But whatever you think of what Fujitsu is doing (or might do in the future),
this is a "new world".

NOTE WELL:
  Warren may have confused ".net" with "supporting internet applications".
The latter is QUITE common and almost all PC compilers DO provide that
capability today. However, that isn't what the book is talking about.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: New Book

- **From:** "398199821" <warren.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2002-03-20T18:34:40+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4D4m8.2367$aF.188037@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

## Thread: Future of object-oriented COBOL? — 44 this month / 55 total

_Topics: [`Object-oriented COBOL`](topics.md#oo)_

### Future of object-oriented COBOL?

- **From:** kimloistudio@hotmail.com (Dan Brown)
- **Date:** 2002-03-19T15:53:44-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c2ab4b04.0203191553.5deaa74b@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi everyone,

  COBOL has been around for 40 years and widely used in business
applications. Will COBOL 2002 ie. object-oriented COBOL be equally
ubiquitous? I'm new to COBOL but just wondering what your comments
are.

Thank a lot.

Dan.
```

#### ↳ Re: Future of object-oriented COBOL?

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@invalid.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-20T13:57:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<pz0m8.8161$Jg4.810525@bin7.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<c2ab4b04.0203191553.5deaa74b@posting.google.com>`

```

"Dan Brown" <kimloistudio@hotmail.com>
>
>   COBOL has been around for 40 years and widely used in business
> applications. Will COBOL 2002 ie. object-oriented COBOL be equally
> ubiquitous? I'm new to COBOL but just wondering what your comments
> are.

Sure. Those programs written 40 years ago are still running and will
continue to run for the next 40 years. They may not be OO, but they're
COBOL.
```

#### ↳ Re: Future of object-oriented COBOL?

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-03-21T11:00:01+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c9918f8_9@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<c2ab4b04.0203191553.5deaa74b@posting.google.com>`

```
Here's one man's opinion:
--------------------------

The future of OO COBOL is doubtful.

Not because there is anything wrong with it... in fact, it is a superb
product.

The reason it is doubtful is because the mainframe arena has strongly
resisted it (rightly or wrongly... the debate has raged endlessly...) and
this has meant withdrawal of support for it by IBM.

(Bill Klein will correct me if I'm wrong here... I've given up trying to
follow what is happening in "Fortress COBOL" ...)

By the time they realise that this is an essential part of distributed
systems (which the Fortress COBOL people are not too keen on anyway) it will
be too late, and COBOL will be relegated to a Batch processing development
Language  for Big Iron. (yes, I know everyone is thinking that CICS will
keep it alive... don't hold your breath. There are developments currently in
the pipeline that will render even CICS pointless. I predict that within 15
years NO mainframes will be doing teleprocessing. It just isn't economically
sensible when machines with equal or better capacity can be obtained for a
third of the price. The WWW and Java will take over the on-line processing.
There is one possible reprieve; that the price of mainframes and the
infrastructure necessary to keep them running, falls dramatically. I believe
this is unlikely, but I could be wrong.)

OO COBOL on Client/Server is excellent and, fortunately, interest in "modern
COBOL" appears to be increasing. We are seeing more youngsters doing COBOL
courses. (Unfortunately, many of these courses are just teaching traditional
Batch COBOL which leaves the students kind of bewildered, and with a
completely wrong impression of the current capabilities of the COBOL
Language. They learn about OOP in the Java part of their course, but never
get to see that the same facilities are availabe through OO COBOL)

I have been using OO COBOL for just over two years now and I find it an
excellent complement to Java. I use it to build Components that can be
deployed on servers and in desktop programming. I use it to link to OLE
servers and so combine all the "standard desktop software" into my COBOL
applications. I am sold. It works for me. However, I am also realistic
enough to realise that unless it is embraced by Fortress COBOL it's days
will be numbered.

The real question we have to ask is: "Is there a place for OO COBOL in the
application development of the Future?".

The answer appears to be: "It depends..."

It depends on what application development you are looking at. For the stuff
I am doing, it is brilliant.

For mainframe programming, the answer appears to be "No". (Personally, I
reject this. There are demonstrable benefits to be obtained from OOP in ANY
environment... OO COBOL is the route to these benefits in the mainframe
environment, but there is such fierce resistance to change in this
environment, (and there is no denying that these are new concepts to the
average mainframe COBOL programmer, so a learning curve is involved), that
the result is the rejection of this Methodology.)

I believe that by the time the COBOL groundswell wakes up to the need for
it, it will be too late. Other OO Languages will have claimed the ground
that was once held by COBOL. (Ths ground is shrinking anyway, see next
paragraph...)

COBOL is axiomatically the best language for developing commercial computer
applications. But the lines are getting blurred. The days when we needed to
maintain source code (for which COBOL is the perfect language) are drawing
to a close. We are entering a future where software development will NOT be
undertaken by EVERY corporation. It will be too expensive to maintain
development on-site, especially when the Company is NOT in the IT business.
Component based solutions will reduce the cost of "tailored packages" to the
point where it would be insane NOT to use them. In this environment the
Source code will be irrelevant. You will simply purchase and "plug in" the
functionality you want at the point where you want it.... Eventually "Smart
Software" ("Wizards", if you like) will work with non-technical end users to
do this.

OO COBOL is a perfect tool for developing commercial Components.

So, there you have it. If you are planning a career as a specialist IT
Component builder, employed by an IT provider, then go for it. If you expect
to use this skill while working in an existing IT department (part of a
non-IT oriented corporation) there is little point in learning it. (At least
at the moment... it MAY change, but, based on the observed reactions and the
evidence over the last couple of years,  I am not optimistic.)

Pete.

Dan Brown <kimloistudio@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:c2ab4b04.0203191553.5deaa74b@posting.google.com...

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Future of object-oriented COBOL?

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>

- **Message-ID:** `<3C991FB3.EC98B300@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<c2ab4b04.0203191553.5deaa74b@posting.google.com> <3c9918f8_9@Usenet.com>`

```


"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:

> Here's one man's opinion:
> --------------------------
>
> The future of OO COBOL is doubtful. <snip>

As much as I try to be optimistic I keep finding myself getting in the doldrums,
but plod on hopefully . I agree with what you have written..

Couple of points. Hasn't been mentioned lately but Bill did point out some time
back that it is economically non-viable for the PC compiler vendors to consider
development costs for OO unless they know they are on to a sure thing. Hence the
majority are playing a waiting game, and at the moment it's down to Fujitsu and
Micro Focus ( Don't know whether Hitachi is purely mainframe or whether they
have an OO offering for PCs - not particularly relevant as they have restricted
marketing to Japan).

Without question OO COBOL is going nowhere unless the big iron gets involved;
I'm thinking primarily of IBM and Unisys, (I have no idea how many of the other
'famous' names exist as separate entities). IBM - like playing with a daisy
pulling off the petals, "Will she, wont she....". Unisys - was a glimmer of hope
a couple of years back when they announced they would be using Hitachi OO - but
that appears to have quietly disappeared.

Other than Collections, yet to be specified in a TR - the OO COBOL specification
is complete - and we certainly wont see any dramatic changes between now and the
cut-off date of December 2002. Based on the J4 Standard, none of the 'watchers'
have indicated any commitment to OO.

It does raise a question about the function of J4 - as we approach the deadline
December 2002, with no whisper of a commitment by the many, as opposed to the
few (three) - why have they spent time since 1996 pissing around with an OO
specification ?

Oh hum.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future of object-oriented COBOL?

- **From:** Gerry Wolfe <gerry.wolfe@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-03-21T02:02:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Xns91D7D5EF97860gerrywolfesympaticoc@206.172.150.13>`
- **References:** `<c2ab4b04.0203191553.5deaa74b@posting.google.com> <3c9918f8_9@Usenet.com> <3C991FB3.EC98B300@shaw.ca>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in
news:3C991FB3.EC98B300@shaw.ca: 

> 
> 
…[43 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Let's turn the discussion inside out -- instead of talking about languages, 
talk about what they're being used for...

There are two type of programming environments -- procedural-driven and 
event-driven.  A procedural environment is yer typical batch process -- the 
code drives the procedure; you code the logic to identify what happens 
next.  An event-driven environment is yer typical online process -- the 
operator (or an external interrupt) drives the procedure; the program code 
is an "object" which reacts to the "event" that has just occured.  

With a procedural environment (batch) the code drives the process; with an 
event-driven environment (interactive) the code reacts to the event (a 
monitor follows the events, and loads the reacting "objects").

An example of how they differ: try to flowchart the logic involved in 
programming an elevator to respond to buttons pushed, floors reached, etc.  
With a "procedural" environment, you'll go around in circles until your 
head hurts.  With an "event-driven" environment, it's relatively simple.  
We're talking about a "shift in paradigm" as far as the comprehension is 
concerned.

COBOL (traditionally) is the king of procedural processes.  There is no 
language out there which is better suited for running batch code on a host 
(let's forget about PL/I... a great language that should have had better 
acceptance didn't). <This will prob'ly raise a bunch of rebuttals; start a 
new thread if you want to rebut>

Do not underestimate the importance of batch processing... it is *much* 
more efficient to stockpile the transactions and process them in one fell 
swoop... for example, there isn't a major credit card company in the world 
which does it's account posting online... they're all stored for the off-
hours batch update.  That's why your payment isn't reflected until tomorrow 
(with exceptions where the transactions are made available for online 
inquiry to make it look like they've already been processed).

COBOL is the only efficient language (remaining) on the big hosts.  

However, we do have a need for interactive processing.  Enter CICS...  a 
bunch of procedures that allow for screen input/output and testing what key 
the operator pushed.  Older than the hills; introduced long before personal 
computers were common...  Prob'ly designed by a bunch of batch guys who 
just wanted to be able to handle screen processing...

The bugaboo is that as soon as you get into interactive processing, you no 
longer want to be using a procedure-driven environment, you need to get 
into an event-driven environment.  CICS doesn't support the event-driven 
model; the programmer still has to code the logic to figure out what key 
the operator pushed and to control the sequence of logic depending on this.

Object-oriented programming works hand-in-glove with event-driven 
processes.  Let Win98 keep track of what key was pushed; simply execute the  
code that was put together to handle that situation.

When you code a program interacting with CICS (whether it be COBOL, PL/I, 
whatever), the majority of the logic of the program is figuring out what 
the hell button was just pushed, and which one was pushed before, yada yada 
yada.  The application programmer ends up having to re-invent the wheel -- 
the equivalent of the Win9x OpSys that simply loads the correct "object" to 
react to the "event"that has just happened.

If CICS were a truly event-driven model, the online mainframe programming 
environment would be a lot different.

Interactive PC-based applications depend on the model driving them -- say 
Win98.  There are a bunch of events that are available (e.g. "button 
pushed").  When you code the logic to handle that little event (an 
"object") it doesn't matter a lot whether you're talking about Visual 
BASIC, Fujitsu PowerCobol or even MS Access... you just have to identify 
what has to be done.  Used them all, you "just" have to pass back the 
correct values in the correct variables.  A "move" statement in BASIC does 
the same as one in COBOL, it just looks a bit different.

So, to summarize (it's about bleedin' time, you prob'ly say!)...

* The most effective use of COBOL will be for batch processing.  There is, 
simply, no other mainframe language better.  You will *NEVER* see a 25,000 
line COBOL program translated into C++.  Nor would you ever try to write 
(or maintain!) such a program in any other language.  PC-weenies, don't 
laugh... there are a lot of programs out there with > 10K lines of source 
code.

* For green-screen online mainframe processing, the only game in town is 
CICS or IMS/DC.  This is not an event-driven (i.e. Object-Oriented) model 
and the application programmer will still have to fudge the event-driven 
model using a procedural language.  Don't bet that IBM (or any other 
mainframe company) will ever offer a replacement.

* OO-COBOL is a nice idea.  However, until there is an event-driven 
processor available to call the objects (i.e. a CICS replacement) we're 
looking at something which doesn't have a lot of merit in the real world.

Just my $0.02Cdn worth...

OK, forum, comment to your heart's content (but be gentle...)
```

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>

- **References:** `<c2ab4b04.0203191553.5deaa74b@posting.google.com> <3c9918f8_9@Usenet.com> <3C991FB3.EC98B300@shaw.ca> <Xns91D7D5EF97860gerrywolfesympaticoc@206.172.150.13>`

Gerry Wolfe wrote:

> "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in
> news:3C991FB3.EC98B300@shaw.ca:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> > <snip>

>
> Let's turn the discussion inside out -- instead of talking about languages,
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> <snip>

Lots of good stuff Gerry but there is one fundamental flaw in your argument. OO
is NOT SOLELY about GUIs and events. Both Simula and Smalltalk designers were
interested in trying to tackle the huge problem of programming in a systematic
way, to overcome application complexity, and logic errors causing programs to
go belly up, and at the same time (1970's) face the problem of restricted
memory.

The concept of objects was introduced together with small granular procedures
(methods) which could constantly be re-used. Coupled with this was the concept
of inheriting from super (superior) classes for 'common' methods. Objects are
NOT just GUI images, but can also be things that we don't display, such as file
systems, or a program performing calculations for interest charges.

It would be perfectly viable to produce an OO application without screens !

This was the path followed by the Xerox PARC team. At the same time they
recognized that having 'pictures' made the whole thing more comprehensible to
end users, rather than playing it 'blind' as happened on mainframes with a set
of JCL cards - and hope the operators used the correct input, (tapes or disks),
and that you got the right output (dear old GIGO).

As regards the 'pictures', unbeknownst to her bosses, Adele Goldberg with a
felonious compatriot slipped some computers out of the front door of Xerox PARC
and tried them on some kids at a local school. The kids reacted favourably to
the pictures - Bingo ! The first GUIs were born. Stevie from Apple got in on
the act, giving us Lisa and leading to Billy G and Windows.

Regrettably much of the activity associated with OO is concentrated on GUIs or
components, but that is only one segment of the story. You don't need events to
trigger either the above file handling or interest calculations. (As an example
look at my recent message showing a class for a generic Line Sequential file -
doesn't it make sense to prove something once and just introduce instances of
it, regardless of how many different file formats you are using ?)

>
> So, to summarize (it's about bleedin' time, you prob'ly say!)...
>

No, I  enjoyed reading it <G>

>
> * The most effective use of COBOL will be for batch processing.  There is,
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

And OO COBOL overcomes the nightmarish problem of your 25,000 line source -
albeit, the invokes it does probably amount to the same (or more !), lines of
source tucked away in other invokeable classes ! But the important point is
that much of that invokeable stuff is reusable with other applications. The
same argument applies with non-OO, (either copyfiles or calling sub-programs),
but OO  provides some nifty handles to make the process so much easier. Example
:-

    Non-OO                                        OO
    Program A call Program B              Program A invoke Program B "method1"
    Program B call Program C              Program A invoke Program B "method2",

                                                            but "method2" is in
Program C - no big deal
                                                            if Program B  is
subordinate and inherits
                                                            from Program C

>
> * For green-screen online mainframe processing, the only game in town is
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> OK, forum, comment to your heart's content (but be gentle...)

Howzat for being gentle <G>

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

A small digression...
I would just point out that the works of Simula actually started way
back in the late 50's, eventually first shown to the public in 1962.
Albeit this was a pre release, as the major release was considered the
release of Simula-67 in just 1967. It might also be handy to know that
Simula was developed at the Norwegian Computing Center in Norway by two
Norwegian gentlemen Kristen Nygaard and Ole-Johan Dahl, that in the
later years has received a lot of prices and honours for their
development, there is yet no Nobel price for Information Technology, but
what ever prices there are that is comparable, they have got it. This is
where Xerox Parc and others picked up the foundation for later languages
like Smalltalk etc. If you want to read the full story, it is here:

	http://www.ifi.uio.no/~cim/sim_history.html

Gisle

> Lots of good stuff Gerry but there is one fundamental flaw in 
> your argument. OO
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
