###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future of object-oriented COBOL?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Gerry Wolfe <gerrydotwolfe@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-03-22T02:10:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Xns91D8D72421978gerrywolfesympaticoc@207.35.177.135>`
- **References:** `<c2ab4b04.0203191553.5deaa74b@posting.google.com> <3c9918f8_9@Usenet.com> <3C991FB3.EC98B300@shaw.ca> <Xns91D7D5EF97860gerrywolfesympaticoc@206.172.150.13> <3C99575F.71D83779@shaw.ca>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in
news:3C99575F.71D83779@shaw.ca: 

> 
> Gerry Wolfe wrote:
> 
snippin' myself here...
> 
> Lots of good stuff Gerry but there is one fundamental flaw in your
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Not disagreeing here...Simula... Simscript... brings back the days of 
academia...

My ramble is concerning the relationship between OO-COBOL and the big batch 
and online IBM mainframe, doing yer day-to-day boring commercial 
applications (which COBOL is all about).

For our typical batch (procedural) aplications, objects could be 
implemented but they are a solution to a problem that doesn't exit.  For 
decades, we have been CALLing common procedures or including them as 
copycodes.  Not sophisticated, but it works.

For the online environment, I would truly love to see an event-driven 
mainframe process that supported objects (whether text- or GUI- presented).  
But, it ain't gonna happen (at least in my lifetime, which goes back to 
punchcard and 1401 days).  There is a humungous investment in CICS, and it 
isn't going to go away.

> 
> It would be perfectly viable to produce an OO application without
> screens ! 
> 

Agreed again, but (IMHO) when related to COBOL i.e. the mainframe it is 
most applicable to the event-driven online screen environment.

> 
> And OO COBOL overcomes the nightmarish problem of your 25,000 line
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
..example snipped...

In the batch environment, not that much code is re-used.  That which is re-
used (record layouts, logic methods) is normally created as a COPY code or 
CALLed as a subroutine.  Again, agreed that OO invokes are more 
sophisticated, but the simple stuff seems to work well enuff...

> 
>> OK, forum, comment to your heart's content (but be gentle...)
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jimmy, Calgary AB

Thanx, that didn't hurt at all!  I can honestly say that this is the most 
objective forum I have seen for a long time in UseNet.  Folks here do a 
great job of discussing controversial topics in an objective manner without 
digressing to name-calling and commenting on posters' ancestry...

>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future of object-oriented COBOL?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-03-22T04:51:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C9AB725.8B19E215@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<c2ab4b04.0203191553.5deaa74b@posting.google.com> <3c9918f8_9@Usenet.com> <3C991FB3.EC98B300@shaw.ca> <Xns91D7D5EF97860gerrywolfesympaticoc@206.172.150.13> <3C99575F.71D83779@shaw.ca> <Xns91D8D72421978gerrywolfesympaticoc@207.35.177.135>`

```


Gerry Wolfe wrote:

> "
> My ramble is concerning the relationship between OO-COBOL and the big batch
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I understand where you are coming from, but could it be in that looking at OO
you are tying its possibilities into the 'known factor' - GUIs. Initially, I
wallowed hopelessly in OO, even just trying to get a handle on the 'event
thing'. I'm considerably more comfortable but still learning - and the learning
is the additional tricks that can be done with OO (and absolutely unrelated to
GUIs).

I've already mentioned the possibility of standard file classes. For an
installation that could mean FOUR PROGRAMS that handle files -  ISAM, Relative,
Sequential, LineSequential . "Ah, but we cover that with copyfiles......", and
when you make a change ? - umpteen programs to change/recompile - as opposed to
FOUR ! (The above would assume a standard way of holding the PrimeKey for all
ISAM files - Pete Dashwood can provide a solution to that, and at a push, (a
little bit of silver in the palm <G>) could come up with a mechanism for
Alternate Key handling.).

2 - Tables - A good feature of  COBOL, except that you have to specify a
maximum size, which is allocated whether or not you use it. The alternative
Collections/Dictionaries - still on the drawing board, although versions
currently available from Fujitsu and Micro Focus. Now I'm sticking my neck out
<G> - when our J4 team comes up with the COBOL spec, I'm willing to bet it
knocks spots off the other language collections/maps/linked lists etc. When
developers realize just how flexible this feature can be - their response will
be "WOW!". Somethig you may not be aware of - other langugages are totally
object driven (you can't 'look' at the object) whereas COBOL will likely
include collections containing either intrinsics (pic x/pic 9) or true objects
for screen display.

>
> For the online environment, I would truly love to see an event-driven
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> isn't going to go away.

Dream on. At either the mainframe level or PCs, a "GUI" approach just isn't
going to happen. I can think of a time when a J4 member perked at a comment
about GUIs with a, "Hmmm, that's interesting......". I responded, "Forget it".
Not because I'm anti GUIs - I'D LUV to see COBOL GUIs. But the fact is, if J4
got serious and sat down NOW to specify GUIs (let's say using Windows as a
model), having got the specification out - and the BIG IF - assuming compiler
vendors bought into it - which developers would actually use the GUI module ?

Developers are already committed to so many other tools, SP2, ScreenIO, VB,
Java, Delphi, calls to Windows APIs etc. - nobody in their right mind is going
to drop a humungous amount of code already written in one of these other
tools/languages, just to be 'neat' and write something in-built to COBOL.
(Meanwhile of course there are some of us using either POWERCOBOL or Net
Express which do have GUI classes - but we are very much in the minority, I
would suggest)

Got to be careful here - because the above reflects the PC Windows approach -
one operating system. Agreed we've also got Unix, Linux and Apple, but the big
money is on Microsoft at the moment. Given the different models of big iron
available, we are talking several different O/S's - and I don't really see them
getting together on this - do you ?

Introduction of COBOL GUIs would suffer the same fate as Report Writer - and
that was caused by an on-again, off-again cycle, until eventually, to be truly
COBOL 2002 compliant, J4 determined vendors must  provide R/W. Who is going to
use it - developers have already made the decision to write using other tools -
and again they aren't going to switch. (Sadly R/W is a good tool arriving at
the wrong time at the wrong place)..

>
> In the batch environment, not that much code is re-used.  That which is re-
> used (record layouts, logic methods) is normally created as a COPY code or
> CALLed as a subroutine.  Again, agreed that OO invokes are more
> sophisticated, but the simple stuff seems to work well enuff...

It really comes down to "What if we had a tool which did this....". In the
mainframe world, until you have at least an outline spec, you really can't
start to let your imagination ramble can you ? What you mention about copy
files is just as relevant to us PC users. And if you are either copying or
calling, and change the routine - exactly same problem with PCs - but OO really
can alleviate a lot of this problem, particularly where the change made is fine
tuning of an invoked class, which in turn is invoking private methods within
Self.. (The words 'Public' and 'Private' are not part of the COBOL spec - but I
expect you get what I mean).

> Folks here do a
> great job of discussing controversial topics in an objective manner without
> digressing to name-calling and commenting on posters' ancestry...

That's OK you SOB and seein' you used the word earlier, are you  a bleedin'
Limey ? <G>

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future of object-oriented COBOL?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-03-22T14:19:40+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a7fe9s$6lg$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<c2ab4b04.0203191553.5deaa74b@posting.google.com> <3c9918f8_9@Usenet.com> <3C991FB3.EC98B300@shaw.ca> <Xns91D7D5EF97860gerrywolfesympaticoc@206.172.150.13> <3C99575F.71D83779@shaw.ca> <Xns91D8D72421978gerrywolfesympaticoc@207.35.177.135> <3C9AB725.8B19E215@shaw.ca>`

```

On 21-Mar-2002, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:

> I understand where you are coming from, but could it be in that looking at
> OO
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> GUIs).

The complications of GUIs make it easy to move to OO.  Having lots of little
components make OO very attractive.

But business mainframe systems weren't designed for GUI.  And interdependent
components are not attractive when our biggest problems are
interdependencies.   So enterprise systems do not use OO CoBOL to replace
their old style CoBOL.  I would LOVE to have sufficient arguments to be able
to set up a pilot program to do enterprise business in OO CoBOL.  That would
be very fun to work on.  But I am not alone in my failure to come up with
such arguments - as nobody else does it either.

Mainframes DO have some integration, where the cost/benefit works. 
Databases are like objects.  Communications are interdependent.

I don't see OO CoBOL being implemented from the mainframe - but it can sneak
in from below or from without.

We can push it to be used by the web teams who are working on the Unix
machines.  We can push it to be used by PC people who use SQL to access the
big iron databases.   And we can tell vendors that we are attracted to their
business suites that are written in OO CoBOL.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future of object-oriented COBOL?

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-03-22T17:45:59+00:00

- **References:** `<c2ab4b04.0203191553.5deaa74b@posting.google.com> <3c9918f8_9@Usenet.com> <3C991FB3.EC98B300@shaw.ca> <Xns91D7D5EF97860gerrywolfesympaticoc@206.172.150.13> <3C99575F.71D83779@shaw.ca> <Xns91D8D72421978gerrywolfesympaticoc@207.35.177.135> <3C9AB725.8B19E215@shaw.ca> <a7fe9s$6lg$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3C9B66FF.90509204@shaw.ca>`

```

On 22-Mar-2002, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:

> Howard Brazee wrote:
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> message.

I did, and have reflected on similar messages in the past.  He doesn't say
anything that contradicts my quoted statement above.  His message was all
about the advantages of non GUI objects.

My interest is in selling management in trying out OO for enterprise CoBOL
functions.  (because it would be fun)  Management would much rather avoid
the complications that make it easy to move to OO.  Isolation of a program
or job is what makes their life easier - integration is what makes their job
harder.  OO tries to be both - but these are opposites - the selling job is
hard.   When they don't see any choice they create their new system using
new technologies.   But they see how reliable their web systems are compared
to their enterprise systems.  They see how easy it is for someone to change
some little thing making the web unavailable.   They don't want to risk a
working enterprise system this way without seeing a significant return on
their investment.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future of object-oriented COBOL?

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **Message-ID:** `<3C9B7C38.D0AB7E25@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<c2ab4b04.0203191553.5deaa74b@posting.google.com> <3c9918f8_9@Usenet.com> <3C991FB3.EC98B300@shaw.ca> <Xns91D7D5EF97860gerrywolfesympaticoc@206.172.150.13> <3C99575F.71D83779@shaw.ca> <Xns91D8D72421978gerrywolfesympaticoc@207.35.177.135> <3C9AB725.8B19E215@shaw.ca> <a7fe9s$6lg$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3C9B66FF.90509204@shaw.ca> <a7fqcn$qnf$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```


Howard Brazee wrote:

> On 22-Mar-2002, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:
>
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> their investment.

Yes, I appreciate the problem you illustrate. Fortunately at the time I got into
this racket, management, (me plus others) were young 'uns with our minds wide
open to new innovations, (as both Pete and Don will attest - it was so bloody
exciting !)  - although we did resist hard sells like ICL's PROSPER which was a
mainframe spreadsheet - no point if we couldn't get the damned bean counters
interested !

Real reason for this message - I goofed on an earlier message (wish I could get
my mind and fingers together <G>). I mentioned the four classes for file
handling and they were the only ones which required change. FALSE. Once you have
them designed and fool proof they NEVER change; that's assuming you make them
generic like I suggested.

The changes occur in your Business Logic classes, when you alter copyfiles..
Without getting too detailed, the full copyfile record only applies to say
EditCustomers class - where you NEED the full record.. The other program
affected by the full record is the CustomerDBI (see below).

Other programs which only require to pick off elements of a record, say get the
telephone number or post/zipcode would not be affected - unless you changed the
sizes for these specific fields.(I think you might probably agree, *most*"
changes to record copyfiles fall into the category of additional fields, rather
than re-categorizing existing ones).

Briefly :

    (1) CustomerEdit (2) CustomerDBI  inherits from  (3) ISAMGeneric class

Edit - invoke CustomerDBI "getRecord" using PrimeKey returning CustomerRecord
(full copyfile) - (1) goes directly to (3), i.e. bypasses (2). Class (3) just
contains a "return with record varying from 1 to x depending on ws-recordSize"

Print - invoke CustomerDBI "getTelephoneNumber" using PrimeKey returning
ws-TelephoneNumber

(1) invokes (2) which in turn invokes (3) invoke super "getRecord" using
PrimeKey returning CustomerRecord (full copyfile) the full record being returned
to (2). Now . Class (2)  'extracts' the Telephone Number and returns it to (1).

Hope that's not too ambiguous.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future of object-oriented COBOL?

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2002-03-22T20:08:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a7g2n6$g95$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<c2ab4b04.0203191553.5deaa74b@posting.google.com> <3c9918f8_9@Usenet.com> <3C991FB3.EC98B300@shaw.ca> <Xns91D7D5EF97860gerrywolfesympaticoc@206.172.150.13> <3C99575F.71D83779@shaw.ca> <Xns91D8D72421978gerrywolfesympaticoc@207.35.177.135> <3C9AB725.8B19E215@shaw.ca> <a7fe9s$6lg$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3C9B66FF.90509204@shaw.ca> <a7fqcn$qnf$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3C9B7C38.D0AB7E25@shaw.ca>`

```

On 22-Mar-2002, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:

>     (1) CustomerEdit (2) CustomerDBI  inherits from  (3) ISAMGeneric class
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> Hope that's not too ambiguous.

No, it is interesting though.  But just knowledge I put on my back burner
without expecting to have an opportunity to use it.

The question is - how does an enterprise get started in something like this?

Nobody throws out their existing enterprise system unless they have to. 
When they do replace a system it is because they bought a product such as a
database that requires it.   Existing mainframe CoBOL programmers adjust to
new databases relatively easily.  Adjusting to OO is harder - but maybe if
they do enough java they will be more ready to think OO.

So what does the enterprise do?   If they don't have to rewrite - nothing. 
If they do have to rewrite - they buy a core package from a vendor, use
their old language, or use a new language.   Since nowadays they have Web
people on site, they will look into Java.  If they already have CoBOL
programmers on site, they consider CoBOL.  (I don't know of anybody
considering CoBOL who do NOT have CoBOL already programmers).  They will
look at tools they have such as Endevor.   This is a migration tool
(migration control is very important to them) - but I don't see that it
would work well with OO.

If they go with OO, they would have to go to the web people to get testing
and migration methodologies developed.  So far, what I have seen with web
tests & migrations is that they aren't nearly as mature as mainframe tests &
migrations.   If the current OO people are designing the system, they will
probably select the language they are familiar with.  We don't want non OO
people designing the system.

So we need either:
1.   Packages written in OO CoBOL or which can at least use CoBOL components
with their OO system.
2.  Web designers using OO CoBOL, so that when we expand their roles into
our place - they are using our tool of choice.

What I don't see happening is having a request for a new or modified
program, and having programmers decide to write this application in OO CoBOL
on the mainframe.   Management won't allow for that.   It might be useful
occasionally - maybe write an OO FTP interface, or an OO module to read VSAM
files or something.  But unless we get a whole system like that, training
and methodologies cost more than their benefit.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future of object-oriented COBOL?

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-03-23T07:00:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C9C275E.36EBEDDC@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<c2ab4b04.0203191553.5deaa74b@posting.google.com> <3c9918f8_9@Usenet.com> <3C991FB3.EC98B300@shaw.ca> <Xns91D7D5EF97860gerrywolfesympaticoc@206.172.150.13> <3C99575F.71D83779@shaw.ca> <Xns91D8D72421978gerrywolfesympaticoc@207.35.177.135> <3C9AB725.8B19E215@shaw.ca> <a7fe9s$6lg$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3C9B66FF.90509204@shaw.ca> <a7fqcn$qnf$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3C9B7C38.D0AB7E25@shaw.ca> <YzSm8.1250$se.105416@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```


398199821 Warren Simmons wrote:

> James,
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> programs OR in OS enhancement?

> <snippage>

>
> > Real reason for this message - I goofed on an earlier message (wish I
…[45 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Jimmy, Calgary AB

Warren,

How best to summarize this ? Mine is one approach which could be one of many.
You don't need to be told that using copyfiles for repetitive routines or
calling sub-programs represents the sensible way go in non-OO. We all know that
there are things we find ourselves typing, over and over again. "Hey ! I
remember doing this before, but with just a slight twist to the code" - so can
we get some mileage out of it, (reusability) ? Yes if we can call that piece of
code with different parameters depending upon which program  is doing the
calling. Non-OO or OO - if we can establish that particular piece of code and
prove it, it can constantly be re-used without additional testing. Incorporate
the code into an existing Business Logic program, and as Howard points out, (his
bugaboo about maintenance), now you have to test each program affected.

Let's stick with COBOL files for the moment, (some of this can be 'slightly'
paralleled if you are using SQL). Non-OO I would say to myself, "Damn, I've got
to include copyfiles for FileX and when it comes to accessing the file, "What's
the syntax again for that particular VERB,and what did I call the different
elements of the PrimeKey ?". Similarly, if I now include perform PARAGRAPHS for
each of the file verbs they again become an integral part of  'this' program
which has to be tested for accuracy - and each time I have code or a paragraph
for a file verb - there's the possibility of either syntax or logic errors,
demanding each entry be tested thoroughly.

So taking just an ISAM file - the verbs are :-

        Open Input
        Open Output
        Open I-O
        Start
        Read using PrimeKey
        Read Next
        Read Previous (?) - I think that one is non-standard
        Delete
        Write
        Rewrite
        Close

(I think I've got them all <G>).

So whether or not I'm talking Customer File or Product File the above are common
routines to both. So now I could, (a) non-OO set up a separate called program
per file or, (b) with OO a separate File Class. The problem with non-OO is
keeping a handle on the file-status in a called program - not so, with OO. I've
got to test access to the Customer and Product Files as each has a separate
program for access. At this stage both file/class programs are unique and can
contain the specific copyfile information.

Each time a large installation goes this route, thorough testing is required for
EACH  new file format introduced.

Now apart from Customer and Product files holding separate contents, the means
of accessing them is identical. Can we get additional mileage by making the
access generic. In OO - Yes. Do you recall my trying to illustrate INVOKE 'NEW'
?

My solution (and by no means the final word), is to create a class GenericISAM.
This contains methods, (liken it to paragraphs or the eventual User Defined
Functions) for each of the file verbs above, with each method having its
appropriate parameters and results. How do we handle Customer and Product
records - the first may be 150 characters and the second only 56. One Solution
:-

FD  Data-File
    record varying from 1 to 1500 characters
       depending on ws-RecordSize.
01  Data-record.
      Data-PrimeKey               pic x(20).   *> Assumes you can get all
PrimeKeys
                                                                *> in first 20
characters
      Data-RestOfRecord         pic x(1480).

The 1500 would be an arbitrary figure picked by an installation - even if it
subsequently had to be upped to 1700, the only program requiring change would be
this ISAMGeneric. Using standard COBOL syntax we control the 'true' size by
ws-RecordSize.

Back to the "NEW" bit. We have a Business class program that needs access to
both Customers and Products - so :-

    invoke ISAMGeneric "new" returning os-CustomerFile
    invoke ISAMGeneric "new" returning os-ProductFile

This is done in the FACTORY (or construction area) of ISAMGeneric.
We have handles to the two files (object references) from os-Customer and
os-Products. We "called" ISAMGeneric twice, but only ONE copy is in memory with
TWO INSTANCES. Part of the initialization is to pass the full filepathname plus
the particular record-size.

In the OBJECT SECTION I have the following information, a separate 'copy' for
each of the above :-

File-status                    ??                                ??
Filename                        c:\customers.dat        c:\products.dat
ws-RecordSize                150                        56

The remainder of the OBJECT SECTION contains methods to use the individual file
verbs. So now I can invoke from the Business program :-

    invoke os-CustomerFile "readPrimeKey" using ws-PrimeKey
                returning ws-CustomerInfo

    invoke os-ProductFile "readNext" returning ws-ProductInfo

Both the above invokes 'hit' the one program in memory - ISAMGeneric.
Off hand I've no idea what the maximum instances of one class can be, (but you
can bet there is a limitation, probably more in terms of memory available ).
Nevertheless I could create 50 or 100 instances, say, each giving back a handle
like Customer and Products. (God forbid, if somebody wants 100 files in one
program, I think I'll snooze into my dotage).

ISAMGeneric is written ONCE and proven ONCE - and can be used for umpteen
different sized ISAM files. (The objective is to put Howard and the rest of the
Maintenance team out of work. Now I *know" why he's such a hard sell on OO <G>)

What I've been describing above looks like this :-

    [CustomerOrders]----------->[Customers - ISAMGeneric(1)]
    [CustomerOrders]----------->[Products - ISAMGeneric(2)]

and what I was originally trying to clarify in my correction was :-

    [CustomerOrders]----->[CustomerDBI]-------->[ISAMGeneric (1)]
    [CustomerOrders]----->[ProductsDBI]--------->[ISAMGeneric (2)]

Does that help ?.

The above is the upside of programming in OO. The downside is that I just don't
think OO COBOL is really going to take off, (the following is cynicism not
sarcasm)  :-

a) As yet no viable mainframe OO compiler

b) Mainframers have convinced themselves that OO has no relevance to them -
other than if they start getting into the Web. So called batch processing
systems are
static, chug along nicely and don't require medicare - that's why large
installations have 100 or more programmers (Bill's quote) - what the hell are
they doing if everything is honky-dorey and they aren't changing or developing
new applications ?

c) CICS, LE and God knows what else, which appear not to have much to do with
COBOL syntax,  seem to reign supreme. (Recall somebody writing he wrote in COBOL
but never used a file verb - instead he was using O/S support).

d) In a sense liken your mainframe O/S to what goes on with APIs in Windows -
It's my guess APIs are used by a minority, although the 'majority' eventually
use them via other packages, COBOL/SP2/ScreenIO GUIs, Visual Basic, Java, etc.

e) Meanwhile the lurkers from the vendors are reading all this. Next time they
go get a coffee and have a chat with their compatriots, "See I told you we
should ignore OO. Here's another message in CLC which says our customers just
aren't interested").

Grrrh !

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future of object-oriented COBOL?

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** Gerry Wolfe <gerrydotwolfe@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-03-24T21:06:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Xns91DBA3C7288EBgerrywolfesympaticoc@207.35.177.134>`
- **References:** `<c2ab4b04.0203191553.5deaa74b@posting.google.com> <3c9918f8_9@Usenet.com> <3C991FB3.EC98B300@shaw.ca> <Xns91D7D5EF97860gerrywolfesympaticoc@206.172.150.13> <3C99575F.71D83779@shaw.ca> <Xns91D8D72421978gerrywolfesympaticoc@207.35.177.135> <3C9AB725.8B19E215@shaw.ca> <a7fe9s$6lg$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3C9B66FF.90509204@shaw.ca> <a7fqcn$qnf$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3C9B7C38.D0AB7E25@shaw.ca> <YzSm8.1250$se.105416@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3C9C275E.36EBEDDC@shaw.ca>`

```
Follow-up to Warren & Jimmy's combined posts below.

Discalaimer: The environment I am refering to is the IBM mainframe... this 
is where the majority of COBOL programming is done.  I've personally used 
all flavors of IBM COBOL code (actually go back to 1401 & S/360 assembler) 
and have used MicroFocus & Fujitsu (which is my fav...)

Another observation: OO is a state of mind as well as a programming syntax 
convention.  Many mainframe folks have been emulating (the main) OO 
concepts via existing COPYCODE and CALL functions for a long time.  To 
regress, yours truly has been writing "structured" code in even assembler 
long before the concept was introduced (and I learned it from others).  
Just because a machine language doesn't support a DO... UNTIL construct 
doesn't mean you can't emulate it...  You can adhere to the 10 commandments 
without religious beliefs etc...

> 
> Now apart from Customer and Product files holding separate contents,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Maybe "identical" within the PROCEDURE DIVISION of a program, but not in 
how they are defined, created, etc... more to follow
> 
> My solution (and by no means the final word), is to create a class
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

The concept of the above ISAMGeneric doesn't work (well) with IBM 
compilers... completely impractical because of the underlining differences 
between the physical file storage methods and access methods.

1. There is a difference in the manner in which variable- and fixed-length 
files are physically stored.  For a variable-blocked file (VB) there is a 
pointer at the beginning of the physical block which identifies the length 
of the entire block.  Immediately following, there is a pointer which 
identifies the length of first record.  These "record length" pointers are 
repeated for each record within the physical block.  The record-access 
routines provided by IBM resolve these automatically -- when you define the 
file as being a VB file (which is (normally) captured from the RECORDING 
MODE clause on the FD which created the file).  If you try to access a 
fixed-blocked (FB) file using an FD referring to a VB file (or vice-versa a 
VB file using an FD referring to an FB file) you will end up with a system-
level abend or failed FILE-STATUS.  No way around this, except as defining 
*all* files (including fixed-length ones) as variable-length.  This adds 
processing overhead which would be unacceptable in the real world.

Going a step further, VSAM is an order of magitude more "advanced" than the 
QSAM VB/FB example above.  Not even close to how VB and FB files are stored 
or the IBM-supplied access methods.

2.  Accessing a file requires a SELECT clause, an FD (File Definition) 
clause, and the various I/O routines such as OPEN INPUT etc.  In the IBM 
world, the linking of a logical file within a program and the physical file 
itself is done thru the SELECT clause

 SELECT YADDA-FILE ASSIGN TO 'YADDADD'

where the YADADD is required in the JCL:

//YADDADD DD DSN=YADDA.WHATEVER,other parameters.

The YADDA-FILE and YADDADD portion is not a variable.  If you wanted to 
make this invocation a generic (OO) routine, you would need to code a 
SELECT statement for every potential file.

This would add some additional issues... if you wanted to use YADDA as both 
an input and output files (e.g. you are reading it, updating, and writing 
as a new version) you would require two separate versions... one pointing 
to the existing (input) version and one pointing to the new (output) 
version.  These would again require separate SELECT clauses, as well as 
separate FD definitions (each with unique internal and external filenames).  
Again, the OO implelentation of COBOL does not allow these to be changed as 
parameters, they would have to be uniquely defined.

To summarize: If you have 500 files in a system (having both input and 
output versions) you would have to specify 1000 separate SELECT and FD 
entries, and include run-time logic to choose which one.  Imagine the 
overhead (and don't think that 500 unique files for an application is 
large, because it isn't).

The suggested ISAMGeneric function would have to incorporate every one of 
these 1000 combinations, as well as logic to choose which pair of these 
1000 combinations to use.  I (personally) wouldn't allow this level of 
overhead in a production environment.

3. The OO approach has a lot of merit.  What a lot of advanced packages 
contain are CALLed routines for each file.  As an example, suppose a file 
is called "AMNA" -- in this case, the name of a file in a commonly-used 
card processing system which contains Name&Address info for the card-
holder.  There would be a routine called AMNAAB (used to access the AMNA 
file in an "access batch" environment).  This routine would contain 
numerous SELECT clauses, the FD statement, and all of the I/O access 
methods previously identified... OPEN INPUT... thru READ whatever thru 
CLOSE).  When an application program CALLs the routine, it specifies the 
access mode to be used (OPEN INPUT, OUTPUT, I/O or DYNAMIC) as well as the 
operation desired (READ NEXT etc.).  This access method routine would be 
coded once, and called by any programming requiring access to that file.

Based on the access-method parameter specified (I/P, I/O, O/P or DYNamic) 
the routine chooses the FD at initiation time.  The choice is stored, and 
subsequent operations are validated against it (i.e. you can't do a WRITE 
against a file you opened as I/P).  For an I/P file, AMNA could reference a 
DD statement called AMNAIP, for the O/P file a separate DD statment called 
AMNAOP, for I/O one called AMNAIO and so on. Each would point to a separate 
physical dataset.

Once the first CALLed access method is put together and debugged, it is 
then cloned for subsequent files.  The only thing that has to be changed on 
the new object is the filename (e.g. change AMNAAB to (for example) AMBSAB 
all the way thru the routine -- done with a generic "change" command in the 
editor), the record length in the FD portion, and the definition of the key 
portion in the I/O for direct-access operations.  This typically takes less 
than a half-hour and is considered to be trivial overhead.

This headspace has been around for decades, long before objects were 
conceived.

> 
> The above is the upside of programming in OO. The downside is that I
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

AFAIK, IBM's current version support the OO syntax.  Just hasn't taken off 
in the world in which I work...

> 
> b) Mainframers have convinced themselves that OO has no relevance to
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Batch systems are *anything but static*.  What is static is (for example) 
the I/O access routine that has been touted as the raison d'etre for OO 
code.  BTW, the part that *wouldn't* change would be the access method 
object identified above as an OO candidate.

Going back to the credit card application I referred to previously...

1. The most typical change would be (for example) interest rate tables, or 
perhaps parameters.  Key word here is "table" -- this is a copycode change.  
Library management packages (e.g. ENDEVOR, CHANGEMAN) know which programs 
use which tables and can automatically flag them and even recompile as 
needed.

2. Suppose we're introducing a new kind of card... same as everything else 
except there are a few things different.

Typical existing code could be:

EVALUTE CARD-TYPE
    	WHEN CLASSIC-VISA
    	    	PERFORM INTEREST-RATE-CALC-CLASSIC-VISA
    	WHEN AEROGOLD-VISA
    	    	PERFORM INTEREST-RATE-CALC-AEROGOLD-VISA
    	WHEN LOW-INTEREST-VISA
    	    	PERFORM INTEREST-RATE-CAL-LOW-INTEREST-VISA
    	WHEN OTHER
    	    	PERFORM deep-doodah-routine
END-EVALUATE

So we introduce a new VISA card, say "no interest" (that'll be the day!!!)

All we have to do is add an additional WHEN condition to the EVALUATE, 
clone the PERFORMed routine for the existing routine which is closest to 
the new one, and make the mods to it.

Using simulators, the only testing that has to be done is to get into the 
EVALUATE statement, enter a CARD-TYPE value which will cause the new 
(cloned) code to be executed, and trace the logic of the new PERFORMed 
routine to make sure the intermediate results are what are expected.

We add (say) 100 lines of new code to an existing 10,000 line program.  
Only the new code has to be changed... shouldn't take more than a couple of 
hours.  <<OK... let's be paranoid... we have to go thru a regression test 
using existing testbases before the mod gets accepted for production.  
Whether the change was done as above or via a change to the original 
object, no real difference>>.

> 
> c) CICS, LE and God knows what else, which appear not to have much to
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

CICS and LE are not in the least related.  CICS is an IBM-supplied ($$$$) 
routine to handle screen & transaction & event processing.  LE is a "new" 
run-time library of routines (I/O, date-handling, etc.).  Used by CICS, 
batch, common routines used by virtually all languages supported by IBM.

CICS is a teleprocessing monitor.  When you are referencing files, the 
files are assigned to the actual CICS region the application is running in, 
not to the actual application program.  The CICS region looks like a batch 
job, when executed it "brings up a CICS region".  The batch job which 
brings up the region has a JCL "DD" statement allocating physical files to 
it.  Once the region is running, no other job can update the physical file 
(depending on options, some can read them).  For that reason, you cannot 
have a CICS application program that directly accesses the physical file 
(because you could/would have simultaneous updates of a record).  Instead, 
the file accessing of a file assigned to this online region cannot use 
existing COBOL syntax commands.  Instead, you issue a CALL to CICS 
requesting the access, and CICS himself resolves the file locking etc. 
needed to prevent simultaneous updating, etc.

That's why CICS file access doesn't look like COBOL.

> d) In a sense liken your mainframe O/S to what goes on with APIs in
> Windows - It's my guess APIs are used by a minority, although the
> 'majority' eventually use them via other packages, COBOL/SP2/ScreenIO
> GUIs, Visual Basic, Java, etc. 
> 

There is a conceptual simularity between CICS CALLs and API calls, but 
(!!!) I would say that the Win98 interface is much more sophisticated than 
CICS -- it's pretty, CICS is alphanumeric.  Then again, it's all 
relative... which is more sophisticated, a Ferrari F1 car (which carries 
little weight, but does it quickly) or a White Freightliner (which carries 
(say) 1000 times the weight but at only 1/4 of the speed).  CICS running on 
a mainframe may only support "green screen" text, but can do it for 10,000+ 
users with <1sec response time.

> e) Meanwhile the lurkers from the vendors are reading all this. Next
> time they go get a coffee and have a chat with their compatriots, "See
> I told you we should ignore OO. Here's another message in CLC which
> says our customers just aren't interested").
> 

Different strokes for different folks.  When you're running a big host box, 
you gotta support (say) 10K users with <1sec response time with (for 
example) the current balance on your credit card account.  Doesn't need 
fancy display, couldn't support it.

Just my $0.02 worth...

rgds to all, g.
```

```
Gerry Wolfe wrote:

> Follow-up to Warren & Jimmy's combined posts below. <snip>

You know, there was a time when I just sat here in my naivetï¿½ and did my own
thing with the COBOL compiler that was available. No communication with the
larger COBOL community.

Eventually discovered the Web and then comp.lang.cobol. Thought that was a good
thing. Now I'm no longer so sure. Whichever way to try to dice it or slice it,
17 years to achieve an as yet incomplete standard, is one Royal Balls Up ! Sad
part is, the developers don't really care, and wont fall over themselves with
enthusiasm when COBOL 2002 actually happens.

While others have addressed mainframe issues, (they are talking to their peers
'in the know'), your message illustrating just some of the hurdles with
mainframe file handling, (albeit Bill qualified the dynamic handling), shows
there really is no such animal as Standard COBOL - and that being the case
there aint gonna be an animal called Standard OO COBOL.

They look alike but it's really Mainframe COBOL and PC COBOL - and perhaps they
should have different standards !

Maybe it's time to give this site a miss.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

- **From:** Gerry Wolfe <gerrydotwolfe@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-03-30T15:39:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Xns91E16C3934E02gerrywolfesympaticoc@207.35.177.134>`
- **References:** `<c2ab4b04.0203191553.5deaa74b@posting.google.com> <3c9918f8_9@Usenet.com> <3C991FB3.EC98B300@shaw.ca> <Xns91D7D5EF97860gerrywolfesympaticoc@206.172.150.13> <3C99575F.71D83779@shaw.ca> <Xns91D8D72421978gerrywolfesympaticoc@207.35.177.135> <3C9AB725.8B19E215@shaw.ca> <a7fe9s$6lg$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3C9B66FF.90509204@shaw.ca> <a7fqcn$qnf$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3C9B7C38.D0AB7E25@shaw.ca> <YzSm8.1250$se.105416@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3C9C275E.36EBEDDC@shaw.ca> <Xns91DBA3C7288EBgerrywolfesympaticoc@207.35.177.134> <3C9FE4C6.3ACD9B94@shaw.ca> <Xns91DFAC4A48430gerrywolfesympaticoc@206.172.150.13> <3CA536E0.39EAD96F@optonline.net>`

```
Liam Devlin <LiamD@optonline.net> wrote in
news:3CA536E0.39EAD96F@optonline.net: 

> Gerry Wolfe wrote:
<snips>
>> 
>> Bill: thanks for the update in variability (is that a word?) of the
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Make all your files VB?

That is not really practical...
VB and FB files have different physical representations... even if a fixed-
length record is written on a VB file, there are additional fields 
(physically) added to the block -- a block length word at the beginning of 
the block, and a record length word at the beginning of each record.  This 
is physically different from FB files, and adds additional overheads.  It 
would be unacceptable in a normal prod'n environment.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future of object-oriented COBOL?

_(thread depth: 18)_

- **From:** Liam Devlin <LiamD@optonline.net>
- **Date:** 2002-03-31T22:49:54+00:00

```
I understand; but if they were all VB, then they'd all have the same

everything for the max? 

I don't think the additional overhead would be a factor in the shops
where I've worked.

changing the filename, maybe it also allows changing some other
parameters, as well, e.g., LRECL, DSORG? Not very hard to do with
Assembler, maybe REXX, too.
```

- **From:** dashwood@enternet.co.nz (Peter E. C. Dashwood)

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3638c46.0203292311.5fbaedd9@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<c2ab4b04.0203191553.5deaa74b@posting.google.com> <3C99575F.71D83779@shaw.ca> <Xns91D8D72421978gerrywolfesympaticoc@207.35.177.135> <3C9AB725.8B19E215@shaw.ca> <a7fe9s$6lg$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3C9B66FF.90509204@shaw.ca> <a7fqcn$qnf$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3C9B7C38.D0AB7E25@shaw.ca> <YzSm8.1250$se.105416@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3C9C275E.36EBEDDC@shaw.ca> <Xns91DBA3C7288EBgerrywolfesympaticoc@207.35.177.134> <3C9FE4C6.3ACD9B94@shaw.ca>`

```
Jimmy,

I am on the road at the moment and haven't had time to follow this
group but I managed to quickly see your post.

Comments below...

"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message 

> You know, there was a time when I just sat here in my naivetï¿½ and did my own
> thing with the COBOL compiler that was available. No communication with the
> larger COBOL community.
>
Many people are still in that boat.
 
> Eventually discovered the Web and then comp.lang.cobol. Thought that was a good thing. Now I'm no longer so sure. Whichever way to try to dice it or slice it,
> 17 years to achieve an as yet incomplete standard, is one Royal Balls Up ! Sad
> part is, the developers don't really care, and wont fall over themselves with
> enthusiasm when COBOL 2002 actually happens.
> 
Man you really are depressed by this, aren't you? There is no point in
blaming the Newsgroup for the actions of J4. Or the COBOL Vendors.
What you are saying is that through this group you have come to
realise what a shambles the COBOL Standard is. I feel exactly the same
but I certainly won't shoot the messenger...<G>

> While others have addressed mainframe issues, (they are talking to their peers
> 'in the know'), your message illustrating just some of the hurdles with
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Don't be too sure. And even if you are right and there isn't, will
that stop you from using it? I intend to continue using the fantastic
tools provided by Fujitsu whether J4 stamps it as "standard" or not.
Because it works for me and does what I want it to...

 
> They look alike but it's really Mainframe COBOL and PC COBOL - and perhaps they
> should have different standards !
>

Despite a lot of pedantic detail being bandied about here, the FACT is
that COBOL CAN be written to run on the mainframe OR the PC (or both),
without change. I know because I've done it. The PC is an excellent
development platform for mainframe COBOL. (There are a number of sound
technical reasons why this is so, which I won't go into here.)
 
> Maybe it's time to give this site a miss.

Well, that is really a completely separate issue. You've been coming
here a long time and stuck with it through thick and thin. Had
laughter and abuse and survived it all. Why would you quit now?

I suggest that you are depressed over the state of Standard COBOL and
what you read here isn't making you feel any better. Right?

Think on this...CLC is an information exchange place. No-one ever
guaranteed you would only hear good news. And no-one ever guaranteed
that everything posted here is ACCURATE.

If you stop watching the TV News, and cancel the subscription to your
local newspaper, you will probably start feeling much better about the
World in general <G>. Nevertheless, you will not be as well-informed
as you were.

The fate of the COBOL language will not be decided by CLC. However,
what goes on here COULD influence it. You can be part of that, but
only if you are here...<G>

Don't give up the ship!

Pete.
```

```
What about protecting data from users?  When "the user" wants access to
stuff imebedded in a data base that is part of my system, I want to be able
to control what they do to that data.  An oop method is by far the safest
way to give them that access.

is
> hard.   When they don't see any choice they create their new system using
> new technologies.   But they see how reliable their web systems are
compared
> to their enterprise systems.  They see how easy it is for someone to
change
> some little thing making the web unavailable.   They don't want to risk a
> working enterprise system this way without seeing a significant return on
> their investment.
```

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
news:a7ng7g$g7r$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On 22-Mar-2002, "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> > to control what they do to that data.  An oop method is by far the
safest
> > way to give them that access.
>
> Right now, the database administrators control this.  What is the
advantage
> of moving this control to the programmer?
>
>
> I thought of another way of getting OO into the mainframe - someone
> (preferably IBM) providing a good suite of objects for enterprise use.

Perhaps I am in a different situation than you ... the data base
administarator is even more of a problem than the programmer.  I sell a
complete system. I go in and supply computer, data base, programs,
everything.  When it stops running, they blame me.

Without OOP, I either give them nothing (the internals are secret) or I give
them everything (provide file layouts and source code examples).  With OOP,
I can give them methods at the object level ... they can get at the data,
but only by retreiving it via my code.  Thay can also update data, but again
only by using my code.  Since it is object code, they cannot change it.

It would seem to me that even within a large organization, the same
principal would apply.

Donald
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future of object-oriented COBOL?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-03-22T08:41:09-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qvGm8.23769$mZ3.2170762@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<c2ab4b04.0203191553.5deaa74b@posting.google.com> <3c9918f8_9@Usenet.com> <3C991FB3.EC98B300@shaw.ca> <Xns91D7D5EF97860gerrywolfesympaticoc@206.172.150.13> <3C99575F.71D83779@shaw.ca> <Xns91D8D72421978gerrywolfesympaticoc@207.35.177.135>`

```
I would disagree with both the major points you made.

Firts of all, I do not see the only future of Cobol as mainframe.  That is
simple egocentricity. I have been using Cobol on Micro's since 1980 or so,
and I know of huge numbers of Vars that do the same.  Sure there are lots of
micky mouse small bussiness systems written in basic and such, but the Cobol
ones are the ones standing the tests of time.

Secondly, in distributed systems running on micro's, OOP's strength has
little to do with GUI's. It has to do with shared code for the bussiness
portion of processing.  For example, I just finished a re-write on a var
system.  A good deal of that code is common with the accounts receievable,
and that portion that is not is actually a data entry system for
receiveables.

All the common files are now OOP.  The "bottom portion" of the code
(customer/contract/product code management) is absolutely common.  Contract
editing, pricing, tax calculation, cuctomer entry and editing, and even the
printing of an invoice is the same code, regardless of whether being done at
the cash register level, or at the warehouse level, or on the final batch
processing of the bulk thirty day receiveables left at the end.  The code is
neater and cleaner than anything I could write twenty years back, and I am
able to maintain a system far more complex and sophistocated as a result.

What you are saying may be true for extremely large and narrow applications
that have no micro's involved at all, but for the smaller and mid sized
companies that use micro's and micro nets, it sure is not.

Donald


Donald

news:Xns91D8D72421978gerrywolfesympaticoc@207.35.177.135...

batch

…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> mainframe process that supported objects (whether text- or GUI-
presented).
> But, it ain't gonna happen (at least in my lifetime, which goes back to
> punchcard and 1401 days).  There is a humungous investment in CICS, and it
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> In the batch environment, not that much code is re-used.  That which is
re-
> used (record layouts, logic methods) is normally created as a COPY code or
> CALLed as a subroutine.  Again, agreed that OO invokes are more
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> great job of discussing controversial topics in an objective manner
without
> digressing to name-calling and commenting on posters' ancestry...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future of object-oriented COBOL?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Brian Stanley" <B.Stanley@its.amc.edu.au>
- **Date:** 2002-03-21T16:24:24+11:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c996e88.0@mariner>`
- **References:** `<c2ab4b04.0203191553.5deaa74b@posting.google.com> <3c9918f8_9@Usenet.com> <3C991FB3.EC98B300@shaw.ca> <Xns91D7D5EF97860gerrywolfesympaticoc@206.172.150.13>`

```

"Gerry Wolfe" <gerry.wolfe@sympatico.ca> wrote in message
news:Xns91D7D5EF97860gerrywolfesympaticoc@206.172.150.13...
>
> Let's turn the discussion inside out -- instead of talking about
languages,
> talk about what they're being used for...
>
> There are two type of programming environments -- procedural-driven and
> event-driven.  A procedural environment is yer typical batch process --
the
> code drives the procedure; you code the logic to identify what happens
> next.  An event-driven environment is yer typical online process -- the
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

While I understand and accept what you are saying Gerry - do the two
environment need different approaches to resolution? Can't they both be
handled by programs that are driven by State Transition Tables? In the
procedural (batch) case the states may be limited in number and the
transitions 'pre-determined' but it is still an effective approach that
exerts strong control over the process.

Brian Stanley
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future of object-oriented COBOL?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Gerry Wolfe <gerrydotwolfe@sympatico.ca>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
"Brian Stanley" <B.Stanley@its.amc.edu.au> wrote in
news:3c996e88.0@mariner: 

>

..snipped myself again...
>
> 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
This is something I'm not familiar with (which may also apply to other 
folks in this newsgroup).  What is a "State Transition Table" and how does 
it apply to the COBOL language?  To backtrack, my original reply-to-post 
referred to the impacts of OO methods regarding event-driven (e.g. 
interactive or realtime) vs: procedure (e.g. "batch") programs.

rgds, g.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future of object-oriented COBOL?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Brian Stanley" <B.Stanley@its.amc.edu.au>
- **Date:** 2002-03-25T18:39:57+11:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c9ed44e.0@mariner>`
- **References:** `<c2ab4b04.0203191553.5deaa74b@posting.google.com> <3c9918f8_9@Usenet.com> <3C991FB3.EC98B300@shaw.ca> <Xns91D7D5EF97860gerrywolfesympaticoc@206.172.150.13> <3c996e88.0@mariner> <Xns91D8D822A5F68gerrywolfesympaticoc@207.35.177.135>`

```

"Gerry Wolfe" <gerrydotwolfe@sympatico.ca> wrote in message
news:Xns91D8D822A5F68gerrywolfesympaticoc@207.35.177.135...
> "Brian Stanley" <B.Stanley@its.amc.edu.au> wrote in
> news:3c996e88.0@mariner:
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> rgds, g.

Arranging a program so that it 'driven' by a State Transition Table is not
applicable to COBOL anymore than it is to any other language. You seemed to
be of the view that COBOL OO methods only have application with
event-driven/interactive/realtime situations and not 'batch' situations.
That these two situations are in someway distinct and require different
approaches. I was suggesting that there are approaches (including State
Transition Tables) that can handle both situation - just as OO methods can
apply to both situations. I agree that there is probably not a lot to be
gained by re-writing well designed/programmed batch systems using OO
methods - but for a new system/application why not? You won't really
appreciate any positives until you have tried it.


Brian Stanley
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future of object-oriented COBOL?

- **From:** stephenjspiro@mail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2002-03-21T16:57:44-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<928495c6.0203211657.19666367@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<c2ab4b04.0203191553.5deaa74b@posting.google.com> <3c9918f8_9@Usenet.com> <3C991FB3.EC98B300@shaw.ca>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote > It does raise a question
about the function of J4 - as we approach the deadline
> December 2002, with no whisper of a commitment by the many, as opposed to the
> few (three) - why have they spent time since 1996 pissing around with an OO
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jimmy, Calgary AB


I dunno, Jimmy.  An ENORMOUS amount of the technical effort on OO
COBOL was provided by IBM, MicroFocus, Fujitsu, Hitachi, Unisys,
Hewlett-Packard and Siemens.  I don't mean just by the J4 members from
these companies, but also by staff "back home" who contributed papers
and/or attended the meetings of the OO subcommittee.  It certainly
looked like commitment to me.
Now that it's done, no-one seems to be in a hurry to implement it....
Part of that may be that many of them implemented OO COBOL based on
(all different) preliminary drafts of the new standard.  Some of these
are flat-out incompatible with the final standard.  To introduce the
final syntax, they will have to develop a compiler option which will
support both versions.

It took a while for the 85 standard to be implemented fully.  Some
parts of the new standard may await customer demand, as happened with
IBM and Report Writer in the 1985 standard -- it was eventually sold
as an add-on.   Let us not mourn the death of the new standard before
it is even officially born!

Stephen J Spiro
Member, J4 COBOL Standards Committee
```

```
Stephen J Spiro wrote:

>
> I dunno, Jimmy.  An ENORMOUS amount of the technical effort on OO
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> looked like commitment to me.

Stephen,

Thanks for the update, and on reflection that's how it should have happened.
Developer staff being the insiders on compilers, coupled with a knowledge of other
languages, would have the requisite skills to visualize a new topic like OO. That
kinda leaves you and me gaping until they put something in black and white <G>.

Ann did mention once three people she personally credited with being the innovators
for OO - sticks in my mind - one from HP, possibly Ray Obin (M/F) and Mr. X.

Can you or Bill tune me into an archive ? Taking the current COBOL 2002 spec, where
is the first document in that format that starts spelling out OO.

>
> Now that it's done, no-one seems to be in a hurry to implement it....
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

As regards the 'many implemented' - surely only three, F/J, Hitachi and M/F ?
(Well, grumble, grumble. grumble - we'll also include IBM). Yes, M/F went from the
early version which was probably close to publication of Ray Obin's handbook based
on the OCTG findings. F/J followed when the spec had become slightly more
definitive. Neither fully complies with the new standard, and the same must apply
to  Hitachi. However, Thane and I have swapped reasonably vanilla flavoured OO
source between F/J and M/F.

As you imply they were gung ho at the beginning - what's happened to the enthusiasm
since ? Assuming they are still in favour of OO, (and no need to put their heads on
the chopping block), would be nice if they would announce their intent, that at
some future undetermined date they will commit to OO. Even such a general comment
would give their customers(developers) pause to think.

> It took a while for the 85 standard to be implemented fully.  Some
> parts of the new standard may await customer demand, as happened with
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Not mourning, just got fingers and toes crossed, that there is a future for OO
COBOL.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future of object-oriented COBOL?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "398199821" <warren.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2002-03-23T03:24:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%zSm8.1251$se.105735@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<c2ab4b04.0203191553.5deaa74b@posting.google.com> <3c9918f8_9@Usenet.com> <3C991FB3.EC98B300@shaw.ca> <928495c6.0203211657.19666367@posting.google.com> <3C9ABEE8.9B4F0F16@shaw.ca>`

```
I hear you loud and clear. Sometimes new ideas are so difficult to grasp,
the believers are the only ones attending
the meetings. In my time, direct access storage was growing fast. Users did
not design their applications to use the
same files that were created elsewhere. Multi-copies of data devoured tape,
and disk, and batch processing. Integrating that
into OUR data instead of mine and yours seemed to hold lots of advantages.
To me it still does. Yet only a few
choose the network data organization in favor of Relational.  They appear to
exist side by side because of "religious"
belief. It looks to me that OO in in the same boat except it does appear to
belong in both worlds.  Lots of user
computer time is devoted to things not solved by batch programs. Utilities
are developed to over come this at each
location. The same kind of a problem happens between the group maintaining
application a or b.  Where to use
OO seems to be when it's the right tool, and the design and rsesearch
preceeding the effort shows how to take advantage of it.
The Unix and PC world are so different from the big iron, that the OS
doesn't give the same features, or make the same
solutions possible unless more work it done to learn what to do to take
advantage of new techniques (OO as an example)

Warren Simmons
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future of object-oriented COBOL?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-03-23T20:10:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C9CE08B.2E7EECC8@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<c2ab4b04.0203191553.5deaa74b@posting.google.com> <3c9918f8_9@Usenet.com> <3C991FB3.EC98B300@shaw.ca> <928495c6.0203211657.19666367@posting.google.com> <3C9ABEE8.9B4F0F16@shaw.ca> <%zSm8.1251$se.105735@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```


398199821 wrote:

> I hear you loud and clear. Sometimes new ideas are so difficult to grasp,
> the believers are the only ones attending
> the meetings.

Warren,

Well on that one I certainly didn't start out as a believer. I was quite happy
with Micro Focus in the DOS world using Screen Section to accomplish what I
needed.
Taking a close look at the CBL_SCREEN features they included, capturing
keystrokes, setting attributes etc., I was able to put off the switch to Windows
and emulate it by producing Menu bars, pop-ups etc.

The DOS version did contain examples of calling Windows APIs, which is the route
that Ken Mullins followed - and successfully. But like many, I was avoiding
Windows like a pariah. Eventually I knew I would have to switch. Calgary being
such a high tech city, the oil company crowd would eventually DEMAND a Windows
look to applications.

So eventually I went looking, CA-Realia, an early version of Fujitsu (didn't
have an integrated IDE at the time). Then eventually my end user said, "Hey,
Micro Focus are offering a product for $800 that does Windows - VISOC (Visual
Object COBOL - subsequently to become Net Express). Jumped at it, because I was
comfortable with the Micro Focus style of presenting info.

I started looking at the examples, invoke this, invoke that, turn this text into
an object and back again. What the bloody hell is this all about. Object
Orientation was an absolutely new concept that I had netiher read about, nor had
any knowledge. Bear in mind I'm a loner up here, so basically what I learned I
had to teach myself - sure there must still be a lot of COBOL programmers in
Calgary, but they are well hidden away.

The GUI part of OO you can consider an extra because it can be attacked in many
ways, API calls, SP2/ScreenIO etc., but other than the APIs I wasn't aware of
the alternatives because I wasn't properly into the Web and hadn't discovered
comp.lang.cobol.

I was of course desperately trying to get a handle on OO. Really there was no
help then for a COBOLer looking at other languages, VB, C or Java, particularly
if you were not familiar with them.

Each time I found a reference to a book which contained the magic words "COBOL"
and "OO" latched together, I bought it. Off the top of my head there are about
four, but they are for the most part full of theoretical crap without true coded
examples.

I can't recall how, but eventually discovered Ed Arranga and Frank Coyle's "OO
COBOL". At long bloody last, here was a book that explained things using a
*real* compiler (Net Express) and summed the whole thing up with a small Library
application at the back of the book giving the source for each class. Everything
really started to fall into place, (with the exception of GUIs which are M/F
specific as they are in Fujitsu's POWERCOBOL). This book represented my first
true building block. Certainly doesn't cover all the nuances of OO, but included
enough to set me on the right path, plus of course, it led me to make my own
particular enhancements.

You could say from this point on that I was reasonably well sold on OO. Then
Ed's associate in cobolreport.com, Will (Wilson Price) produced his "Elements of
OO COBOL". This one is tutorial in style, geared to students, and very
successfully used at universities in the States. (Again it uses Net Express as
the compiler). Chapter by chapter, Will reinforced what I had initially picked
up from Ed's book As a result of talking to Will on the phone to order the book,
he mentioned he would send a paper and diskette which covered file handling -
and it is THIS that I got my file handling approach from. Now with a clear
concept of three tier programming in OO, I became TOTALLY SOLD on OO.

So as you can see, fell into it by accident, incrementally increased my comfort
with the concept and then became a RAVING convert <G>.

The area I eventually have to dabble in is the COBOL to Web link. At the moment
I'm concentrating on SQL - initially prodded by you know who, and currently
using the M/F ESQL Assistant tool to define formats and generate source for SQL
queries

> In my time, direct access storage was growing fast. Users did
> not design their applications to use the
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I think where the confusion occurs regarding mainframes with "Shall we, shan't
we, use/have OO...?" is the difference between the two O/S approaches.

Windows/Unix/Linux/Apple families offer an O/S which you can access direct with
APIs, ( I think <G>), or alternatively, whether or not it is a programming
language, word processing or spreadsheet package, use the the particular
language/package with its own syntax, and under the wraps they are ALL
eventually making calls to the O/S via APIs. You have the ability to work in PC
COBOL and never give consideration to APIs or what they actually do - the
gateway between you and the APIs is the COBOL language.

I know nothing of mainframes, just what I glean from messages here. For example,
while IBM offers COBOL, so much of the inter-play is geared to the O/S with
CICS, LE etc.,  and whereas on a PC we use the traditional COBOL file verbs for
access, (albeit the compiler is addressing the O/S), I get the impression that
is not the case mainframe wise. You can use the O/S add-ons to perform functions
on the mainframe that we associate with traditional COBOL syntax on PCs. (I
recall Bill saying he was blown away when he realized the significant difference
between mainframe and PC file handling).

While the philosophy of the tie in between languages, (including COBOL) and the
O/S on mainframes continues, I doubt that OO will ever get a look-in, other than
producing links to languages like Java to do the "screen stuff". As Stephen
said, they all, including IBM, contributed to the OO spec - but certainly at the
moment it appears contrary to the mainframe philosophy. (To clarify that a
little - the concept of OO is to 'stand apart' from the O/S, so that you have
the ability of re-use; you haven't if its application is dependent upon O/S
support packages).

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
The following vendors offer some level of OO COBOL support:

Fujitsu-Siemens
Fujitsu Software Corporation
(Note - multiple compilers on this second one(Unix, Windows, .NET),
the first is Mainframe)
IBM
Hitachi
PerCOBOL
MicroFocus

Who's left?

HP
COMPAQ (Including DEC)
RM
AcuCorp
TinyCOBOL

Looks like a pretty fair ratio to me.  I expect at least some of those
in the bottom group to begin implementation.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Future of object-oriented COBOL?

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>

```
ROFL!

There IS no Java hype. There WAS a couple of years ago. Today it is an
established Language with a growing codebase.

And there's nothing wrong with that. It is good for the the things it is
intended to do. In the same way COBOL is.

I have used CICS almost since it was invented and I have worked in the place
where it WAS invented and had conversations with people who worked with some
of the people who invented it. I am NOT anti-CICS.

If you had followed this NG for any length of time you would know that I am
NOT affected by hype on ANY product. Been in this game too long and seen it
all come and go...

As for nobody knowing where the Web is going, speak for yourself.

It is not hard to extrapolate from the existing data. While the form and
delivery mechanisms may well be subject to change, there is no doubt that
the lives of most of us are going to be integrated with the WWW in the same
way that TV and telephones pervade our lives.

The Market place demands cheap, fast, on-line access. The Web delivers it;
CICS doesn't. Goodbye CICS.

Bury your head in the sand if you want, but don't say I didn't try to warn
you...<G>

Pete.
```

```
still using such old hardware (keep in mind OS/390 R10 require R&I) I prefer
to

keep CobAnal in the archive and build CobAnalz for z/OS. CobAnal will be
frozen while

CobAnalz will be supported within the next years.

have the need

for this. If you agree just wait until I finshed the code

Regards Roland
```

<a id="t-def2c97fcb"></a>
## Thread: Fujitsu and MS Access datatypes — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Fujitsu and MS Access datatypes

- **From:** Robin Lee <robinlee@rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-20T01:09:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C97E004.352BB29B@rr.com>`

```
I've been given an Access database which I am to populate using Fujitsu
COBOL. However, if my program makes any reference to fields defined in
Access as "Memo" datatype, the program build fails.

I've noticed on the ODBC control Field property sheet that these items
have their "spectacles" icon greyed-out, and PowerCOBOL identifies them
as being over two gigabytes in size!

I've looked thru a few Access reference books but they seem to be
procedure oriented and rather low on substance regarding datatypes. 
Could anyone give me a few pointers here please?

Also, I haven't gotten to this point yet, but I think I recall reading
that the Currency datatype is Pic s9(14)v9(4).  Is this correct?  Any
help would be greatly appreciated! Thanks!
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu and MS Access datatypes

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-03-21T10:09:56+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c9918f6_9@Usenet.com>`
- **References:** `<3C97E004.352BB29B@rr.com>`

```

Robin Lee <robinlee@rr.com> wrote in message
news:3C97E004.352BB29B@rr.com...
> I've been given an Access database which I am to populate using Fujitsu
> COBOL. However, if my program makes any reference to fields defined in
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Memo items are "best avoided" when using ACCESS with COBOL. If you set the
Host Variable to be Picture X(nnn) where "nnn" is a large number (don't
exceed the compiler limit...I'm not sure what it is from memory, but say you
set it to 2048 (well within the limit)) then when you SELECT... INTO it,
ACCESS will load the HV with as much data as you have provided for. (in this
case 2048 bytes).  If you really have a database with 2 GIG Memo fields on
it (whose bright idea was THAT?! <G>) you will probably need to "preprocess"
using some ACCESS code. If I were you, I'd really try and get thos fields
removed. Consider setting up some attached tables that can grow in a more
controlled manner than just having a giant "bin" to put stuff in. I'm sure
you know that Memo fields cannot be indexed, so anything that is put there
will take FOREVER to search (irrespective of whether it is searched in COBOL
or ACCESS or any other form of access to the data.)

The bottom line is that I know of no way you can actually load these fields
into embedded SQL. It's not a restriction in COBOL; it is a restriction in
the ODBC driver (guess the designers of this software expected people to be
rational when they design databases....<G>)

> I've looked thru a few Access reference books but they seem to be
> procedure oriented and rather low on substance regarding datatypes.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> help would be greatly appreciated! Thanks!

Yes, you are correct. The currency data type is held as a 64 bit floating
point number  (comp-2 in Fujitsu COBOL) that is really a FLOAT where the
decimal is ALWAYS fixed to 4 places, by convention.  (COBOL s9(14)v9(4)).
You CAN set the host variable to be comp-2 and it will return it, but there
can be obscure problems in doing this. One way that always works (and I
therefore ensure that my data definition generator uses it) is to define the
host variable as: pic s9(14)v9(4).  (this is DISPLAY format by default).
This works correctly every time and is therefore useful...

If you have further problems with data definitions you should pick up
DECLGEN. It will analyse your tables and generate COBOL Host Variable
definitions (that work...) and COBOL Record layouts for inclusion in
programs. If you are doing this for your personal use, I'll let you have it
free, otherwise it costs $US 100.00. (Site license $US 500 - Runs under Win
32 and is written in PowerCOBOL).

Pete.

----------------------------------------------------------

----------------------------------------------------------        
                http://www.usenet.com
```

```
> ACCESS will load the HV with as much data as you have provided for. (in
this
> case 2048 bytes).  If you really have a database with 2 GIG Memo fields on
> it (whose bright idea was THAT?! <G>) you will probably need to
"preprocess"
> using some ACCESS code. If I were you, I'd really try and get thos fields
> removed. Consider setting up some attached tables that can grow in a more
> controlled manner than just having a giant "bin" to put stuff in. I'm sure
> you know that Memo fields cannot be indexed, so anything that is put there
> will take FOREVER to search (irrespective of whether it is searched in
COBOL
> or ACCESS or any other form of access to the data.)
>
> The bottom line is that I know of no way you can actually load these
fields
> into embedded SQL. It's not a restriction in COBOL; it is a restriction in
> the ODBC driver (guess the designers of this software expected people to
be
> rational when they design databases....<G>)
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> You CAN set the host variable to be comp-2 and it will return it, but
there
> can be obscure problems in doing this. One way that always works (and I
> therefore ensure that my data definition generator uses it) is to define
the
> host variable as: pic s9(14)v9(4).  (this is DISPLAY format by default).
> This works correctly every time and is therefore useful...
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> programs. If you are doing this for your personal use, I'll let you have
it
> free, otherwise it costs $US 100.00. (Site license $US 500 - Runs under
Win
> 32 and is written in PowerCOBOL).
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>                 http://www.usenet.com
```

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>

```
Mail me privately. (remove "nospam" from my mail address). Tell me what you
want it for and, if it is for personal use, I'll let you have it free.

I wrote this tool (DECLGEN) for my own use and I am not attempting to market
it (yet). A number of people (and one or two organisations) have found it
useful. The latest release is full Win32 and automatically detects your
database engine. It uses direct access to RDB internals (via OO PowerCOBOL)
to analyse the structure of your DB and generates COBOL pictures and data
structures that are compatible with it. It is a small part of a much larger
system for converting ISAM/VSAM-KSDS to Relational Database. (ISAM2RDB).

Pete.
```

<a id="t-0516d683f1"></a>
## Thread: Perl Meets COBOL — 8 msgs

### Perl Meets COBOL

- **From:** sanface <member@mainframeforum.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-20T04:14:15-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c9852e7$1_3@news.teranews.com>`

```
An article you've to read to know more about perl

http://www.perl.com/pub/a/2000/05/cobol.html
```

#### ↳ Re: Perl Meets COBOL

- **From:** epc8@juno.com (E P Chandler)
- **Date:** 2002-03-20T10:45:20-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7f64e2ff.0203201045.2a99a92e@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3c9852e7$1_3@news.teranews.com>`

```
sanface <member@mainframeforum.com> wrote in message news:<3c9852e7$1_3@news.teranews.com>...
> An article you've to read to know more about perl
> 
> http://www.perl.com/pub/a/2000/05/cobol.html

This is a fascinating article because it demonstrates so well two very
different ways of thinking under two very different environments. In
it, COBOL programmers take a class in Perl, without the expected
experience in C like languages or a Unix environment. Although
experienced, both instructor and students hit some roadblocks. It's a
great example of pointing out what programmers take for granted!

First, Perl has variable length records with explicit end of line
markers (newlines). But on the mainframe, record length is maintained
by the system, not manifested by the data itself.

Second, Perl doesn't have record oriented I/O. The program reads data
and then splits it up into fields, whereas in COBOL, the work of
spltting up the input data is done by the data structure itself, not
by program code. (Something a Pascal programmer *would* be comfortable
with.)

[Slightly O.T., except that file transfer issues are relevant here.]
Point #1 hit me bluntly a few years ago when I used FTP to transfer a
file from an IBM mainframe running MTS (Michigan Terminal System) to
my PC running MS-DOS. I had not specified a text transfer, so I got a
binary image of the file. OK, I thought, since I had a program that
translated EBCDIC to ASCII. To my horror, the output file was all
smashed together with no line breaks at all. Obviously MTS stores
record lengths, but they're not in the data!

I wonder what it would be like for Perl programmers to learn COBOL?
```

```
get so used to things being one way that it's often hard for them to
understand why it may be done other ways.
```
