## Thread: Performance Delta - Dynamic vs Static calls — 5 msgs

### Performance Delta - Dynamic vs Static calls

- **From:** Johnboy <member@mainframeforum.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-20T04:14:17-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c9852e9$2_3@news.teranews.com>`

```
Greetings!

Has anybody compiled any statistics on the differences in resource
consumption and performance between dynamic and static COBOL calls in an
LE environment? Especially interested in statistics for the CICS
environment.

Thanks in advance,

John Chase United Stationers, Inc. Des Plaines, IL USA
```

#### ↳ Re: Performance Delta - Dynamic vs Static calls

- **From:** george_proost <proost@spagirik.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-20T04:15:31-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c985333$1_3@news.teranews.com>`
- **References:** `<3c9852e9$2_3@news.teranews.com>`

```
 wrote:
  > Use of the EXEC CICS LINK command in an LE runtime environment can mean
  > an increase in CPU cycles. Programs that issue many LINK commands seem
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
  > supports read-only program storage.

Hi John,

I found some interesting info, short and concise on performance. No
real figures but good guidelines on how to accomplish acceptable usage
of cycles.



http://www.its.state.ut.us/contents/services/cics/programmerhints.shtml-
UTAH State -- Full text

Below is a small extract.
```

#### ↳ Re: Performance Delta - Dynamic vs Static calls

- **From:** JimMoore <member@mainframeforum.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-20T04:15:32-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c985334$1_3@news.teranews.com>`
- **References:** `<3c9852e9$2_3@news.teranews.com>`

```
Just one quibble with the text in the previous post. It is not the CALL
'literal' format itself that makes the call static.

This is under the control of the DYNAM/NODYNAM compiler option.

If a progam is compiled with DYNAM, the CALL 'literal' form of the call
will be just as "dynamic" as the CALL identifier-1 format.

When the DYNAM option is used, the COBOL compiler will generate the
equivalent of a LOAD (SVC 06) followed by a BALR to the entry point (a
"dynamic" call) for all user-coded CALLs.

When the NODYNAM option is used, the compiler generates V-Type constants
in the ESD of the object deck for all coded CALLs. The binder (linkage
editor) must then resolve these addresses.

When the CALL identifier-1 format is used, the call is ALWAYS dynamic,
regardless of the DYNAM/NODYNAM compiler option.

Related topics: The "Is Initial" clause of the Program-ID, the Reentrant
(RENT) compile option
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Performance Delta - Dynamic vs Static calls

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-20T05:34:23-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a79s8v$mfl$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3c9852e9$2_3@news.teranews.com> <3c985334$1_3@news.teranews.com>`

```
Although the distinction between DYNAM/NODYNAM is *usually* true in
determining whether CALL "literal" is or is not dynamic/static, the
environment (in this context) was CICS and the use of the DYNAM compiler
option is prohibited there.
```

#### ↳ Re: Performance Delta - Dynamic vs Static calls

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-20T05:41:43-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a79shp$kbq$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3c9852e9$2_3@news.teranews.com>`

```
If you are interested in all aspects of performance (Including CALL
information), see:

   http://www-4.ibm.com/software/ad/cobol/library/

and select the "compiler" that is most appropriate.  (I believe that they
all address CALLs vs EXEC LINKs)
```

<a id="t-76312cceaf"></a>
## Thread: Dynamic File Allocations in COBOL — 2 msgs

### Dynamic File Allocations in COBOL

- **From:** COBOLMAN <member@mainframeforum.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-20T04:14:18-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c9852ea$1_3@news.teranews.com>`

```
Someone asked this at work and I have never heard of it so maybe the
MFF can help.

It is my understanding that there is a way to dynamically allocate files
from inside a Cobol program. For example, Lets say I have an input that
needs to be split into a varying number of outputs. Rather than having
hardcoded DD statements for every possible output file, the DDs can be
allocated dynamically from the program itself. On one day there may be
10 output DDs and the next may have 30 output DDs based on the input?

COBOL in use is COBOL II and COBOL for MVS
```

#### ↳ Re: Dynamic File Allocations in COBOL

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-20T05:30:36-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a79rsu$hm$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3c9852ea$1_3@news.teranews.com>`

```
Not possible (directly) in VS COBOL II or IBM COBOL for MVS & VM  (By using
calls to SVC99 or even ISPF, it can be done with these compilers)

It is available directly, however, with IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM (V2R2)
   and
IBM Enterprise COBOL for z/OS & OS/390.

See:
 http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/igypg205/1.7.3
```

```
    It is not clear (to me) exactly what sort of program you are using.

    Is this the DOS based workbench, and what version?   If it is
```

### Slightly OT: tab-delimited, pkzip, and mainframe to PC xfer

- **From:** Ed_Stevens@nospam.noway.nohow (Ed Stevens)
- **Date:** 2002-03-20T16:07:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3c98afe5.183457778@ausnews.austin.ibm.com>`

```
A co-worker just brought me an interesting problem.  He came to me because I'm
apparently the only guy in the shop working on the "WinTel" platforms that also
has a mainframe cobol background and remembers what a "yellow card" is.  (I
still keep one in my desk, btw.)

His COBOL program is writing a file that is destined to become a tab-delimited
file on a Windows platform.  He has other programs that do the same thing
(creating a file for the Windows platform) without the tab-delimited
requirement.  For those, he writes the file, runs it through PKZIP on the
mainframe, downloads it through TSO (IND$FILE) as a binary transfer.  When
unzipped on the PC everything is hunky-dorey.  I don't understand how the
EBCDIC-ASCII translation occured unless it is being done as the file is being
zipped.

For the current problem of needing to create a tab-delimited file, he tried
inserting x'09' for the tab, but when unzipped on the Windows side, it comes out
total garbage.

He has no control over the file once it hits the Windows platform, so solutions
requireing further manipulation  on that side are not acceptable.
```

```
DD
```

```
Kyle

Following the thread, it seems obviously that the zipped file is not
translated to ascii (not the zip file itself!!).

As you mentioned that the file without the TAB works fine, I would
think that either PKZIP was not called with the proper options
(DATA_TYPE must be set to TEXT on this particular case), or that the
default configuration is pointing to DATA_TYPE=DETECT

Have your man have a look at this option.


Cheers

FF
```

```
I don't know about that... the file is in EBCDIC on the mainframe; who 
knows what happens to the tab-characters during the mainframe Zipping?

Next the file is translated during the ftp to the PC... who knows what 
happens to the Zipped tab-characters during this?

Finally the file is unZipped on the PC... who knows etc. etc.?

I know that *I* don't know what happens to tab-characters under any one of 
these conditions, let alone all three... but I *do* know that the file 
needs to be gotten across in good shape.

So... it seems that if time and money would allow it the file should be 
sent unZipped, no?  Obviously... no, a 'full VAN' is required and the 
unZipped file would cost 'thousands per month' to transfer.

What is a 'full VAN'?  I have no idea.  A 56K modem is rather speedy and 
telephone-minutes are rather cheap... how can this file cose so much to 
transfer?  I have no idea.

Might be interesting to find out... but only one person here, at the 
moment, seems to know.

DD
```

```
A VAN is a Value Added Network.  Due to requirements beyond my control I must use this
method to send this file to this partner.  When using VAN's, there is a per byte charge
incurred.  I have used this method successfully with many other partners.  The only
exception this time is the tab delimited feature.  What I meant by "full" was the unzipped
version of the file in its original form.  This is a quite lengthy demographics file (4meg
with future growth to 8).  Even if I were emailing it I would zip it for speed.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Slightly OT: tab-delimited, pkzip, and mainframe to PC xfer

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Kyle Cothron <kyle.cothron@nnn.nissan-usa.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-22T09:27:56-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C9B4D7C.985E7DB@nnn.nissan-usa.com>`
- **References:** `<3c98afe5.183457778@ausnews.austin.ibm.com> <a7ah0b$dmf$1@panix1.panix.com> <3C98E9D3.EE329344@nnn.nissan-usa.com> <a7artf$cc7$1@panix1.panix.com> <3C98F8F4.E9824662@nnn.nissan-usa.com> <ko8i9uk9tm1euou826a0rth0qduui1eb3f@4ax.com>`

```
You got an important piece of the puzzle.  I needed to specify -TEXT.  Turns out that PKZIP
attempts to determine text or binary if not specified.  Normally is chooses text but with
the tab character it chose binary.  I specified -TEXT and all is well.  I also needed to
use hex 05 instead of hex 09 for proper translation.  Thanks for the help!

Frederico Fonseca wrote:

> On Wed, 20 Mar 2002 15:02:44 -0600, Kyle Cothron
> <kyle.cothron@nnn.nissan-usa.com> wrote:
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
> FF
```

```
I do not know whay you mean by 'VAN' and in these days of telephone-calls

>this is the wrong venu for this issue.  Thanks
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Slightly OT: tab-delimited, pkzip, and mainframe to PC xfer

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <frederico_fonseca@eudoramail.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-21T23:22:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7eqk9uc4tehbq3cjsk89dg53cn0ka0keof@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<3c98afe5.183457778@ausnews.austin.ibm.com> <a7ah0b$dmf$1@panix1.panix.com> <3C98E9D3.EE329344@nnn.nissan-usa.com>`

```
For those that have not yet understood the problem!!.

PKZIP for MVS has a command line option  -DATA_TYPE that has a default
value of "detect".
This means that if this option is not set in the command line, then it
will search each individual file that is being compressed, and if any
"binary" character is found then it will treat the whole file as
binary, thus not perform any ebcdic to ascii conversion. The "tab"
character is among the ones that make it "binary" type.

So in order to make this file work the PKZIP command line needs to
include the "-DATA_TYPE=text" option so it converts the file to ascii
without trying to determine the file type automatically.


FF



On Wed, 20 Mar 2002 13:58:11 -0600, Kyle Cothron
<kyle.cothron@nnn.nissan-usa.com> wrote:

>The file downloaded properly without the zip.
>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>> DD
```

```
//SYSIN DD *

-ADD
-INFILE(MYFILE)
...
...

The manuals can be downloaded at
http://www.asizip.com/support/manuals/

PDF format (zipped)


Regards
```

#### ↳ Re: Slightly OT: tab-delimited, pkzip, and mainframe to PC xfer

- **From:** Richard Fuller <rfuller1@pdq.net>
- **Date:** 2002-03-21T21:33:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C9A5192.5060006@pdq.net>`
- **References:** `<3c98afe5.183457778@ausnews.austin.ibm.com>`

```
PKZIP on the mainframe is doing your EBCDIC to ASCII translation, 
jawohl? You can change the translation tables for this product to yield 
pretty much whatever translation you want. I.e. you can modify those 
tables. Or, probably just check them to see what character is being 
translated into an x'09' and create your EBCDIC mainframe file accordingly.
Richard


Ed Stevens wrote:

>A co-worker just brought me an interesting problem.  He came to me because I'm
>apparently the only guy in the shop working on the "WinTel" platforms that also
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Slightly OT: tab-delimited, pkzip, and mainframe to PC xfer

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-03-22T07:23:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C9ADBFA.43FC4C23@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<3c98afe5.183457778@ausnews.austin.ibm.com>`

```
Ed Stevens wrote:
> 
> For the current problem of needing to create a tab-delimited file, he tried
> inserting x'09' for the tab, but when unzipped on the Windows side, it comes out
> total garbage.

If the unzip on the Windows actually worked, ie a file was extracted
with a good name, then the structure of the zip envelope has not been
trashed.  I would expect that the zip envelope would need conversion
from EDCDIC to ASCII because, for example, there is a file name in
there.

>  I don't understand how the
> EBCDIC-ASCII translation occured unless it is being done as the file is being
> zipped.

As he is transferring the zipped file in binary it would seem that pkzip
on the mainframe is writing its envelope in ASCII and also converting
the text file to ASCII as it zips.

When there are tabs (x"09"?) in the file perhaps PKZip thinks that the
file has binary data and does not convert to ASCII resulting in the file
being left in EDCDIC.

Use a different separator.
```

#### ↳ Re: Slightly OT: tab-delimited, pkzip, and mainframe to PC xfer

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-03-22T07:56:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C9AE3C3.BE7862E1@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<3c98afe5.183457778@ausnews.austin.ibm.com>`

```
Ed Stevens wrote:
> 
> 
> For the current problem of needing to create a tab-delimited file, he tried
> inserting x'09' for the tab, but when unzipped on the Windows side, 
> [it] comes out total garbage.
  ^^^^

Is your 'it' referring to the file being total garbage or to the 'tab'
being total garbage ?

Is x"09" the EDCDIC character for 'tab'.  If it is just the 'tab' that
is garbage then find out what hex character you should really be using.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Slightly OT: tab-delimited, pkzip, and mainframe to PC xfer

- **From:** Kyle Cothron <kyle.cothron@nnn.nissan-usa.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-21T16:05:00-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C9A590C.D2A095B8@nnn.nissan-usa.com>`
- **References:** `<3c98afe5.183457778@ausnews.austin.ibm.com> <3C9AE3C3.BE7862E1@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
The entire file was garbage.  There was no PKZIP errors.  The Hex '09' is a
"non-displayable field" in ISPF.  Your point about translation has merit.  I am
not 100% positive about the translation.

Richard Plinston wrote:

> Ed Stevens wrote:
> >
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> is garbage then find out what hex character you should really be using.
```

#### ↳ Re: Slightly OT: tab-delimited, pkzip, and mainframe to PC xfer

- **From:** Kyle Cothron <kyle.cothron@nnn.nissan-usa.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-22T09:45:41-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C9B51A5.4D148B97@nnn.nissan-usa.com>`
- **References:** `<3c98afe5.183457778@ausnews.austin.ibm.com>`

```
My Hat is off to Frederico Fonseca and docdwarf.  The ultimate resolution had 2
parts.  PKZIP was switching off the EBCDIC to ASCII translation because it found
what it considered to be binary data in my file.  This required and overide of -TEXT
to force the conversion.  Also I was using the wrong side of the yellowcard.  The
tab character is a hexdecimal 05.  We were looking at the ASCII side.  Thanks for
all the help!

> apparently the only guy in the shop working on the "WinTel" platforms that also
```

## Thread: IBM 1401 — 5 msgs

### IBM 1401

- **From:** vsnyder@math.jpl.nasa.gov (Van Snyder)
- **Date:** 2002-03-20T20:55:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a7at0j$8qd$1@nntp1.jpl.nasa.gov>`

```
I'm trying to preserve some of the history of IBM 1401. I'm hoping to find
Autocoder, Cobol, Fortran, RPG, Sort 6, Sort 7, IOCS, ... tapes or decks,
manuals, user-developed applications, the engineering drawings that CE's
used for field maintenance, or any other IBM 1401 memorabilia (including
equipment!).

Do you have any of this stuff, or know anybody who might?

My list of manuals is http://math.jpl.nasa.gov/~vsnyder/1401/1401-docs.html.
Do you have any manuals that I don't have, or newer editions of the ones I
do? If you have any, would you consider sending photocopies?  The
originals ought to be sent to a proper museum.  The most scholarly of
these is the Charles Babbage Institute at the University of Minnesota. 
Don't forget to take a tax deduction for a charitable contribution.

The only software I have is SPS, SPS-2, two-tape Autocoder, the
diagnostics, and a nice core-dump routine from Germany.

Do you remember how to install two-tape Autocoder?  We have a listing file that
was output from the assembler.  It has images of object cards interspersed.  It
doesn't load correctly.  I can give you the details of how it fails if you're
interested.  Comments in the listing (July 1962) have the initials RBR.  Do you
have any idea who that was?

Thanks in advance,
```

#### ↳ Re: IBM 1401

- **From:** Gerry Wolfe <gerrydotwolfe@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-03-21T02:09:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Xns91D7D720E32FDgerrywolfesympaticoc@206.172.150.13>`
- **References:** `<a7at0j$8qd$1@nntp1.jpl.nasa.gov>`

```
vsnyder@math.jpl.nasa.gov (Van Snyder) wrote in
news:a7at0j$8qd$1@nntp1.jpl.nasa.gov: 

> I'm trying to preserve some of the history of IBM 1401. I'm hoping to
> find Autocoder, Cobol, Fortran, RPG, Sort 6, Sort 7, IOCS, ... tapes
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Guess there aren't any 1401 programming language newsgroups!  I would love 
to hear follow-ups on this one.  1401 was the first computer I programmed 
(using SPS) and I still remember some of the tricks and the bizarre design 
e.g. card reader input went into loc 001-080, punch went from 101-180, 
printer 201-232, X1/X2/X3 hard-mapped to specific core memory locations.

We used to code initialization logic in the reader/punch/printer locations 
before doins I/O operations... you didn't have much choice with 16K (max).

And... I'll never forget the impact of missing a wordmark...

Now... THAT was programming!  One shot at a compile, entering fixes at the 
console, patching object decks... the "good old days"...
```

<a id="t-286f751ce5"></a>
## Thread: NOADV — 4 msgs

### NOADV

- **From:** Frank Swarbrick <infocat@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-20T19:14:08-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C9941F0.B306C951@sprynet.com>`

```
A recent message stated that if you use the NOADV compile option and
remove the "after positioning" part of an OS/VS COBOL program you can
convert it easily to newer COBOL's which don't support the "after
positioning" language.  It also mentions something about using
RECFM=FBA.

Out shop runs VSE/ESA, not MVS or it's later names.  Can someone explain
to me what RECFM=FBA means?  This absolutely does not work, as far as I
can tell, on any VSE COBOL's.  All that it accomplishes is printing the
control character as the first character of each line.  I'm guessing
there's no VSE equivalent of this RECFM=FBA, but I'd love to hear it if
there is!
```

#### ↳ Re: NOADV

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2002-03-21T02:41:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020320214137.12553.00002236@mb-fw.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3C9941F0.B306C951@sprynet.com>`

```
> Can someone explain
>to me what RECFM=FBA means? 

Fixed Block Ansi - the output contains the Ansi characters for printer control.

FBM = Fixed Block Machine where the printer control characters are machine
dependent.

In MVS we put the output to a file defined FBA and a record length of 133
instead of 132 (the FD in the program I believe is 132 though), and then use
the utility IEBGENER to print out the report when necessary.

It's been 7 years since I was anywhere near a VSE shop and I don't think we did
anything like this there so I'm not sure what to tell you.  

Eileen
```

#### ↳ Re: NOADV

- **From:** bardsfamily@yahoo.com (Alexa)
- **Date:** 2002-03-21T08:07:05-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<80ae94d9.0203210807.2c9aa15c@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3C9941F0.B306C951@sprynet.com>`

```
I passed on your question to one of our VSE peopl and this is what he
said.  I hope it helps.

The "RECFM=FBA" is a parameter of the DCB option in MVS JCL and
indicates that a print file will be fix blocked and contain ASA type
printer carriage control characters in the first position of the
record written by the Cobol program.  There is NO equivalent parameter
in VSE JCL for specifying printer carriage control characters.  All
this information comes from the Cobol program in VSE.

The compiler option ADV/NOADV indicates whether or not the compiler
needs to adjust the size of the print record by one byte to contain
the carriage control character.

The "ADV" compiler option is only meaningful if the
WRITE.....ADVANCING format of the write statement is used when
printing lines.  If ADV is specified in this case, the compiler will
add one byte to the record length of the print file to accommodate the
carriage control character.

If the record length, and print line layout, already contains a byte
for the carriage control, then the NOADV compiler option should be
used.

01  PRINT-LINE.
     12  FILLER            PIC X(01).
     12  PRINT-REC      PIC X(132).

The Cobol for VSE Programming Guide contains a good explanation of the
use of this compiler option.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: NOADV

- **From:** Frank Swarbrick <infocat@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-21T18:37:17-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C9A8ACD.9AC4CDFD@sprynet.com>`
- **References:** `<3C9941F0.B306C951@sprynet.com> <80ae94d9.0203210807.2c9aa15c@posting.google.com>`

```
Alexa wrote:
> 
> I passed on your question to one of our VSE peopl and this is what he
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> use of this compiler option.

Thanks.  Actually, I know how NOADV works, and I use it.  I was just
hoping to avoid changing all of my "AFTER POSITIONING CC" to "AFTER
ADVANCING 1" or "AFTER ADVANCING PAGE".  Looks like I can't.  Ah well. 
We've gone this long without it, so I guess we'll live with it...  We
could, I guess, write to a VSAM file with the CC in position 1 and use
DITTO to print it, but that would take an extra job step and a lot of
temp files.  Not likely!  :-)

Thanks again.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL vs RPG

- **From:** "Dave" <begleyd@chartermi.net>
- **Date:** 2002-03-21T09:42:31-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<u9js9uepjsp816@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<2a5e3706.0203201916.4cf775a2@posting.google.com>`

```
"Samlai" <RB227C@motorola.com> wrote in message
news:2a5e3706.0203201916.4cf775a2@posting.google.com...
: I find that similarity between COBOL and RPG. Actually, any
different
: of these two languages? What are its strengths and weaknesses?

With the latest free format version of RPG, the differences are slowly
disappearing.   Using free format along with the built in functions, I
can write RPG code that is very close to COBOL.

The biggest difference is that variables with the same name contain
the value.  There is no qualifying statements.  At first this seems
like a problem, but once you get used to it, it works great.
```

```
> RPG can be convert to COBOL, isn't then RPG a Turing Machine either?

But COBOL cannot be converted to RPG, so, no RPG is not a Turing Machine.

RPG is closer to the Report Writer subset of COBOL ("RPG" = Report Program
Generator) or Crystal Reports than it is to a programming language.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL vs RPG

- **From:** anote_un@hotmail.com (Anote Sangkunakupt)
- **Date:** 2002-03-26T05:01:37-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ce2d2dc.0203260501.43ecef73@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<2a5e3706.0203201916.4cf775a2@posting.google.com>`

```
RB227C@motorola.com (Samlai) wrote in message news:<2a5e3706.0203201916.4cf775a2@posting.google.com>...
> I find that similarity between COBOL and RPG. Actually, any different
> of these two languages? What are its strengths and weaknesses?

Yes, RPG is very similar to COBOL. But it also has some different as
well. For example, some old RPG programs are coded by RPG life cycle
so that it is not very eary to convert to COBOL. Some command such as
MOVEL (move left), I found that it is not easy to convert too (or I
may not know the command).

The strength of COBOL is easy to read but long to write. For RPG is
short to write but if you have a lot of nest-if and nest-loop, there
are very messy to follow.

If I have to select, I go with COBOL.

Anote
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL vs RPG

- **From:** "Dave" <begleyd@chartermi.net>
- **Date:** 2002-03-26T11:16:18-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ua17lmm130t7c@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<2a5e3706.0203201916.4cf775a2@posting.google.com> <7ce2d2dc.0203260501.43ecef73@posting.google.com> <a7ptnq$kpb$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
news:a7ptnq$kpb$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
:
: On 26-Mar-2002, anote_un@hotmail.com (Anote Sangkunakupt) wrote:
:
: > Yes, RPG is very similar to COBOL.
:
: Similar in what way?  (I haven't used RPG in 20 years but I don't
remember
: any particular similarities).

In it's latest free form version, with "bulit-in functions", there is
almost no difference.  The data specs are just like working storage,
you can define constants, etc.  A bult-in function example: when you
are readng through a file, instead of using indicators out in some
specific column, to check for end of file, it is just;

Read Filename
If  %eof(filename)
  xxxxxxx
Endif

(Or you could use, not %eof).

For random reads, RPG uses 'Chian', but then you just check, "%found",
instead of "invalid key".

There are no more columns, no more indicators needed.  My work is
about 50% COBOL, 50% RPG and it's a breeze, although sometimes I type
"MOVE *BLANKS" in my COBOL program (instead of "SPACES") which gives
me a compile error.

Dave
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL vs RPG

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2002-03-27T04:25:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020326232553.14073.00000276@mb-ce.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<a7ptnq$kpb$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
>From: "Howard Brazee" howard.brazee@cusys.edu 
>Date: 3/26/02 8:44 AM Eastern Standard Time

>
>Similar in what way?  (I haven't used RPG in 20 years but I don't remember
>any particular similarities).
>

Same here.  In fact I think RPG was the first 'programming language' I learned
in college, followed by FORTRAN.  Didn't start COBOL until the 2nd year.

About all I remember was at the time it was very positional, all those dang
indicators, level breaks and other stuff.

The other thing I remember was a particular payroll program fondly called
'chinese dentist' by some, that was contained in 2 boxes of cards (way back
when) and used all the idicators twice (at least).  The powers that were deemed
the program should be converted to COBOL (after the turn of the decade and it
deducted a full decade of deductions from everyone's paycheck because the year
field was only 1 position).  Someone had read several ads by companies that
claimed to convert RPG to COBOL and invited potential customers to send in a
sample to judge their product. We sent in this program.  It blew 3 companies
out of the water, and the 4th actually produced code and then demanded to know
what kind of trick program we were sending to them.

The COBOL code produced was even worse that the RPG program.  One of the senior
people finally coded it 'by hand'.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL vs RPG

- **From:** Steve Thompson <sthompsonNOSPAM@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-31T22:14:09-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CA7D081.8966D4BC@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<2a5e3706.0203201916.4cf775a2@posting.google.com>`

```
Samlai wrote:
> 
> I find that similarity between COBOL and RPG. Actually, any different
> of these two languages? What are its strengths and weaknesses?

Well, from an old RPG programmer that had to learn COBOL the
hard way (one week to read the book and have a working fix
for a cobol based payroll program or find a new job...), I
think I can answer this.

RPG was designed around the idea that one does I/O then
computes then does I/O. So, the general programming cycle
was as follows:

A) Read an INPUT file
B) Set control break level indicators on
C) If control break level indicators on, do control time
CALCULATIONS
D) IF control break level indicators on, do control time
OUTPUT
E) Interrogate input record to set general indicators
F) check to see if matching record indicators and other
special indicators need to be set
G) Move data from input record to fields
H) Detail time calculations
I) Detail/Exception OUTPUT
J) Go to A  

COBOL attempts to approximate this when one uses the REPORT
WRITER feature. However, it takes more code and time to set
up all of this in COBOL initially.

For RPG & RPGII, you have no control over OPEN and CLOSE of
your files. I believe this changed with the advent of
RPGIII.

If you post to me for more info or what have you, I am
currently on assignment at a client site and am only home on
weekends...
```

## Thread: Help on COMPUTE operands — 10 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Help on COMPUTE operands

- **From:** "Federico Ghigo" <f.ghigo@dicaf.it>
- **Date:** 2002-03-21T16:30:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OUnm8.13252$S52.396359@news2.tin.it>`

```
Hallo !
Is there anyone who can tell me if I can do an exponential computation with
the COMPUTE ? Which is the operand ?
I have a MF Object Cobol v.4.0, with the FUNCTION I've been able to find
only the EXP end EXP10 functions in which i cannot decide the base (it is
respectively 'e' or 10).

I have to compute something like this:

COMPUTE COMODO = WA-IMP * (1 - TAX / 100) ^ (GIORNI / 365).

(it's a financial actualization)

where for me ^ is the exponential symbol (the one I'm looking for in COBOL,
if exists).

Note that (GIORNI / 365) of course can be a decimal.

Thank you for your attention.
```

#### ↳ Re: Help on COMPUTE operands

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2002-03-21T12:20:59-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a7d4pr$bt0$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<OUnm8.13252$S52.396359@news2.tin.it>`

```
In article <OUnm8.13252$S52.396359@news2.tin.it>,
Federico Ghigo <f.ghigo@dicaf.it> wrote:
>Hallo !
>Is there anyone who can tell me if I can do an exponential computation with
>the COMPUTE ? Which is the operand ?

I think that exponentiation is non-standard... but in MF COBOL it is 
accomplished by **, like

COMPUTE TWO-CUBED = (2)**(3)

>I have a MF Object Cobol v.4.0, with the FUNCTION I've been able to find
>only the EXP end EXP10 functions in which i cannot decide the base (it is
>respectively 'e' or 10).

Usually one works in base 10; e is for natural (Naperian) logarithms and 
is 2.7182818284... or something like that, as my memory - which is, 
admittedly, porous - has it.

(I also remember 2.30259 as the conversion factor between lan(x) and 
log(x)... but it has been a few decades)

>
>I have to compute something like this:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>if exists).

As mentioned, try ** instead of ^.

As the Germans used to say... bona fortuna!

DD
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Help on COMPUTE operands

- **From:** "Tom Morrison" <t.morrison@liant.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-21T18:50:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OXpm8.69527$Ym.3685912752@newssvr30.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<OUnm8.13252$S52.396359@news2.tin.it> <a7d4pr$bt0$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:a7d4pr$bt0$1@panix1.panix.com...
[snip]
> I think that exponentiation is non-standard... but in MF COBOL it is
> accomplished by **, like
>
> COMPUTE TWO-CUBED = (2)**(3)

Nope, quite standard.  In ANSI X3.23-1985 (the latest standard!), section
6.2.2 "Arithmetic Expressions" the ** operator for exponentiation is on the
list.

Tom Morrison
Liant Software Corporation
```

#### ↳ Re: Help on COMPUTE operands

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@invalid.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-21T17:31:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<LNom8.90443$1g.7493744@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<OUnm8.13252$S52.396359@news2.tin.it>`

```

"Federico Ghigo" <f.ghigo@dicaf.it> wrote in message
news:OUnm8.13252$S52.396359@news2.tin.it...
> Hallo !
> Is there anyone who can tell me if I can do an exponential computation
with
> the COMPUTE ? Which is the operand ?
> I have a MF Object Cobol v.4.0, with the FUNCTION I've been able to find
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Change " ^ " to " ** "

Such that: COMPUTE A = B ** C

subject to the rules that:
1. B not = 0 and B not > 0
2. you don't have something like (-1)**0.5
```

#### ↳ Re: Help on COMPUTE operands

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-03-21T20:02:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%_qm8.1075$UN2.972@nwrddc02.gnilink.net>`
- **References:** `<OUnm8.13252$S52.396359@news2.tin.it>`

```
Federico Ghigo <f.ghigo@dicaf.it> wrote in message
news:OUnm8.13252$S52.396359@news2.tin.it...
> Hallo !
> Is there anyone who can tell me if I can do an exponential computation
with
> the COMPUTE ? Which is the operand ?
> I have a MF Object Cobol v.4.0, with the FUNCTION I've been able to find
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> COMPUTE COMODO = WA-IMP * (1 - TAX / 100) ^ (GIORNI / 365).

It doesn't matter if you use base 'e' or base 10 or base whatever, as long
as you use matching-base logarithm and exponentiation functions (LOG and EXP
below):

   COMODO = WA-IMP * EXP(LOG (1-TAX/100) * (GIORINI/365))

MCM
```

#### ↳ Re: Help on COMPUTE operands

- **From:** "WOB" <wobconsult@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-21T19:47:35-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a7duea$n20$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<OUnm8.13252$S52.396359@news2.tin.it>`

```
It's represented as two asterisks.

Compute a square root example:

03 WS-SQRT    PIC S9(08)V07 PACKED-DECIMAL.
03 WS-VALUE PIC S9(15)         PACKED-DECIMAL.

COMPUTE WS-SQRT = (WS-VALUE ** .5)

HTH....

Bill
```

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2002-03-21T13:48:02-05:00

```
In article <gk7k9uo3k63tufdns2c3jf26hvf9dll1ds@4ax.com>,
Ron  <ron1@address.below> wrote:
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>>Since the transmission regimen is specified the next step is to look at 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>arbritrary contentent.

The file on the mainframe contains appropriate characters.

The file on the mainframe, when transferred uncompressed to the PC, 
generates a file on the PC containing appropriate characters.

The file on the mainframe, when compressed, transferred and uncompressed, 
generates a file on the PC containing inappropriate characters.

Since transfer without compression generated appropriate characters and 
transfer with compression/decompression (both by PKZip) generated 
inappropriate characters then a reasonable conclusion is that the mangling 
of the data was caused by...

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: Slightly OT: tab-delimited, pkzip, and mainframe to PC xfer

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-21T12:54:03-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a7da8f$8ou$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3c98afe5.183457778@ausnews.austin.ibm.com> <3C99EC67.848098F7@nnn.nissan-usa.com> <a7d11e$s4l$1@panix1.panix.com> <gk7k9uo3k63tufdns2c3jf26hvf9dll1ds@4ax.com> <a7d9t2$1js$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
Actually,  my guess is that this is not a problem with "PKZIP" - as such -
but rather a problem with the fact that PKZIP on the mainframe and on the PC
are not the same product (and/or same release).  In other words, they are
using (slightly) different "compression" algorithms and what gets compressed
on the mainframe (via one algorithm) is NOT getting expanded on the PC with
the same algorithm.

My guess is that DD *might* agree with this (and isn't saying something
particularly different).  However, some people may not be aware of the
distinction of multiple "products" called PKZIP - and changes in compression
routines between (among?) them.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Slightly OT: tab-delimited, pkzip, and mainframe to PC xfer

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2002-03-21T14:42:33-05:00

```
In article <a7da8f$8ou$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>,
William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:
>Actually,  my guess is that this is not a problem with "PKZIP" - as such -
>but rather a problem with the fact that PKZIP on the mainframe and on the PC
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>routines between (among?) them.

Version discrepancy is another possibility, sure... also that whacy 
EBCDIC-to-ASCII translation going on.  Consider:

According to my Yellow Card x'09' ASCII is 'HT' ('Hard Tab'?) and EBCDIC 
'SPS'; x'05' is EBCDIC 'HT' and ASCII 'ENQ'.  What would result from 
coding x'09' on the mainframe, zipping, transferring/translating and 
unzipping is anyone's guess.

DD

><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:a7d9t2$1js$1@panix1.panix.com...
>> In article <gk7k9uo3k63tufdns2c3jf26hvf9dll1ds@4ax.com>,
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Slightly OT: tab-delimited, pkzip, and mainframe to PC xfer

- **From:** Kyle Cothron <kyle.cothron@nnn.nissan-usa.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-22T09:33:10-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C9B4EB6.FE9AD5CF@nnn.nissan-usa.com>`
- **References:** `<3c98afe5.183457778@ausnews.austin.ibm.com> <gk7k9uo3k63tufdns2c3jf26hvf9dll1ds@4ax.com> <a7d9t2$1js$1@panix1.panix.com> <a7da8f$8ou$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <a7dd39$fts$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
Turns out you are correct on the Hex translation.  We erroneously chose 09.  I
changed it to 05 and got the correct translation for tab.  Before this I had to
specifiy -TEXT to force this conversion.  Turns out that PKZIP interrogates the
file to determine the type of compression to use.  The presence of
non-displayable characters tripped it up.  Forcing the TEXT option got the
desired results.  Thanks for your help on this.
```

```
at the original problem. How did you know that the resulting file was
garbage? I'm guessing that if you had examined a dump of the "garbage
file" on the PC you would have found EBCDIC characters, and the source
of your problem would have been apparent much more quickly.
```

```
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
>DD

One thing the original poster could do to see if PKZIP compression is
causing a problem is to create a PKZIP file with no compression.  This
is an option in PKZIP.  He could create such a file with a couple of
small tab delimited files, download it to the PC, unzip it and see
what happened.

Regards,

reducing it to the question, "How would the Lone Ranger handle this?"
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

<a id="t-66a326e946"></a>
## Thread: Compaq COBOL with Java on OpenVMS Alpha — 1 msgs

### Compaq COBOL with Java on OpenVMS Alpha

- **From:** don.braffitt@compaq.com (Don Braffitt)
- **Date:** 2002-03-23T03:03:37-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<14c5ce2f.0203230303.547ba4d7@posting.google.com>`

```
We had a customer request for an example of using Java with
Compaq COBOL on Alpha.  I had previously posted an example
for Tru64 UNIX:

  http://groups.google.com/groups?q=java+cobol+braffitt&hl=en&btnG=Google+Search
    
Here is JNI_EXAMPLE.COM modified to work with Compaq COBOL on
OpenVMS Alpha.

- Don Braffitt
  Compaq COBOL and SORT project leader
    
$! From JNI_EXAMPLE.COM
$ @SYS$COMMON:[JAVA$131.COM]JAVA$131_SETUP
$ SAY = "write sys$output "
$ say "1. compiling jni5.java"
$ javac "jni5.java"
$ say "2. generating header file jni5.h"
$ javah -jni jni5
$ say "3. compiling jni5c.c"
$ opts = "/prefix=all /float=ieee /ieee=denorm " + -
         "/names=as_is /reentrancy=multithread /stand=ms /nolist"
$ cc 'opts' /include=(SYS$COMMON:[JAVA$131.INCLUDE...]) -
  /obj=jni5c.obj jni5c.c
$ say "4. compiling jni5cob.cob"
$ opts = "/float=ieee /nolist"
$ cobol 'opts' jni5cob.cob
$ say "5. linking jni5"
$ GLB_OPT :== global_symbol_option
$ LIB_OPT :== library_option
$ @dcom$:scan_globals_for_option jni5*.obj 'GLB_OPT'.opt
$ OPEN/WRITE library_opt_file 'LIB_OPT'.opt
$ write library_opt_file "GSMATCH=LEQUAL,1,1"
$ write library_opt_file "jni5c.obj"
$ write library_opt_file "jni5cob.obj"
$ write library_opt_file "JAVA$JAVA_SHR/SHARE"
$ close library_opt_file
$ DEBUG_SWITCH :== /nodebug/notrace
$ IMAGE_NAME :== jni5
$ link/''DEBUG_SWITCH'/SHARE/exec=java$'IMAGE_NAME'_shr.exe -
  'LIB_OPT /OPT,'GLB_OPT /OPT /NOMAP
$ say "6. defining a logical for the new shareable image"
$ def/job/log java$'IMAGE_NAME'_shr sys$disk:[]java$'IMAGE_NAME'_shr.exe
$ say "7. running jni5"
$ java jni5
$done:
$!    
$ type jni5.cob
identification division.
program-id. JNI5COBLONG.
environment division.
data division.
linkage section.
01 llong pic s9(9) comp.
procedure division using llong.
p1.     add 1 to llong.
        exit program.
end program JNI5COBLONG.

identification division.
program-id. JNI5COBSTRING.
environment division.
data division.
working-storage section.
01 wloc pic 9(9) comp.
linkage section.
01 lstring pic x(100).
procedure division using lstring.
p2.     call "FINDNULL" using lstring giving wloc.
        move "2" to lstring(4:1).
        exit program.
identification division.
program-id. FINDNULL.
environment division.
data division.
working-storage section.
01 wloc pic 9(9) comp.
01 wsub pic x(100).
linkage section.
01 lstring pic x(100).
procedure division using lstring giving wloc.
p3.     unstring lstring delimited by "
        exit program.
end program FINDNULL.
end program JNI5COBSTRING.
$!
$ type jni5.java
class jni5 {
  public static native long   jni5long(long arg1);
  public static native String jni5string(String arg1);

  static {
    System.loadLibrary("jni5");
  }

  public static void main (String[] args) {
    System.out.println("Java JNI5 long   = " + Long.toString(jni5long(1)));
    System.out.println("Java JNI5 string = " + jni5string("Str1"));
  }
}
$!
$ type jni5c.c
#include "jni5.h"
#include <stdio.h>
#include <string.h>
void JNI5COBLONG(long *arg1);
void JNI5COBSTRING(char *arg1);

JNIEXPORT jlong JNICALL Java_jni5_jni5long (JNIEnv *env, jclass this,
    jlong arg1) {
  long retlong;
  retlong = arg1;
  printf("C    JNI5 long   = %d (before call to COBOL)\n", retlong);
  JNI5COBLONG(&retlong);
  printf("C    JNI5 long   = %d (after  call to COBOL)\n", retlong);
  return retlong;
}

JNIEXPORT jstring JNICALL Java_jni5_jni5string (JNIEnv *env, jclass this,
    jstring arg1) {
  const char *tmp = (*env)->GetStringUTFChars(env, arg1, 0);
  char retstring[100];
  strcpy(retstring, tmp);
  printf("C    JNI5 string = %s (before call to COBOL)\n", retstring);
  JNI5COBSTRING(retstring);
  printf("C    JNI5 string = %s (after  call to COBOL)\n", retstring);
  (*env)->ReleaseStringUTFChars(env, arg1, tmp);
  return (*env)->NewStringUTF(env, retstring);
}

$! end of example
```

## Thread: COBOL FAQ — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Meta: FAQs, group policy, charter`](topics.md#meta)_

### COBOL FAQ

- **From:** wmklein@ix.netcom.com (William M. Klein)
- **Date:** 2002-03-23T11:30:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol,news.answers,comp.answers,alt.answers
- **Message-ID:** `<computer-lang/cobol-faq_1016882987@rtfm.mit.edu>`

```
Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-Modified: 2000/07/02
Version: 3.01
URL: http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm
Reply-To: wmklein@ix.netcom.com


                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Sunday, July 2, 2000

The COBOL FAQ has moved.  To view the most recent version, please check out the
web version at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send private
email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

The following is Appendix B.5 - Changes to create Version 3.01

 o Recorded new location of the FAQ
 o Updated some old (bad) web links
 o Added some new COBOL books (particularly those written or contributed to - by
frequent comp.lang.cobol submitters).
 o Added information about LegacyJ (PERCobol) and changed the company name from
"Synkronix" to "LegacyJ".
 o Updated information for changes in product and company names and addresses
where available
 o Updated the URLs for downloading the draft of the next COBOL Standard and
CobCy
 o Started the Samples/Example Section
 o Tried to get rid of most specific references to Windows/NT, Windows/95,
Windows/98, and Windows/2000.
```

## Thread: Printing in NetExpress — 15 this month / 16 total

### Printing in NetExpress

- **From:** "Dave" <begleyd@chartermi.net>
- **Date:** 2002-03-23T13:16:39-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<u9phju5feelg60@corp.supernews.com>`

```
I've been away in the AS/400 world for a while so my NetExpress is a
little rusty.

I went back to work on an application and whenever I open the printer,
the application stops.  It used to work, and the only difference is
that my default printer is now a USB printer (Epson C80), instead of a
parallel printer.  I thought assigning to "PRINTER" just picked the
default printer.

It's pretty simple:

In the File-Control
           SELECT PRTFIL
             ASSIGN PRINTER.

In the program
           OPEN OUTPUT PRTFIL.

It just stops without any explanation and is stuck. I put a file
status on the select statement, but no status is reported.

I'm still running 3.0, but seriously considering upgrading.  I hate to
waste $650 every year for nothing.
```

```
:

"ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL" to the SELECT statement.  Now the
program gets past the OPEN statement, but freezes at the first WRITE
statement.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Printing in NetExpress

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <frederico_fonseca@eudoramail.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-23T23:02:35+00:00

```
On Sat, 23 Mar 2002 17:32:08 -0500, "Dave" <begleyd@chartermi.net>
wrote:

>More....
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>in half.

This happens a lot with USB printers.
As you are using win2000 try and do the following.
1- Share the printer as "name1".
2- Add a new printer and say it is a network printer and that you
wanna use this new printer as the default.
When you are asked for a printer name just browse and search for
printer "name1" in your own computer.
3- In the COBOL program print to this printer

This should work fine.

FF
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Printing in NetExpress

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <frederico_fonseca@eudoramail.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-24T22:55:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3gls9usb05tmrpk530q06st37opojgit9q@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<u9phju5feelg60@corp.supernews.com> <u9q0ibodsu324b@corp.supernews.com> <gq1q9u83piieaa4r5s861jajhor68pbeqp@4ax.com> <u9sc2m5jj7vu1f@corp.supernews.com>`

```
On Sun, 24 Mar 2002 15:00:46 -0500, "Dave" <begleyd@chartermi.net>
wrote:

>"Frederico Fonseca" <frederico_fonseca@eudoramail.com> wrote in
>message news:gq1q9u83piieaa4r5s861jajhor68pbeqp@4ax.com...
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>what you said.  What did you mean, in step 3?

Basically and assuming that the printer is working properly from 
other Windows applications this would work as a "work around".

By doing like I told you are no longer printing to a "local" printer
but to a network printer so it should work fine.

So a basic setup is
Local printer HPxxx (USB or PARALLEL) shared as "HPshared"
A new printer named "hpserverX" is set-up pointing to printer
"\\server\HPshared" (set this one as the default printer)

In the COBOL program you either print to the default printer or select
"hpserverX" directly.

Was this what you've tried??

If it does not work can you try and print from a command line (do a 
dir >\\server\HPshared )

Tell us what happens.

FF
```

```
Aren't those CGI printers? If so, you *cannot* send text to them. They
simply will move the head around a little and then just act like you didn't
send them anything. (*sigh*)

You have to tap into the Windows printing system and use the driver to RIP
that text into something
the printer eats. To be honest, I don't always have a lot of success doing
that. One reason I
*never* buy CGI printers anymore - even if it does cost a couple hundred
extra bucks to get a printer
with PostScript, or at least PCL in it.

-Paul
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Printing in NetExpress

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** graham@olympic.co.uk (graham)

- **Message-ID:** `<8a02b0a1.0203261026.4f5d666a@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<u9phju5feelg60@corp.supernews.com> <u9q0ibodsu324b@corp.supernews.com> <gq1q9u83piieaa4r5s861jajhor68pbeqp@4ax.com> <u9sc2m5jj7vu1f@corp.supernews.com> <3gls9usb05tmrpk530q06st37opojgit9q@4ax.com> <u9u3of2c10ak54@corp.supernews.com> <u9u79vh040e972@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"Dave" <begleyd@chartermi.net> wrote in message news:<u9u79vh040e972@corp.supernews.com>...
> Something I should have done a long time ago, I hooked up the printer
> with a parallel cable and it still doesn't work.  (For some reason, I

Are you sure that the printer works at all? The obvious thing is the
WINDOWS printer test page...

I would agree with what Frederico said earlier and have found that the
approach does usually work but we do have one instance where it
doesn't (and I will have to visit that customer shortly and sort it
out somehow!) so it obviously doesn't work in all situations.

In essence we found that while WIN95 seemed fine when printing to
"LST" or "LPT1" later WINDOWS weren't so keen on it. An alternative is
to use the UNC name as the file name so actually OPEN the file
"\\server\printer-name" where "printer-name" is the share name (but
you've got to create the printer as a local shared printer") and we
found that worked in most cases (except as above!)

Finally the best solution seems to be to be the most complicated and
that is to user the PC_PRINTER_xxx routines (although it raises other
issues and problems, as I've asked about here); these do appear to get
into the WINDOWS printer interface reasonably successfully and in the
instance above where the old style (OPEN/WRITE etc.) does not work the
PC_PRINTER_xx do write successfully to the same printer that cannot be
accessed otherwise!

graham
```

```
So, do you change your programs to print everything to a file and than
use the PC_PRINTER_xx stuff to print the file?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Printing in NetExpress

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** graham@olympic.co.uk (graham)
- **Date:** 2002-03-27T10:47:15-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a02b0a1.0203271047.285d649@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<u9phju5feelg60@corp.supernews.com> <u9q0ibodsu324b@corp.supernews.com> <gq1q9u83piieaa4r5s861jajhor68pbeqp@4ax.com> <u9sc2m5jj7vu1f@corp.supernews.com> <3gls9usb05tmrpk530q06st37opojgit9q@4ax.com> <u9u3of2c10ak54@corp.supernews.com> <u9u79vh040e972@corp.supernews.com> <8a02b0a1.0203261026.4f5d666a@posting.google.com> <ua1iqga90dnb8b@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"Dave" <begleyd@chartermi.net> wrote in message news:<ua1iqga90dnb8b@corp.supernews.com>...
> 
> Could I ask a stupid question?  If I use the PC_PRINTER_xx routines,
> than I can't print a line at a time like a regular, old-fashion
> program?  Because PC_PRINTER_OPEN needs a document name?

No...PC_PRINTER_OPEN is set to browse a WINDOWS network and returns a
Printer Selection Window (and font selection, if desired), as in the
Help...

"A set of bits to define printer options:
bit 0  Display a printer control dialog to allow you to define the
printer characteristics (cannot be used with bit 2 or bit 3)
bit 1  Display a font dialog box to allow you to define the default
font for the document
bit 2  Print in portrait orientation (cannot be used with bit 0 or bit
3)
bit 3  Print in landscape orientation (cannot be used with bit 0 or
bit 2)
bit 4  Display a progress indicator dialog box"

so setting "flags" to 3 you get to choose the printer *and* the font.
The "document title" is just a name you give the document that would
appear in the printer spooler entry (or some such) but it is *not* a
file name.
PC_PRINTER_WRITE then is used for sending data and PC_PRINTER_CONTROL
for line and form feeds as required.


> So, do you change your programs to print everything to a file and than
> use the PC_PRINTER_xx stuff to print the file?

just PC_PRINTER_OPEN, gets the printer_handle, if succesful that is,
and then use the handle in ..._WRITE ..._CONTROL and ..._CLOSE

> I work almost all the time in an AS/400 shop and I am just not keeping
> up with the way to use NetExpress.  None of the samples on the web
> site seem to address reading through files and printing a report.  All
> the samples deal with printing documents.

I hadn't looked at the examples, I'm afraid!


graham
```

<a id="t-85293dd547"></a>
## Thread: multiple record format in a data file — 8 this month / 9 total

### multiple record format in a data file

- **From:** "Sim" <schowe@pacific.net.sg>
- **Date:** 2002-03-24T10:42:52+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a7jehn$3v7$1@newton.pacific.net.sg>`

```
Hi
I have difficulty describing my record format of my input data file for my
file description.

my data files goes like this.

AAA03
AAA056
AAA065
AAA078
BBB02
BBB088
BBB092

first line AAA indicate a group of people beloging to group AAA with 3
people inside

Next 3 subsecquent line will be the group they are in follow by amount of
apple they have.
therefore first people has 56 apples, second people have 65 apples
after the third people, it will be a newgroup with 2 people.

But how am i suppose to do my FD with multiple record format?
I tried doing a group FD
such as 01 Group-People.
           05 Group    pic   xxx.
           05 People   pic    xx.
        01 Group-Amount.
           05 Group    pic   xxx.
           05 Amount   pic   999.

but I still failed. Any help will be appreciated.
```

#### ↳ Re: multiple record format in a data file

- **From:** lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca
- **Date:** 2002-03-24T03:27:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-hdrW4Hc5vvRd@thishost>`
- **References:** `<a7jehn$3v7$1@newton.pacific.net.sg>`

```
On Sun, 24 Mar 2002 02:42:52 UTC, "Sim" <schowe@pacific.net.sg> wrote:

> Hi
> I have difficulty describing my record format of my input data file for my
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
> but I still failed. Any help will be appreciated.

How about

01  group-people.
  02  group-name pic xxx.
  02  group-number pic 99.
01  group-amount.
  02  grpa-name pic xxx.
  02  grpa-amount pic 999.

That is given that the file really is variable length.

01  group-people.
  02  group-name pic xxx.
  02  group-number pic 99.
  02  filler pic x.
01  group-amount.
  02  grpa-name pic xxx.
  02  grpa-amount pic 999.

would do it if the file is fixed length.

The SELECT clause would determine if the file is
regular "sequential" or "line sequential" (a PC text file).
```

#### ↳ Re: multiple record format in a data file

- **From:** "Sim" <schowe@pacific.net.sg>
- **Date:** 2002-03-24T16:54:35+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a7k4am$ekv$1@newton.pacific.net.sg>`
- **References:** `<a7jehn$3v7$1@newton.pacific.net.sg>`

```
I found a solution. Here it is.

.
.
.
SELECT Apples
              ASSIGN TO "apples.txt".
   DATA DIVISION.
       FILE SECTION.
       FD   F100-GROUP-APPLE
            RECORD CONTAINS 20 CHARACTERS
            DATA RECORD IS W100-INPUT-DISPLAY.

WORKING STORAGE SECTION.
01  W100-INPUT-DISPLAY
      05  W110-GROUP.
            10 W111-GROUP                    PIC   XXX.
            10 W112-GROUP-PEOPLE    PIC   99.
      05  W120-GROUP.
            10 W121-GROUP                     PIC XXX.
            10 W122-APPLES                    PIC 999.

Therefore, when i need to put in my first read record,
I can use put THIS STATEMENT  "  READ APPLES INTO W110-GROUP."
Similiar, If  i need to read my second line i can put "READ APPLES INTO
W120-GROUP"

After attending my second lecture, I just realised it can be done in this
manner. Just wanna share with people in this newsgroup with this sample
answer.

Thanks a lot

"Sim" <schowe@pacific.net.sg> wrote in message
news:a7jehn$3v7$1@newton.pacific.net.sg...
> Hi
> I have difficulty describing my record format of my input data file for my
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: multiple record format in a data file

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-03-24T16:14:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C9DFB60.FE903409@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<a7jehn$3v7$1@newton.pacific.net.sg>`

```
Sim wrote:
> 
> Hi
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> people inside

Why ?  Why do you have these lines ?  It is possible to determine how
many exist by counting the number of records with the matching key, so
this is somewhat redundant.

If you must have different record types then use a 'record type' field
(if you can).  Change this to be:
     type C=count, P=Person
      | sequence 3 digit
      | |  value 3 digit number in group/number of apples
      v V  V
   AAAC000003
   AAAP001056
   AAAP002065
   etc.

This could be an indexed file (your file cannot because there is no
uniqueness), the key would be the first 7 characters.
 
> Next 3 subsecquent line will be the group they are in follow by amount of
> apple they have.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> but I still failed. Any help will be appreciated.

You probably can't tell if it is a group record or people record, though
the first of a code should be the group.

But what about it 'fails', does it fail to compile or does it fail to
open, or does the program just not work.

You may have to send more details, such as your select.  If you have
ORGANIZATION SEQUENTIAL (or no organization) then you may be getting
complete nonsense, if the file has been created with an editor then use
ORGANIZATION LINE SEQUENTIAL.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: multiple record format in a data file

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-24T09:02:41+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<t3uq9ug3tlgrlgujrols9l1to0mu9rqngg@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<a7jehn$3v7$1@newton.pacific.net.sg> <3C9DFB60.FE903409@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
On Sun, 24 Mar 2002 16:14:24 +0000 Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
wrote:

:>Sim wrote:
 
:>> Hi
:>> I have difficulty describing my record format of my input data file for my
:>> file description.
 
:>> my data files goes like this.
 
:>> AAA03
:>> AAA056
:>> AAA065
:>> AAA078
:>> BBB02
:>> BBB088
:>> BBB092
 
:>> first line AAA indicate a group of people beloging to group AAA with 3
:>> people inside

:>Why ?  Why do you have these lines ?  It is possible to determine how
:>many exist by counting the number of records with the matching key, so
:>this is somewhat redundant.

1. This is probably a homework assignment. But, even if not:

2. One quite often adds summary totals when shipping tapes. This allows the
receiving side to detect something wrong. You do use checksums, do you not?

:>If you must have different record types then use a 'record type' field
:>(if you can).  Change this to be:
:>     type C=count, P=Person
:>      | sequence 3 digit
:>      | |  value 3 digit number in group/number of apples
:>      v V  V
:>   AAAC000003
:>   AAAP001056
:>   AAAP002065
:>   etc.

Real world case - you do not control the input formats.

:>This could be an indexed file (your file cannot because there is no
:>uniqueness), the key would be the first 7 characters.
 
:>> Next 3 subsecquent line will be the group they are in follow by amount of
:>> apple they have.
:>> therefore first people has 56 apples, second people have 65 apples
:>> after the third people, it will be a newgroup with 2 people.
 
:>> But how am i suppose to do my FD with multiple record format?
:>> I tried doing a group FD
:>> such as 01 Group-People.
:>>            05 Group    pic   xxx.
:>>            05 People   pic    xx.
:>>         01 Group-Amount.
:>>            05 Group    pic   xxx.
:>>            05 Amount   pic   999.
 
:>> but I still failed. Any help will be appreciated.

:>You probably can't tell if it is a group record or people record, though
:>the first of a code should be the group.

Exactly. If the group id is different it is the start of a new group.

:>But what about it 'fails', does it fail to compile or does it fail to
:>open, or does the program just not work.

I would expect compile, but I could be wrong.

:>You may have to send more details, such as your select.  If you have
:>ORGANIZATION SEQUENTIAL (or no organization) then you may be getting
:>complete nonsense, if the file has been created with an editor then use
:>ORGANIZATION LINE SEQUENTIAL.

One guesses that the FD does not match the record.
```

#### ↳ Re: multiple record format in a data file

- **From:** "Blitz" <fraggle74@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-26T06:39:21+11:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d2Ln8.913$Go6.90874@ozemail.com.au>`
- **References:** `<a7jehn$3v7$1@newton.pacific.net.sg>`

```
try this

in FD
leave 01 group-people     without the subsets
         01 group-amount    without the subsets
in working storage
declare
     01 group-people-in
           05 Group    pic   xxx.
           05 People   pic    xx.

    01 group-amount-in
           05 Group    pic   xxx.
           05 Amount   pic   999.

later on in the prog when you are reading the records you can use a read
into line eg
for reading   bbb02 line : read blah blah file into   01 group-people-in
                   bbb88 line : read blah blah file into 01 group-amount-in

hope this helps
```

### arrow keys

- **From:** "Bj���rn M���ller" <G7b9@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-24T14:28:39+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a7kkcs$lmo07$1@ID-63521.news.dfncis.de>`

```
how can i link an event on pressing the arrow-keys on my keyboard
(right,left,up,down) in a cobol-program??
for example:
"if arrow-key right is pressed
    read next."


thx
bjoern
```

#### ↳ Re: arrow keys

- **From:** lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca
- **Date:** 2002-03-24T14:20:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-eGFYdsrQUhjQ@thishost>`
- **References:** `<a7kkcs$lmo07$1@ID-63521.news.dfncis.de>`

```
On Sun, 24 Mar 2002 13:28:39 UTC, "Bjï¿½rn Mï¿½ller" <G7b9@aol.com> wrote:

> how can i link an event on pressing the arrow-keys on my keyboard
> (right,left,up,down) in a cobol-program??
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

You need to use some kind of logic to read the key presses. How you 
retrieve the key press depends on the facilities provided by the 
compiler runtime libraries. If the run time libraries do not provide 
keyboard control routines you will have to write your own using your 
operating system API calls.

To do it properly you should a have the "keyboard scanning" routine 
running on its own thread (if your compiler supports multi-threading) 
and it starts another thread that handles the key press event if you 
want special treatment for that key. This is one of the areas that OO 
COBOL can do a good job if the compiler supports running different 
classes on different threads.
```

#### ↳ Re: arrow keys

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-03-25T06:41:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C9EC67C.E72E19F2@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<a7kkcs$lmo07$1@ID-63521.news.dfncis.de>`

```
Bjï¿½rn Mï¿½ller wrote:
> 
> how can i link an event on pressing the arrow-keys on my keyboard
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>     read next."

What environment (operating system), which compiler and how are you
currently interacting with the keyboard (eg accept) ?

If this is DOS/Console/Terminal then start by looking up SPECIAL-NAME.
CONSOLE IS CRT CRTSTATUS IS variable.  and ACCEPT FROM CRT.

You may nned to read your manuals on the subject, such as 'Screen
handling'.
```

```
least

without
any kind of troubles.

Gianni
```

#### ↳ Re: Power Cobol 6.1 X Vrs 5.0

- **From:** "Denny Brouse" <denden@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2002-03-27T03:36:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<A6bo8.722$PN5.118992023@newssvr16.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<3C9F441A.137A961E@attglobal.net>`

```
Carlos,

I do basically what Gianni suggested regarding the laptop, except that I
don't "transfer in" and "transfer out".  Since I could not afford two 6.1
licenses for home use, I save my 6.1 projects in a unique folder, i.e.
MYPROJECT and I make sure the project file (myproject.ppj) is in the same
folder.  Then I network the folder over to my laptop and recompile the
project using Version 5.0 that I have on the laptop.  Obviously, if you are
using some features exclusive to 6.1, then this will not work.  But for most
of what I do, it's worked fine since 6.1 was released.  I think I've
encountered some minor exceptions with certain control properties, but I
can't recall what they were at this point in time, and I usually found a
different way to accomplish the same task using 5.0.  If I start using 6.1
features, then I'll "transfer in" and "transfer out" as Gianni suggested.

Also, if you ever upgrade hard drives as I did, you may need to call Fujitsu
to get your license reinstated.  Fujitsu was most helpful in doing this, but
it took a day or two. The Maxtor drive image utility that came with the new
hard drive did not copy the Fujitsu protection scheme over properly.  Just
something to keep in mind if your hard drive actually crashes and you have
clients waiting for fixes.

Regards,

Denny

news:3C9F441A.137A961E@attglobal.net...

…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
```


#### ↳ Re: executable file with netexpress

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-03-25T18:36:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C9F6D88.8DC22671@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<a7nlkl$mrsmv$1@ID-63521.news.dfncis.de>`

```


"Bjï¿½rn Mï¿½ller" wrote:

> hi
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Haven't looked in a while, but I'm fairly certain both 3.0 and 3.1 came with  a
small starter handbook - and this was one of the things covered. There's more to
this in terms of distribution of the end product (Look at on-line help on
"Distributing your Application", support DLLs etc.),  and I'm assuming you are
using the IDE :-

1 - create project and add source files. The sub-directory holding your source
will have sub-directories \Debug and \Release
2 - Top Left it reads "Generic Debug Build" - from "Project" dropdown, selecting
Build or whatever gets your "ints" into the \Debug sub-directory
3 - to get your EXE(s) change the 'Generic Debug Build" to "Generic Release
Build" and then build again; results stored in \Release sub-directory.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
    With the old 16 bit product (workbench), they had a separate program
(generate) that greated GNT or OBJ files from the INT file.

    I suspect that the same sort of thing might be possible with NETX,
but I do not know of any sure way to do it without source code from
the IDE.  You might be able to do it from the command line.

    Of course, if you DO have the source code ...

"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:3C9F6D88.8DC22671@shaw.ca...
>
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> > can someone give an step-by-step explanation how to create a executable
file
> > from
> >  a *.int in netexpress 3.0??
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Haven't looked in a while, but I'm fairly certain both 3.0 and 3.1 came
with  a
> small starter handbook - and this was one of the things covered. There's
more to
> this in terms of distribution of the end product (Look at on-line help on
> "Distributing your Application", support DLLs etc.),  and I'm assuming you
are
> using the IDE :-
>
> 1 - create project and add source files. The sub-directory holding your
source
> will have sub-directories \Debug and \Release
> 2 - Top Left it reads "Generic Debug Build" - from "Project" dropdown,
selecting
> Build or whatever gets your "ints" into the \Debug sub-directory
> 3 - to get your EXE(s) change the 'Generic Debug Build" to "Generic
Release
> Build" and then build again; results stored in \Release sub-directory.
>
> Jimmy, Calgary AB
>
>
```

#### ↳ Re: executable file with netexpress

- **From:** MRandall3@aol.com (M. Randall)
- **Date:** 2002-03-25T20:17:06-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e2d21d15.0203252017.6cad25f@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<a7nlkl$mrsmv$1@ID-63521.news.dfncis.de>`

```
I am using Net Express 3.1, I would think that it is similiar
procedure as 3.0.  I believe that all you would have to do is right
click on the file you want to create as executable - from the left
pane of the IDE.  One of the options is "package selected files" and
then select "executable file (exe)".  And you can add any other files
to it that you want packaged in that executable.

I hope that helps,
Mike
```

## Thread: moving data from pic 99 to pic 99 — 8 msgs

### moving data from pic 99 to pic 99

- **From:** "Blitz" <fraggle74@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-26T06:31:00+11:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<oWKn8.911$Go6.90548@ozemail.com.au>`

```
hi all

When copying a numeric number (num1   pic 99).  that has an error in it eg
8x to another (num2 pic99) instead of copying 8x to the new variable, it
changes to 81 or 82 or 83 so on depending on the different errors i type in
(8x,8y so on). Is there anyway of copying the original variable across
without it changing and without have to declare num2 as pic x.

thanx craig
```

#### ↳ Re: moving data from pic 99 to pic 99

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-03-25T15:15:53-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hzLn8.3978$ra.574555@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<oWKn8.911$Go6.90548@ozemail.com.au>`

```
I expect that what you are doing (by mistake) is converting all the signed
numbers to unsigned numbers. Are you sure that it is not supposed to be
picture S99.?  If the data is not numeric, then why not describe it as
picture XX, then check it to see if it is numeric before trying to do
something with it?


Donald

"Blitz" <fraggle74@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:oWKn8.911$Go6.90548@ozemail.com.au...
> hi all
>
> When copying a numeric number (num1   pic 99).  that has an error in it eg
> 8x to another (num2 pic99) instead of copying 8x to the new variable, it
> changes to 81 or 82 or 83 so on depending on the different errors i type
in
> (8x,8y so on). Is there anyway of copying the original variable across
> without it changing and without have to declare num2 as pic x.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: moving data from pic 99 to pic 99

- **From:** "Tom Morrison" <t.morrison@liant.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-25T20:33:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<AQLn8.3366$fu.1174687849@newssvr11.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<oWKn8.911$Go6.90548@ozemail.com.au>`

```
Use reference modification:

MOVE num1 (1:) TO num2 (1:).

Reference modification effectively converts the reference-modified operator
to alphanumeric.

Tom Morrison
Liant Software Corporation

"Blitz" <fraggle74@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:oWKn8.911$Go6.90548@ozemail.com.au...

> changes to 81 or 82 or 83 so on depending on the different errors i type
in

> without it changing and without have to declare num2 as pic x.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: moving data from pic 99 to pic 99

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-25T14:55:36-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a7o35h$nv5$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<oWKn8.911$Go6.90548@ozemail.com.au> <AQLn8.3366$fu.1174687849@newssvr11.news.prodigy.com>`

```
Note:
  Although Tom's suggestion will work (as an extension) with many compilers
REGARDLESS of the usage of Num1 and Num2, that isn't Standard - unless Num1
and Num2 are both explicitly or implicitly USAGE DISPLAY.

If they are actually COMP-? or Binary or Packed-Decimal, you can do either
of the following:

05 Num1-Alpha.
    10 Num1  Pic 99 Binary.
05 Num2-Alpha.
    10 Num2  Pic 99 Packed-Decimal.
     ...
Move Num1-Alpha to Num2-Alpha

***
or

05 Num1  Pic 99 Binary.
05 Num1-Alpha  Redefines Num1  Pic X.  *> depending on size of Pic 99 Binary
fields
05 Num2  Pic 99 Packed-Decimal.
05 Num2-Alpha redefines Num2  Pic XX.
     ...
Move Num1-Alpha to Num2-Alpha
     ...
Move Num1-Alpha to Num2-Alpha

   ***

The bottom-line is that when you define a "sending field" as numeric, the
data MUST "match" the picture (and USAGE) - or otherwise, results are
"undefined" (conversions, abnormal terminations, whatever may occur)
```

#### ↳ Re: moving data from pic 99 to pic 99

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-25T23:13:14+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<n93v9ugfpnt5fkagiubdnja5f9flfj9dh5@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<oWKn8.911$Go6.90548@ozemail.com.au>`

```
On Tue, 26 Mar 2002 06:31:00 +1100 "Blitz" <fraggle74@hotmail.com> wrote:

:>When copying a numeric number (num1   pic 99).  that has an error in it eg
:>8x to another (num2 pic99) instead of copying 8x to the new variable, it
:>changes to 81 or 82 or 83 so on depending on the different errors i type in
:>(8x,8y so on). Is there anyway of copying the original variable across
:>without it changing and without have to declare num2 as pic x.

Well, technically 8X is a valid signed number so you are not getting a data
check if on an IBM mainframe.

There is no way to directly move a non-numeric into a PIC 99 field. You can
move it to the group container, i.e.,

        05  number-container.
            10  num2  pic 99.

        move num1 to number-container.
```

#### ↳ Re: moving data from pic 99 to pic 99

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@invalid.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-26T00:42:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<buPn8.54303$7b.5583354@bin7.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<oWKn8.911$Go6.90548@ozemail.com.au>`

```

"Blitz" <fraggle74@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:oWKn8.911$Go6.90548@ozemail.com.au...
> hi all
>
> When copying a numeric number (num1   pic 99).  that has an error in it eg
> 8x to another (num2 pic99) instead of copying 8x to the new variable, it
> changes to 81 or 82 or 83 so on depending on the different errors i type
in
> (8x,8y so on). Is there anyway of copying the original variable across
> without it changing and without have to declare num2 as pic x.
>
> thanx craig

Why?

If you're going to have to change something, why eliminate one of the
possible fixes?

That said, how about:

   02  ORIG-NUM-X.
      05  ORIG-NUM   PIC 99.
...
   02  NEW-NUM-X.
      05  NEW-NUM   PIC 99.

...

MOVE ORIG-NUM-X TO NEW-NUM-X.
```

#### ↳ Re: moving data from pic 99 to pic 99

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2002-03-26T17:29:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Zd2o8.7410$se.697234@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<oWKn8.911$Go6.90548@ozemail.com.au>`

```
When you have "check numeric operands" turned off,
some compiler/runtimes will twist (for lack of a better
term) non numbers into numbers, depending on
the exact picture, runtime, etc, etc when certain
operations are performed.

I am assuming usage display.

To answer your question, NO.

You could do a group move.  Reference modification
turns any move into a group move.

news:oWKn8.911$Go6.90548@ozemail.com.au...

> 8x to another (num2 pic99) instead of copying 8x to the new variable, it
> changes to 81 or 82 or 83 so on depending on the different errors i type
in

> without it changing and without have to declare num2 as pic x.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: moving data from pic 99 to pic 99

- **From:** "Tom Morrison" <t.morrison@liant.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-26T21:00:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fk5o8.3592$LP1.1277517858@newssvr11.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<oWKn8.911$Go6.90548@ozemail.com.au> <Zd2o8.7410$se.697234@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
"Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@worldnet.att.net> wrote in message
news:Zd2o8.7410$se.697234@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net...
[snip]
> You could do a group move.  Reference modification
> turns any move into a group move.

Not to be too picky, but it is more accurate to say that a reference
modified numeric, numeric edited or alphanumeric edited operand is treated
as if it were an alphanumeric item.

In the particular case of a MOVE statement, consider the following:

01  AA  pic X(10) value "0123456789".
01  BB  pic 9(6).
01  CC  value "012".
    03  DD pic 9 occurs 3.
.....
MOVE AA(1:3) TO BB.

The result in BB is 000012.

MOVE CC to BB.

The results are quite different.

The first MOVE is _not_ turned into a group move.  A group move is not
equivalent to an elementary alphanumeric move.  (ANSI X3.23-1985, 6.19.4,
(4) b.3 and (5))

Tom Morrison
Liant Software Corporation
```

```
You can pick some real pretty ones. How about "Downsizing",
```

## Thread: Examples of Cobol calling WinAPI32 Functions — 7 this month / 9 total

### Examples of Cobol calling WinAPI32 Functions

- **From:** ysk@kimsoft.com (Young Kim)
- **Date:** 2002-03-25T16:53:34-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<69df745d.0203251653.3fe05dc7@posting.google.com>`

```
http://www.kimsoft.com/api-cobol/api-cobol.htm

Free examples of calling Windows API32 functions in COBOL are
available at the above web.

The examples were compiled and run with MicroFocus NetExpress 3.1. 
They were adapted from the C/C++ examples given in Microsoft Windows
2000 API
SuperBible, Richard J. Simon, SAMS.  I have taken the liberty of
recasting them in COBOL. COBOL commands were used as much as possible
for C/C++ statements that had no direct bearing on the API functions.

I recommend that you read Mr. Simon's outstanding book as you download
and test these examples.  I have found win32.hlp a 'must' supplement
to the SuperBible.  On many occasions, I had to compile and run the
C/C++ examples in order to find Windows constants and libraries not
covered in the standard windows.h.  Fortunately, SuperBible comes with
a CD that has the C/C++ examples - both source and object.
```

### Re: Off-topic: Rephrasing "employment terminated"?

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@NOSPAM-hot.rr.com>
