```
For heavens sake - rip it out and don't put it in there. Nobody is really going to be interested in what you 
did 14 years ago anyway. Write up what you have done in the 13 years you have been at your current job - 
including all the classes you have taken, successful projects, and emphasis on the technologies you have
mastered. 

-Paul

  <bgwillia@vcu.edu> wrote in message news:a7o5d1$4jga$1@mercury.vcu.edu...

  Hi, 

  I about to be laid off my COBOL job in about a month because of budget cuts. This is after thirteen years of loyal service.  As I was reviewing my resume and application forms, I noticed that I had to use the phrase "employment terminated" when I got fired from a job thirteen years ago (insubordination, or "I just didn't agree with the jerk of a boss at that time") .  When I asked my soon-to-be-former director about the phrase in my form, he said a hiring director would now see the phrase "employment terminated" as a red flag on the form. 

  Can anyong suggest a way of rephrasing or softening the phrase "employement terminated" without lying? 

  Thanking all in advance, 
  Boyce Williams
```

<a id="t-0f5d8c9649"></a>
## Thread: Off-topic: Rephrasing "employment terminated"? — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### Re: Off-topic: Rephrasing "employment terminated"?

- **From:** Ed Guy <ed_guy@paralynx.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-25T20:45:05-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3C9FFCD1.2E1F@paralynx.com>`
- **References:** `<a7o5d1$4jga$1@mercury.vcu.edu>`

```
bgwillia@vcu.edu wrote:
> 
> Hi,
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
> "employement terminated" without lying?

If you are phrasing the resume, why say anything at all about your reasons for leaving?

Your subsequent 13 years with one employer proves you aren't a job hopper.

Remember (and this is from my experience as a hiring manager) that if somebody has over 
a hundred resumes to go through for a job opening he is likely to do a "first cut" of 
five to ten SECONDS per resume looking for reasons to cut down the pile by excluding 
people.  Don't give him one this easy.
```

<a id="t-d333aba1d1"></a>
## Thread: COBOL.NET : DateTime.Now access? — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

### COBOL.NET : DateTime.Now access?

- **From:** christian@wenz.org (Christian Wenz)
- **Date:** 2002-03-26T07:42:03-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<27938ff7.0203260742.f78d920@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi,

I was playing around with COBOL.NET and wrote a tiny application that
reads the current time from the system and prints it in an <asp:labal
/> field.
This is what I got so far:

ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
CONFIGURATION SECTION.
REPOSITORY.
    PROPERTY PROP-TEXT AS "Text"
    CLASS SYS-DATETIME AS "System.DateTime"
    CLASS SYS-STRING AS "System.String"
    CLASS SYS-OBJECT AS "System.Object"
    CLASS EVENTARGS AS "System.EventArgs".
OBJECT.
PROCEDURE DIVISION.
METHOD-ID. BUTTON-CLICK.
DATA DIVISION.
WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
77 DATENOW OBJECT REFERENCE SYS-DATETIME.
77 DATE OBJECT REFERENCE SYS-STRING.
LINKAGE SECTION.
77 EVENT-SOURCE OBJECT REFERENCE SYS-OBJECT.
77 EVENT OBJECT REFERENCE EVENTARGS.
PROCEDURE DIVISION USING BY VALUE EVENT-SOURCE EVENT.
    INVOKE DATENOW "ToString"
        RETURNING DATE.
    SET PROP-TEXT OF OUTPUT TO DATE.
END METHOD BUTTON-CLICK.
END OBJECT.


My problem now is that I cannot find out the current date. Usually,
this could be done by using DateTime.Now and then invoke "ToString" on
the result. However, this code will not work:

    INVOKE DATUMNOW "Now" 
        RETURNING DATUMNOW.

-- it just says that method "Now" cannot be found.

If I am using this:
    INVOKE DATUMNOW "get_Now" 
        RETURNING DATUMNOW.

--- it says that no method get_Now with the provided interface exists.
Can anyone shed some light here please? Thanks!
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL.NET : DateTime.Now access?

- **From:** "ronald leenheer" <r.leenheer@wanadoo.nl>
- **Date:** 2002-03-27T00:20:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1f8o8.22$LI.217@castor.casema.net>`
- **References:** `<27938ff7.0203260742.f78d920@posting.google.com>`

```
Why not the old fashioned way?

WORKING STORAGE.
 01   SYS-DATE     PIC 9(6).
 01   SYS-DATE-RED REDEFINES SYS-DATE.
        03  SYS-YY        PIC 99.
        03  SYS-MM       PIC 99.
        03  SYS-DD        PIC 99.

And in your procedure area:

        ACCEPT SYS-DATE  FROM DATE.


gr. Ronald Leenheer

Hello,

I have an error in my program compiled with MSCOBOL 5

+-----------
| Error accessing file: c:\mesobs\test.txt
|
| OBS-PRN Segment RT: Error 105 at Cobol PC 1800
+------------

In my manual: 105 Memory allocation error!

bytes. I use W95 switched to DOS.

error, this program run very well on the same platform.

Alain.

<a id="t-f57239fb97"></a>
## Thread: db2 deadlock — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### db2 deadlock

- **From:** aort527@hotmail.com (alex ortiz)
- **Date:** 2002-03-26T11:02:04-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c3f04d21.0203261102.7b892a2d@posting.google.com>`

```
I encounter a deadlock situation on certain db2 rows when
doing an SQL.  Is there like a wait event I can invoke to
temporarily suspend my program?  I can always put the
thing in a LOOP, but I really don't want to do it.

After my program had wait(ED), I can now retry my SQL.....
```

#### ↳ Re: db2 deadlock

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-26T13:17:39-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a7qhgk$q33$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<c3f04d21.0203261102.7b892a2d@posting.google.com>`

```
You don't tell us your compiler or operating system, but my GUESS is that
you are running on MVS or OS/390 or z/OS.

If so, you can call an IBM-supplied routine

   ILBOWAT0

Passing it a full word binary field with the number of seconds to wait.
HOWEVER, the parameter passed to this routine must be "below the 16M line" -
so either compile your program with DATA(24) - or use a "pass thru" routine
to move the data below the line.
```

<a id="t-effa208512"></a>
## Thread: Off-topic: Rephrasing "employment terminated"? — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### Re: Off-topic: Rephrasing "employment terminated"?

- **From:** Calvin Crumrine <Calvin_Crumrine@dced.state.ak.us>
- **Date:** 2002-03-26T12:43:50-09:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CA0EB95.139FEAE9@dced.state.ak.us>`
- **References:** `<a7o5d1$4jga$1@mercury.vcu.edu>`

```
bgwillia@vcu.edu wrote:

>
> Hi,
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> Boyce Williams

The problem is that you got fired. I can't think of a good way to
disguise that. If you'd quit then you could say that you'd left for
health reasons-working for a jerk is bound to be stressful & everybody
knows that stress is bad for your health.

Overall, I'd say leave it off. It's old enough that it probably won't
add anything to your experience 'score' and if they find out about it
(and they might check even before noticing how old it is, if you list
it) then it's certain to count against you no matter how old it is.
```

## Thread: Help — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Help

- **From:** "Terry Maccarone" <tmaccaro@rochester.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-27T02:41:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bjao8.29665$v%.8454154@typhoon.nyroc.rr.com>`

```
Hi!  I'm taking a Cobol Class at my community college.  This is simple program but it will not run properly.
It compiles clean but I can't "animate" the code to finish the entire program.
*************************************************
      * The WS-Area-01 declares all variables that    *
      * will be used in the program                   *
      *************************************************
        01 WS-AREA-01.
           03 ARE-THERE-MORE-RECORDS       PIC X VALUE 'Y'.
           03 ITEM-PRICE-AMT               PIC 99999V99 VALUE 0.
           03 QUANTITY-AMT                 PIC 9(3) VALUE 000.                    
           03 NET-SALES-AMT                PIC 9(5)V9(2) VALUE 0.
           03 TAXES                        PIC 9(4)V9(2) VALUE 0.
           03 WS-TOTAL-COUNT               PIC 9(3) VALUE 000.
           03 WS-TOTAL-SALES-AMT           PIC 9(7)V99 VALUE 0.
           03 WS-TOTAL-TAXES               PIC 9(5)V99 VALUE 0.
           03 WS-TOTAL-NET-SALES           PIC 9(7)V99 VALUE 0.
      *************************************************
      * The Heading-Out record is what will be shown  *
      * as the first header of the sales report       *
      *************************************************     
        01 HEADING-OUT.
           03                PIC X(17) VALUE 'THERESA MACCARONE'.
           03                PIC X(6)  VALUE SPACES.
           03                PIC X(23) VALUE 'USED GOODS SALES REPORT'. 
           03                PIC X(34) VALUE SPACES.
        01 DETAIL-LINE-OUT.
      *************************************************
      * The First-Line-Out is the first detailed line *
      * header that will show on the sales report     *
      ************************************************* 
           03 FIRST-LINE-OUT.
              05         PIC X(10) VALUE SPACES.
              05         PIC X(7)  VALUE 'ACCOUNT'.
              05         PIC X(13) VALUE SPACES.
              05         PIC X(4)  VALUE 'ITEM'.
              05         PIC X(11) VALUE SPACES.
              05         PIC X(4)  VALUE 'SALE'.
              05         PIC X(6)  VALUE SPACES.
              05         PIC X(5)  VALUE 'SALES'.
              05         PIC X(6)  VALUE SPACES.
              05         PIC X(8)  VALUE 'NET SALE'.
              05         PIC X(1)  VALUE SPACES.
              05         PIC X(3)  VALUE 'TAX'.
              05         PIC X(2)  VALUE SPACES.             
           03 SECOND-LINE-OUT.
              05         PIC X(10) VALUE SPACES.
              05         PIC X(6)  VALUE 'NUMBER'.
              05         PIC X(13) VALUE SPACES.
              05         PIC X(6)  VALUE 'NUMBER'.
              05         PIC X(9)  VALUE SPACES.
              05         PIC X(6)  VALUE 'AMOUNT'.
              05         PIC X(6)  VALUE SPACES.
              05         PIC X(3)  VALUE 'TAX'.
              05         PIC X(8)  VALUE SPACES.
              05         PIC X(6)  VALUE 'AMOUNT'.
              05         PIC X(2)  VALUE SPACES.
              05         PIC X(4)  VALUE 'STAT'.
              05         PIC X(1)  VALUE SPACES.
      ************************************************
      * The Sales-Data-Out is the output record      *
      ************************************************                        
       01 SALES-DATA-OUT.
          03                 PIC X(4) VALUE SPACES.
          03 ACCT-NUM-OUT    PIC X(20).
          03                 PIC X(3) VALUE SPACES.
          03 ITEM-NUM-OUT    PIC X(12).
          03                 PIC X(2) VALUE SPACES.
          03 SALES-AMT-OUT   PIC $ZZ,ZZZ.99.
          03                 PIC X(2) VALUE SPACES.
          03 SALES-TAX-OUT   PIC Z,ZZZ.ZZ.
          03                 PIC X(4) VALUE SPACES.
          03 NET-SALE-OUT    PIC ZZ,ZZZ.99.
          03                 PIC X(1) VALUE SPACES.
          03 TAX-STAT-OUT    PIC X(3).
          03                 PIC X(2) VALUE SPACES.
      ***********************************************
      * The Footer-Out is the record that will hold *
      * the information to print out the footer for *
      * the sales report                            *
      ***********************************************    
       01 FOOTER-OUT.
          03                 PIC X(6)  VALUE 'TOTALS'.
          03                 PIC X(2)  VALUE SPACES.
          03                 PIC X(12) VALUE 'SALE COUNT ='.
          03 TOTAL-COUNT     PIC ZZ9.
          03                 PIC X(14) VALUE SPACES.
          03 TOTAL-SALES     PIC $Z,ZZZ,ZZZ.99.
          03                 PIC X(1)  VALUE SPACES.
          03 TOTAL-TAXES     PIC ZZ,ZZZ.ZZ.
          03                 PIC X(1) VALUE SPACES.
          03 TOTAL-NET-SALES PIC Z,ZZZ,ZZZ.99.
          03                 PIC X(6) VALUE SPACES. 
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
      *************************************************
      * The Purpose of the Main Module is to control  *
      * the main processing and call other modules    * 
      * to perform certain functions                  *
      *************************************************
       100-MAIN-MODULE.
          OPEN INPUT SALES-RECORDS
               OUTPUT FILE-OUT
          PERFORM 200-HEADINGS
          PERFORM UNTIL ARE-THERE-MORE-RECORDS = 'N'
          READ SALES-RECORDS
            AT END
               MOVE 'N'TO ARE-THERE-MORE-RECORDS
            NOT AT END
            PERFORM 300-PROCESS-RECORDS
          END-READ
          END-PERFORM
          MOVE WS-TOTAL-COUNT TO TOTAL-COUNT
          MOVE WS-TOTAL-SALES-AMT TO TOTAL-SALES
          MOVE WS-TOTAL-TAXES TO TOTAL-TAXES
          MOVE WS-TOTAL-NET-SALES TO TOTAL-NET-SALES
          MOVE FOOTER-OUT TO RECORD-OUT
          WRITE RECORD-OUT AFTER ADVANCING 2 LINES            
          PERFORM 500-END-OF-JOB          
          STOP RUN.
      *************************************************
      * The purpose of the 200-Headings Module is to  *
      * print out the Headings for the report         *
      *************************************************        
       200-HEADINGS.
          MOVE HEADING-OUT TO RECORD-OUT
          WRITE RECORD-OUT AFTER ADVANCING PAGE
          MOVE SPACES TO RECORD-OUT
          WRITE RECORD-OUT AFTER ADVANCING 1 LINE
          MOVE FIRST-LINE-OUT TO RECORD-OUT
          WRITE RECORD-OUT AFTER ADVANCING 1 LINE
          MOVE SECOND-LINE-OUT TO RECORD-OUT
          WRITE RECORD-OUT AFTER ADVANCING 1 LINE.
      **************************************************
      * The purpose of the 300-Process-Records Module  *
      * is to process each record and compute the      *
      * sales tax if there is one. It also calls the   *
      * 400-Calculations Module.                       *
      **************************************************             
       300-PROCESS-RECORDS.                 
          MOVE ACCT-NUM TO ACCT-NUM-OUT
          MOVE ITEM-NUM TO ITEM-NUM-OUT
          MOVE ITEM-PRICE TO ITEM-PRICE-AMT
          MOVE QUANTITY-SOLD TO QUANTITY-AMT
          COMPUTE NET-SALES-AMT = ITEM-PRICE-AMT * QUANTITY-AMT             
          MOVE NET-SALES-AMT TO NET-SALE-OUT   
          IF TAX-STATUS EQUAL 'N'
            MOVE ZERO TO SALES-TAX-OUT            
            MOVE 'TAX' TO TAX-STAT-OUT            
          ELSE
            COMPUTE TAXES = (ITEM-PRICE-AMT * QUANTITY-AMT)* .08
            MOVE TAXES TO SALES-TAX-OUT                         
            MOVE SPACES TO TAX-STAT-OUT
          END-IF
          MOVE SALES-DATA-OUT TO RECORD-OUT
          WRITE RECORD-OUT AFTER ADVANCING 1 LINE
          PERFORM 400-CALCULATIONS.
      ***************************************************
      * The 400-Calculations module keeps a running     *
      * total of the number of items sold, sales,       *
      * taxes and net-sales                             *
      ***************************************************     
       400-CALCULATIONS.
       COMPUTE WS-TOTAL-COUNT = QUANTITY-AMT + WS-TOTAL-COUNT   
       COMPUTE WS-TOTAL-SALES-AMT = ITEM-PRICE-AMT + WS-TOTAL-SALES-AMT
       COMPUTE WS-TOTAL-TAXES = ((ITEM-PRICE-AMT * QUANTITY-AMT)* .08
        + WS-TOTAL-TAXES)
       COMPUTE WS-TOTAL-NET-SALES = (ITEM-PRICE-AMT * QUANTITY-AMT)
        + WS-TOTAL-NET-SALES.
      ****************************************************
      *  The 500-End-of-Job module closes out all files  *
      *  that are opened up at the begining of the       *
      *  program                                         *
      ****************************************************              
       500-END-OF-JOB.
           CLOSE SALES-RECORDS
                 FILE-OUT.
```

              05         PIC X(2)  VALUE SPACES.
           03 SECOND-LINE-OUT.
              05         PIC X(10) VALUE SPACES.
              05         PIC X(6)  VALUE 'NUMBER'.
              05         PIC X(13) VALUE SPACES.
              05         PIC X(6)  VALUE 'NUMBER'.
              05         PIC X(9)  VALUE SPACES.
              05         PIC X(6)  VALUE 'AMOUNT'.
              05         PIC X(6)  VALUE SPACES.
              05         PIC X(3)  VALUE 'TAX'.
              05         PIC X(8)  VALUE SPACES.
              05         PIC X(6)  VALUE 'AMOUNT'.
              05         PIC X(2)  VALUE SPACES.
              05         PIC X(4)  VALUE 'STAT'.
              05         PIC X(1)  VALUE SPACES.
      ************************************************
      * The Sales-Data-Out is the output record      *
      ************************************************
       01 SALES-DATA-OUT.
          03                 PIC X(4) VALUE SPACES.
          03 ACCT-NUM-OUT    PIC X(20).
          03                 PIC X(3) VALUE SPACES.
          03 ITEM-NUM-OUT    PIC X(12).
          03                 PIC X(2) VALUE SPACES.
          03 SALES-AMT-OUT   PIC $ZZ,ZZZ.99.
          03                 PIC X(2) VALUE SPACES.
          03 SALES-TAX-OUT   PIC Z,ZZZ.ZZ.
          03                 PIC X(4) VALUE SPACES.
          03 NET-SALE-OUT    PIC ZZ,ZZZ.99.
          03                 PIC X(1) VALUE SPACES.
          03 TAX-STAT-OUT    PIC X(3).
          03                 PIC X(2) VALUE SPACES.
      ***********************************************
      * The Footer-Out is the record that will hold *
      * the information to print out the footer for *
      * the sales report                            *
      ***********************************************
       01 FOOTER-OUT.
          03                 PIC X(6)  VALUE 'TOTALS'.
          03                 PIC X(2)  VALUE SPACES.
          03                 PIC X(12) VALUE 'SALE COUNT ='.
          03 TOTAL-COUNT     PIC ZZ9.
          03                 PIC X(14) VALUE SPACES.
          03 TOTAL-SALES     PIC $Z,ZZZ,ZZZ.99.
          03                 PIC X(1)  VALUE SPACES.
          03 TOTAL-TAXES     PIC ZZ,ZZZ.ZZ.
          03                 PIC X(1) VALUE SPACES.
          03 TOTAL-NET-SALES PIC Z,ZZZ,ZZZ.99.
          03                 PIC X(6) VALUE SPACES.
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
      *************************************************
      * The Purpose of the Main Module is to control  *
      * the main processing and call other modules    *
      * to perform certain functions                  *
      *************************************************
       100-MAIN-MODULE.
          OPEN INPUT SALES-RECORDS
               OUTPUT FILE-OUT
          PERFORM 200-HEADINGS
          PERFORM UNTIL ARE-THERE-MORE-RECORDS = 'N'
          READ SALES-RECORDS
            AT END
               MOVE 'N'TO ARE-THERE-MORE-RECORDS
            NOT AT END
            PERFORM 300-PROCESS-RECORDS
          END-READ
          END-PERFORM
          MOVE WS-TOTAL-COUNT TO TOTAL-COUNT
          MOVE WS-TOTAL-SALES-AMT TO TOTAL-SALES
          MOVE WS-TOTAL-TAXES TO TOTAL-TAXES
          MOVE WS-TOTAL-NET-SALES TO TOTAL-NET-SALES
          MOVE FOOTER-OUT TO RECORD-OUT
          WRITE RECORD-OUT AFTER ADVANCING 2 LINES
          PERFORM 500-END-OF-JOB
          STOP RUN.
      *************************************************
      * The purpose of the 200-Headings Module is to  *
      * print out the Headings for the report         *
      *************************************************
       200-HEADINGS.
          MOVE HEADING-OUT TO RECORD-OUT
          WRITE RECORD-OUT AFTER ADVANCING PAGE
          MOVE SPACES TO RECORD-OUT
          WRITE RECORD-OUT AFTER ADVANCING 1 LINE
          MOVE FIRST-LINE-OUT TO RECORD-OUT
          WRITE RECORD-OUT AFTER ADVANCING 1 LINE
          MOVE SECOND-LINE-OUT TO RECORD-OUT
          WRITE RECORD-OUT AFTER ADVANCING 1 LINE.
      **************************************************
      * The purpose of the 300-Process-Records Module  *
      * is to process each record and compute the      *
      * sales tax if there is one. It also calls the   *
      * 400-Calculations Module.                       *
      **************************************************
       300-PROCESS-RECORDS.
          MOVE ACCT-NUM TO ACCT-NUM-OUT
          MOVE ITEM-NUM TO ITEM-NUM-OUT
          MOVE ITEM-PRICE TO ITEM-PRICE-AMT
          MOVE QUANTITY-SOLD TO QUANTITY-AMT
          COMPUTE NET-SALES-AMT = ITEM-PRICE-AMT * QUANTITY-AMT
          MOVE NET-SALES-AMT TO NET-SALE-OUT
          IF TAX-STATUS EQUAL 'N'
            MOVE ZERO TO SALES-TAX-OUT
            MOVE 'TAX' TO TAX-STAT-OUT
          ELSE
            COMPUTE TAXES = (ITEM-PRICE-AMT * QUANTITY-AMT)* .08
            MOVE TAXES TO SALES-TAX-OUT
            MOVE SPACES TO TAX-STAT-OUT
          END-IF
          MOVE SALES-DATA-OUT TO RECORD-OUT
          WRITE RECORD-OUT AFTER ADVANCING 1 LINE
          PERFORM 400-CALCULATIONS.
      ***************************************************
      * The 400-Calculations module keeps a running     *
      * total of the number of items sold, sales,       *
      * taxes and net-sales                             *
      ***************************************************
       400-CALCULATIONS.
       COMPUTE WS-TOTAL-COUNT = QUANTITY-AMT + WS-TOTAL-COUNT
       COMPUTE WS-TOTAL-SALES-AMT = ITEM-PRICE-AMT + WS-TOTAL-SALES-AMT
       COMPUTE WS-TOTAL-TAXES = ((ITEM-PRICE-AMT * QUANTITY-AMT)* .08
        + WS-TOTAL-TAXES)
       COMPUTE WS-TOTAL-NET-SALES = (ITEM-PRICE-AMT * QUANTITY-AMT)
        + WS-TOTAL-NET-SALES.
      ****************************************************
      *  The 500-End-of-Job module closes out all files  *
      *  that are opened up at the begining of the       *
      *  program                                         *
      ****************************************************
       500-END-OF-JOB.
           CLOSE SALES-RECORDS
                 FILE-OUT.



READ SALES-RECORDS
            AT END
               MOVE 'N'TO ARE-THERE-MORE-RECORDS
            NOT AT END
            PERFORM 300-PROCESS-RECORDS
          END-READ

Could the lack of a space between 'N' and TO be causing the error?
```

## Thread: UNSTRING DELMITED BY ALL SPACES, SPACES? — 10 this month / 11 total

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### UNSTRING DELMITED BY ALL SPACES, SPACES?

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2002-03-27T04:43:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020326234354.14073.00000278@mb-ce.aol.com>`

```
Well one of the Big 3 has come up with a better idea and is now having their
suppliers download new pricing information from their web site into CSV files. 
My users now have to push the data to the mainframe and I now have to parse it
out into a usable (by me) form.  Getting them to push it would take a whole
other forum so on to the parsing....

The first thing I do is unstring delimited by commas - no problem.  It is the
next part I can't quite get right.  

One of the fields from the first unstring is the part number.  In its final
form it should contain 3 fields, the first 7 positions, the 2nd 9 positions,
and the 3rd 7 positions. Each field must be right justified.  

Examining the raw data I found that it is one field at its longest 45
positions.  The first 7 positions have leading spaces mostly 3 to 4 positions
(the final field will have 3 leading spaces and 4 characters).  This is
separated from the next portion of the part number by 2 or 3 spaces, and the
last piece of the number is separated from the 2nd by 1 space.  There a lots of
trailing spaces.

I've defined 3 fields in WS, each larger than the final fields.  I've coded 
UNSTRING WS-PART-NO DELIMITED BY ALL SPACES INTO
    WS-PART-1
    WS-PART-2
    WS-PART 3
END-UNSTRING

And I don't get the last piece of the part number.

I code:
UNSTRING WS-PART-NO DELIMITED BY SPACE INTO
  WS-PART-1
  WS-PART-2
  WS-PART3
END-UNSTRING

and get nothing.

Do I have to 
UNSTRING WS-PART-NO DELMITED BY ALL SPACES INTO
  WS-PART-1

Do I have to do the ALL SPACES and get the first one separated with the next 2
combined, and then do still another UNSTRING using BY space to get the last 2
separated?
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020326234717.14081.00000249@mb-ce.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<20020326234354.14073.00000278@mb-ce.aol.com>`

```
to continue - dang keyboard - hit the send too soon...

Do I have to code
UNSTRING WS-PART-NO DELMITED BY ALL SPACES INTO
  WS-PART-1
  WS-PART-23
END-UNSTRING

and then

UNSTRING WS-PART-23 DELIMITED BY SPACE INTO
  WS-PART-2
  WS-PART-3
END-UNSTRING
?

…[19 more quoted lines elided]…

next 2

last 2

Do I have to code
UNSTRING WS-PART-NO DELMITED BY ALL SPACES INTO
  WS-PART-1
  WS-PART-23
END-UNSTRING

and then

UNSTRING WS-PART-23 DELIMITED BY SPACE INTO
  WS-PART-2
  WS-PART-3
END-UNSTRING
?

---
DELIMITED BY SPACE means each space is a delimiter; DELIMITED BY ALL SPACE
means that consecutive spaces count as one space for delimiting purposes.
Beyond that, I'd like to see an (some) example(s) of the data.

P.S.,
In decomposing the CSV file, did you account for commas within a data field,
surrounded by quotes?

1,2,3,"Boston, MA", 4, etc.
```

Field is (using x for a space):
xxxxxAAAAxxxxxBBBBBBxCCxxxxxxxxxxxx

one space between B and C

Anywho...

I was playing around with it today and found that after the first UNSTRING with
the comma delimiter the part number nicely fit into a field that I can use
reference modification on for the first two pieces, move a chunk for the last
piece and do a perform varying to justify it correctly .  So for now problem
solved.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: UNSTRING DELMITED BY ALL SPACES, SPACES?

- **From:** "Tom Morrison" <t.morrison@liant.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-28T16:24:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ftHo8.72412$mo2.740963389@newssvr30.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<8clo8.111758$2q2.9604599@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <20020327233503.25237.00000576@mb-ci.aol.com>`

```
"YukonMama" <yukonmama@aol.com> wrote in message
news:20020327233503.25237.00000576@mb-ci.aol.com...
[snip]
> However just for kicks I'll try that piece of code suggested with the
pointer.

Eileen,

Yes, here it is; plug-and-play.  No loops, no reference modification, no
thrills...

       identification division.
       program-id.  unstr.
       data division.
       working-storage section.
       01  IN-PTR  PIC 999.
       01  PART-NO-SUB-COUNT PIC 999.
       01  WS-PART-1    PIC x(20) JUST.
       01  WS-PART-2    PIC x(20) JUST.
       01  WS-PART-3    PIC x(20) JUST.
       01  WS-PART-NO   PIC X(50)
                        VALUE "     AAAA     BBBBBB CC            ".
       procedure division.
       a.
           MOVE 1 to IN-PTR.
           MOVE 0 to PART-NO-SUB-COUNT.
           INSPECT WS-PART-NO TALLYING IN-PTR FOR LEADING SPACES.
           UNSTRING WS-PART-NO
               DELIMITED BY ALL SPACES
               INTO     WS-PART-1    WS-PART-2    WS-PART-3
               POINTER IN-PTR
               TALLYING PART-NO-SUB-COUNT
           END-UNSTRING
           DISPLAY "sub count=" PART-NO-SUB-COUNT.
           DISPLAY WS-PART-1.
           DISPLAY WS-PART-2.
           DISPLAY WS-PART-3.


Results:
sub count=003
                AAAA
              BBBBBB
                  CC

If the part number really consists of numbers, then you may simply change
the PIC x(20) JUST to PIC 9(18) and that will work too.

Don't forget to put in the error handling suggested in my original post.

Best regards,
Tom Morrison
Liant Software Corporation
```

#### ↳ Re: UNSTRING DELMITED BY ALL SPACES, SPACES?

- **From:** "Tom Morrison" <t.morrison@liant.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-27T15:57:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<wZlo8.3753$lW1.1375580361@newssvr11.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<20020326234354.14073.00000278@mb-ce.aol.com>`

```
"YukonMama" <yukonmama@aol.com> wrote in message
news:20020326234354.14073.00000278@mb-ce.aol.com...
> Examining the raw data I found that it is one field at its longest 45
> positions.  The first 7 positions have leading spaces mostly 3 to 4
positions
> (the final field will have 3 leading spaces and 4 characters).  This is
> separated from the next portion of the part number by 2 or 3 spaces, and
the
> last piece of the number is separated from the 2nd by 1 space.  There a
lots of
> trailing spaces.

I think this tells it.  Your first field has leading spaces, so the UNSTRING
is seeing an immediate delimiter.  Here is a suggestion:

01  IN-PTR  PIC 999.
01  PART-NO-SUB-COUNT PIC 999.
...
MOVE 1 to IN-PTR.
MOVE 0 to PART-NO-SUB-COUNT.
INSPECT WS-PART-NO TALLYING IN-PTR FOR LEADING SPACES.
UNSTRING WS-PART-NO
        DELIMITED BY ALL SPACES
        INTO     WS-PART-1    WS-PART-2    WS-PART 3
        POINTER IN-PTR
        TALLYING PART-NO-SUB-COUNT
    ON OVERFLOW
        <handle malformed part number>
        <caused by blank part number>
        <or too many subfields>
END-UNSTRING
IF PART-NO-SUB-COUNT NOT = 3
    <handle malformed part number>
    <too few subfields>
END-IF
```

#### ↳ Re: UNSTRING DELMITED BY ALL SPACES, SPACES?

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-03-27T11:18:16-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<pgmo8.5826$oz6.1574031@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<20020326234354.14073.00000278@mb-ce.aol.com>`

```
I would suspect a non printing character in one of the "space" positions.
"All space" should do the trick, but will not if it is actually something
like "space tab space".  I often find it usefull to do a few examine
replacing statements before the unstring to handle anything non-standard.
For example:

    EXAMINE WS-PART-NO REPLACING ALL X'09' BY SPACE.
    UNSTRING WS-PART-NO DELIMITED BY ALL SPACE
        INTO ....

Donald

INSPECT WS-PART-NO REPLACING ALL X'09' BY SPACE.

#### ↳ Re: UNSTRING DELMITED BY ALL SPACES, SPACES?

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-03-27T11:30:51-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dsmo8.5828$oz6.1577724@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<20020326234354.14073.00000278@mb-ce.aol.com>`

```
PS to that last ... having thought about it for a few minutes, I think the
last post is probably correct ... I was thinking about you saying that you
looked at the data with a text editor, and that the offending field had
leading spaces.  I'd bet money that the offending field really has a tab at
the beginning, and that your text editor is showing it as spaces.  I also
had the thought, though, that tab is probably not a hex 9, since you are
working on a mainframe. An examine replacing tab with space (on the main
string before you start) should do the trick, if that is the case.

Donald


"YukonMama" <yukonmama@aol.com> wrote in message
news:20020326234354.14073.00000278@mb-ce.aol.com...
> Well one of the Big 3 has come up with a better idea and is now having
their
> suppliers download new pricing information from their web site into CSV
files.

parse it
> out into a usable (by me) form.  Getting them to push it would take a
whole
> other forum so on to the parsing....
>
> The first thing I do is unstring delimited by commas - no problem.  It is
the
> next part I can't quite get right.
>
> One of the fields from the first unstring is the part number.  In its
final
> form it should contain 3 fields, the first 7 positions, the 2nd 9
positions,
> and the 3rd 7 positions. Each field must be right justified.
>
> Examining the raw data I found that it is one field at its longest 45
> positions.  The first 7 positions have leading spaces mostly 3 to 4
positions
> (the final field will have 3 leading spaces and 4 characters).  This is
> separated from the next portion of the part number by 2 or 3 spaces, and
the
> last piece of the number is separated from the 2nd by 1 space.  There a
lots of
> trailing spaces.
>
> I've defined 3 fields in WS, each larger than the final fields.  I've
coded
> UNSTRING WS-PART-NO DELIMITED BY ALL SPACES INTO
>     WS-PART-1
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> Do I have to do the ALL SPACES and get the first one separated with the
next 2
> combined, and then do still another UNSTRING using BY space to get the
last 2
> separated?
```

---

<a id="t-edf334b601"></a>
## Thread: 'Sleeping' Jobs - Pausing w/ ILBOWAT0 — 21 this month / 28 total

_Topics: [`Jobs, careers, recruiting, salary`](topics.md#jobs)_

### 'Sleeping' Jobs - Pausing w/ ILBOWAT0

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2002-03-27T11:29:46-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a7ss1q$sli$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```

Just something to watch out for, actually.

I have a job that I want to run at night, 9:pm (2100hrs), after the 
billing cycle had completed and before any of the early-morning crew 
entered any new charges.  I'm a consultant/contractor/hired gun; such 
folks are not allowed to have remote access to the mainframe.  (I am sure 
there is a Very Good Reason for this).  Rather than disturb the operators 
with an unusual request I decided to code a wait-step based on a 
utility-program to call ILBOWAT0 for a parm-determined period.

I found the basic code here (thanks, Mr Jacot!) and expanded it a bit, 
giving it a routine to check a PARM in D HH MM SS format, convert this to 
seconds and loop through CALLs to ILBOWAT0.

I submitted the job at 1558 and a delay of 5hr 3min... and came in the 
next day to find it had been CAN'd.  Seems that after almost an hour of 
swapped-out waiting the system began to throw console messages of:

16.49.36 JOB00676  **MYUID01 HAS BEEN IN WAIT STATE FOR -*5*- MINUTES**CHECK SYSTEM
16.55.53 JOB00676  **MYUID01 HAS BEEN IN WAIT STATE FOR -*5*- MINUTES**CHECK SYSTEM
17.02.11 JOB00676  **MYUID01 HAS BEEN IN WAIT STATE FOR -*5*- MINUTES**CHECK SYSTEM

... and Ops, being... Ops, saw this as an anomaly worthy of cancellation.

A few emails back-and-forth with the Ops Manager got a notice sent to 
folks which stated 'If job MYUID01 tells you it is waiting then let it 
wait' and all went well.

('... all went well' includes things like getting zero rows returned for 
an SQL statement containing:

AND DATE_TIME_STAMP BETWEEN  CURRENT TIMESTAMP
                        AND (CURRENT TIMESTAMP - 24 HOURS)

... but that's why things get tested, aye)

Anyways... folks might want to see what happens at their sites when a job 
is waiting for a couple of hours, both during business hours and 
off-hours.

DD
```

Jack Sleight <jacksleight@hotmail.com> wrote:
>Doc,
>
>I just KNEW you were destined to become famous.

Oh boy... will they sing Great Songs 'round campfires, telling tales in 
cunning rhyme of my skills, my cunning and my sanitary habits?  Will 
mothers put their children to bed and admonish them 'You be good, now, or 
you'll *never* grow up to be like DocDwarf!'?  Will young women, swoon, 
starry-eyed o'er the thought of me and sneer at their preening swains, 
deriding their finest efforts with 'Not bad... for the likes of *you*; 
DocDwarf could have done better with both eyes tied behind his back!'?

I can hardly wait!

>
>If you tire of the notoriety and they give you access to CICS, you
>could write a transaction that STARTs another one @ 6 AM, and that, in
>turn, submits the JCL to execute your job.

Ummmmm... wouldn't I need to get a TranID defined?

But now that you have me thinking... I could use an old copy of MicroFocus 
1.7 to put together a program that runs on my 386 laptop and just queries 
the system clock... and then at 9:pm I could have it run a batch file that 
sets up an ftp session to the mainframe and submits the job to the 
INTRDR... only problem with that is that'd tie up a telephone-line.

DD

##### ↳ ↳ Re: 'Sleeping' Jobs - Pausing w/ ILBOWAT0

- **From:** Liam Devlin <LiamD@optonline.net>
- **Date:** 2002-03-30T04:03:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CA538FD.D8AF124@optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<a7ss1q$sli$1@panix1.panix.com> <8a2d426e.0203271541.5f8baace@posting.google.com>`

```
Jack Sleight wrote:
> 
> Doc,
…[50 more quoted lines elided]…
> > DD

FWIW, I like this approach. I think you could avoid the wait messages to
Ops if you code your program to wait in small chunks of time until you
get the time you want, e.g., 3-5 seconds. That'll keep the job active,
but not chew up much in the way of resources. You may need a TIME= parm
or find out which test JOBCLASS lets your job hang around for hours.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 'Sleeping' Jobs - Pausing w/ ILBOWAT0

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2002-03-29T23:34:38-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a83f8u$410$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<a7ss1q$sli$1@panix1.panix.com> <8a2d426e.0203271541.5f8baace@posting.google.com> <3CA538FD.D8AF124@optonline.net>`

```
In article <3CA538FD.D8AF124@optonline.net>,
Liam Devlin  <LiamD@nospam.optonline.net> wrote:

[snippimente]

>FWIW, I like this approach. I think you could avoid the wait messages to
>Ops if you code your program to wait in small chunks of time until you
>get the time you want, e.g., 3-5 seconds.

How interesting... my approach was, of course, to keep the number of CALLs 
down to a minimum by keeping the number-of-seconds per CALL at 9999; I did 
not think to try to avoid the Ops msgs by coding less 'efficiently'.

>That'll keep the job active,
>but not chew up much in the way of resources. You may need a TIME= parm
>or find out which test JOBCLASS lets your job hang around for hours.

Well, the problem has been solved (after Ops cancelled the job three days 
running despite many emails to everyone involved saying 'Don't CAN this 
job!'); they've put it in the Prod cycle to run at the right time so we 
can test it before we put in a Change Control to move it to Prod.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 'Sleeping' Jobs - Pausing w/ ILBOWAT0

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Joseph Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au>
- **Date:** 2002-03-29T19:19:21+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<290320021919218117%josephk@iinet.net.au>`
- **References:** `<a7ss1q$sli$1@panix1.panix.com> <01c1d602$48f0bde0$550a0e3f@chottel> <a7v6aa$rjt$1@panix1.panix.com> <01c1d6c0$30561fa0$5631e641@chottel> <a80kpf$7q4$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
In article <a80kpf$7q4$1@panix1.panix.com>, <docdwarf@panix.com> wrote:

> 
> It might be... or it might be a part of the standard that companies 
> disable, as happens frequently with PRTY.  Can any light be shed on this 
> by the JESJockies in the house?
> 
It is categorically NOT a valid JES2 statement.
JES2 does NO scheduling - in terms of time or date execution - of jobs.

It may be a JES3 statement, however, I am unfamiar with JES3 and so I
may be wrong.

Usually, these types of scheduling events are handled by an external
product like OPC - now Tivoli Workload Scheduler (TWS) or similar.
```

I don't think it is JES3 as I am pretty sure we have JES2.  I will run a
test on Monday and post the results.  If I remember the messages in the job
log correctly the job tries to run immediately and gets requeued back into
the input queue and waits there until the date and time coded is reached. 
This has the advantage of not tying up an initiator.

```
How about using JCL/JES:

/*SCHEDULE 21:00  mm/dd/yy

docdwarf@panix.com wrote in article <a7ss1q$sli$1@panix1.panix.com>...
> 
> Just something to watch out for, actually.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> folks are not allowed to have remote access to the mainframe.  (I am sure

> there is a Very Good Reason for this).  Rather than disturb the operators

> with an unusual request I decided to code a wait-step based on a 
> utility-program to call ILBOWAT0 for a parm-determined period.
> 
> I found the basic code here (thanks, Mr Jacot!) and expanded it a bit, 
> giving it a routine to check a PARM in D HH MM SS format, convert this to

> seconds and loop through CALLs to ILBOWAT0.
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 16.49.36 JOB00676  **MYUID01 HAS BEEN IN WAIT STATE FOR -*5*-
MINUTES**CHECK SYSTEM
> 16.55.53 JOB00676  **MYUID01 HAS BEEN IN WAIT STATE FOR -*5*-
MINUTES**CHECK SYSTEM
> 17.02.11 JOB00676  **MYUID01 HAS BEEN IN WAIT STATE FOR -*5*-
MINUTES**CHECK SYSTEM
> 
> ... and Ops, being... Ops, saw this as an anomaly worthy of cancellation.
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> Anyways... folks might want to see what happens at their sites when a job

> is waiting for a couple of hours, both during business hours and 
> off-hours.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: 'Sleeping' Jobs - Pausing w/ ILBOWAT0

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2002-03-28T04:19:42+00:00

```
>From: docdwarf@panix.com
>Date: 3/27/02 11:29 AM Eastern Standard Time
>

>  I'm a consultant/contractor/hired gun; such 
>folks are not allowed to have remote access to the mainframe. 

So am I and I have remote access to the mainframe.  Being that my house is 56
miles from my desk I dang well better have :)

>Anyways... folks might want to see what happens at their sites when a job 
>is waiting for a couple of hours, both during business hours and 
>off-hours.

During business hours I get a call at my desk from the helpless desk  - "Hey -
ya know job such and such is waiting - want me to kill it?"

On off hours the phone rings, and rings, and I finally realize I'm not dreaming
and I hear "Hey - did I wake you?"  "No - I'm bright eyed and bushy tailed at
2:30 in the gd morning" "Well .... uh ..... did ya know job so and so was
waiting?"  "Yeah - didn't you get my e-mail telling the job would sit around so
we wouldn't be having this conversation?"  "Yeah - but I wasn't sure what you
meant. You want me to kill it?"  "NO!"  "Ok - want me to tell operations that
all is well?" "Yeah - do that" "ok - night".   30 mintues later we go through
the same conversation again.
```

## Thread: IBM VisualAge Cobol in Windows 2000. — 5 this month / 7 total

### IBM VisualAge Cobol in Windows 2000.

- **From:** riju.parambath@providentcompanies.com (kpriju)
- **Date:** 2002-03-27T10:43:32-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dc662413.0203271043.5a0f6d76@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi All, 

We are trying to move one of our OS2/COBOL//DB2 UDB (on AIX)
application to Windows 2000/Visual age Cobol/DB2 UDB (on AIX)
application.

We are finding it difficult to compile Cobol programs in Visual age
(basically initial set up issues). Has anybody in the list done this
type of conversion before? Could anybody point us in the right
direction (websites or any other resources) regarding the visual age
initial setup?

Your help in any direction will be greatly appreciated. 
Thanks,
Riju
```

#### ↳ Re: IBM VisualAge Cobol in Windows 2000.

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-27T15:50:34-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a7terc$age$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<dc662413.0203271043.5a0f6d76@posting.google.com>`

```
I think (given input from one of my "usually reliable sources" is that you
are trying to compile on Windows 2000.  Although I think that is a supported
RUN-TIME environment, I do NOT think that any current version of IBM
VisualAge COBOL supports COMPILING under Windows 2000.  (NT - yes, 2000 -
no).

Check your supported compiler "environment".
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: IBM VisualAge Cobol in Windows 2000.

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@NOSPAM-hot.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-28T02:05:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OTuo8.44320$J53.1454761@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<dc662413.0203271043.5a0f6d76@posting.google.com> <a7terc$age$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Well, yes and no. No, the compiler will not support generating 390 load
modules on a PC.
Yes, you can compile on a PC, but what the beastie does is ftp the file to
the mainframe and
compile it there, then return the jobstream and SYSOUT to you.

The setup is somewhat complex, but once working, is really beautiful. The
same facility exists on
the AS/400 too, with something called CODE/400.

I will say you can edit much faster in Windows than in ISPF, even if you
*(like me)* love ISPF. :)
-Paul

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM VisualAge Cobol in Windows 2000.

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-27T21:24:03-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a7u2lm$2gk$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<dc662413.0203271043.5a0f6d76@posting.google.com> <a7terc$age$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <OTuo8.44320$J53.1454761@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`

```
Paul,
  Your statement is correct when you say
    "Yes, you can compile on a PC"

but what you are missing is that there are LIMITED numbers of "PC" operating
systems that are supported for VisualAge COBOL.

See:
  http://www-4.ibm.com/software/ad/cobol/va/

which explicitly LIMITS the compiler to running on a Windows NT desktop (no
Windows 2000, no Windows XP, no other Windows or OS/2 operating systems).

 IBM announcement 200-107  (dated May 9, 2000 )

viewable at:

http://www1.ibmlink.ibm.com/cgi-bin/master?request=announcements&parms=S&xh=
xM53F%24AuHb3GLy1USenGnN9332&xhi=usa.main&type=ANNO&smonth=02&sday=27&syear=
2001&emonth=02&eday=27&eyear=2002&searchwords=&content=WORDS&n%240=200-107&n
%241=&n%242=&n%243=&n%244=&n%245=&n%246=&n%247=&n%248=&n%249=#@3h@061@

goes on to distinguish between program object code execution vs compiler
execution and says,

"For application execution, use one of the following:
  a.. Microsoft Windows NT 4.0 plus Service Pack 4
  b.. Microsoft Windows 95
  c.. Microsoft Windows 98 "
No where does it list Windows 2000 or Windows XP
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM VisualAge Cobol in Windows 2000.

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>

```

> I will say you can edit much faster in Windows than in ISPF, even if you
> *(like me)* love ISPF. :)

about editing CoBOL programs or JCL.

## Thread: Several Homework Questions please — 12 this month / 13 total

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Several Homework Questions please

- **From:** rhamm90210@icqmail.com (Ryan Hamm)
- **Date:** 2002-03-27T14:09:31-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b7dbb410.0203271409.4a2f358d@posting.google.com>`

```
I've searched every place I can find on the net (ZingCOBOL) and a
couple of books such as Structured COBOL Programming second edition,
Structured COBOL Programming Flowchart edition.  I've found answers to
most of the questions asked but a few aren't findable.  The teacher of
the class has said we probably won't find them all so I come here to
hope someone will be friendly enough to give me a couple of answers:

1.  In COBOL, the default storage for a numeric field is
________________
A.  zoned
B.  packed
C.  compressed
D.  computational

From two different sources it says that Display is the default usage
type but I can find nothing that says what the default storage type
is.

2.  A structure variable can not contain another structure variable. 
True or False

Since I can't find what a structure variable is I have no clue how to
answer this.  We had 45 questions and these are the two most stumping.
 Help would be appreciated on these two questions.
```

#### ↳ Re: Several Homework Questions please

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <donald_tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2002-03-27T17:27:44-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MGro8.5996$oz6.1720344@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<b7dbb410.0203271409.4a2f358d@posting.google.com>`

```
"Ryan Hamm" <rhamm90210@icqmail.com> wrote in message
news:b7dbb410.0203271409.4a2f358d@posting.google.com...
> I've searched every place I can find on the net (ZingCOBOL) and a
> couple of books such as Structured COBOL Programming second edition,
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>

None of the above.  Cobol cannot have a numeric field without a picture
clause, so there is no such thing as a default.  The storage MUST be
defined.

> 2.  A structure variable can not contain another structure variable.
> True or False
>

False, though I have never heard it called a structure variable in Cobol, I
am assuming you mean something like:

    01 this-is-a-record.
        02 this-is-a-group.
            04 this-is a-variable    picture x.

> Since I can't find what a structure variable is I have no clue how to
> answer this.  We had 45 questions and these are the two most stumping.
>  Help would be appreciated on these two questions.

Donald
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Several Homework Questions please

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2002-03-27T23:19:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vsso8.25433$n8.25381@nwrddc02.gnilink.net>`
- **References:** `<b7dbb410.0203271409.4a2f358d@posting.google.com> <MGro8.5996$oz6.1720344@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Donald Tees <donald_tees@sympatico.ca> wrote in message
news:MGro8.5996$oz6.1720344@news20.bellglobal.com...
> "Ryan Hamm" <rhamm90210@icqmail.com> wrote in message
> news:b7dbb410.0203271409.4a2f358d@posting.google.com...
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> defined.

The PICTURE clause does not define the USAGE. If no USAGE clause is present
for a numeric data item, and no group-level USAGE is in effect for that item
DISPLAY is assumed.

Therefore, the correct answer is what DISPLAY usage is :"zoned decimal"

BTW, USAGE COMP-1 or COMP-2  numeric data items do NOT use a PICTURE clause.
(Neither do pointers, but those are not really numeric fields).

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Several Homework Questions please

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-27T17:14:56-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a7tqqh$4lh$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<b7dbb410.0203271409.4a2f358d@posting.google.com> <MGro8.5996$oz6.1720344@news20.bellglobal.com> <vsso8.25433$n8.25381@nwrddc02.gnilink.net>`

```

"Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote in message
news:vsso8.25433$n8.25381@nwrddc02.gnilink.net...


> BTW, USAGE COMP-1 or COMP-2  numeric data items do NOT use a PICTURE
clause.

Ummm... As an advocate of portability, I must comment.

All of the COMP-n USAGEs are extensions to standard COBOL, be that standard
'68, '74, '85 or the upcoming 2002.   Some implementations of standard COBOL
have NO COMP-n extensions at all.

What one implementor chooses to mean by COMP-1 in an implementation as an
extension to standard COBOL on a certain machine has nothing to do with what
another implementor chooses to mean by it on another (or even the same)
machine.

I know of one implementation that, as you suggest, allows COMP-1 through
COMP-4, disallows PICTUREs on COMP-1 and COMP-2, and requires PICTUREs on
COMP-3 and COMP-4.  All four are marked as extensions to the standard (in
this case, ANSI X3.23-1968) in the documentation.

I know another implementation  that requires PICTUREs on COMP-1 and COMP-2,
doesn't allow them on COMP-4 and COMP-5, and doesn't allow COMP-3 at all.

COMP-2 in the second implementation is a whole lot like COMP-3 in the first;
COMP-4 and COMP-5 in the second are sort-of-similar-in-philosophy (though
not at all alike in internal structure) to COMP-1 and COMP-2 respectively in
the first, and COMP-1 in the second implementation has no parallel in the
first at all.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Several Homework Questions please

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-03-28T11:57:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CA3052A.29F72201@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<b7dbb410.0203271409.4a2f358d@posting.google.com> <MGro8.5996$oz6.1720344@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```

> > 1.  In COBOL, the default storage for a numeric field is
> > ________________
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> defined.

Actually 'DISPLAY' is the default.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Several Homework Questions please

- **From:** epc8@juno.com (E P Chandler)
- **Date:** 2002-03-28T08:59:45-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7f64e2ff.0203280859.7e20c713@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b7dbb410.0203271409.4a2f358d@posting.google.com> <MGro8.5996$oz6.1720344@news20.bellglobal.com> <3CA3052A.29F72201@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message news:<3CA3052A.29F72201@Azonic.co.nz>...
> Donald Tees wrote:
> 
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Actually 'DISPLAY' is the default.

The original question is *very badly* written.

1. What does the standard say about how DISPLAY items are stored?

2. I've seen various methods of storing DISPLAY items on the PC
including:

                     (1)    (2)    (3)
 digit  ebcdic     ascii  ascii  ascii
          char      char   char   char
u  0-9     0-9       0-9    0-9    0-9 
+  0-9   {,A-I     {,A-I    0-9  @,A-I
-  0-9   },J-R     },J-R    p-y    P-Y

           hex       hex    hex    hex  
         f0-f9     30-39  30-39  30-39
         c0-c9  7b,41-49  30-39  40-49
         d0-d9  7d,4a-52  70-79  50-59

(1) ibm-pc cobol 1.00, cobol "6.50", ms-cobol 2.2
(2) ms-cobol 4.5 (mf)
(3) fujitsu 3.0 (win32)

Note that the signed digits in column (1) don't fit a simple bit field
scheme.

3. Other than to explain the history of computers, why do people use
the obsolete term "zoned decimal" anymore anyway, particularly when
teaching? It doesn't mean much to someone who has never seen a real
live punched card.

4. How about bit field or bitmapped or nibble or nybble or half byte,
etc.
(Please don't flame me about non eight bit bytes <g>.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Several Homework Questions please

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2002-03-29T08:40:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CA4286F.7FAED6D7@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<b7dbb410.0203271409.4a2f358d@posting.google.com> <MGro8.5996$oz6.1720344@news20.bellglobal.com> <3CA3052A.29F72201@Azonic.co.nz> <7f64e2ff.0203280859.7e20c713@posting.google.com>`

```
E P Chandler wrote:
> > > > 1.  In COBOL, the default storage for a numeric field is
> > > > ________________
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> The original question is *very badly* written.

I suspect that the question is archaic and probably originated in the
60s or 70s.

> 1. What does the standard say about how DISPLAY items are stored?

No.  The question was asking how CLASS NUMERIC items are stored if the
method used to store them is not specified.  ie what is the default
_USAGE_.  That is reasonably clear because: 1) it is quite specific
about NUMERIC, which is a class, and 2) 3 of the answers are actual
USAGE options (as long as you recognise what zoned decimal is), the 4th
is spurious.


> 2. I've seen various methods of storing DISPLAY items on the PC
> including:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>           char      char   char   char

What has the 'EDCDIC char' got to do with it on a PC ?

Regardless, you are simply indicating that it is 'zoned' with a
different bit used as the '-' zone, plus in (1) it also uses a 'signed'
zone.

> 3. Other than to explain the history of computers, why do people use
> the obsolete term "zoned decimal" anymore anyway, particularly when
> teaching? It doesn't mean much to someone who has never seen a real
> live punched card.

The EDCDIC character set is simply a zoned set of bits deriving directly
from punched card bit patterns which had been in use for decades
before.  The variations of decimal encoding you noticed derives from
several points: different companies used different encodings for '-';
emulating EBCDIC zonings in ASCII was done in different ways.  <shrung>
this is nothing to do with the question anyway.

> 4. How about bit field or bitmapped or nibble or nybble or half byte,
> etc.

Why ?, do you thing they may be a possibility for being the default for
a CLASS NUMERIC ?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Several Homework Questions please

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** epc8@juno.com (E P Chandler)
- **Date:** 2002-03-28T21:06:34-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7f64e2ff.0203282106.389a12a0@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b7dbb410.0203271409.4a2f358d@posting.google.com> <MGro8.5996$oz6.1720344@news20.bellglobal.com> <3CA3052A.29F72201@Azonic.co.nz> <7f64e2ff.0203280859.7e20c713@posting.google.com> <3CA4286F.7FAED6D7@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message news:<3CA4286F.7FAED6D7@Azonic.co.nz>...
> E P Chandler wrote:
> > 
> > The original question is *very badly* written.
 
> I suspect that the question is archaic and probably originated in the
> 60s or 70s.
 
> What has the 'EDCDIC char' got to do with it on a PC ?
 
One mapping uses the same characters except they are in ASCII. So the
relationship between digits and characters is more complex than simple
"zoning". Even if zones are used, the zoning scheme varies.

I'm trying to illustrate the variety in the representations using the
question as a foil for discussion.

I wonder what "zoned decimals" look like on pre 360 IBM systems,
Burroughs, Univac or a PDP-10? What do they look like in SIXBIT or
FIELDATA?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Several Homework Questions please

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Edward Reid <edward@paleo.org>
- **Date:** 2002-03-29T19:55:07-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01HW.B8CA771B0ACA16F70F75D0D0@news-east.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<b7dbb410.0203271409.4a2f358d@posting.google.com> <MGro8.5996$oz6.1720344@news20.bellglobal.com> <3CA3052A.29F72201@Azonic.co.nz> <7f64e2ff.0203280859.7e20c713@posting.google.com> <3CA4286F.7FAED6D7@Azonic.co.nz> <7f64e2ff.0203282106.389a12a0@posting.google.com>`

```
On Fri, 29 Mar 2002 0:06:34 -0500, E P Chandler wrote
> I wonder what "zoned decimals" look like on pre 360 IBM systems,
> Burroughs, Univac or a PDP-10? What do they look like in SIXBIT or
> FIELDATA?

The current Unisys incarnations of the old Burroughs systems use 
EBCDIC, and the same zoned signs as the IBM 360 and its successors. 
There are two major Burroughs lines which are extinct; they used the 
same representation.

Packed decimal is a horse of a different color; though the digits are 
the same, just about everything else is different, and the usual rule 
is that for practical purposes they are incompatible.

The IBM 1401 used six-bit characters, so the internal representation is 
inherently incompatible with the 360. However, IIRC the punched card 
representation was the same -- just an overpunch in row 11 or 12 for 
negative or positive.

I note that the standard specifically leaves the representation of the 
sign up to the implementor when the SIGN clause is not specified. So as 
for the original question -- "In COBOL, the default storage for a 
numeric field is" -- the answer  "zoned" is just plain wrong. The 
standard allows implementation of the default sign as a separate 
character.

Edward Reid
Looking for work, will program 029 keypunch drums
```

#### ↳ Re: Several Homework Questions please

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@invalid.com>

"Ryan Hamm" <rhamm90210@icqmail.com> wrote in message
news:b7dbb410.0203271409.4a2f358d@posting.google.com...
> I've searched every place I can find on the net (ZingCOBOL) and a
> couple of books such as Structured COBOL Programming second edition,
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> is.

I assume you have a picture associated with each of these. While the storage
may depend on the picture (for example, the picture may contain editting
characters), I would have to pick "zoned" (although "zoned" is not a word
found in the COBOL community). The actual, common, and OFFICIAL answer is,
as you discovered, DISPLAY.

There is no such thing as "Compressed" storage. This is a trick answer.

>
> 2.  A structure variable can not contain another structure variable.
> True or False

I've been doing this for many decades and I have no idea what a COBOL
"Structure Variable" might be. It does not appear in any of the (many)
reference books I have, nor have I ever heard it used. Must be something
your instructor appropriated from C++ (which does have such a thing).

see: http://www.geocities.com/sstutor/structures.htm

Seems as if a structure variable (in C++) is a COBOL group item. If so, the
answer is TRUE.

>
> Since I can't find what a structure variable is I have no clue how to
> answer this.  We had 45 questions and these are the two most stumping.
>  Help would be appreciated on these two questions.
>
```

<a id="t-a38d3f11da"></a>
## Thread: How do you call a java program from a Cobol CICS program? — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### How do you call a java program from a Cobol CICS program?

- **From:** mwhitney26@aol.com (MWhitney26)
- **Date:** 2002-03-27T23:12:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20020327181215.19030.00000435@mb-fm.aol.com>`

```
Does anyone know how to call a Java program from a CICS COBOL program?  We are
using CICS TS 1.3.
```

#### ↳ Re: How do you call a java program from a Cobol CICS program?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-27T21:26:06-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a7u2gg$anv$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<20020327181215.19030.00000435@mb-fm.aol.com>`

```
From my usually reliable source,

"1) Java does not run in CICS 1.3
2) In CICS V2 you can use EXEC CICS LINK between COBOL and Java

3) We are working on allowing INVOKE between Java-based OO COBOL and Java
under CICS

4) You can't ever use CALL statements with Java, you can only INVOKE methods
that you inherited from classes.

5) You can use connectors from Java in WebSphere to start CICS transactions
in COBOL, but that is the wrong direction, I think."
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How do you call a java program from a Cobol CICS program?

- **From:** "Roland Schiradin" <schiradi@tap.de>
- **Date:** 2002-03-28T22:24:42+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a82naq$2deh$1@innferno.news.tiscali.de>`
- **References:** `<20020327181215.19030.00000435@mb-fm.aol.com> <a7u2gg$anv$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Bill,

CICS TS 1.3 support Java, but only compiled JAVA using the HPJ compiler.
Also the JVM isn't reusable so it might not a good option to use JAVA in
case of performance.

Roland
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Euro sign unrecognizible under TSO

- **From:** "Trent Douthat" <tdouthat@comcast.net>
- **Date:** 2002-03-30T05:06:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<EJbp8.477276$pN4.33507997@bin8.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<a7vbf1$2gl$1@newsreader.mailgate.org> <3ca394cc.13976216@enews.newsguy.com> <a81bgs$pv4$1@newsreader.mailgate.org>`

```
The hexidecimal value for the Euro symbol in EBCDIC is X'9F'. If the S/390
hasn't been patched with the character set changes then X'9F' may only show
up as the international currency symbol.

If you haven't got the keyboard map in your emulator set up so that one of
the keys inputs a hex 9F byte then you will have to SET HEX ON when you are
in edit mode in ISPF and manually enter the hex value of the byte to see
what character set you machine is using.
```

It happens occasionally, but only out of ignorance.  You are not
legally allowed to say "we fired him."  You can only confirm
employment dates and state whether he is eligible for re-hire.  That
last one might send up the red flag, but you'd be surprised how many
fired employees are eligible for rehire.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Off-topic: Rephrasing "employment terminated"?

- **From:** Steve Thompson <sthompsonNOSPAM@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-31T22:36:54-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CA7D5D6.51742A66@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<a7o5d1$4jga$1@mercury.vcu.edu> <zu6o8.102194$2q2.8621038@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <n49o8.41046$J53.1272791@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <a7t6kk$3dkq$1@mercury.vcu.edu> <CEF7414E6241F296.EF07288D1A4B07B7.D30D76E9D0C65343@lp.airnews.net> <koabau4290q7c17m2pj9c74o3ka1g09vl9@4ax.com>`

```
Ramone wrote:
<snip>
> It happens occasionally, but only out of ignorance.  You are not
> legally allowed to say "we fired him."  You can only confirm
> employment dates and state whether he is eligible for re-hire.  That
> last one might send up the red flag, but you'd be surprised how many
> fired employees are eligible for rehire.

>>>> I am not a lawyer. Any attempt to use this posting as legal advice makes this posting null and void. <<<<


I'm pretty sure you are wrong on this one. According to the
HR department of a former employer, there was a lawsuit over
the fact that some company DID NOT disclose that an employee
was fired (let go for cause). The company that hired this
person would not have hired them had they known about the
situation. It turns out that the employee that had been
fired from their prior position had the same set of problems
after being hired at the new company. Seems that the problem
is that this time they did more than threaten to do
something illegal...

So, yes, a company has the obligation to answer this
question. If they do not, then they open themselves to an
action should their failure to disclose derogatory
information result in problems. However, to protect
themselves, they can (and probably should) require that
reference requests must be done in writing with a hold
harmless clause.

Let me give a for instance: You terminate an employee for
threatening another with violence. You fail to disclose this
to a prospective employer. They then hire this former
employee, and a few months later they "go postal" and kill
someone.

Another for instance: You terminate an employee for
embezzlement. You don't disclose this and they are hired by
another company that is involved in the handling of large
sums of money. The person here figures out how to embezzle
money with the new employer... Guess who may be on the hook
for trebled damages?!?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Off-topic: Rephrasing "employment terminated"?

- **From:** Ed Guy <ed_guy@paralynx.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-31T20:59:10-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3CA7E91E.669E@paralynx.com>`
- **References:** `<a7o5d1$4jga$1@mercury.vcu.edu> <zu6o8.102194$2q2.8621038@bin4.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <n49o8.41046$J53.1272791@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <a7t6kk$3dkq$1@mercury.vcu.edu> <CEF7414E6241F296.EF07288D1A4B07B7.D30D76E9D0C65343@lp.airnews.net> <koabau4290q7c17m2pj9c74o3ka1g09vl9@4ax.com> <3CA7D5D6.51742A66@ix.netcom.com>`

```
Steve Thompson wrote:
> 
> Ramone wrote:
…[39 more quoted lines elided]…
> for trebled damages?!?

You can't run into that kind of trouble if you confine your answer to position title and 
dates of employment and state that you will give no further information - you then owe 
no "duty" to another potential employer.  You certainly CAN run into that sort of 
trouble if you give more information than that and another employer hires the person in 
reliance on it.

My standard answer (whether I was running a government OR a private IT shop) was "It is 
our policy to give out only dates of employment and position titles unless we receive a 
written release of liability from the former employee concerned". 
```

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Not exactly homework...

- **From:** praul@isot.com (Paul Raulerson)
- **Date:** 2002-03-28T10:07:21-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<40f8f32c.0203281007.265380e7@posting.google.com>`

```
Well, either I am going completely crazy, or I had best go find a job
where I only have to deal with one compiler at a time, and don't
switch back and forth constantly... :(

On second thought, I think I vote for the 'crazy' rule. 

I just had to switch some code from the mainframe to an AIX machine. 
Loaded up IBM's COBOL compiler, and foudn that the runtime is failing
anytime I specify ALTERNATE RECORD KEYS.

This is happening on both AIX 4.3 and on AIX5L, so I am utterly
mystified.  There are no reports on the IBM WebSites about it, and a
quick google search didn't turn up any complaints. I know it *used* to
work, or at least, I *THINK* it used to work. I have old code with
ALTERNATE RECORD KEYs in them that used to run. Go figure.

Oh, if I leave out the ALTERNATE keys, it works just fine. I suppose I
must be loosing my mind. Or maybe it is just time to retire. (*sigh*)
Anyway, if anyone sees a OGTWS error in this, please let me know. The
system fails on an
OPEN OUTPUT. It does not matter if there are one, two, or more
Alternate indexes defined, it fails so long as there is at least one.
THe relevant definitions are reproduced below from a test program I
wrote to test this nonsensical behavior with. The initial run of the
program will coredump, but at least attempts to create the files. The
second run (where some of the files are existing and schould be
scratched) causes an interesting error. It is reproduced below the
code.

Thanks
-Paul

           SELECT DATA-FILE-DF01
             ASSIGN TO
               'DF01'
             ORGANIZATION IS INDEXED
             RECORD KEY IS DF01-INDEX
             ALTERNATE RECORD KEY IS DF01-IPADDRESS WITH DUPLICATES
             ALTERNATE RECORD KEY IS DF01-DATE WITH DUPLICATES
             ACCESS MODE IS DYNAMIC.

       DATA DIVISION.
       FILE SECTION.
       FD  DATA-FILE-DF01.
       01  DF01-RECORD.
           05 DF01-INDEX                        PIC X(9).
           05 DF01-IPADDRESS                    PIC X(15).
           05 DF01-DATE.
              10 THE-MONTH                      PIC 9(2).
              10 THE-DAY                        PIC 9(2).



Error Mesasge:

IWZ200S  Error detected during OPEN for file 'DATA-FILE-DF01'. File
status is:
         30.           
Message routine called from offset 0x314 of routine _iwzCallUseProc.  
       _iwzCallUseProc called from offset 0x1608 of routine
_iwzIndexOpen.          _iwzIndexOpen called from offset 0x354 of
routine _iwzGenericOpen.          _iwzGenericOpen called from offset
0x1a4 of routine RDLOG1.
IWZ901  S  Program exits due to severe or critical error.
IOT/Abort trap(coredump)
```

- **Date:** 2002-03-28T13:13:19-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<40f8f32c.0203281007.265380e7@posting.google.com>`

```
I do NOT know that this is the problem, but I find the
  'DF01'
(as a literal) suspecious for a file with alternate record keys.  The
following is the information that I see about assignment-names in the
Programming Guide for COBOL Set,

"assignment-name
You can specify ASSIGN TO assignment-name to specify the target file-system
ID

and the file of the name as known to the system directly, or you can set a
value

indirectly by using an environment variable.

If you want to have the system file name identified at OPEN time, you can
specify

ASSIGN USING data-name. The value of data-name at the time of the execution
of

the OPEN statement for the file is used and has the system file
identification

optionally preceded by the file-system type identification.

The following example illustrates how inventory-file is dynamically (by way
of a

MOVE statement) associated with a file /user/inventory/parts.

SELECT inventory-file ASSIGN USING a-file...

FILE SECTION.

FD inventory-file ...

MOVE "/user/inventory/parts" TO a-file

OPEN INPUT inventory-file

The following example illustrates how inventory-file is dynamically (by way
of a

MOVE statement) associated with the index file parts and the alternate index
files

altpart1 and altpart2, associated with the fully-qualified DCE Cell-Relative
name

(/.:/Encina/sfs in this example) for the Encina server.

SELECT inventory-file ASSIGN USING a-file...

ORGANIZATION IS INDEXED

ACCESS MODE IS DYNAMIC

RECORD KEY IS FILESYSFILE-KEY

ALTERNATE RECORD KEY IS ALTKEY1

ALTERNATE RECORD KEY IS ALTKEY2

FILE SECTION.

FD inventory-file ...

WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

�) VALUE ' '.
```

Example, the first line 

  QOWN means field name = QOWN
  3    maens QOWN's length =  3 charactors
  COLHDG('OWNER') means column heading = OWNER  
Hope this help

## Thread: Help with xpediter, S0C4 when compiling. — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Help with xpediter, S0C4 when compiling.

- **From:** reverandevan@hotmail.com (Evan)
- **Date:** 2002-03-29T05:58:57-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<836e18bc.0203290558.459b4305@posting.google.com>`

```
I'm having a problem compliling a large program in Xpediter batch. I
keep on getting a 0C4, has anyone seen this happen before? or could
tell me what to do.

Thanks
Evan
```

#### ↳ Re: Help with xpediter, S0C4 when compiling.

- **From:** "Larry Kahm" <lkahm@bellatlantic.net>
- **Date:** 2002-03-29T15:06:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Lp%o8.1858$9d3.968@nwrddc01.gnilink.net>`
- **References:** `<836e18bc.0203290558.459b4305@posting.google.com>`

```
First thing that comes to mind is a question:  do you have enough region to
run the compile?  In other words, has this program successfully compiled
before?  It's the end of the month, is your site constrained on available
storage?  Make sure your batch job is not running a REGION= parm.

Second thing:  Contact your site's systems programmer and report the
problem.  They should take a look at Compuware's support center (FrontLine)
and see if there are any logged problems that are similar.

Hope that helps.
```

---

<a id="t-7f572ea08b"></a>
## Thread: interfacing mfcobol isam to PERL — 2 this month / 3 total

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### interfacing mfcobol isam to PERL

- **From:** mark_f_edwards@excite.com (mark edwards)
- **Date:** 2002-03-29T17:15:41-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<428ac1d0.0203291715.7d7562e8@posting.google.com>`

```
hello all -

i have just invested a great deal of time and effort getting perl to
talk to mfcobol isam files.  so far all i have done is read, open
input, but it appears that you can do anything cobol can do and a lot
more using perl.

i realize that there are a couple of modules on cpan (ISAM & CISAM)
that work well; except mfcobol has that IDXFORMAT"4" that is not the
informix c-isam data structure.  actually i am curious to know where
it came from, since it looks absolutely nothing like IDXFORMAT"0".

this posting is just testing the waters to see if anybody else is
interested.  forgive me if i have offended some die-hard cobol types
here.

thank you,
mark
```

#### ↳ Re: interfacing mfcobol isam to PERL

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2002-03-30T05:07:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<NKbp8.12995$Eb5.1192282@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<428ac1d0.0203291715.7d7562e8@posting.google.com>`

```
idxformat is similar to idxformat "0" (On pc's), but
remove limitations on number of records that may
contain the same thing in a key that allows
duplicates.  The standard "0" allows no more than
65,535 records with the same thing in a key field.

Also improves speed under those conditions.



    0   System specific default (for this COBOL system, same as 3).
    1   C-ISAM format.
    2   Micro Focus Level II format.
    3   The format used by this COBOL system.
    4   An optimized form of the format used by this system, for fast
        duplicate key handling.
    5   Btrieve format (with ANSI conformance emulation).
    6   Btrieve format (without ANSI conformance emulation).


There may be more in NetX.



"mark edwards" <mark_f_edwards@excite.com> wrote in message
news:428ac1d0.0203291715.7d7562e8@posting.google.com...
> hello all -
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> mark
```

## Thread: Programming, Compiling, Bebugging Explanation Please — 3 msgs

### Programming, Compiling, Bebugging Explanation Please

- **From:** "Greg" <SinfulGreg@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-30T04:58:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<SBbp8.138705$af7.70302@rwcrnsc53>`

```
Network Blitz
I would like to know if someone could explain what I need Software wise to
start learning programming?
I spent a lot of time learning about the phisical parts and building them,
Testing voltages and how they work but just realized after all that I don't
even know where to start on the programming. I have took QBasic for a couple
of months a few years back.
Do I need a different Compiler and Debugger for MS VB than I do for C++ or
Pascal?
What does the Compiler do? Does it change the language into assembly
language? Or the opposite? I don't have to write in the 1 and 0's I hope?
Yikes!
What the easiest language to start with that will help me understand?
I guess what I am also wondering is if when you are writing Programs if you
have to write registry entries, or be concerned with the hardware in the PC
or if that is done by the Debugging and Compiling programs.
Any and all explanations will help.
Send them directly to me if you want.
Thanks
Greg
```

#### ↳ Re: Programming, Compiling, Bebugging Explanation Please

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@invalid.com>
- **Date:** 2002-03-30T13:49:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Nnjp8.481986$pN4.33873015@bin8.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<SBbp8.138705$af7.70302@rwcrnsc53>`

```

"Greg" <SinfulGreg@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:SBbp8.138705$af7.70302@rwcrnsc53...
> Network Blitz
> I would like to know if someone could explain what I need Software wise to
> start learning programming?
> I spent a lot of time learning about the phisical parts and building them,
> Testing voltages and how they work but just realized after all that I
don't
> even know where to start on the programming. I have took QBasic for a
couple
> of months a few years back.
> Do I need a different Compiler and Debugger for MS VB than I do for C++ or
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> I guess what I am also wondering is if when you are writing Programs if
you
> have to write registry entries, or be concerned with the hardware in the
PC
> or if that is done by the Debugging and Compiling programs.
> Any and all explanations will help.
> Send them directly to me if you want.
> Thanks
> Greg

You DO have to write using ones and zeros. Often there are not enough of one
or the other, so you have to improvise.

Stick with hardware.

Oh, you might take the introductory programming course at your local junior
college - just to get a passing familiarity.
```
